### [email redacted] — 2012-09-21T16:59:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-53172>

If you are interested in joining in on a group chat/skype/conference, I am trying to coordinate one.

It's informal (meaning not part of any of Jack's programs). . . like a virtual "let's meet for coffeee" kind of thing.

Depending on what times work for the majority, and how many are interested at any given time - we are going to try to do a video conference. .. failing that an online chat or conference call.

SO if you are interested email me:

[[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

Let me know your forum ID and what times work best, what time zone you are in and whatever else you think might be relevant.

Cavemam

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-26T17:32:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-54425>

Lyndra and Patty - so sorry! Organizing it now. . . I'll let you know on here on next thing that goes out. I had your email wrong Patty, and Lyndra I left you out because you were already in my email list and blah blah excuse.

We've got a lot of folks now! I'm kinda excited!

We have 18 people counting me. . .too many for group video. The chat window from Spreecast seemed to work well for everyone.

So here is what I propose: I will set up a spree cast and be the host (producer) for this one. We can kinda see how it goes. . worst case scenario you just have to look at me type and we can chat like we did last night during the webinar.

Also, Friday night seems to be the "best" time for many. Probably we should switch it up frequently so the same people are not left out for every time.

SO, make some alternate suggestions/threats/ideas here. . .but I'll go enter yall into a spreecast group and send out invites today

6:00 p.m. MST/ 7:00 CST.

Also, I think it should be like a you-call-it poker game. Different hosts for each chat and the host can bring topics or rules or ideas.

Let's jump in, see how it works then we can do more planning for future ones.

I am doing the settings as "open to anyone with link", and I'm going to delete it after we are done. Mostly because I don't want my rude smart ass comments out in the world for very long

Cavemam

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-11T16:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-57587>

I did some snooping and testing, it looks like when someone drops out - or opens a new window and double opens the webcast, it leaves them up as a Guest. . . .so I'm unsure if we had unannounced guests last time, but there is a possibility we didn't. I'm a little dissapointed frankly. I'll show a little more skin this time and see if it helps our hit counts.

At this point, I don't think we are popular enough to need to worry about security - but let's keep investigating better formats. I didn't see a lot of hands waving excited to do a full multi-person video conference, and once we get more than 10 people it becomes a tad ordealish (read gotta pay). I do think there is probably a BETTER medium than spreecast, as the chat window is so small.

I liked this as it took me all of 1 min to set up and it's intuitive, anybody can use/run it. Seems like really good platform in general, no real techy glitches and it's free!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-14T18:36:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58121>

> **BJK77;59554 wrote:** A real campfire + Malbec is where this needs to go . . . no degradation! A live group meet-up would be great one of these days.

I agree. Since that isn't something likely to become a frequent enough community engagement - how about we try for a.m. or mid-day chat next time? A cold bath chat?

I think being able to "see" is a nice aspect of the process. . . but we have to go with what people are comfortable with and perhaps the "both" option does seem to be the best of our choices.

Personally, I like the laughs and jokes as much as the info - so I kinda do like the format of speakers + chat. . It keeps the flow with fewer awkward pauses imho .Iinput from others? My thought is when it's live the fun stuff is that, fun without really harming or mis-interpreting the message. I mean, if we can't joke about all of us old ladies and sex and the merits of cougarific, what else is there!

I don't want to move into territory of being disrespectful to anybody talking, so I think there has to be a certain comfort level of "we are among friends" kind of vibe going on.

And we need more of the guys to join us.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-14T19:53:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58137>

I agree on the live meet up...that would be such a dream!

Not just for the campfire at night but also just cooking and eating together with folks who GET it about eating....and don't have to have cakes, donuts and other junk food around to have a great time!! We could hike together, jump in the lake or river together (most lakes and rivers near where I live, you're not allowed to ugh!)

Wouldn't it be fun to share recipes and other practices we do/use...Maybe even have a real retreat....practicing implementing stuff that would benefit everyone on this path....especially for newbies who get to feel empowered in making changes (if they are ready)...but also just share on a deeper level too....

I can see it now....a real epi-paleo retreat living in tune with nature at a camp or resort....where the food and lifestyle is getting us closer to optimal one meal and one CT at a time...we all go to bed with the sun and up with it too...sleeping in tents or even outside.

I would TOTALLY love that...

As to time for future chats, I too think doing it earlier is a great idea. I actually ended up completely darkening my PC and just listening to the people on camera; I missed the chat part but I am so early in the leptin Rx, I didn't want to jeopardize (or as little as possible) my (hopeful) progress on the circadian front, LOL. Though I have to say - Cavemam, Kathylu, Gretchen, KiwiLauren, y'all looked AMAZING!!!! And it was really great to 'see' you. Here's to hope for thick, beautiful hair!!

You have NO idea how great it is to know you guys are out there too....though I am SO new here, I feel like you guys are my tribe and I so love the inspiration y'all give me to push myself (and not be called crazy for the way I eat!).....woohoo!!

A human brain can not form without great functioning thyroid that is filled with brain specific nutrients.
>
>
>
>
>
> Alkaline Phosphatase (25.00 - 150.00)
>
> mid point average(25, 150)=87.5
>
> range (150-25)=125
>
> 25% below mid point =87.5-0.25\*87.5=65.625
>
> 25% above mid point =87.5+0.25\*87.5=109.375
>
> dr Kane flags items +-25% of range
>
> \---------------------------------------------------------------
>
>
>
> Dr Kruse, if below 60 anorgasmic
>
>
>
> dr Kane
>
> if Alkaline Phosphatase is low, it correlates with low:
>
> Vitamin C
>
> Zinc
>
> Phosphatidylcholine
>
> vit D3
>
>
>
> .
>
> \---------------------------------------------------------------------
>

>
>

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-15T00:53:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58203>

I'd like to put three things out for discussion re: upcoming group chat/modern campfire calls:

1) As others have stated, let's try to find a time when it's daylight for most everyone. When I do consults with Dr Kruse it is usually mid morning here and early afternoon there. Are there folks from Europe trying to get on these calls? That will add another dimension. But perhaps we can alternate times. Something like a Saturday afternoon for the US folks maybe?

2) I would like to encourage everyone to take a turn on camera who isn't feeling too shy to do this. I realise some do not want to be on camera at all and that's totally OK. At the same time, I think there are some who would be OK with being on camera but don't necessarily feel they have something 'special' to say. What encouraged me to get on camera (all I did was ask a question!) was how lovely it was to connect with the faces of others. I'd love to meet more of you that way.

3) I know in advance of our last call many people submitted ideas/topics/questions they wanted addressed. I'm really keen to hear the opinions of others, but I'd like to make sure these things are prioritised while also being flexible with our time (and making sure we can joke around -like Cavemam, this aspect is the most appealing part to me... I like connecting with you all in a way that isn't all about serious stuff and heavy thinking). I think we are all going to default to listening to whatever Dr Kruse has to say and I always gain so much from him. With these calls, I want to equally hear from everyone else, too. When Dr Kruse is there I totally want him to be answering our questions (if he's OK with that), but I want the content to be driven by the whole group (some of which was submitted in advance), not just by what's on Dr Kruse's mind. I hope that doesn't sound ungrateful. I am aiming to have something different here with these calls, something that is both social and learning. Does that make sense?

Please feel free to disagree. I'd like to hear everyone's thoughts.

I love that....I totally love the social/fun piece of it (though I'm still so new) too....though I also crave the learning too

### kathylu — 2012-10-15T16:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58298>

I like the idea of a meet-up too! Probably impractical for most of us though. Colorado in the summer is a real draw...I'd try to make it. I could also visit my cousins in Longmont, so there's extra incentive.

I can see two major results from these chats. One is the support and camaraderie formed when friends get together to talk. It's really nice to be able to put a face on the people who post at the forum. Also, the support is great. It is wonderful to be able to discuss the details (humor and frustrations) of this lifestyle with people who really get it!

The second result could be enhancing our understanding of the science behind the epi-paleo lifestyle. I have a suggestion about this. If you have a question, be willing to come on screen and discuss it. Also, do your homework first. If you have a question about metformin (for example), research metformin. Understand how it functions in the body and what biochemical pathways it affects. You may answer your own question that way, but if not, you will be prepared to discuss the details in a meaningful way.

I like the idea of submitting general topics in advance...that way we can all do our homework! Also, I noticed that when Jack joined us, the energy of the chat changed, people were more reluctant to speak. That's understandable, since we are all a little in awe of Dr. K, but I would still like to see the chat be a discussion among us all. So maybe we could all look at where that feeling comes from and work to reduce it.

### kathylu — 2012-10-15T16:25:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58300>

This is probably not the right place to post this, but I complained (again!) about my trouble with cycloset and nausea. Gretchen suggested that I try to eat more fat at breakfast and Dr. K seconded that. It's funny, but that hadn't occurred to me, I was actually reducing the fat in my diet because of the nausea (it just didn't appeal to me). Anyway, I did an about-face and started to eat a seafood meal drenched in paleo mayo (olive oil) and sure enough, it helped! I guess sometimes we have to get out of our own way.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-15T17:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58316>

I'm loving all this feedback on the chat! I hope everyone feels comfortable saying what they think here. Please do it, even if you are uncomfortable!!

I think one of the things sub-optimal in our world today is people don't seem to be able to communicate and disagree and offer/take/reject others opinions and feedback in a respectful manner that allows for real connection.

The strength of any community and relationship is how they handle disagreement. On the internets this usually ends in a flame-war situation, so I'm excited that we seem to be on the right path.

So please offer your opinion and feelings - we won't all be agreeing with you. we will have different opinions - and I think we can navigate those things and get past them.

### curves64 — 2012-10-15T18:56:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58330>

I am salivating at the chance to have regular "in person" discussions. I'm a total extrovert so communicating without being able to see, hear, taste and touch you feels unnatural for me. This is the first forum I've ever been remotely attracted to but I feel like I didn't read the rule book, and don't know how to communicate in this way. Most of the time I hang back rather than participate. So, please count me in, and let me know if I can help facilitate in any way. I love the intelligence, compassion and open-mindedness in which you all explore these ideas.

### curves64 — 2012-10-15T19:20:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58339>

> **Cavemam;59843 wrote:** How Ironic! Curves, welcome to introvert world! I think by nature of this format, it attracts a ton of introverts and seems odd to extroverts. Now you can take that uncomfortable feeling and have a little connection with the next introvert you are around in person - we are uncomfortable in the "in person"!

You have no idea how hard that lesson has landed. I want to reach out and hug every introvert I meet, but you all probably wouldn't like that -- am I right? LOL. Give me a room full of people any day, and I'll make 10 new friends. Here on the forum, I feel completely vulnerable and uncomfortable. It made me think of my daughter and me. She is an introvert with respect to the way she processes information. I'd want to "talk things over," and she'd want to be left alone to process things by herself. Made my head hurt until I figured out there is no right or wrong. we just approach things from a different perspective.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-15T20:39:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58369>

I see a couple of purposes in the chats. One as kathylu & shijin suggest, using it as a chance to have an in-depth conversation about a health topic. That means studying in advance and being prepared. The other purpose is building the "tribe" as Cavemam put it. For me, that's just random chatter that isn't too scripted so there's no "performance anxiety" about whether you know enough to have the floor and speak. Can we satisfy both needs in one chat? That certainly happens in real life (imagine sitting around the campfire and sometimes you're deadly serious and a moment later laughing till you hurt).

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-15T23:59:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-58416>

You asked for respectful disagreement, Mam, so here I go out on a ledge....

I actually don't think our self-organised calls are the place for Dr Kruse to air his complaints. Every one of us reads everything he writes on the forum and blog. If he wants to complain about things, we will all read and listen. For me, the calls are about mutual-support and shared-learning. They are without a hierarchy... we can all be on camera (or not), we can all ask questions (or not), we can all give answers (or not). One of us is not more qualified than any other, though we each have strengths and weaknesses. This is not aimed as criticism of Dr Kruse in any way, but I think if you join one of our calls it should be because you want to learn and you want to connect with others.

> The strength of any community and relationship is how they handle disagreement. On the internets this usually ends in a flame-war situation, so I'm excited that we seem to be on the right path.
>
>
>
> So please offer your opinion and feelings - we won't all be agreeing with you. we will have different opinions - and I think we can navigate those things and get past them.

Yeah C, I know you don't like the woo woo hippie stuff and I live for it  Somehow we get along anyways  Pretty cool.

### curves64 — 2012-10-19T00:03:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-59043>

> **kathylu;60582 wrote:** Guilty admission. I ran out to pick up a movie from Redbox last night and I forgot I had the Uvex glasses on. I know they say you are not supposed to drive with them on, but my vision seemed perfectly fine, no problem. So, I may do it again!

Now, when I'm out on the freeway without them, the oncoming headlights are way too strong and I get a headache.

### ealachan — 2012-10-19T00:08:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interested-in-skype-chat-with-other-members-here.3804/post-59049>

I drive with mine on frequently, but it's always late at night /early in the morning (4:30am) when there are no pedestrians around, and I live out in the country. The only issue I've ever had while driving with them on is that headlights tend to blend right in with all the other street lights / outside lights on peoples' houses....so sometimes a car will literally look like it's coming out of nowhere. Slightly disconcerting!

### curves64 — 2012-10-19T00:30:12+0300

# Thread: Cycle changes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2012-09-22T02:48:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53341>

I'm wondering if anyone else has experienced changes in their cycles when starting implementing many of the things here.

Since my consult in early Aug., I've been eating seafood/fish 1-2 times per day (most days) and eating mostly epi-paleo, wearing blu-blockers at night, eating 4 hours before bed, and have added supplements, including DHEA.

My most recent cycle was about 23 days, when normally I'm at 30+. I usually have PMS, cramps - this time, nothing (which was really exciting!). But, the cycle is also longer than normal. I'm assuming that this is a good sign that things are changing, but also checking that maybe it's not.

My sleep has gotten better, and even if I wake up during the night, I feel so much better during the day- I'm not getting the tired feeling during the day like I was. The only thing is I'm still getting cravings and really wish they would stop.

I haven't started any other hormones - I have an appointment upcoming to talk about BHRT, which I'm really looking forward to. I'm guessing there will be more changes coming once I add BHRT!

### ealachan — 2012-09-22T16:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53399>

I've definitely noticed changes in my cycle, yes. I used to run about 30 days, quite regular, pretty bad PMS the week before it started, heavy flow the second day. After a couple months doing the Leptin Reset, my PMS was noticeably better, and cramping was much less. This last time (since starting to eat more seafood and tightening up my light exposure), I was at 29 days, had barely any PMS symptoms in the week before my period, and not one single cramp. My period because a non-event, which was GREAT. Hoping the trend continues!

For what it's worth, I'm not doing any BHRT, either.

### diane — 2012-09-22T17:02:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53400>

Thanks so much for the reply ealachan! I had assumed I would see cycle changes, but this was pretty fast and pretty different - so wasn't sure what to make of it.

I'm definitely going to do BHRT - given my age (45) and my labs. I actually had an appointment this week, but had to reschedule because of an immovable work meeting - and the closest appointment was almost a month away! I guess more and more people are getting on the train LOL.

My sense is that a lot of the change was from seafood - plus some of the supplements I'm taking for leaky gut. And seeing that you had a similar reaction - that might be the key!

### ealachan — 2012-09-22T18:34:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53411>

I'm not actively avoiding BHRT, for what it's worth...I just a) haven't had any tests done yet and b) am only 32 years old.

I agree that the changes were pretty fast for me, too. I had only really been ramping up the fish in earnest for about two weeks when my last period showed up, and the difference was pretty noticeable.

### Endless — 2012-09-22T19:19:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53425>

My cycles have been regular before starting the LR, but TOM was very short and heavy. This month I had much more cramps and clotting, and it lasted for 8 days! I'm hoping it's more of a detox thing than a permanent change.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-22T23:38:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53488>

I went for DEEEEEEP Ct after the Webinar - I was doing 29-35 degrees for 30-45 mins a show 4X a week. I ended up with a 15 day bleed and freaked out. Got all the goods checked out and was back to norm when I stopped CT for the summer. I figure it was 35 year old broken down crap coming out!

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-23T01:11:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53508>

My cycle has been all over the place since starting the LR and working my way toward optimal since I began June2011. Initially I didn't have a cycle - what nursing a 14mth old. 2 months into the LR I started cycling again, and discovered that my migraines were 100% hormonally driven. I started CT mid Feb - saw some changes in my cycle length, added BHRT saw my cycle bounce from 32 days to 26 days. I've also seen my migraines decrease, but not go away. I've also seen the length of my TOTM go from 5-10days down to 3 days w/1 day of spotting. I'm cycling ever 26/27 days. I need more Pg. as I'm still estrogen dominant, along w/having PCOS.

### Endless — 2012-09-23T03:09:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-53533>

> I went for DEEEEEEP Ct after the Webinar - I was doing 29-35 degrees for 30-45 mins a show 4X a week. I ended up with a 15 day bleed and freaked out. Got all the goods checked out and was back to norm when I stopped CT for the summer. I figure it was 35 year old broken down crap coming out!

I have not attempted those temps yet, but that explanation seems to make sense...I'm hoping that's the case for me too.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-25T15:00:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-54094>

My cycles have been alternating since May. One month it is 24-25 days, the next it is 42-43 days. I've read that the ovaries alternate releasing eggs so I think one of mine may be running out? Not sure how to test for that. Does that mean that I'm nearing menopause? I'm only 42.

### MsYo — 2012-09-26T02:23:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-54309>

My cycles are 28 days like clockwork. I was being pressured into a hysterectomy (44, 2 myomectomies, and fibroid pressure). DIM, SSS tonic (iron) and vitamin D was a noticeable improvement in less than a month. Added a low dose progesterone and started paying attention to circadian rythms. My periods are beyond "normal" in flow and length. My weight has stopped bouncing all over the place too. I'm looking forward to my educational consult to conquer that since all other issues are progressing NICELY.

By the way, Ealachanm I had my first myomectomy at age 28..... chances are my hormones were off then but no one checked. Make sure you check especially if you have other issues.

### MsYo — 2012-09-26T02:28:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-54314>

> **kjgraffin;54798 wrote:** My cycles have been alternating since May. One month it is 24-25 days, the next it is 42-43 days. I've read that the ovaries alternate releasing eggs so I think one of mine may be running out? Not sure how to test for that. Does that mean that I'm nearing menopause? I'm only 42.

Sounds like something is not functioning properly. You PG/E2 ratio is probably off. I'm doing a saliva hormone panel for 30 days however, I've done the blood draw on day 21. My wellness doctor prefers the saliva tests for more complete data.

### melrito — 2012-09-26T15:15:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-54398>

> **ealachan;54050 wrote:** I'm not actively avoiding BHRT, for what it's worth...I just a) haven't had any tests done yet and b) am only 32 years old.
>
>
>
> I agree that the changes were pretty fast for me, too. I had only really been ramping up the fish in earnest for about two weeks when my last period showed up, and the difference was pretty noticeable.

I am 36 and despite a totally normal 28 day cycle with no PMS at all, I finally had a hormone panel done to have a baseline - and found out my hormones were in the toilet... so I would definitely recommend getting tested when you can, even if it does prove to be good - at least then you can see when things start to go downhill before you fall off the cliff ;-)

### margor_j — 2014-02-07T03:21:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cycle-changes.3814/post-108810>

hi girls,
when i first went to paleo (about 4 years ago) i had drastic improvements in my period within 1-2 cycles. however, as i started to get fatter and fatter and puffing up like a beeyatch, they went haywire again as well.
iv always had a 'problem' period - with PMT, bloating, cravings, headaches (omg, the headaches are HIDEOUS) BUT despite all that, regular as clock work, almost down to the hour.

so, im about 3 months into this site now and doing my best in all areas - including having relocated to a much better place, daily earthing on the beach, meditation, more water etc..

iv just finished a period. id say the PMT was about 60% reduced - no tears, and although i felt big, i didnt feel as big as usual (showered with the lights off tho, lol!) and it started, and then i had nothing for two days and then we were off. thats not happened a lot to me but yeah, i have had that before. but i had no cramps, no back pain and i didnt get the henous headaches that usually hit me at the end of it - which i did the month before. they can last up to a week - i wake up with them and theyre so hideous, i have to get out of bed. i cannot lie in bed.. like a vice around my head.

im only recently back onto transdermal progesterone (dr tim). id say iv upped seafood tho im not really too keen on scoffing too much tinned tuna, but im doing my best! its still seafood, right? tho a hit of tuna with avocado a good breakfast does make!

oh, the other thing that can go awry for me is sleep - before my period. which hit me full force this last period - but also, i was in a hotel, so who knows what was around me (emfs - tho there was no wifi in my room). but i wonder about flying to - i can travel up to 32 hours to go to work, but this was close - a 30 min flight. (i do the grounding JK recommends while im up in zee air) i still had the coffee cravings and cravings for carbs a couple of days BUT this time around i felt like living death for the two days prior - which is also new for me. zero energy, headache and SO fatigued it was ridic - the second i started my cycle, that all lifted. wtf!? hormones.. lol

mags


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: A nice way to get lots of Iodine throughout the day
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 37
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-09-22T08:42:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-53370>

I am doing algae-water these days. I love it.

I just take a glass jug and fill it with water, add some algae, I have many different kind and I vary them. Today I have carrageenan, yesterday it was wakame. I drink the water when thirsty, I ususally drink one jug / day. I eat the algae left too, together with my meal when the water is gone. Sometimes I use the same algae for 2 days, I just fill fresh water in the can.

It feels really good! I am imagine this must be the healthiest water ever...

### Inger — 2012-09-22T21:22:35+0300

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-23T05:57:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-53558>

> **angieonthegogo;54084 wrote:** How do you make your tea?

I pour hot water over a teaspoon or two of crushed arame. Let it steep, stir, and sip. I have also mixed arame with green tea. very good. I am finding a good way to introduce and accustom myself to the seaweed flavors is to make broths or teas with just the seaweed and sip it. I have done this with bladderwrack, kombu,wakame,hiziki, kelp and dulse. They are all different and interesting.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-29T16:07:31+0300

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-29T17:54:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55227>

Caution: Shameless show-off about one of my kids to follow!

One of my children is very intuitive about what he eats (the other 4 aren't). He has been that way since the beginning. He has plenty of issues too. If only I had gotten out of his way sooner, and known what I know now, I bet things would be different... Hashimoto's diagnosed (thanks to me at age 3), Celiac diagnosed (again, thanks only to me, at age 5).

Since age 5 (me feeling very defeated by all of this), I tried to get out of his way, and let him eat more what he wants (within parameters, of course), and less of what I thought he should eat. He's been happy to eat mainly fish and beef. His vegetable choices are few, and often they will change dramatically after a few months time. His fruit choices are few, and often only a serving or so of each fruit per season, often when we go out picking. Then he won't touch it again, often until the next season. He has always eaten limited grains (even the Weston Price soaked grains I used to make). He will have dairy, preferring the raw milk I pick up, and generally only cheeses that come from outside the country (KerryGold). He goes long stretches without wanting to eat (does not feel the need to snack). He's not perfect. He'll still love a piece of cake if it's offered to him. Sugar seems to destroy his intuitive sense, at least temporarily.

At the point of his celiac diagnosis 4 years ago, he was anemic, hypothyroid (because the old endocrinologist believed in keeping him at the highest TSH point to better stimulate his own gland - don't even get me started talking about this it makes my blood boil), mentally fragile, speech delayed, completely miserable. 4 years later, he is academically working 1-3 years ahead of his grade level, he sprints faster than any of the children in his school (small school where the grades 2-6 are taught together. He is in 4th grade, but can work at whatever rate suits him), can spontaneously do 15 pull-ups, whereas most of the other children struggle to do 1-2. All this with 2 auto-immune diseases. I blame the seafood! Now if only I could get him to put an oyster in his mouth...

Shameless show-off now finished. If only I could find the way for my other children to tap this intuitive sense that he seems to have so well. I would love to hear how Inger evolved this intuition. My impression from reading her posts is that it wasn't always this way...? They are all eating some form of fish daily now...

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-29T18:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55232>

So glad you wrote this! I love it! Proud mommy feelings are the best thing in the world - my intuition is that happyness is good for your health, too.

None of us here are coming from perfection - it doesnt exist, and for all those times fate landed you on the negative side of the coin flop just means that you got the positives a coming. Life finds balance. If your pendulum swung one way, it will always swing back later.

Anywhooo - I wanna see a happy pic of the brood.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-29T20:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55248>

If we get out of their way they often know what's best for them.

Thanks for sharing!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-01T23:51:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-nice-way-to-get-lots-of-iodine-throughout-the-day.3818/post-55682>

> **ealachan;56835 wrote:** What do you mean by you feel it in your thyroid? I don't seem to have had any adverse reactions since I started eating a lot of kelp every day, but I'm curious what i should look out for.

A feeling of tightness. Like something gently pressing on your throat. Like a tshirt that is up too high in front.

I put a small amount of sea salt in my palm and eat it, and drink plenty of water, and the feeling soon goes away.

I felt this way when taking lugols. I ignored it, and continued, and ended up with a bromaderma on my face in a week.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-02T00:12:31+0300

BTW, Inger, I liked the seaweed water. I put some dried seaweed in a quart jar, added filtered water, and set it in the fridge. I think I added too much seaweed, because it expanded and pretty much filled the jar. The taste was a nice ocean flavor.

My excess iodine symptoms have abated. I'll probably try some next week, but it will just be a tablespoon of the water next time. Or perhaps I'll measure the seaweed; be less generous.

_The findings of the genetic analysis may explain why early humans hung around their lakes for so long. It implies that to support complex brain function, they needed large amounts of the long-chain PUFA docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) - found in shellfish and fish. # Thread: Canadian First Nations Traditional Foodways
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-22T21:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53463>

There's some information on that in this documentary about Dr. Jay Wortman's work among First Nations peoples on the west coast in Canada, where he works trying to cure diabetes and other metabolic syndrome diseases with a low-carb diet.

Interestingly, one of the staples of the traditional diet was "oolikan grease" - an oil high in monounsaturates made from tiny fish, whose numbers are now unfortunately threatened.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;bjTmdvFH3gQ\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bjTmdvFH3gQ\[/video\]\[/video\]

Most of the documentary is taken up with what they did to combat the health problems but what historical information is given is interesting.

Jay Wortman has, apparently, travelled around Canada, interviewing elderly indigenous people, trying to find out more about their ancient foodways.

I heard he had offered to speak at AHS and was snubbed. After all why would anyone want to hear a medical doctor who works firsthand with people's problems and who has also personally done oral history investigations into the diets of pre-agricultural populations?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-22T22:36:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53469>

he was snubbed..........they do not want the message out that something else might be better.

### maggie — 2012-09-22T23:28:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53486>

I remember reading about this a couple of years ago and being completely fascinated learning about the existence of the Grease Trail--like the Silk Road! ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grease\_trail](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grease_trail) It was a trading route between coastal and inland peoples that made these valuable foods like fermented oily fish available to noncoastal tribes. IIRC, these foods were particularly valued because they were considered necessary for fertility. I wonder how many more of these traditional trading patterns there are that just invisible to us. I mentioned this in a thread on the Evolutionary Psych blog after Dr. Harris made a comment to the effect that a lot of hunter gatherers never ate a fish in their lives, but nobody picked up on it. I think this would be an awesome subject for a book--studying evolutionary nutrition through ancient trade routes.

I think Dr. K's writings are providing an epistemological lens that will bring a lot of seemingly unrelated stuff into focus--I remember the articles that were being discussed here on the baby who survived the morgue and about European peasants who slept the winters away. Anyway, fuel for the imagination if nothing else...

**

That's dramatic language. "Tethered** to the water". When people who know about brain chemistry speak of water-sourced foods as having that kind of importance, one had better take notice.r />

That's egg on the face for people who talk a lot about our evolutionary past, but who don't know that the most important phase in it took place around water.

As for consumption of fish and shellfish, the interesting thing is that on this issue even the mainstream knows better. Here is advice on diet from Britain's National Health Service:

> A healthy diet should include at least two portions of fish a week, including one of oily fish.
>
>
>
> That is because fish and shellfish are good sources of many vitamins and minerals. Oily fish – such as salmon and fresh tuna – is particularly rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids, which may help to keep your heart healthy.
>
>
>
> Most of us should have more fish in our diet, including more oily fish. ... Shellfish includes prawns, mussels and langoustine ... a good source of selenium, zinc, iodine and copper

[http://www.nhs.uk/Livewell/Goodfood/Pages/fish-shellfish.aspx](http://http://www.nhs.uk/Livewell/Goodfood/Pages/fish-shellfish.aspx)

### Michael — 2012-09-23T17:49:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53598>

> **Jack Kruse;54214 wrote:** CW does realize the benefit of fish........you wonder why paleo does not? I have my own ideas.

I could take a stab in the dark and might very well be going astray.

I think Cordain is probably correct to say that a lot of people in the dietary field are fairly rudderless. They lack any clear overarching theoretical framework.

However, I think this might mean that they expect any foodstuff might be "good" or "bad" with no particular rhyme or reason to it. If they're told fish is good and it doesn't seem to conflict with anything else they already believe, they'll be quite ready to accept that. Someone like Crawford at London Imperial publishes some papers and what's in them does filter down. And maybe dieticians don't think about _why it should be that we seem to be almost _dependent on shellfish and fish; but they're quite prepared to accept that it's desirable that they should be in the diet and take a handful of immediate reasons for that - that they're good sources of DHA, Selenium, Zinc, etc.

Paleo's problem might be that it _does_ have a framework - but it's not the right one. Or rather it has a aspiration to be in line with evolutionary biology. But in practice something more than a vague genuflection in the direction of natural selection is necessary. By itself that tends to be too abstract to tell you much. You need more models than that, and you need them to be closer to the phenomena. So, for example, you need a model for how encephalization took place. If you assume that's explained by what paleoanthroplogists have been speculating for getting on for a century now (plus a few updatings along the way), you're going to be badly out.

I have a feeling that this is a generalized problem with Cordain, learned and interesting as he undoubtedly is. He puts his faith in the models he's using and doesn't see that if there are problems with them, then it puts a big question mark against his conclusions. Here's another one: I have a hard time believing that early man in East Africa was eating a Zone-style 40:30:30 macronutrient ratio (and, moreover, that that wouldn't vary seasonally). I don't know where his model might be wrong, but I have an intuitive sense that it probably is. He's taken to saying to interviewers that the Paleo Diet isn't "his" diet but simply the human diet and how people have been eating for 2.6 million years. I don't know that's so much a statement of humility as a backhanded claim that what's he's offering is beyond challenge_. But in truth there remains a gap between "what men and women were eating in the Upper Paleolithic" and "The Paleo Diet™ ", the latter only approaching the former insofar as the models in use from which the reconstruction has been attempted are valid.

I enjoyed The Paleo Answer_ and thought it a superb book. However, if it were everything it should be it would carry a chapter specifically on fish that would discuss the shoreline hypothesis and the importance of the high level of brain-specific nutrients in seafoods. It would also carry quite specific recommendations to eat these foods regularly, too. Unless that's explicitly done the average reader is not going to take the message to heart.

I think one of the reasons why the average reader wouldn't is a species of romanticism. It seems to me that there's a popular image of the "caveman" as someone who carries a club and eats a lot of meat (probably raw). Now I've nothing against meat, and nothing against eating it raw, but it's possible to be over-influenced by images. I think meat is perhaps seen as macho.

That macho image of the caveman is probably what the anthropologists who suggested that the Venus figurines were being made by palaolithic men to masturbate to had in their minds. Only they thought of this hairy individual with his raw meat in his hand and _shuddered._ Others, of course, find the thought of machismo appealing.

This site was recently posted by someone at MDA. It's interesting that it both sells meat and says it's friends with various crossfit organizations:

[http://www.paleowales.co.uk/friends-of-paleo-wales](http://http://www.paleowales.co.uk/friends-of-paleo-wales)

Flogging yourself silly at a gym is, of course, macho, too.

Perhaps an over-civilized culture that tends towards vegetarianism and thinks that at best a very little meat, especially if there's no blood or bones and you can't see a vein, is partly to blame. A certain sort of person thinks, "Sod that." And some kind of counter-reaction like this is probably to blame for that rather odd bacon meme. Bacon is certainly tasty and "comforting", but it's been vilified so long as notoriously fatty and dangerously nitrate-laden that people probably wish to cock a snook at the nay-sayers.

So I think meat, and a lot of it, probably has a romantic image for some people. Therefore, best to say explicitly that there is an alternative that they ought consider at least X times per week.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T18:20:48+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T02:45:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-53951>

nice..............because I like this thread so much I am going to give you a preview of a blog coming in about 12 months........

**CPC: 12Autism**

ASDs (autism spectrum disorders) are hypothesized as one of many adaptive human cognitive variations that have been maintained in modern populations via multiple genetic and epigenetic mechanisms. Introgression from "archaic" hominids (adapted for less demanding social environments) is conjectured as the source of initial intraspecific heterogeneity because strict inclusive fitness does not adequately model the evolution of distinct, copy-number sensitive phenotypes within a freely reproducing population.

Evidence is given of divergent encephalization and brain organization in the Neanderthal (including a ~1520 cc cranial capacity, larger than that of modern humans) to explain the origin of the autism subgroup characterized by abnormal brain growth.

Autism and immune dysfunction are frequently comorbid. This supports an admixture model in light of the recent discovery that MHC alleles (genes linked to immune function, mate selection, neuronal "pruning," etc.) found in most modern human populations come from "archaic" hominids. The manner in which BDNF and melatonin are programmed and de programmed to do this on a nanoscopic level are just being elucidated by modern science.

Mitochondrial dysfunction, differential fetal androgen exposure, lung abnormalities, and hypomethylation/CNV due to hybridization are also presented as evidence of this phenomena...............What we do know now is that we have more control of these diseases than we currently think if we understand how to alter our behavior before the offspring is sprung.

### Michael — 2012-09-25T09:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-54067>

> **Jack Kruse;54626 wrote: nice..............because I like this thread so much I am going to give you a preview of a blog coming in about 12 months........
>
>
>
> CPC: 12Autism**
>
> ASDs (autism spectrum disorders) are hypothesized as one of many adaptive human cognitive variations that have been maintained in modern populations via multiple genetic and epigenetic mechanisms. Introgression from "archaic" hominids (adapted for less demanding social environments) is conjectured as the source of initial intraspecific heterogeneity ...

OMG. So it's suggested that it's a reversion to something much more archaic.

And this also makes a nice tie to the other thread:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4177-Interesting-paper-on-DHA](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4177-Interesting-paper-on-DHA)

That makes perfect sense. My understanding is that it's often (always?) linked to an inability to filter things out. There's so much information coming in all the time, via the senses, you can't attend to it all -- or not and get on with any complex task.

So, for example, the autist Stephen Wiltshire, originally written about by Dr. Oliver (Awakenings) Sacks many years ago can "draw Rome from memory":

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jVqRT\_kCOLI\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;jVqRT\_kCOLI\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jVqRT\_kCOLI\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;jVqRT_kCOLI)

There's a woman who's a professor somewhere - autism doesn't always preclude attainment - who checks out livestock situations for farmers. So she can walk a route the animals would follow to a slaughterhouse, and she knows why they will get spooked and stop at a certain place. It will be something quite minor that a normal person would miss - maybe they can see a light at that point on their walk - something like that. The point is said to be that she can see everything, like the animals would do, and doesn't "filter out" what seems to be irrelevant, as we do.

If you're doing what the animals do, it would make sense that that's something archaic.

The GAPS protocol is interesting stuff. But you get the impression reading it that Dr. Campbell-McBride feels that the brains of autists are getting poisoned in real time by waste metabolic products and so on, and if only their guts could be sealed and these substances stop crossing the blood-brain barrier all would be well. She probably doesn't actually say that. She probably says that the term "autism" probably covers different things, and that the symptoms (or some of them) may be reversible for some (but not all) patients.

It does seem more likely that in at least some cases this is something intractable and permanent. It seems intuitively likely, as soon as someone's said it, that this is something much more archaic getting "switched on". And what's going to have switched on the archaic trait? Something environmental, maybe pre-natal, the biggest factor here likely being food choices, though disturbed light-cycles might be another.

It would be the ultimate irony if the conditions of the modern world were causing our offspring to revert to something more archaic.

There is nowhere in the paleosphere where anyone would give you a taster of something to come as interesting as this. Nowhere.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T15:05:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-54095>

"It would be the ultimate irony if the conditions of the modern world were causing our offspring to revert to something more archaic."

Dear Mike,

That is precisely what I have been knocking the door on for the last 18 months. Become cold dark and sleepy and boring and watch your inner masterpiece form in front of your mirror without much work. When you let the nano machinery in you that has been forged by millions of years of empiric design work for you alone some amazing \*\*\*\* happens to you. You job is to just get out of your own way.........and it is hard to do in the modern world today.

I'm not a scientist but reading today's news and then here brought these thoughts to mind: Accord sky is falling news it is not just our financial and governmental systems that are about to fail but our food and energy systems also. ### AKMan — 2012-09-25T22:16:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-54236>

> **Jack Kruse;54626 wrote:** ASDs (autism spectrum disorders) are hypothesized as one of many adaptive human cognitive variations that have been maintained in modern populations via multiple genetic and epigenetic mechanisms.

From today's news:

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-boosting-natural-marijuana-like-brain-chemicals.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-boosting-natural-marijuana-like-brain-chemicals.html)

American and European scientists have found that increasing natural marijuana-like chemicals in the brain can help correct behavioral issues related to fragile X syndrome, the most common known genetic cause of autism.

Fragile X syndrome is caused by a mutation of the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome. People born with it are mentally disabled; generally experience crawling, walking and language delays; tend to avoid eye contact; may be hyperactive or impulsive; and have such notable physical characteristics as an elongated face, flat feet and large ears.

\---so maybe our ancestors smoked pot??

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-29T19:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-55238>

> **Michael;54269 wrote:** Some of that is dubious. On page 70 we're told that "man lived and thrived on a diet composed of eighty to ninety percent protein". A surprising claim which the authors look to have taken from Boyd Eaton.
>
>
>
> But by comparison the brain seems curiously absent from Cordain's books.

It seems to me that many use the word protein when they should be using the word "meat". Meat contains protein but protein is not meat. In that context people that ate 80-90% meat would have been getting a good amount of fat and lots of other compounds from the meat. It is unfathomable how the bulk of early man's diet would have consisted of 90% amino acids or am I missing something?

This reminds me of a tv documentary in which archeologists have located one of the very earliest cities on earth. The city was inland but there were fishing folk on the coast who traded sea products for agricultural products grown by the inland folk. I believe it was in South America somewhere.

### AKMan — 2012-09-29T20:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-55251>

An article was in our paper last week. It talks about Eskimo Potatoes. There is a plant here in Alaska that extends well into the arctic called H. Alpinium [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eskimo\_potato](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eskimo_potato) that the Eskimos used as food. Apparently this tuber was collected in large numbers and eaten by the Inuit and others when food was scarce.

So maybe the Inuit wasn't all-meat as thought.

"A named place: Troth Yeddha� goes before geographic board next month

Published September 23, 2012

By Jim Kari Fairbanks Daily News Miner

Community Perspective For more than 75 years, numerous Athabascan experts from the Tanana Valley, such as Moses Charlie, Peter John, Howard Luke and Evelyn Alexander, have proudly shared the place name for the ridge that underlies the University of Alaska Fairbanks campus.

Troth Yeddha�, or �Indian potato ridge,� refers to Hedysarum alpinum, a plant with an edible root that is commonly found in Alaska stream beds. This is the only indigenous vegetable with food value for Alaska�s Athabascan people."

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-29T20:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-55253>

the real safe starch? LOL

### nonchalant — 2012-09-30T02:34:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canadian-first-nations-traditional-foodways.3827/post-55314>

The Eskimo potato is also called sweet vetch.

Interesting. We have common vetch all over the place here. Beautiful flowers.

Hi, I kind of can't really figure out, which amount of iodine is best. As much as I can get? I read somewhere in a comment something about 300 mcgs. I'm confused and would be very grateful to get a hint.

Another question I have would be this - obviously my brain should get some more iodine -

does it have a lot of advantages to eat the seafood raw? I have great problems to get non-precooked seafood (I live in the Alps, so I get canned seafood - which is per German laws precooked... Frozen fish would be an option I think.)

Any advice would be greatly appreciated.

# Thread: Optimal Bribery!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-23T00:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53504>

I read sometime ago, that despite CW, bribery really works for teaching kids things.

So, I had that in mind this afternoon when. . .

I finished by cold bath, advised my son it was time for him to hit the Cold Tub (last time he did 10mins all on his own).

He wasn't really in the CT mode, wanted to quit and not sit down after putting his feet in.

So, I bribed. For each Minute you stay (up to a max of 15) you can have free choice on the ipad. Sitting outside after the bath.

he's listening to an audiobook, planning his junk ipading, and gettin some 65 degree CT.

Winter is coming. . .

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T00:50:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53505>

yeah what works for dogs works for kids........ and husbands.......

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-23T01:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53510>

some days we use bribery to get the kids to eat dinner.... some days its bribery to clean up their toys... when they're 4 & 2... what you bribe them w/is different... these days its the promise of grilled squid for lunch hehehehehehe

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T01:37:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53511>

[http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf](http://http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf)

LOL

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-23T02:05:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53518>

> **Jack Kruse;54153 wrote:** [http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf](http://http://carmine.se.edu/cvonbergen/Incentives.pdf)
>
>
>
>
>
> LOL

Heh, I'm considering the positive physical benefits of CT as the type of reward that could be considered self driven. . meaning he will feel there is benefit in the action.

Besides, last time I checked it was a bad move to hold your 6 yr old in a tub of cold water against his will

### diane — 2012-09-23T16:35:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53586>

Interesting - I've never believed that bribery works long term. Rewards - yes, but not every time, change it up and they need to be tied to intrinsic motivators. The issue with bribes is that there is always the choice to take the bigger and better bribe - or to negotiate for more.

I don't have kids, but have dogs. And I don't train my dogs with treats. It may take longer to actually figure out what makes your dog tick, but once you do and you connect with that - the results and bond are amazing. Then the learning part is easy.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-23T20:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53653>

I learned quite a while that rewarding kids for chores, cleaning up, etc, produced a horrible result, and horrible atmosphere. Everyone pits against each other, nobody does an ounce more than his "share," and every bit that I want them to do is like pulling teeth. Everyone screams about the unfairness of the whole thing while it's ongoing, and finally, I feel like dumping their reward on their heads! And this is when everyone gets a "reward." Ugh!!!

I do have expectations though. Like if they want to watch their TV show after dinner (this will need to move to earlier now that it's getting dark earlier), they need to have completed their music practice (lessons they all voluntarily have asked to have on instruments they picked). And if they want to continue with the lessons, they are responsible for completing the practising (20 min/day is the sweet spot for my age bracket). Another good one that works really well in my house has to do with making lunches for school. If you don't participate in the lunch making, you will not have a lunch the next day!! It's tough to follow-through with it though. I had to 2 weeks ago for my six year old (compromised and sent her with a can of fish only). I don't like to think of it as punishment, just "natural consequences."

Back to CT. I find that if I leave the bathtub full, they will often times partake, even compete with each other!!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-23T21:03:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53665>

hehe clearly I'm the mercenary mom! I have no qualms about it as far as CT goes. In reading through these other posts - I guess we have established clear "expectations" for most things. . .and don't really bribe in general, but for some reason this one feels like the good outweighs the bad! In all kinds of educational studies - they HAVE found that learning for a particular task is improved with an appropriate reward. I realize it does not speak to overall behavior and what that sets the person up for later in life. . . .

I'm going with it on this one as I see the reward (outcomes of CT) to have both components (external/internal) of a reward. He clearly still feels a sense of accomplishment, knowing that he CAN do it (bragging to daddy after that I DID IT!). . .Your cautions are noted however , and I will for sure be watching out for that.

I am going to use random reward though, I would never directly reward every time. As I think I've said before, it's probably a very good thing I never got myself in a postion to experiment on people. I'm sure I would be breaking all kinds of laws of state as well as of humanity.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-24T06:37:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53751>

> **Cavemam;54146 wrote:** I read sometime ago, that despite CW, bribery really works for teaching kids things.
>
>
>
> So, I had that in mind this afternoon when. . .
>
>
>
> I finished by cold bath, advised my son it was time for him to hit the Cold Tub (last time he did 10mins all on his own).
>
>
>
> He wasn't really in the CT mode, wanted to quit and not sit down after putting his feet in.
>
>
>
> So, I bribed. For each Minute you stay (up to a max of 15) you can have free choice on the ipad. Sitting outside after the bath.
>
>
>
> he's listening to an audiobook, planning his junk ipading, and gettin some 65 degree CT.
>
>
>
> Winter is coming. . .

awesome idea! I'm totally using this with my 8 year old. We really limit media time, so this might work well. When we first started CT in the pool last spring he thought it was fun and novel, but after a couple times decided he would rather not be cold. Hubby and I CT'ed in the pool yesterday at 63 degrees and I couldn't get him interested at all!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-24T18:15:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bribery.3832/post-53808>

> **Trisha;54409 wrote:** awesome idea! I'm totally using this with my 8 year old. We really limit media time, so this might work well. When we first started CT in the pool last spring he thought it was fun and novel, but after a couple times decided he would rather not be cold. Hubby and I CT'ed in the pool yesterday at 63 degrees and I couldn't get him interested at all!

That was my experience, he would be in the right frame of mind for it about 1X every two weeks. . .and now the whole family is CTing, we run one bath, then take turns so I want him doing it when it's "cold bath time".

At first it was fun, and we enjoyed the game of timing how long he stayed - and I would stay in there cheering him on, but once we hit 10 mins, he had zero interest in continuing. I totaly get how bribes can be a negative - but I'm going to experiment with this one.

So we limit media too. He gets 1/2 hr a day of media, and that is when he cooperates and willingly participates in "being part of the family" (aka chores).

So as a practical "stay at home working mother" thing, I work from home and he only goes to school 3 hrs day - so when I have phone calls that can't have a noisy boy in the background, he gets the ipad and can do freeplay (usually spongebob or angrybirds).

I don't like that, but it's our little routine and it keeps me home with him instead of at an office.

# Thread: Hard to understand
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-09-23T15:58:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53580>

A friend of mine, suddenly dropped dead at 56 yrs old. That is so hard to understand. He was always positive, friendly, in a good shape, active full life, no health problems.

He was the last one I would ever expect this to happen. There are so many others I know that are good candidates but not him.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T17:46:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53597>

I dont know why this is hard to understand..........time is not promised and nothing is guaranteed......we all know it but when it happens and we see it it shocks us........it should not. It should motivate us to live in the now, not in the past or the present. I see people die every week in the prime of their life..........maybe that is why I look at it differently than you.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T19:57:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53635>

A family member's work partner got a new job. He had a "beer belly" as the only indication of disease and switched to nightshift in the new job. 2 weeks later he suddenly dropped dead.

Needless to say, this family member is now not only listen to my never ending monologues about optimal health and the importance of \*light cycles\* but asking quite a few questions and coming up with surprising suggestions on how to improve his state of health. He has a beer belly.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-23T20:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53640>

I remember a chapter title from a book I read a long time ago. **_Death as an Advisor_**

My dad died when I was 22 of stomach cancer. No one had died in my family before that. I now try to use death as an advisor. It's sad when someone dies an early death. Hopefully that will bring "life awareness" to those around them.

I'm sorry about your friend Destiny. Always hard to have happen around us. I'm glad you are helping yourself and setting the example to those around you that striving for optimal is a better way to live.

### Destiny — 2012-09-23T20:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53642>

> **Jack Kruse;54239 wrote:** I dont know why this is hard to understand..........time is not promised and nothing is guaranteed......we all know it but when it happens and we see it it shocks us........it should not. It should motivate us to live in the now, not in the past or the present. I see people die every week in the prime of their life..........maybe that is why I look at it differently than you.

I understand there is no guarantee but it shocked me that this happened to a healthy happy go lucky person.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-23T20:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53645>

> **Destiny;54284 wrote:** I understand there is no guarantee but it shocked me that this happened to a healthy happy go lucky person.

This is why I now believe in the labs. They don't lie. A person that looks good and feels good on the outside may not be that way on the inside. I had a friend drop dead in his sleep (ok I guess he didn't drop but dead nonetheless) at 42, the _picture_ of health. Yah, I guess not too healthy.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-23T20:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53647>

> **Destiny;54284 wrote:** I understand there is no guarantee but it shocked me that this happened to a healthy happy go lucky person.

there is a lot of people who think I am the picture of perfect health.... when I touch on having health issues their eyes get real big and the unavoidable answer is "but you look so healthy and I KNOW you eat SO healthy??? that can't be true."

### Destiny — 2012-09-23T20:10:37+0300

### debbiemikus — 2012-09-24T00:03:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hard-to-understand.3842/post-53700>

Destiny,

My condolences on the loss of your friend, but I do understand how it can happen if we listen to CW. >
> I'm sorry about your friend Destiny. Always hard to have happen around us. I'm glad you are helping yourself and setting the example to those around you that striving for optimal is a better way to live.

I know this to be true from experience. My first husband died in a car accident at 27, I had just had my 25th birthday and we had a 7 month old son. When someone close to you dies it makes you value the gift of life and realize how important it is to love and care for those around you. There are no guarantees for anyone. I no longer dwell on the what ifs that used to haunt me, but try to savor moments with my family and friends with the awareness that our tomorrow's are limited.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Saw this article this AM about early man

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-23T17:14:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53592>

This was posted in my feed at facebook this morning. I thought it was interesting and placed the knowledge of organized hunting further back in history.

[http://m.guardian.co.uk/science/2012/sep/23/human-hunting-evolution-2million-years?cat=science&type=article](http://http://m.guardian.co.uk/science/2012/sep/23/human-hunting-evolution-2million-years?cat=science&type=article)

To me, the implication is that our species may be even older than 2 million years or the development away from the coastal zones was faster.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T17:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53595>

"What was not clear was the source of that meat." again this article is using the savanna hypothesis as its starting point and perspective. We already know from a physiology standpoint you cant build a human brain from and ape brain eating meat alone..........and there in lies the blind spot. You must look at the problem not as a bone collector ( all the people cited here were or are bone collector's) you need to look at the problem from a brain physiologic perspective to see what makes us different. Evolutionary metabolic neurologic taxonomy is where that problem is solved. Read Cunnane, Crawford etc.........then come talk to me. Lipid neuro chemistry is the missing link for bone collectors. This is why their theories do not add up.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-09-23T19:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53621>

Thanks Dr., I had forgotten that you had mentioned lipid nuero chemistry development. I just knew it did not ring quite true with what you have been teaching.

### AKMan — 2012-09-23T19:58:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saw-this-article-this-am-about-early-man.3843/post-53638>

Up in Northern Alaska, there are stone fences made by the first Inuits to corral caribou into an area they could be slaughtered and then heaped in piles where they would remain frozen for the winter months when food was scarce.

Seems to me, as soon as early modern man realized how smart they were, they started doing things like this which eventually led to our decline. Once we started living together, we had to come up with ways to feed everybody...organized hunts, herding, agriculture. This may have taken us away from our early start by the sea, but homo lived pretty well like this for a long time. As soon as we started grinding wheat, fattening cows, and processing sugar and oil it went to hell pretty fast. Then artificial light and the iPad. The smarter we get the dumberer we is.

# Thread: Interesting paper on DHA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-23T20:25:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53654>

Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA): An Ancient Nutrient for the Modern Human Brain

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3257695/)

Among other things:

> The late archaic humans (Neanderthals) sourced protein predominantly from the red meat of wolves, large felids and hyenas. There was no evidence of fresh water aquatic species or marine sources of protein in the bone collagen of the Neanderthal samples. In striking contrast, seafood consumption was a â€œstapleâ€￾ of the diet of the early modern humans (Mid-Upper Paleolithic period). Depending upon geographical region, freshwater or marine sources of protein constituted between 10-50% of the diet for the early modern humans. Freshwater sources occurred along rivers and included fish and/or water fowl and marine sources were coastal and included fish, shellfish and small slow-moving animals such as turtles/tortoises. When the radio isotope values were aggregated, the between-group difference in the values was statistically significant (p = 0.005).

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-23T20:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53658>

truth bomb alert? Maybe why the Neanderthals died out.........they forgot fish!

### AKMan — 2012-09-23T21:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53672>

From the abstract:

"Modern humans have evolved with a staple source of preformed docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) in the diet. An important turning point in human evolution was the discovery of high-quality, easily digested nutrients from coastal seafood and inland freshwater sources. Multi-generational exploitation of seafood by shore-based dwellers coincided with the rapid expansion of grey matter in the cerebral cortex, which characterizes the modern human brain."

Hmmmm....where have we heard that? This is a full-text paper, hard to find these for free. Everybody should read this. Good find!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-24T03:28:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53722>

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:47:11+0300

From BG 2 CAVEMAM:

Another one of my thought experiments comes into focus:

This begs the question, why are we all focusing in on the paleolithic template when the pot of gold maybe before this era when our guts first were naturally selected for leakiness? This made me wonder, was the current version of the paleo diet really the most Optimal diet for humans or do we just merely function better on it when we compare it to a post agricultural diet of today?. In my view, there is an Epi Paleo solution for modern humans where we may even do better than subsisting on a modern paleolithic diet? The reason for this is that we can never heal a leaky gut because a leaky gut is a human trait built in by evolution. This is why the paleo diet works because it limits its leakiness to reasonable levels but I don’t think it does for Optimal levels of functioning. Why? Our brain has been shrinking since the late paleolithic. This tells me we are moving away from optimal.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:50:45+0300

BG 2 is so solid.........it's not funny. It is foundational for the entire QUILT

Our past, and homo’s prologue, is fossilized in our modern genome…….not in our bones.

And that story begins in the neurochemistry of our brain's construction.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-24T19:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53828>

Thank you so much for posting this, Michael. It may have given me a hint.

"Linoleic acid (LA) and alpha-linolenic acids (ALA) are the two traditionally recognized essential fatty acids. Linoleic acid (LA) was the first to achieve nutritional essentiality status, some 80 years ago \[14\]. It is required for the normal function of the stratum corneum ceramides, which prevent trans-epidermal water loss. Dietary deficiency is characterized by severe scaly dermatosis that is reversible by administration of LA but not ALA \[15\]. Deficiency is not common in the Western diet, which is characterized by excess intake of LA."

I'm going to apply LA on my hand rash I've had since Spring. Rubbing on coconut oil isn't helping, and now the rash has enlarged and progressed to what looks like eczema now. I've been avoiding linoleic acids for almost a couple years now. I have applied olive oil twice today, and the itchiness has abated. I'll continue, and look for some evening primrose oil or grapeseed oil to try topically as well.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-24T19:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53830>

> **Monte;54440 wrote:** Size isn't the only thing that matters... Complexity and efficiency are more important.
>
>
>
> Take a look at the modern curve of cpu progression. It would make sense others follow similar patterns.

Ah, but brains do NOT organize and process info the same way computers do. Sticking to that model is why AI went nowhere. . brains and neural networks and feedback loops is an essential difference.

Size is relevant (we are going on the argument that bigger mammal brains related to intelligence are proof of epi theory). But it sounds like an operation story not a creation story?

Here we are now in the same position as Neanderthals in theory. A big brain that is not being operated properly.

I have a theory, Getting too smart for our own good is simply another form of pandemic to correct an imbalance in the natural order.

Nature says ooops, back a few steps. Need to course correct.

### Michael — 2012-09-24T19:55:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-paper-on-dha.3849/post-53837>

> **Cavemam;54492 wrote:**
>
> Here we are now in the same position as Neanderthals in theory. A big brain that is not being operated properly.

It looks like we may be.

There's an interesting idea that perhaps the thyroid in Neanderthals was not as good as ours, so that their need for iodine was even higher than ours. I guess that's something that no-one will ever be able to prove, but it's an intriguing thought. Essentially it would mean that having spread into inland areas and stopped eating seafood and food from rivers, the impact of that movement, unknown to them, would be devastating.
