# Thread: AI Food AVoidance Add-On
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-23T22:25:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53689>

Can we discuss this a bit? In addition to the foods we don't eat because they're not epi-paleo friendly, an individual with an auto-immune condition also avoids egg, all dairy (except ghee), nuts, what else? Are there some foods we avoid temporarily that we may "get back?" What are they? Fruit? How do we know they are safe for us? We test our antibody levels after x months of consuming the food? Watching for more obvious reactions following ingestion I would assume...

But what I really want to gain better insight into is the _why_ of these extra avoidances. What does eating egg (or dairy, or nuts, or whatever no-no) do in body of the auto-immune person that worsens disease? I assume they worsen antibody levels, but how? If there is a blog that I am forgetting, I would love to be redirected.

### LinD — 2012-09-23T23:42:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53698>

For starters:

[http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/)

What is a leaky gut syndrome? It is the complex biochemical reactions that occur in the gut appear to be the genesis of where inflammation initially passes in to our body. We need to realize this and avoid eating the things that cause this inflammation. As I have said for close to five years now this means strict avoidance of omega 6′s, all grains and especially wheat of any kind and very limited fructose (fruit or synthetic sources) The gut associated lymphatic tract (GALT) is the first place where our immune systems interact with the outside world. This occurs right below the intestinal brush border and is our first line of defense. It seems to me that evolution has dictated that this is precisely where the battle between health and disease begin in humans and why our immune system is set up ready on that battle front.

So clearly defense number one is to avoid the dietary sources of inflammation I mentioned above. Many of the low carb diets out there for weight loss completely negate their benefits because they fail to limit omega six fats.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-24T01:37:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53709>

everybody is so focused on the food with this..... is there other things that will make ABs go up? Other than iodine without enough selenium.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-24T02:03:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53711>

On the heals of re-reading the leaky gut blog, and at the risk of looking really thick skulled (again), I will ask more:

So, we avoid nuts because of their O6 content. What about once our O3/O6 ratios are improved? Is there something else that makes the nuts a bad source of O6s in the diet? What about eggs makes them inflammatory? I get it that nightshades contain a compound (solanine, if I remember right) that is inflammatory and so will never be a good choice for us. What is in dairy that is inflammatory?

### LinD — 2012-09-24T06:29:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-food-avoidance-add-on.3851/post-53750>

> **Jack Kruse;54387 wrote:** A1 Dairy is not good for any human........if you got Hashi's it can kill you slowly and softly below your ability or your doc ability to even know it. Go read Keith Woodford's book on it. Read up on DPPIV and BCM-7. Then you'll have your answer.

Part of an Amazon review of Keith Wooford's book;

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1603581022?ie=UTF8&force-full-site=1&ref\_=aw\_bottom\_links](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1603581022?ie=UTF8&force-full-site=1&ref_=aw_bottom_links)

"The danger from A1 milk is in its protein structure. The beta-casein in A1 milk has a structure that allows a short molecule called beta-casomorphin-7, or BCM-7, to split off during digestion. This small protein is the "devil in the milk", and it can cause various problems, ranging from juvenile diabetes, autism, schizophrenia, allergies, to auto-immune diseases such as MS and Parkinson's.

The evidence is quite compelling. The conclusion is that we should not drink A1 cow milk, or eat products made from it."

Dang... And we grew up consuming tons of dairy and cereal. I was doomed. (MS diagnosis, age 28)

# Thread: I call foul
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-24T03:35:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53723>

I was at a farm today, and watching the chickens. Then I realized - I don't think I've eating chicken since June. Not by design, it wasn't anything I tried doing. Just happened.

I remember the first time Jack said "foul is foul" months ago, I was allll like - oh no he didn't! But here I am cluck free - except for the systematic ingestion of their babies.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-24T03:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53724>

Yep same here. Chicken has no appeal. We still have a bird or 2 in the freezer but I have no desire for them. Might as well thaw them out & cut them up for the dog

What's funny is my grandfather used to call chicken foul. The only fowl he'd eat was pheasant

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T03:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53725>

From my FB page: [http://thechart.blogs.cnn.com/2012/09/19/report-worrisome-levels-of-arsenic-in-rice/](http://http://thechart.blogs.cnn.com/2012/09/19/report-worrisome-levels-of-arsenic-in-rice/)

And you thought rice was a "safe" starch according the current paleo meme right? Now FDA says levels of Arsenic in rice is "worrisome." Lung and bladder cancer are potential results from Arsenic toxicity. Rice is grown in water and ground water is the carrier of the Arsenic. How many products are in your house that contain Arsenic? Do you know? Arsenic in feed for poultry results in chicken poop with Arsenic. Then that poop is spread in the water that is used to grow rice. If you read Brain Gut 6 ([http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/](http://http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/)) you would not be eating any fowl.

The world of modern medicine can be a dangerous place to visit for any human; not because of the physicians, hospitals, corporations, and government rules that are inherently sinister. It is this way because of the people in those groups who refuse to do anything about altering it. They just are not aware of what they do not know and they refuse to look for a better way

So ask yourself now what foods in your house contain rice? Baby food for one. Be smart and avoid the "safe starch", rice. When you know better you do better.

### Huck — 2012-09-24T04:26:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53738>

Are farm ducks OK or just wild ducks? Wild ducks eat seafood, farm ducks don't.

### AKMan — 2012-09-24T07:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53759>

If you get a wild duck, eat it all...meat, gizzard, heart, liver, kidneys, lungs. Put the rest (head, bones, skin, feet) in your crockpot covered in water and make some awesome stock. Wild duck ain't farmed duck!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-24T20:17:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53852>

In my house, we have really never been fans of fowl but ate it for variety. We haven't had chicken in so long. Duck I wouldn't mind every now and again but the duck fat is pure heaven! Hey, I wonder if duck fat is sat fat or other.....do you think it would work with shrimp/fish preparation?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-24T20:20:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53854>

_Duck fat is high in beneficial unsaturated fats...........At 33%, duck fat's saturated fat content isn't terribly low, she points out. In fact, it's on par with chicken fat (about 30% saturated fat) and pork fat (39% saturated fat)._

[http://articles.latimes.com/2011/mar/27/health/la-he-nutrition-duck-fat-20110320](http://http://articles.latimes.com/2011/mar/27/health/la-he-nutrition-duck-fat-20110320)

I'm going to try duck fat with our fishy meal tonight!

### ealachan — 2012-09-24T20:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53857>

Ugh, my mom was OBSESSED with chicken when I was growing up. Of course, she was under the impression that beef and fatty pork would kill you instantly (hyperbole, but still...), and fish was out of the question because she hated to cook, so we had some iteration of boneless, skinless chicken breast seriously like 4 or 5 nights a week for YEARS (and I spent all of those years getting fatter and fatter...hmmm). I do still eat chicken sausage fairly regularly because we like it and it's a good brand with no weird stuff in it, but other than that, I'm quite happy to have basically kissed cluckers goodbye.

### Destiny — 2012-09-25T02:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-53947>

We used to eat chicken all the time. I have not made any chicken for a while.

I don't know what possessed me but I made an organic chicken yesterday. We could not finish it.

It did not taste good at all. No more chicken! Not even once in a blue moon.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-25T16:01:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54107>

> **Destiny;54622 wrote:** We used to eat chicken all the time. I have not made any chicken for a while.
>
>
>
> I don't know what possessed me but I made an organic chicken yesterday. We could not finish it.
>
> It did not taste good at all. No more chicken! Not even once in a blue moon.

I feel this way... and for some reason turkey is doing the same thing for me - just the thought of it yuck! NO.DESIRE.TO.EAT.FOWL.MEAT.

I now understand why we had LAMB every year at Christmas instead of turkey. FOWL SUXs. and tastes like crap

### kathylu — 2012-09-25T16:18:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54110>

Well, I disagree. Maybe it's because of the way I roast it, but I think chicken is pretty tasty! We'll continue to eat it for variety, though I have to admit I don't eat it often. Mostly because there's only so much I can eat in a day and the epi-paleo diet means eating a lot of uncommon foods...so not much room left for anything else.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-25T19:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54152>

My kids used to love chicken and ask for it daily. I really haven't been making it much since I read Jack's statement "fowl is foul." I think I may have cooked it once a month or so since then and I didn't even eat it - just no desire for it. Now that I'm thinking about it, the kids are just not requesting it like they used to either.

I did order a turkey for Thanksgiving back in July so I suppose we'll have that, but I think I'll also come up with some yummy seafood dishes as well so we have a nice variety.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-25T21:26:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-54213>

Had chipotle today and put it on salad out of curiosity. Wasn't tasty, or bad - kind of a bland neutral.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-01T18:49:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-55594>

If you can find REAL chicken you're in for a treat. A real chicken is about 2-3 pounds, slender and tasty beyond belief. What you all have been eating in the past is frankenchicken. And don't tell me they don't use hormones to make 'em bigger. They are tough, flavorless, just nasty.

We had a small family grocer here in town (and how I wish they hadn't closed up shop) who carried the real deal. The first one I ever bought from them I cooked up in our baby rotisserie (which I just realized sounds like it was meant to cook babies in ... it's the actual NAME of the appliance, just to clear things up) and we ate the entire thing in one sitting it was SO good. I've tried doing a frankenchicken in the rotisserie and it's still just plain nasty.

My grandparents raised chickens and we had a fresh one every Sunday.

If you're near a Mennonite colony you might be able to get the real thing. I'm pretty sure that's where Bino's (the grocer) got theirs.

### kathylu — 2012-10-01T19:05:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-55598>

So true Darlene! As a child, we had "Sunday chicken dinner" well, every Sunday. We raised them ourselves and there is a far cry difference in texture and flavor when you eat a chicken that can eat it's natural diet, mostly bugs, seeds, grasses and fruit with a little supplemental grain. You also learn exactly where your food comes from, a valuable lesson for everyone.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-01T19:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-call-foul.3853/post-55609>

I'm a voice of dissent too. I love chicken. I limit it to about once/10 days or so, for variety. We usually have a seafood breakfast, and a leftover lunch (kids pack for school). I almost always find high quality birds (pastured). The meat is far more "developped" for lack of a better word, than the stuff from the grocery stores. That stuff is almost chalk-like in texture. My kids don't really like it either. Just this morning I picked up raw milk (from Jersey cows), and drove past the pastures where the chickens were being pastured under movable coops. Sadly, the man who handles the cows (whom I've known for years) got attacked by one of the bulls two weeks ago, and is in intensive care down in Boston. He is stable, but sustained many injuries. The bull is already slaughtered... I suggested they feed it to him at the hospital lol! Poor man...

# Thread: The real cause of the French Paradox.......is epi-paleo......not paleo.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T04:08:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/post-53736>

The current US Institute of Medicine recommendation for ALA intake is 1.6 g/day for men and 1.1 g/day for women, 10% of which can be consumed as EPA + DHA \[33\]. This proportion represents the current mean intake of EPA + DHA in the American diet, approximately 100 mg per day. In most Western countries, the current recommendations for the prevention of coronary heart disease from health bodies are approximately 500 mg per day from EPA + DHA, the equivalent of two oily fish meals per week \[33\]. When nutrients are consumed, either as fortified food or supplements, for the purpose of the prevention and/or treatment of disease, the nutrients are also increasingly known as â€œnutraceuticalsâ€￾ \[34\]. The nutraceutical dosages of EPA and DHA are generally much higher than their physiological or basal levels, which remain largely undetermined. During pregnancy, some recommend 200 mg/day DHA, while intakes up to 3 g per day of DHA have been shown to be safe \[35,36\].

France is the only country where recommendations specifically for DHA are provided by health bodies at 120 mg for men and 100 mg for women per day. A recent survey of 4884 French men and women found that on average this target was far exceeded by estimated intakes of 273 mg/day for men and 226 mg/day for women \[37\]. In addition, the total long chain omega 3 fatty acids (EPA + DHA) intakes in France are in line with the recommended nutraceutical doses for the prevention of heart disease, at an estimated 497 mg/day for men and 400 mg/day in women. The French estimates for preformed DHA (250 mg/day), predominately from seafood, are much higher than estimates from other Western countries, such as 70 mg/day in the US, 90 mg/day in Australia, and 170 mg/day in Germany. These observations may in part contribute towards the â€œFrench Paradoxâ€￾, the lower incidence of heart disease despite the diet rich in saturated fatty acids \[29\].

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-24T04:45:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/post-53742>

I just finished reading the whole paper. Great stuff. DHA. The section on neurodevelopment is stunning. Not only is breastfeeding critical, but the mother's DHA intake is also critical.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T04:49:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/post-53744>

Bob I told people at Paleo Fx I had lots of proof.........welcome to Brain Gut series.......where the knowledge field is leveled for you to examine.

Just wait til BG 13 when I begin to teach you about the science of bioenergetics..........more science paleo forgot to study.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-25T19:42:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-real-cause-of-the-french-paradox-is-epi-paleo-not-paleo.3854/post-54166>

you will when BG 13 goes live.

# Thread: Hello from Ontario, Canada
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-24T06:24:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-53749>

Hi there,

I've come to Dr. Kruse after being told by a dear family friend about the site and about Dr. Kruse's phenomenal learning site.

I am writing to you concerning my mother, she is 51 years old and has suffered 3 strokes, and 4 TIAs through out her life. Originally, all the doctors were dumbfounded at how such a healthy woman could experience these strokes. The neurologists attributed the strokes to the fact that my mother is a migraine sufferer, and there is a relation between migraines and stroke. After months of appointments she was then told the strokes may have been caused by the "hole in her heart", and after 4 years of searching for the right PFO closure device to help her-- she was able to get the surgery.

We were told in 2010, after the surgery, she must stay on Cholesterol medication and Blood thinners for life, but that she should not experience another attack (if that was the problem).

Since my mother's last stroke in 2008, she was left with a constant throbbing pain in her head-- she describes it as a heart beat pounding in her head...which eventually turns into a headache by the end of each day. She's been unable to concentrate for long periods of time, and has been experiencing migraines even more frequently (a few a month).

Moreover just this month, my mother experienced another TIA. They believe it may not have developed into a stroke because of the blood thinners she's taking. Since the TIA, the last 2 weeks she has had 4 days of debilitating migraines -- days only Tylenol 3's, dark rooms, and sleep will help!

The doctors attributed this last episode to the migraine she was experiencing for 5 consecutive days before the TIA. In my opinion, the diagnosis of having a migraine does not help provide a root cause. There must also be a cause for the migraines then... shouldn't there?

I feel helpless watching her suffer every day.

I feel like we need help now, the doctors we've seen have tried medications after medications to stop her headaches, throbbing, and migraines--- nothing has worked, they caused more negative side effects than anything. We then saw a naturopathic doctor to get detoxed, tested for allergies, Vitamins/Nutrient tests, etc. -- there was a some improvement but the chronic pain remained.

I'm wondering where to go from here? I'm not sure if this is the right place to have come, but I feel good putting my trust in a doctor who is trying to help patients with only positive energy and a true desire to help better the lives of his patients!

Thank you for reading!

~ Stef

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T20:52:08+0300

You will need to pay attn to the next blog that comes in the monster Brain Gut series......it will begin to open the door to this problem. I would also tell you to get her B12 and B5, B6 levels, and her homocysteine level.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-25T05:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54032>

### nonchalant — 2012-09-26T01:07:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54281>

Stef, glad to have you here. Sorry to hear about your mother's problems. I hope she can talk to Dr. Kruse soon. You might also want to read about the Leptin Reset, especially about controlling exposure to light while the sun is down. Does she watch much TV or is she on a computer much? The bright blue light from these devices, as well as fluorescent and LED lights can aggravate migraines.

### mafixit — 2012-09-26T03:39:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54327>

I am also here because at 48 years young I have been diagnosed with 'chronic migraine' which causes daily headaches and 24/7 dizziness for the past 1-1/2 years. No one yet has tried to help me figure out the cause of the migraines - they only want me to take everything from calcium channel blockers to anti-depressants to Botox to 'control' them. Most of the doctors I have seen have suggested this is 'hormonal' but have not given me any ways to correct whatever imbalance is lurking in my system, until here and now. I have seen Mayo Clinic PCP's, Neurologists, ENTs, etc.

I don't want to 'control' these migraines, I want to be rid of them. So here I am, at the suggestion of my chiropractor. I am getting together my suggested labs and have hopes that we will find a root cause of my migraines and dizziness. I have started to read all suggested articles and am frequenting the forums on a daily basis. Fingers are crossed. I am willing to do what it takes.

My thoughts are with your mom. You are a special daughter for taking such a deep interest and caring so much to come here.

Karen from Arizona

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-26T06:06:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54355>

Thank you for your reply.

I find it so interesting that you brought up the blue light from all of the LED lights and TVs, I was just given that advice recently. Thank you for pointing right to the article that I can read more about it.

... and yes, unfortunately, the TV becomes her "relaxing" tool when she gets headaches --- perhaps not such a good idea it seems...

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-26T06:13:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54357>

Karen, I'm so sorry to hear that! I really do understand your pain and frustration. We've gone through the same process with my mom, all with out real answers.

I hope we can both get to the root of the problems! I wish you the best of luck Karen.

... I'm starting to get to reading all of the articles too! I feel we are at the stage of doing what it takes to feel GOOD AGAIN!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-26T18:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-ontario-canada.3855/post-54465>

> **stefm;55087 wrote:** ... and yes, unfortunately, the TV becomes her "relaxing" tool when she gets headaches --- perhaps not such a good idea it seems...

As a first step, you can reduce the brightness of the TV, and change the colors to a "warmer" setting or turn down the blue a bit. She also might be interested in wearing the orange Uvex brand goggles while watching TV or after dark. They cost about $8 at Amazon. They might make a huge difference for her migraines. And also improve her sleep.

# Thread: Glutathione
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T20:00:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53838>

So I was just about to hit send on an email to my doc about how glutathione is increased in cold water swimmers, this from an abstract I found on the forum. [Uric acid and glutathione levels during short-term whole body cold exposure](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0891584994900302)

Since glutathione is a major anti-oxidant, I want a lot of it, and this abstract suggests my ocean swims might be helping me make more of it.

But then over to the right I saw links to other articles about cold exposure. Clicking on one or two, I find abstracts of studies suggesting the opposite. For instance, [Chronic cold exposure affects the antioxidant defense system in various rat tissues](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0009898103001712), and others like it that show up on the right.

But it looks like these tests were done without any adaptation. Just plunged rats into cold and kept them there for a while. So adaptation is the key to resisting the negative effects and getting the positive ones, right?

Also, rat studies. I know Dr. Kruse has said rats do not equal humans. Does anyone have other tests about cold adaptation improving glutathione production in humans? Now I'm curious. Thanks.

### Rosekelly — 2012-09-24T22:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53889>

I got it right. In [this article](http://http://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736\(05\)76723-4/fulltext), the conclusion is that acute cold immersion produces different effects than regular cold water swimming. "These findings indicate that glutathione metabolism and function might be impaired during acute cold water immersion but can be preserved during chronic or repeat immersion." Not only preserved, but improved, according to the other study.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-24T22:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53891>

I just saw this.......I think CT 4 and 6 answer this. Chronic adaptation is the key to success for inflammation control and optimal performance as time evolves. This will be tangentally hit in BG 13.

### freesia — 2012-09-24T22:54:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glutathione.3869/post-53895>

> **Rosekelly;54559 wrote:** I got it right. In [this article](http://http://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736\(05\)76723-4/fulltext), the conclusion is that acute cold immersion produces different effects than regular cold water swimming. "These findings indicate that glutathione metabolism and function might be impaired during acute cold water immersion but can be preserved during chronic or repeat immersion." Not only preserved, but improved, according to the other study.

This probably helps explain why I found CT challenging the first couple of weeks then it just got sweeter and sweeter - like the seasoned CTers told me it would...and I couldn't imagine!

PS Chronic adaptation. So worth it!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-26T06:40:57+0300

> **Trisha;55075 wrote:** Interesting... gives me something to think about and look into! Thanks for your insight

I think there is another possibility. Most are reporting that it is with latter babies that this is a problem. Somewhere on this forum Dr K talked about how subsequent pregnancies deplete women of DHA and if they aren't eating the right diet (and matching circadian cycles, etc). In other words, if we don't refuel and refeed our bodies, our own health suffers. I would guess that it takes high levels of immunity and health to breastfeed optimally. We know the quality of our milk is effected. And it may even be a dual-directional problem... our milk is not up to scratch therefore our babies sleep more poorly and need more milk which isn't up to scratch. Also, if our DHA starts to be trashed (along with our hormone panel, as MamaGrok points out) then our inflammation rises. Higher inflammation means our 'tolerance' for a lot of things goes down.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-26T06:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-54367>

\[h=1\]Oxytocin and social affiliation in humans\[/h\]

-   [Ruth Feldman](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0018506X12000098#)

-   Department of Psychology and the Gonda Brain Sciences Center, Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 52900, Israel
    Abstract

A conceptual model detailing the process of _bio-behavioral synchrony_ between the online physiological and behavioral responses of attachment partners during social contact is presented as a theoretical and empirical framework for the study of affiliative bonds. Guided by an ethological behavior-based approach, we suggest that micro-level social behaviors in the gaze, vocal, affective, and touch modalities are dynamically integrated with online physiological processes and hormonal response to create dyad-specific affiliations. Studies across multiple attachments throughout life are presented and demonstrate that the extendedoxytocin (OT) system provides the neurohormonal substrate for parental, romantic, and filial attachment in humans; that the three prototypes of affiliation are expressed in similar constellations of social behavior; and that OT is stable over time within individuals, is mutually-influencing among partners, and that mechanisms of cross-generation and inter-couple transmission relate to coordinated social behavior. Research showing links between peripheral and genetic markers of OT with concurrent parenting and memories of parental care; between administration of OT to parent and infant's physiological readiness for social engagement; and between neuropeptides and the online synchrony of maternal and paternal brain response in social-cognitive and empathy networks support the hypothesis that human attachment develops within the matrix of biological attunement and close behavioral synchrony. The findings have conceptual implications for the study of inter-subjectivity as well as translational implications for the treatment of social disorders originating in early childhood, such as autism spectrum disorders, or those associated with disruptions to early bonding, such as postpartum depression or child abuse and neglect. This article is part of a Special Issue entitledOxytocin, Vasopressin, and Social Behavior.

* * *

\[h=4\]Highlights\[/h\]►A conceptual model of bio-behavioral synchrony is proposed for the study of human affiliation. ►Affiliation is expressed by unique physiology and behavior synchronized online among partners. ►OT supports the three prototypical social bonds in humans— parental, pair, and filial. ►Brain, genetic, and hormonal markers of OT interact with behavior to support bonding across life.

### MamaGrok — 2012-09-26T16:04:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-54405>

> **KiwiLauren;55097 wrote:** I think there is another possibility. Most are reporting that it is with latter babies that this is a problem. Somewhere on this forum Dr K talked about how subsequent pregnancies deplete women of DHA and if they aren't eating the right diet (and matching circadian cycles, etc). In other words, if we don't refuel and refeed our bodies, our own health suffers. I would guess that it takes high levels of immunity and health to breastfeed optimally. We know the quality of our milk is effected. And it may even be a dual-directional problem... our milk is not up to scratch therefore our babies sleep more poorly and need more milk which isn't up to scratch. Also, if our DHA starts to be trashed (along with our hormone panel, as MamaGrok points out) then our inflammation rises. Higher inflammation means our 'tolerance' for a lot of things goes down.

In my case, my fifth baby found me giving the best milk to date (not fat-deficient, and the proof was in the teeth that didn't rot out like all the other babies' had!), and my omega 3 was higher than ever (after several years of working on the 3:6 ratio). My body was very, very depleted in babies 1-4, but I worked hard for three years before the last conception, with sugar addiction being the main remaining problem (and of course, as I now know, untreated thyroid disease). I think that for me, the hormones are the last remaining bastion of dysfunction after years of working on these issues.

Interestingly, this baby slept like a rock from the beginning. She's the only one who never needed to nurse much at night, which was very fortunate for me, since I couldn't stand it!

I second that!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-28T00:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-54879>

For me, oxytocin comes best in it's natural package. With children, that means within the relationship (just like with partners). In other words, if the relationship is bad, then there is a problem. I am speaking only for myself here. My children are my greatest blessings and my best teachers. They are also my main motivation. If I didn't have them, I know my drive to be healthy, stay healthy and live well would be reduced. If there are times when I start to see my children solely as stressors in my life, I know our relationship (and my mind/heart) needs work. Because I can be crushed by a bus tomorrow and the state of my hsCRP isn't going to matter to my children... but how our relationship was will. Not to say that I'm not working hard on inflammation, thyroid, etc. Just to say that right here, right now is just as important... I don't want to wait until I'm 'all better' to have love in the centre of my life.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-28T02:11:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-54902>

> **bigknitwit;55309 wrote:** My kids are oxidizing me at a much faster rate than otherwise... My youngest is going to preschool 2 glorious mornings/week and then I "reduce." The older ones are getting oh so tired of hearing the Momologues about food, light, the state of general health, etc!! Clearly, having children is far from optimal!

Please understand I wrote this tongue in cheek. My children are my life, and have been since the day my oldest was born. They are, by far, the main reason I am so focused on getting better. I need to fix me to become working template, so that I can heal them... I do still "reduce" on Tuesday and Thursday mornings though  It's the only time I really get to be inside my own head, and it's brand new this school year...

### billy — 2014-10-31T08:48:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-145753>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20626426](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20626426)
"this small nine amino acid peptide is involved in a wide variety of physiological and pathological functions such as sexual activity, penile erection, ejaculation, pregnancy, uterus contraction, milk ejection, maternal behavior, osteoporosis, diabetes, cancer, social bonding, and stress, which makes oxytocin and its receptor potential candidates as targets for drug therapy."

Yeah but can if fix my sink?

Pubmed continues...
"In addition, not only the hormone itself and its binding to OTR, but also its synthesis, storage and release can be endogenously and exogenously regulated to counteract pathophysiological states."

Exogenous! And this article is 4 years old... I hadn't heard of oxytocin before, this place is pretty cool.

### Craig Craddock — 2014-12-01T08:31:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-ftw.3879/post-148914>

Hi folks
This is my first post here, but I have been reading and studying Dr. Kruse's work for several years.
Regarding oxytocin, I can share my experiences in using it approximately every other day for the past three years. I obtain 20 iu troches of oxytocin from International AntiAging Systems in UK. I have purchased "smart drugs" from them for 15 years. I use 1/3 to 1/2 of the troche or lozenge when I go to work in the supplement department of the local coop, or when I want to be more at ease socially. I believe that my early overenthusiastic use of oxytocin contributed to or facilitated my having an extramarital affair. This controverted the results of some recent study which suggested that men using oxytocin were less likely to stray from their mates. I am more cautious about how much and with whom I use it now. I do feel at ease and connected with people more-so when I take the oxy. A very good book available from my same source is Passion Sex and Long Life, The Incredible Oxytocin Adventure, by Dr. Thierry Hertoghe. There are cautions and contra-indications, but overall very encouraging. Cost is approx $5.00 per lozenge, or around $100.00 for 24 troches, including shipping from UK.
It's great to get your oxytocin pumped by touch, physical contact, lovemaking, etc., but if you aren't getting it that way, then exogenous supplementation may be worth the turn-on, so that you can feel that warmth and connection, if you have been missing it. I am a bit shy about posting this here, but Dr. Kruse has created a great educational forum with his blogs and webinars, so I am just going to take the risk of coming out of my oxytocin cave, and present this as my small contribution to our colloquy.
FYI: I am 69, in good health, take many herbs and supplements, since I have been in natural foods retail since 1970, living in Ashland, OR for the past 34 years.
If you are interested, here is the website: [http://www.antiaging-systems.com/search/o](http://www.antiaging-systems.com/search/o)
PS: My photo was taken after I took two troches of oxytocin and noticed that my hair was beginning to stand erect, so I banded it and took the selfie in disbelief.
I think that my humor has improved too--more willing to be playful.

# Thread: I received my new set of labs today and thought I would share for educational purpose
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 35
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T02:54:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-53953>

My labs are pretty good but I thought I would share with you one of the things I have learned about my own biology that seems to be unique to me.........for 8 consecutive yrs in Sept my Vitamin D craters suddenly as the light cycle changes. I was 98 in the beginning of August and I had my labs done on Sept 22 and my D has fallen to 55 on 10,000 IU of D3 and 15 mgs of K2.

My DHEA also fell by 50%.........in the same period.

My testosterone also dropped about 15%.......

My IGF-1 is stable.

My ASI is rock solid as usual.........

My gut is humming along and my TSH is pristine and my free T3 is in the top quartile of the range and my pregnenolone remains stable..........

What does this all means for me? It means I drop all fruit and I begin my ketogenic climb in my diet while ramping up my seafood and uping my vitamin D3 levels and the most important move............

I begin daily CT..........and I go deep. This move alone will bring me back to allostasis........at least it has for the last 5 yrs. I'll let you know how I roll on my next lab draw.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-25T02:59:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-53954>

Oh, wow. From 98 down to 55 in one month?!?!

Sounds like the light cycles are a BIG FACTOR, as I once heard this neurosurgeon say...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T03:02:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-53955>

They are for me........but I expected it. It is the 8th straight yr it has happened........I found this out only by testing every Q.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-25T07:22:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-54056>

Is anyone else starting to feel like labs are some of the most personal things to share with others? Lol. I'm feeling very private about my steroid hormone panel..... I have to look at ALL the labs posted on here. I just think it's so cool and tells you so much about a person. Way more than a first date would ever tell you. I think Match.com needs to add a section for labs and hormone panels. It's like, i can see what you eat, I can see your sex drive, I can see your if you're a stressed out person, .... etc. and you can't lie either. You can write and say a lot of things about yourself... but if your T is in the toilet and your cortisol is in the sky.... I probably don't want to date you no matter how cute you might appear on your profile pic.

Maybe I'll become a matchmaker for singles who uses labs to make the matches, heehee. What do you all think?

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-09-25T22:25:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-received-my-new-set-of-labs-today-and-thought-i-would-share-for-educational-purpose.3884/post-54239>

O and I just thought. While Jack is always striving for Optimal but yet stating the importance of minding the seasons and cycles, it's important to remember his past and where he was coming from---broken. He said b/c of his 23andme labs indicates why he should have to alter his D levels even if there might be a natural evolutionary explanation. See perhaps in evolution there is the normal cellular inflammation etc. but maybe for him he needs to intervene since if left alone it could continue to plummet which is NOT in tune with evolution. So yes, he's Optimal but I don't think we should expect him to look like a god or something.

It's all about knowing yourself and for many people this requires a 'reverse' process of past damage. So while using evolution and ancient pathways harnessed by using the cold, it is important to understand that these are tools and a guide to help with that reversal process. It is not exactly possible to just 'copy' literally what ancient humans did...because they did not live in our current conditions with broken-ness. This is why Jack emphasizes to lose one's dogma. I hope my understanding is as good as I feel it is at this point.

### AKMan — 2012-09-25T03:08:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-mystery-for-jack-dark-dna.3885/post-53957>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-dark-dna-brain-day-night.html#ajTabs](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-dark-dna-brain-day-night.html#ajTabs)

Long stretches of DNA once considered inert dark matter appear to be uniquely active in a part of the brain known to control the body's 24-hour cycle, according to researchers at the National Institutes of Health.

"We are only beginning to understand how the pineal gland helps maintain the body's 24 hour rhythms."

# Thread: New website goes live at 4 am CT (ish) on Tuesday, September 25
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 51
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tellmisty — 2012-09-25T04:09:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54002>

Hi ya'll,

Okay, we are getting ready to take the new site live tomorrow morning. A few things of note:

This is your LAST CHANCE to [buy consults at the current prices](http://http://jackkruse.com/educational-consults/). Once the new site goes live, the price of consults will depend upon your membership level.

If you want to buy an email lab consult, [you may do so until the new site is live without being a member](http://https://www.paypal.com/cgi-bin/webscr?cmd=_s-xclick&hosted_button_id=TM7EZCKMA4MM2). Once the new site is up, Karnivores (new name: Silver Klub) can buy email consults at $99, and Klub members (new name: Gold Klub) get a free email lab consult with their membership.

If you've paid for a Karnivore membership and want to upgrade, there is no way for us to pro-rate your membership, unfortunately, with the old site (this is a PayPal limitation). We are changing this with the new site, though ... we've set up a different merchant solution that will give us some additional flexibility so we can prorate for you if you want to upgrade. Unfortunately, you won't be able to upgrade to the Kruse Klub (Gold Klub) and get the included monthly consult (at $248 monthly). If you want the monthly consult included (best price for consults!), [this is your last chance to upgrade.](http://https://www.paypal.com/cgi-bin/webscr?cmd=_s-xclick&hosted_button_id=X3GB45N5XXLPL)

The new site is going to be transitioned over in stages. First, we're putting the new template with a better-organized membership area. Over the course of the next two weeks, you will see content on the site modified and a lot of new content come online. The forums will be transitioned over in the next couple weeks, too; we are going to do our best to link your forum profile with your member login so you will have ONE login instead of two.

If you have any issues with the new site, you will be able to open a support ticket rather than having to email me for help. These tickets will be easier to track.

This process isn't going to be perfect ... we will have to iron out the kinks as we go, so be patient with us! My marketing team is awesome ... they are working so hard on this project and we are stoked about the new functionality we'll have in the new site. We are making a LOT of things a lot easier, working to make content more accessible, etc.

During the webinar tomorrow night, we'll take a few minutes to review the site's functionality with you and answer any questions at the end. At some point, I'll be making a call for feedback, too, so we can get your help in polishing things out.

Looking forward to talking to you guys tomorrow night on the webinar!

Misty

### tellmisty — 2012-09-25T09:31:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54069>

Alright; I don't think launching a new website is Epi-Paleo. ;-) My circadian biology is probably jacked for the next few days ... \*sigh\*

Been working all night with the programmers ... finished everything I could tonight and am going to bed. Thank God for the wonderful guys who are bringing this home ... once we get things moved over, we'll have to start a thread to express our gratitude to them for all their hard work!

I'm building out the Getting Started section tomorrow with Dan ... it's gonna rock. We've been sketching it on paper for weeks; it will come to life tomorrow! All member levels are in, framework is SO much better ... can't wait for you guys to experience it bug-free! Hopefully that will be, well, tomorrow. At least for the first round of roll-outs.

No more consults at current prices, and no more new Kruse Klub registrants until tomorrow ... all have been deleted off the site to make way for the switch. See you on the other side!

> **KiwiLauren;54962 wrote:** Misty, a question re: the new site. Log in now works (thanks for sending us new details!!), but when you go to your membership area there is no indication if you are a previous Kruse Klub member and therefore grandfathered in to the ability to still have monthly 30 minute consults for free. (It assumes 30 minute consults are $199). Is this going to be kept on private record so that those of us who were Kruse members before the switch can just continue to use Tungle and schedule a monthly consult? Or is there another mechanism? We'll be able to tweak that, Lauren, with a special membership level for you. But that's not high on the list. Right now we have to get some basics fixed and get ready for the webinar tonight!

Be patient with load times ... they're trying to get the site into maintenance mode to tweak some things, but traffic is just too heavy. We'll get there!

Jack - the forums are still in vBulletin. We're going to port all forums over to a new system that's integrated with users' memberships, but we couldn't do that until new site went live and a bunch of preliminary membership things were taken care of. I'm expecting new forums in the next few days...

### DarleenMB — 2012-09-26T18:15:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54447>

From my standpoint it's a mess. I tried to set up the silver membership yesterday so I could get in on last night's webinar. IT went NOWHERE. I got the email with new p/w info and this morning it doesn't work.

I do NOT like the new way to pay. I prefer Paypal because I know it's secure, I don't have to give YOU my cc info and I can manage my subscription easily. There's no way that I could see to cancel should I decide to do that. I don't like that.

Oh and had I know about being "grandfathered" in on the monthly consult I'd have signed up sooner Thanks a lot.

Yeah, I'm not happy this morning. Partly because I'm down with ear infections and partly because this shouldn't have gone "live" until it was working.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-26T18:52:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54464>

I paid twice for this month's webinar. I paid on the 18th and I paid on the 24th. The webinar took place on the 25th. I knew about this and I still don't like it. I got less than 6 days of nothing for $48. I think it would be a very nice gesture to refund those people who upgraded to Kruse Klub and ended up paying twice for the webinar. I didn't get anything during those 6 days. So I don't understand why I don't get a refund.

Honestly, since I also wasn't able to access the webinar library during those 6 days properly and I know others weren't as well, I really think this situation requires a refund. JMHO

Darlene, I'm not trying to "slap you down," but rather point out that we did our part in communicating the changes. It seem that what you were trying to do is "slap ME down." Whether or not you want to acknowledge it, your post was rude and disrespectful, and it's no way to try to get help. I have answered HUNDREDS of emails over the past week, supporting our members and doing my best for this community. I tend to be very patient with our members and any issues that they're having because I truly want to help, but I'm not going to tolerate rudeness and ingratitude. So take that for what it's worth. No one owes you anything here. This is an I-want-to-be-here community, and you can leave at anytime. Spreading negativity and vitriol is not an option.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54725>

> I have answered HUNDREDS of emails over the past week,

Honestly not trying to slap anyone here but if there is that much confusion going on then may I respectfully suggest there is a problem somewhere else then with members' brain fog. I don't know how many emails you usually answer, but I, for one, am witnessing the same questions over and over again being asked.

You can't see what you are not looking for. Maybe there is a blind spot? You can either get upset at the things you don't like or you can welcome them as more knowledge. And knowledge is power as one wise member on here taught me once. There used to be a very relaxed and welcoming spirit on this forum. In the past weeks/months upset and anger has been building. If I were you, I'd be curiously investigating what happened instead of getting distracted by the personal attack you are appear to be perceiving.

Depersonalize. You're attempting to fix a problem, not a person. Leave that part up to Dr K, he's got the required thicker skin for that

### tellmisty — 2012-09-27T17:40:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-website-goes-live-at-4-am-ct-ish-on-tuesday-september-25.3893/post-54747>

And knowledge is power as one wise member on here taught me once. There used to be a very relaxed and welcoming spirit on this forum. In the past weeks/months upset and anger has been building. If I were you, I'd be curiously investigating what happened instead of getting distracted by the personal attack you are appear to be perceiving.
>

> Depersonalize. You're attempting to fix a problem, not a person. Leave that part up to Dr K, he's got the required thicker skin for that

Souldanzer, the forums are not a place to air your grievances. Period. None of us on the technical side are monitoring forums. I have made my email address available and get plenty of people emailing. There is nothing, NOTHING you've said on the forums, souldanzer, that we are not aware of and working on fixing. This entire project--the new website--is about addressing all the issues that I've seen and members have reported. It's annoying that you'd insinuate that we need to listen or "be curious." Helloooooo ... new website ...

It is not helpful or useful for people to come on the boards to bitch and complain over and over again. Good constructive feedback sent to me or communicated via support will be addressed. We have communicated openly and consistently all the changes that are coming and we're doing the best we can in the meantime. No one is entitled to anything. If you don't like the experience, you don't have to stick around, and in the meantime, we are revamping everything and doing our best to make it better.

You seem to be suggested that it's okay for people to be rude or disrespectful, that they're "entitled" and we need to tolerate it and "toughen up." Jack nor I need to put on tough skins so that people can be as rude and impatient and disrespectful as they want to be while making their requests. The negativity you are describing does not need to be tolerated by me or Jack or any of the other members.

I understand the frustration, and as PROOF of my understanding and Jack's understanding, we have a new website and programmers working around the clock. If anyone has a problem or issue, open a support ticket or email me.

### ealachan — 2012-09-27T19:45:34+0300

Also, for anyone having trouble actually getting ON to the blog itself, it looks like the "blog" link in the header on the forums is toast, but all the other ones appear to redirect to the new site. If you're on the forums and you click the "blog" link in the header, you'll just get a bunch of code crap, but if you hit the "Home" link in the header, it will take you to the main [www.jackkruse.com](http://http://www.jackkruse.com) page, where you will (presumably) see blog entries.

Just figured I'd share my experience so to help out any others who may have been having trouble.

# Thread: Help me justify a ASI to my PCP
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-25T04:22:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54011>

Soooooo,

Will you-all help me figure out HOW to describe to my PCP that I need to get an ASI on top off all these other tests?

The reason in my head is that something is keeping me from converting steroid/cortisol hormones. . thus low D, P, E.

The real thing I need to get a grip on communicating is WHAT knowing the ASI numbers will mean as far as what I do to treat it?

If all the things " to do" to work on this pathway are behavior/food things - there probably isn't much more I could be doing.

I kind of want to know this too. . . the only thing I can think of is that perhaps the Cytomel if my circadian stuff looks off.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-25T04:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54019>

You don't need him for that. You buy it online. It's never covered by insurance.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-25T04:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54022>

Tis post makes no sense to me.......you order your own ASi and tell your doc to kiss your ass.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-25T05:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-justify-a-asi-to-my-pcp.3895/post-54026>

> **Souldanzer;54701 wrote:** You don't need him for that. You buy it online. It's never covered by insurance.

Hubby's was covered - fep blue (federal employee blue cross). I can't remember the reason. . .but I like to be an expert on these things before having the conversation with Doc.

Yeah, those animations are really clever. As to the seafood, I guess at that point he was keeping it up his sleeve.

Occasionally there'd be a hint, like when he'd said that a particular low-carb researcher "didn't get it" although he kept citing the Inuit diet. In retrospect I guess "it" was probably that a tremendous amount of that ketogenic diet is seafood-based. Some Inuit did take a fair bit of big game, but fish was a major part of the diet. Of course, they live somewhere cold, and dark in winter, as well ...

And thinking about it now Stefansson, perceptive as he was, didn't see the significance of that - not in Canada nor Iceland. Yes, there was little to no bread available in early-medieval Iceland, but also a heck of a lot of the food was sourced from the _sea_, and maybe that was important, too.

# Thread: Factor X Smoking Gun--Myelination
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/factor-x-smoking-gun-myelination.3915/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-09-26T01:30:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/factor-x-smoking-gun-myelination.3915/post-54291>

Jack talks about this quite frequently. This study examined differences in primates and humans concerning brain development. So much for us being 99% identical!

A mixed background group of US researchers has found in studying chimpanzee brains, that development of myelin, the fatty sheath that covers the connections between nerve cells, occurs at a different rate than for humans, and as they write in their paper published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, the differences might account for the greater instance of mental disorders in people than in both chimpanzees and other primates, such as macaques.

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-myelin-differences-chimps-humans.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-09-myelin-differences-chimps-humans.html)

Human babies are born with almost no myelin at all. Shortly after birth, they have a spurt of sorts, with a lot of developing occurring and continuing on slowly thereafter for some thirty years. Other primates, in contrast, begin developing myelin while still in the womb and it keeps up just until the animal reaches sexual maturity, at which point, it stops completely.

The researchers speculate that the differences in myelin development may explain some of the unique mental abilities of people, as being born with little to no myelin, **allows for more openness to learning from the environment as opposed to relying on information passed on through genes**. They also suggest those same differences might be putting people at more of a risk of developing mental illness though, as the possibility of something going wrong during the process grows greater over longer periods of time.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-26T06:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/factor-x-smoking-gun-myelination.3915/post-54353>

BG 13 hits this........

### Marccus — 2012-10-29T06:54:20+0200

# Thread: Stefansson
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-26T15:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54404>

Here's what occurred to me _apropos Stefansson. He always talks about an "all-meat diet", although I think he's probably including fish in that - but that's the _name he uses.

I think he was probably influenced in his thinking by knowing that Nansen and Johansen, when over-wintering after the push "farthest north" ate only meat. He was interested in people saying that you needed fruit and vegetables - being fixated on those foods. He said Nansen and Johansen's experience showed that that's just not so. And there's much more of the same. He proved his case in Bellevue Hospital. But here's the thing: you might go a year or ten years or even a lifetime on an "all-meat diet" but that doesn't make it optimal.

And was it was Nansen and Johansen did anyway? Because I thought: Wait a minute, what_ meat were Nansen and Johansen eating? And I thought that if the meat was coming from sea-mammals maybe that would be relevant. What if they were eating seal, for example? I assume seals have adequate supplies of the right sort of fatty acids - to protect them from the cold water. Besides, DHA and a selection of other nutrients are available to them, because they're present in other organisms lower down the food chain. Do people let the word "meat" mislead them and forget to ask which meat?

So what I did was to download the textfile of the second volume of Farthest North_ from project Gutenberg:

[http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/34120](http://http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/34120)

I just used the "find" function in a text editor to search for the word "meat" and see what I came up with.

> Johansen
>
> has time to give it a ball just as it is disappearing, and it floats
>
> while I harpoon it--the first and only bearded seal (Phoca barbata)
>
> we have seen yet--and we have abundance of food and fuel for upward
>
> of a month.
>
>
>
> > Our cookery was as simple as possible. It consisted in boiling
> >
> > bear's flesh and soup (bouillon) in the morning and frying steak
> >
> > in the evening. We consumed large quantities at every meal, and,
> >
> > strange to say, we never grew tired of this food, but always ate it
> >
> > with a ravenous appetite. We sometimes either ate blubber with it or
> >
> > dipped the pieces of meat in a little oil.
>
>
>
> When at last the task was completed, and we had two great heaps
>
> of \[walrus\] blubber and meat on shore, well covered by the thick walrus hides,we were not a little pleased.

All those animals are eating fish and/or smaller sea-animals.

While doing this exercise I also serendipitously found something else I hadn't even dreamed of finding.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-26T16:07:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54406>

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-26T17:38:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54428>

Good find, Michael. Another book by Nansen that might be of interest, is "Eskimo Life" from the year 1893.

It is [available as a free download](http://http://archive.org/details/eskimolife00archgoog).

Here's an excerpt:

CHAPTER VI

COOKERY AND DAINTIES

One feature of the Greenlanders' daily life, which to

us seems strange enough, is that they have no fixed

meal-times ; they simply eat when they are hungry, if

there is anything to be had. As already mentioned,

the hunters often go the whole day without anything

to eat. They have a remarkable power of doing

without food, but to make up for this they can con-

sume at a sitting astonishing quantities of meat,

blubber, fish, etc.

Their cookery is simple and easy to learn.

Meat and fish are eaten sometimes raw or frozen,

sometimes boiled, sometimes dried; and sometimes

meat is allowed to undergo a sort of decomposition

or fermentation, when it is called mikiak, and is eaten

without further preparation. A dish of this sort,

which is very highly esteemed, is rotten seals'-

heads.

The blubber of seals and whales is generally eaten

raw. My dainty readers will of course shudder at

the very thought of eating raw blubber ; but I can

assure them that, especially when quite fresh, it is

very good. It has a sweetish, perhaps rather

mawkish, taste, reminding one of cream, with nothing

of what we should call an oily or fishy flavour ; this

does not make itself felt until the blubber has been

boiled or roasted, or when it has grown rancid. There

are still people, no doubt, who believe that the Eskimos

are in the habit of drinking train-oil, although even

Hans Egede has pointed out that this is a mistake.

That they do not always refuse it, however, when it

comes in their way, 1 was able to assure myself at

Godthaab; for I always saw our old maid-servant

Eosina take a sip or two out of our lamp when she

was cleaning it in the morning, and, as she usually

did, had filled the vessel a little too full. It did not

seem at all to disagree with her.

They also preserve the stalks of angelica in train-

oil, preparing them, according to Saabye's account,

in the following peculiar fashion ; \* A woman takes a

mouthful of blubber, chews it, and spits it out, and

so continues until she thinks she has enough. When

the angelica-stalks have steeped for a certain time in

this liquid, they are taken out and eaten as dessert

with much appetite.'

Of vegetable food, the primitive Greenlanders used

several sorts ; in addition to angelica, I may mention

dandelions, sorrel, crowberries, bilberries, and different

kinds of seaweed. One of their greatest delicacies is

the contents of a reindeer's stomach. If a Green-

lander kills a reindeer, and is unable to convey much

of it home with him, he will, I believe, secure the

stomach first of all ; and the last thing an Eskimo

lady enjoins upon her lover, when he sets off rein-

deer-hunting, is that he must reserve for her the

stomach of his prey. It is no doubt because they

stand in need of vegetable food that they prize this

so highly, and also because it is in reality a very

choice collection of the finest moss and grasses which

that gourmet the reindeer, picks out for himself. It

has undergone a sort of stewing in the process of

semi-digestion, while the gastric juice provides a

somewhat sharp and aromatic sauce. Many will no

doubt make a wry face at the thought of this dish,

but they really need not do so. I have tasted it,

and found it not uneatable, though somewhat sour,

like fermented milk. As a dish for very special

occasions, it is served up with pieces of blubber and

crowberries.

So... Rotten seal's head smoothie, anyone?

Fascinating stuff, this. I had to look up train oil. It is "blubber oil".

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-26T17:41:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54432>

I remember my mother introducing me to Steffansson's second wife Evelyn Nef when I was a kid. That was a treat because I was already interested in arctic exploration. My mother also told me about the "all meat" diet and how so many people ridiculed VS despite the results....

In Newfoundland, seal meat is still available and eaten regularly. All the misinformation about the seal hunt aside....this was an important source of nutrients for indigenous people and european settlers alike for thousands of years. It is a very rich, gamy meat with a slight fishy taste...kind of like venison that's been brined in sea water.

Will apply flame suit now.

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T19:37:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54476>

So when this was written, the writer was in the arctic and had been for some time, eating nothing but bear meat and fat. Previously they were on a ship, The Fram, eating God-knows-what, probably lots of biscuits and dried fish. After leaving the ship and moving to an inland dwelling of some sort that had comfortable chairs, they gained weight.

I would guess that life on a ship is calorie demanding and they probably weren't eating all that good. Lots of cold, wind exposure and possible bouts of diarrhea and vomiting. When they moved into a climate-controlled, comfortable dwelling and ate as much meat and fat as they wanted, why wouldn't they gain weight.

Now, if they were on a Disney Cruise eating at the buffet every day and getting massages on the Lido Deck, it might be a different story...

### Michael — 2012-09-26T19:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54481>

> **AKMan;55247 wrote:** After leaving the ship and moving to an inland dwelling of some sort that had comfortable chairs, they gained weight.
>
>
> That seems to be what Nansen thought:
>
>
>
> it must be our laziness that has done it.

But if you make people exercise, their resting metabolic rate falls. Steve Phinney has shown that with subjects locked in metabolic wards.

I suppose at this point most of the paleosphere would now say that they must have "overeaten".

Maybe bear meat and seal oil is "hyperpalatable". I'm being sarcastic there, because of course, they told us you needed fat _and sweet _and_ salt, and perhaps some MSG thrown in too, to make you "eat too much".

Why would they be they eating too much? Why don't their hormonal systems tell them when they've had enough?

But here's another thought: was it fat_ that they put on? See these answers from Jack on the "Ask Jack" board:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?3866-CT-vs-exercise](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?3866-CT-vs-exercise)

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4187-Muscle-gain-Strategy](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?4187-Muscle-gain-Strategy)

### AKMan — 2012-09-26T19:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54483>

> **Michael;55252 wrote:** I suppose at this point most of the paleosphere would now say that they must have "overeaten".

I would maybe say they were underweight when they arrived and eating good food put the weight back on, in fat and muscle mass. But I get what you are saying.

However, life in the arctic is so much different that anything most can relate to. Overwintering scientists at the South Pole experience a well-documented problem: Polar T3 Syndrome ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar\_T3\_syndrome](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar_T3_syndrome) ). The near total darkness and exposure to cold temps cause an increased muscle uptake of T3 resulting in hypothyroid symptoms. I know from living near the Arctic Circle for the past 10 years and working outside in Arctic conditions that calories in-calories out is not applicable here at all. It's just when you drive your heated car to work, run in to your heated job all day, then watch TV all night eating whatever, you will gain weight.

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-27T11:10:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54709>

There are rock carvings in a number of different locations in Norway. The most impressive ones are probably the ones in [Alta](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rock_carvings_at_Alta) in northern Norway. They were discovered in 1973 and the oldest of them date back to about 5000 BC. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and is the largest collection of rock carvings in Northern Europe.

Several carvings show evidence of fishing. Here's one. Not sure if that's net or a trap of some sort.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-27T14:19:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54722>

Regarding Polar T3 syndrome: What animals live in polar regions? Mostly marine mammals and now humans. What do they have in common and how do they differ? Both are mammals but the sea mammals rely on their environment to produce their dietary needs while humans create their own polar enviroment at base camp with decidely non polar food. Do you see why humans get Polar T3 syndrome when other polar mammals do not? One eats a steady diet of what the local environment dishes out and humans create a mismatch..........then we cause an epigenetic effect and call it a disease instead of realizing it is self induced dietary restriction. In cold , we need a superior diet in DHA and iodine. BG 13 tells you why.

### Michael — 2012-09-27T15:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stefansson.3923/post-54730>

One (the wildlife) eats what's available locally; the other (people) brings stuff in.

I guess Stefansson, God bless him, had got part of the way there. He knew expeditions should be getting their food locally, although he saw the problem solely in terms of vitamin C:

> no matter how good the juice of limes (or lemons), it is difficult to carry, it deteriorates, and you may lose it, as by a shipwreck. The thing to do is to find your antiscorbutics where you are, pick them up as you go.

Don't bring all your food: get it where you are. And that's the basis he worked on.

This business of bringing everything you possibly can, for all the world like Henry Boot and his luggage in _Black Mischief_, seems to have been much more common.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-09-27T18:05:36+0300
