# Thread: Getting discouraged - Need suggestions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 41
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-26T17:53:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54436>

I've been reading here for the past several months and I've been doing the Leptin Rx and CT for almost 2 months now.

I'll try to keep it short so you guys don't have to read a rambling book about me.

I need help. Since I started doing the leptin reset and CT, I can't sleep through the night and most of those nights I have to get up a eat something.

I've only slept through the night 2 times in the past 2 months. I wake up without an alarm clock (but I was doing that before) at about 6:45 or 7:00 every morning and I go to bed when it's dark (which is about 9:00 here right now). I wake up in the night for about 2.5 hours every night. All I want to do when I wake up in the morning is turn over and go back to sleep for 4 more hours.

Some nights I wind up feeling like I'm starving. I don't usually wake up hungry, it's _because_ I'm awake for so long that I get hungry. Sometimes I just let my stomach growl and I can still go back to sleep. But most nights it gets pretty bad. My stomach hurts so much it makes my back ache and then I feel sick (like hypoglycemic sick) and I know I've got to drag myself downstairs and eat something. Last night was really bad. I almost passed out in the kitchen while trying to fix myself some beef. (And I've passed out a lot in my life - I'm not being hyperbolic.) This makes me suspect low blood sugar. I feel totally fine during the day (after breakfast) and can easily go 6 hours or more without eating.

After BAB (which is always salmon, shrimp, and tuna) I eat a small lunch (I'm not hungry so I just eat enough so my stomach doesn't hurt when I take my vitamins and pills), and then I eat a medium sized dinner (beef or lamb or something.) I've tried to eat more at dinner so maybe I won't get hungry at night but that isn't really working and I'm concerned that eating a bigger meal is just causing my cortisol to rise at night and causing more problems. I'm going to try eating a small lunch and small dinner today and see if that helps. But I really don't know what else to try.

And before you ask, no I haven't had any tests done. I know that would help a lot (and I do plan to do it in the future), but my anxiety problems are pretty severe and going to get tests done would take a serious amount of tranquilizers to get me through and then I'd be screwed up for days. Kind of a catch 22 thing goin for me.

If you have any suggestions (that don't involve me leaving the house) please let me know. If this doesn't get better soon I'm going to have to go back to eating right before I go to bed. I have to get a real night of sleep.

Female - 31(32 at end of Oct.) y.o. - 5'3

Weight (this morning): 172.5 BF% - about 43%

Eating:

I started low-carbing about a year ago (Atkins) but gradually changed to Primal/paleo. So I've been primal (I was eating some dairy) for about 7 months before coming here. I started out at 231 (highest was 235) and lost around 50 pounds before starting CT. I'm losing weight still so I'm not complaining about that (but it's crazy sporadic now). I was losing about 4 pounds/month but last month (doing CT 5 days a week) I lost 6.

CT: I'm doing 2 days on/one day off. So that's 5 days a week in 50-55 degree water. Started out doing it at night but switched to doing it in the morning. (Seeing if it would help my sleep.)

Diagnosed:

Dysthymic Depression/with bouts of major depression. I take Luvox for this right now. Depression problems since I was about 13. Diagnosed at 16.

Anxiety disorders - pretty bad...like sometimes (ok...lots of times) I can't leave the house (and I live with my parents) and I've passed out during attacks. I'm actually ok as long as I don't experience ANY stress. (Yeah, that works about as well as you'd imagine.) I take a tranquilizer as needed for this (but I try to avoid it cause they makes me feel tired)

Endometriosis/Dysmenorrhea - Period started when I was 9 and I've had problems since then. When I was about 16 I started seeing a gyno and he put my on BC pills. Still had too much pain so he completely stopped my period with BC pills. I now take two lo-ovral a day.

Gallbadder - Removed in my early 20's and until recently I had chronic diarrhea since they took it out. I take Welchol to stop the liquid pooping.

GERD - Been on PPI's since I was about 17. But have recently (in the past month) gotten completely off them (I've been weaning myself off for almost a year). Yay!

I also have chronic back problems. Most recently my pelvis is tipped out of place and my chiropractor has been working on me. The CT has helped with the pain.

Vitamins etc...

Breakfast:

7 keto DHEA

Vit. D

NAC

MSM

Ester C

Bitter Melon

DIM

(This is also when I take one of my BC pills)

Lunch:

MSM

Multi Vit.

Iodoral

Vit K

Probiotic

Chelated Magnesium

Ester-C

B Complex

(I also take my Welchol at lunchtime.)

Dinner:

CoQ10 (ubiquinol)

MSM

Bitter Melon

(I also take Welchol, BC, and Luvox now.)

1 1/2 hours before I sleep:

Mag. Threonate

### nonchalant — 2012-09-26T18:30:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54453>

Hi Anglgrl, welcome to the forum. I think you will find some folks here that might have some good suggestions.

The first thing that I would try would be to switch the Magnesium Threonate and the Chelated Magnesium. Take the threonate earlier in the day, and take the larger dose of chelated mg just before bed. I know it helps with sleep. I worked my way up to 1,000 mg of chelated mg.

What are you doing right now for CT? Face dunks, ice packs, or baths? The face dunks may help for anxiety, so you could try one of those when you feel anxious.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-26T19:11:01+0300

Hi Nonchalant,

Thank you for answering

The mag Threonate is 600mg and the chelated is just 200mg. The mag does knock me out. I'm already tired but it really makes me fall asleep. (I'm thinking about upping the afternoon chelated to 300 but that's as high as I think I can go before it makes me groggy.)

I'm taking CT baths. I was doing face dunks at the end of my bath but it kept making my sinus clogged up so I quit. No spot CTing or anything...just the baths. (45 min.) And almost all of me is in the water (boobs, upper chest, shoulders are out). I was hoping the CT would help with my depression and anxiety but I haven't noticed any difference yet.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-26T19:19:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54471>

Re: waking up hungry, I'm wondering if you are getting enough fat in your diet, especially dinner. I eat seafood for dinner and since I don't eat sat fat with seafood, I try to chug a little extra cod liver oil to make up for it. I haven't been hungry in the middle of the night since starting to eat high fat, low carb. Some people may need more than others. Not sure about fat when you don't have a gallbladder though....maybe ox bile but not sure. I'm sure someone else will chime in on this to help.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-26T19:44:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54479>

The kind of low BG wake up in the middle of the night is usually related to adrenal/liver problems. Your BG drops while you are asleep b/c your liver isn't functioning correctly when your body should be in fat-burning mode. Your cortisol then kicks in b/c that's a survival situation and you wake up and need to eat.

Pattycakes- I'm eating an average of 71% of fat a day. I could try to go higher (I've done it before) and I've thought about doing it again. I'll try it tonight. Thank you.

If I can stay asleep I don't get hungry. (Or if I can fall back to sleep before I get ravenous I don't wake back up.). I'm sure I've got liver and adrenal damage so I'll still look into it more.

I appreciate all the suggestions.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-26T22:41:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54557>

### cramernh — 2012-09-27T00:51:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54603>

> **And before you ask, no I haven't had any tests done. I know that would help a lot (and I do plan to do it in the future), but my anxiety problems are pretty severe and going to get tests done would take a serious amount of tranquilizers to get me through and then I'd be screwed up for days. Kind of a catch 22 thing goin for me.
>
> If you have any suggestions (that don't involve me leaving the house) please let me know.**

If you are covered by medical insurance, call up the Member Services Dept using the phone number located on your card. I would suggest talking with a representative who can research any Lab companies that are not only accepting your insurance, but who could also make house-calls.

For the months of June and July I was laid up at home with multiple fractures of my Right heel - so no driving for me. Thankfully we have Quest Diagnostics here (about 8 facilities within a 20 minute commute), and they were able to come to my home to do some lab work that one of the specialists ordered.

Check it out!

### caroline — 2012-09-27T06:48:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54695>

are you using the blue blocker glasses? following circadian cycles is so important. Please do listen to Dr. Kruse - getting labs will tell you so much and you can start addressing any issues. Good luck

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-27T19:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-54769>

Wow. I didn't expect all these replies when I checked in today.

I really appreciate all the suggestions.

I know I need to have labs/consult done. I know I'd get better faster with them. I going to be real honest with you - I can't deal with this. Just thinking about this is pushing me over the edge. I barely slept last night because I was so anxious about Dr.Kruse saying I needed to have labs done.

And now I'm crying every time I think about it this morning. I don't think I can handle optimal right now. I know a lot of you will think I'm making a foolish choice and I'm just making it harder for myself down the line. I am aware and I'm willing to live with that. Sometimes just getting through the next day is all I can handle. But, who knows, in a week I could feel totally ok and be ready to do it. You never know with me.

I am going to keep trying to sleep through the night. And I'm going to do some searches on adrenal/liver supplements/detox/health and at home testing for labs.

Pattycakes: I did try eating more fat last night at dinner. It didn't help last night but I'm going to keep it up for a week or so.

SeaHorse: I have been looking into this (but just at ZRT - I'll look into the other one you suggested too.) Thanks

cramenrh: That is a great suggestion and I have considered it. But, the idea of a stranger coming to my house to take my blood sounds almost as nerve-wracking as going to the doctor/hospital to have it done. It's being around people that makes me nervous (strangers are worse, but sometimes it happens around people I know very well). But you're right, being in my house would take away one panic inducing factor.

caroline: Yes, I do have the blue-blocking sun glasses. Lately I haven't had to use them though because I've been going to bed right after sunset (and not using any artificial light once it's dark). Maybe I should try putting them on earlier while it's still light (since it's darker earlier now). I could put them on at like 5 or 6. Thanks for the idea

### cramernh — 2012-09-27T19:58:36+0300

>

> cramenrh: That is a great suggestion and I have considered it. But, the idea of a stranger coming to my house to take my blood sounds almost as nerve-wracking as going to the doctor/hospital to have it done. It's being around people that makes me nervous (strangers are worse, but sometimes it happens around people I know very well). But you're right, being in my house would take away one panic inducing factor.
>
>
>
> caroline: Yes, I do have the blue-blocking sun glasses. Lately I haven't had to use them though because I've been going to bed right after sunset (and not using any artificial light once it's dark). Maybe I should try putting them on earlier while it's still light (since it's darker earlier now). I could put them on at like 5 or 6. Thanks for the idea**

**

I can only imagine how high your cortisol levels are right now.....

You have to ask yourself are you willing to let your health decline? If the answer is no, you have to come to grips with what is going on in your life if the mere reading someone's post gave you that much stress to the point you couldnt sleep....

You most certainly have other factors here, psychologically speaking if you have what appears to be, phobias with people, people coming into your home, going to have basic care performed, etc.

Please tell me you are under the care of a Behavioral Health Specialist... honestly speaking here, no one is going to take care of you except yourself. Dr. Kruse simply agreed that you do need to get labs.

You are willingly allowing yourself to get worked up over labs... willingly allowing yourself to come up with reasons for not getting the care you need. I dont mean this to come off blunt in an evil way but Im going to say it: You have to get a grip here otherwise the very stress you are causing becomes more of a problem for you and just adds to your current situation. Its an unnecessary stress you are allowing yourself to get bent over....You do have the ability to control things if you are willing to come to terms with - "its just a blood draw"**.

You can do all the searching you want with regards to adrenal/liver supplements/detox/health - but Im going to tell you right now - anything that you read that you _think_ is going to help - DONT believe in it, in fact - bring the reading material with you to your PCP. No matter what, your PCP will tell you "before you do anything, Im going to want to send you for labs to get some readings done"... The lab work is inevitable.

My own mother (and yes, Im going to say it), was downright **STUPID** in thinking it was ok to try every Tom, Dick and Harry's magic remedy - it was going to cure this, that and the other thing. I asked her "did you talk with your PCP first?" she had the balls to say "No, I want to take this and if it works, THEN Ill tell my PCP"... I looked at her again and pointed to her 12 medications (She is a cardiac patient who survived ARDS), and said "are you willing to risk having a drug reaction? Common sense dictates you should be talking with your PCP before you take ANYTHING that could have a negative impact on your current health - are you really willing to risk doing this? This is very careless decision making on your part"... She tried to read the riot-act, but I retorted right back... I told her had she made the right choices in her health two years ago, she might have prevented the two heart attacks, stroke and pericarditis she experienced - instead youre willing to make another bad decision and risk something all over again?.... I got sick and tired of her foolish ways...

Im telling you right now - get the labs.... use some common sense. Find a supportive friend to go with you that 'knows' you and can give you the support that you need to get the labs done. Whether a friend, family member - or even someone in the office you know that you are comfortable with to hold your hand.... there are always compassionate staff members who are more than willing to help hold a patient's hand to get through this. And please - dont be like my mother... UGHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHH

### cramernh — 2012-09-27T20:01:40+0300

Instead of easily falling back to excuses, take a step forward to find solutions.... you CAN do this!!!!!

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-28T18:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-55019>

cramernh - I usually just sigh in resignation and walk away when I read or hear things like this. But I feel that if I don't say something, people like you will continue to believe that it's ok to think the things you do.

Mental illness, at it's most basic, is thinking/feeling/doing things that you have no control over. I'm not just being negative or in a mood. I can't will my way out of this anymore than a cancer patient can will their cancer away. I have no control over the thoughts and feelings that I have. I cry unexpectedly and without reason some days - I have no control over it. I think about killing myself at random times during the day for no reason - I have no control over it. This is what makes having a mental illness so bad - you have no control over your own thoughts. You seem to think you can logic my panic away. If you are looking for logic in mental illness you're going to be searching a long time. My brain knows exactly what is going on...it just over reacts to it - against my will.

You know what else is bad about having a mental illness? People treat you like it's your choice that you're sick. Like I'm making excuses to keep from being happy. These people seem to think that if you just try harder you can work your way out of it. (You can do it!)

Willpower and a positive attitude aren't going to regulate my serotonin and dopamine levels.

I'm not mad or anything. I know you are just trying to help.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-28T18:16:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-55022>

Caroline - I haven't been using the blue-blockers when watching TV and when looking at the computer. (But I'm hardly on the computer.) But I can start wearing them while watching TV. Brilliant. Thanks.

I did order some tests from ZRT yesterday and I told dad I wanted to have some tests done at the hospital when I felt ok enough to do it (and I make it through the night without eating).

Thanks for the suggestions and help, everybody!

### hazyjane — 2012-09-29T23:58:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-55292>

I wonder if you have an autoimmune thyroid situation going on here (the gallbladder thing and the mood disorders make me wonder...) I think it might be good to lay off the Iodorol until you know more about your thyroid antibodies, as it could be making things worse and contributing to the sleep problems.

Have you ever tried l-theanine or inositol for your anxiety? Those would be worth looking into (and l-theanine is super safe and can be used as needed!)

Good luck and hang in there

### Sleuth72 — 2012-10-14T13:42:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-discouraged-need-suggestions.3928/post-58076>

I am a supervisor and counselor in a mental health/chemical dependency clinic. A year ago, I had the worst trauma of my life come out of nowhere and I was forced to use all the "skills" I am always pushing on my clients. It was NOT easy. I was also put on a cocktail of drugs for about nine months (ativan, Effexor, Lexapro, Wellbutrin) even though I have always been anti-AD for myself. I had been seeing a therapist who push, push, pushed them....finally I gave in and each subsequent drug messed me up in different ways. I finally got off the last one (Wellbutrin) about a month ago, and I say NEVER AGAIN! The only thing that truly worked for me was changing my situation, perspective, thinking, attitude and outlook on life - and I had to do this over and over. I wrote myself notes and reviewed them nonstop to program things into my brain. This was exhausting, but over time began to take root. I also found Dr. Kruse during this time and I believe this greatly helped me gain empowerment and to realize that if I do not adapt to change, I will die unhappy. I also decided I really, really wanted an optimized life, to reduce stress, and to stop letting people jerk my strings like a damned puppet. I don't think I truly believed depression was real until I went through this trauma, and it helped me to be more empathetic toward my clients. It also helped me to be a bit more of a hardass (LOL) - your life is what you make it - no one can fix it but you - humans are human and can fail you, and at the end of the day you are ultimately stuck with yourself and must love and accept yourself the best you can. I am so happy I got off the drugs... and have given up trying to please the world. anglgrl I hope you get your labs done and keep working toward mastery of yourself through small, incremental steps

### Souldanzer — 2012-10-15T01:26:20+0300

Regardless of what you have allowed yourself, you can will yourself out of negative thinking if you willingly choose to do so. I have many behavioral health patients who are capable, through proper training, education, and adjustment protocols learn how to handle one thing at a time. Some are doing it without medications. I tend to have alot of faith in my patients at work because they want to get better and work extremely hard at the very core of their being and have just as many ups and downs as a person with no mental illness. So I dont take excuses - plain and simple.

wow. that's a strong statement. and nothing like my experience. at all. I doubt even Dr K would agree with that after what was discussed last night.

# Thread: Negative Reaction To Testosterone IM injections
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freshveggies — 2012-09-26T17:59:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/post-54439>

Hi, my husband started testosterone IM injections 4 weeks ago at a small dose of 100mg per week. He seems to be not feeling better on them and has some negative symptoms. Symptoms are lower back pain, depressed feeling, decreased sex drive with increased sensitivity, increased urination at night with less deep sleep and some chest pain. He just drew labs and will be seeing the doctor next week about this, but as of now does not want another injection based on how the first 4 weeks went. Any thoughts aoubt this?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-26T21:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/post-54521>

I doubt its the T.......but the route could make a difference.

### freshveggies — 2012-09-26T22:24:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/post-54551>

Thanks. Only thing that changed was adding the T. We shall see what the doctor recommends.

### Huck — 2012-09-26T22:38:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/post-54555>

Does he have labs for total and free testosterone, vit d, dhea-s, dht, estrodial, and sbhg? Did he have a PSA test? I know many, including Jack, don't think PSA is a good indicator of prostate cancer, but I think it can be used as indicator of prostate inflammation. Some of his symptoms sound like prostate inflammation to me.

### freshveggies — 2012-09-26T22:57:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/post-54559>

> **Huck;55326 wrote:** Does he have labs for total and free testosterone, vit d, dhea-s, dht, estrodial, and sbhg? Did he have a PSA test? I know many, including Jack, don't think PSA is a good indicator of prostate cancer, but I think it can be used as indicator of prostate inflammation. Some of his symptoms sound like prostate inflammation to me.

DHEA-S 164 range 35-570

PSA .47 range 0-4.0

Vit D 82 range 30-100

Started DHEA and Pregnenolone mid July based on Labs. I will have to get the other labs later. Started T Aug 31.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-27T00:53:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/post-54605>

PSA is worthless......even the urology literature no longer supports its use. there is a few forms of Testosterone out there......I'd suggest talking to the doctor to just switch him to another form of the injectable.

### freshveggies — 2012-09-27T02:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/negative-reaction-to-testosterone-im-injections.3929/post-54631>

> **Jack Kruse;55403 wrote:** PSA is worthless......even the urology literature no longer supports its use. there is a few forms of Testosterone out there......I'd suggest talking to the doctor to just switch him to another form of the injectable.

Thanks. I will check into that.

# Thread: Product Selection  Dosing of Curcumin  Resveratrol in my N=1 Experiment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-selection-dosing-of-curcumin-resveratrol-in-my-n-1-experiment.3936/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Larry — 2012-09-26T21:26:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-selection-dosing-of-curcumin-resveratrol-in-my-n-1-experiment.3936/post-54524>

I am posting this write up for my father who spent 40 years working as a Pharmaceutical researcher and supplement developer. He wanted to share his personal findings to hopefully help some other people. He reads the blogs and threads at times but does not post so he asked me to post it for him. Up front, I am certainly unqualified to answer any questions about his writings but I thought some of you might enjoy tossing his thoughts back forth.

**Product Selection and Dosing of Curcumin and Resveratrol in My N1 Experiment.**

I recently have begun taking both curcumin and resveratrol because I have amyloidosis of the bladder and right ureter, in the hope of clearing out the amyloid deposits.

I am using Meriva (Curcumin Phytosome) 500 mg. from Doctorâ€™s Best, twice daily. And Resveratrol 5 mg. once daily in the AM along with a single Lecithin Triple-Strength Softgel, 400 mg. (35% phosphatidylcholine from soy) from Swanson.

I have pain and physical movement problems caused by osteoarthritis and lumbar stenosis. These serve as an indicator of medication effect. The degree of relief obtained from these symptoms tell me whether or not the supplements are getting into my system (in some meaningful way).

I find a significant improvement in these physical symptoms since starting on the above regimen. I should like to share with you what I think is allowing these improvements.

An Italian herbal company by the name of Indena, has developed a whole series of herbal products that have better oral absorption. They coined the term â€œphytosomeâ€￾ to designate these products. Curcumin and resveratol are among those listed on their company web site.

Indena has adopted the name of Meriva for their phospholipids-curcuminoids complex product, which is the product I am taking. I cannot find a supplier of the other phytosome products, especially the resveratrol product, even though they have given it a name, Rexatrol.

I recently read the LE Path Final 3 paper (understanding only the general thrust of what was being said), and definitely wanted to try adding resveratrol to the supplements I take.

Not being able to find a phytosome product, I made a Rube Goldberg attempt to simulate one by taking regular resveratrol along with lecithin. In this my N1 experiment the combination is working for me.

I have been taking the Meriva product alone for several weeks, and was able to note some physical pain and movement relief. That improvement seemed to escalate over the passing days. Then I added the resveratrol and lecithin combination, about 10 days ago, and the improvement was (in my case) just short of dramatic, and each day I seem to be getting better.

For those who might be interested in learning about these phytosome products you can find some good descriptions in the following paper by Dr. Paris Kidd. Alternative Medicine Review Volume 14, Number 3 2009.

I should point out that the curcuminoids constitute slightly less than 20% of the 500 mg. Meriva capsule. Hence the discernable effect (in my body) of the relatively small dose of curcumin is noteworthy. The same thing seems to apply to my Rube Goldberg combination of 5 mg. resveratrol with 400 mg of lecithin.

As a possible explanation for the effectiveness, especially of the small resveratrol dosage, I refer to the following paper: J Control Release. 2008 Feb 18;126(1):1-9. Epub 2007 Nov 7.

This paper speaks of a lecithin adduct with valpoic acid, and of its significant absorption into the lymphatic system in the gut, thus bypassing the liver and its first pass metabolism.

In the LE Path Final 3 paper by Gregory S. Bambeck Ph.D and Michael Wolfson J.D., M.B.A.

â€œLife Extension Metabolic Pathway Map Reveals New Phytonutrient Candidatesâ€￾ the authors speak of the first pass metabolism of resveratrol as a reason for its poor performance when taken orally (see bottom of p. 27).

It is my guess that the lecithin moiety of the lecithin adducts of phytonutrients like resveratrol and curcumin allow significant gut absorption thru the lymphatic system, and thus some distribution to the tissues before they become metabolized for excretion, thus allowing for effectiveness of small doses.

I take many other dietary supplements every day, and it could be that I hit some unknown lucky combination, so this may not be cause and effect here. But I can say that I have been lucky, or blessed, and being thankful for it, I wanted share it with others.

L. Daher

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-26T21:27:58+0300

### Larry — 2012-09-27T15:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/product-selection-dosing-of-curcumin-resveratrol-in-my-n-1-experiment.3936/post-54731>

Nonchalent, I don't know if CO would work but here is my understanding of why the lecithin works well in combination with resveratrol or curcumin. Per my father's explanation: lecithin has an opposite charge, so to speak, of both resveratrol and curcumin whereby they bind together very well. By binding I believe it increases the absorption of the supplement and that better absorption helps you take a smaller dose which is of course less expensive. Lecithin is dirt cheap. Sorry if I did not explain it well.

# Thread: Hello from Pasadena, CA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-pasadena-ca.3947/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-09-27T00:08:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-pasadena-ca.3947/post-54585>

Hello All,

I have been on my latest weight loss journey since January of this year and everything I read leads me right back here. I have worked hard to get these first 30 lbs off, 60 more to go, and am now enjoying the beauty of actually eating to loose weight. Who would have thought.

I am a stay at home mother of 2 and I am constantly searching for information on how to keep my kids from having to deal with weight issues like I have for my whole life.

I am super excited to have found this site and can't wait to really get into what it has to offer. I have been following a paleo diet for about one week now and am shocked at the difference so far. I sure can't wait for the carb blues to go and will rejoice in the 5 lb weight loss so far.

Thank you for listening to my ramblings and I can't wait to read more.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-27T00:38:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-pasadena-ca.3947/post-54593>

Hello and welcome............

### LinD — 2012-09-27T00:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-pasadena-ca.3947/post-54597>

Congrats, Whitner, on the 30# loss, and best wishes for the next 60#. Not sure if baby steps or big leaps are recommended, but just do it!

Welcome! Read all the blogs, plus the comments. I'm still learning.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-27T00:59:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-54612>

How does everybody afford to eat this way. I live in Southern California and all the good quality meat and seafood costs so much around here. How does everybody do it? Is it better to get high quality meats/seafood or organic produce? Our food bill is extremely high for a family of 4.

Looking for advice

Thanks in advance.

> Looking for advice
>

### nonchalant — 2012-09-27T01:25:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-54626>

I've saved lots of money by skipping all the grassfed meats I used to buy. That was so expensive.

I get my seafood at the asian grocery store for much less per pound, often $3-$5. I get frozen scallops for $6/lb.

And I don't buy as much produce any more. I haven't budgeted it out, but the amount I save on skipping the beverage, baking, canned goods, and dry goods asiles really help out. Plus I have now stopped buying things I used to buy at the drug store, like toothpaste, deodorant, hair coloring, lotion, etc. Homemade is much better.

I'm still spending lots on supplements and tests, but I'm gradually decreasing the supplements. I've stopped paying for dental insurance. I've dropped my long-term health care coverage too. Oh, and dropping 4 prescriptions per month is saving me about $95.

### cramernh — 2012-09-27T01:31:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-54629>

> **Nonchalant;55425 wrote:** I've saved lots of money by skipping all the grassfed meats I used to buy. That was so expensive.
>
> I get my seafood at the asian grocery store for much less per pound, often $3-$5. I get frozen scallops for $6/lb.

If I put my order in at our Asian grocery store here, I can get whole salmon (wild) for $4.99/lb ... Its a great price and the savings are rediculous! I bring it home, I do the 'cleaning', and with other seafood scraps, Ill boil up a seafood stock with that while Im fileting the seafood and prepping it for meals and such.. I never get the weird looks from the asians when I purchase it but the looks from the American shoppers is PRICELESS! LOL!!!!! They think its rediculous to get a whole fish.... I simply tell them "at least Im not the sucker paying $9.99/lb for them to do the work! Im getting TWICE as much and nothing goes to waste"... shuts them up every time!

BWAHAHAHAHAHAHHA

### Dali Dula — 2012-09-27T02:03:52+0300

I have found that as I incorporate more spices and sea veg into my meals they become much more filling(satisfying?). If you have access to bulk spices and sea veg( like the food coop) this can be affordable. And don't forget the fat.

We are all blessed to be very healthy, I'm the unfortunate one who is over weight.

### [email redacted] — 2012-09-27T02:37:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-54642>

Also, you eat a LOT LESS volume over time with this. Our dinner plates now look completely different, smaller meals. No snacking.

There is a transition, but once you start rolling you find out it's easier done than said. Your calories might not change much, but when you are eating 60+ percent fat, it's pretty darn cheap per calorie. Even with the good coconut oil.

We were a kinda spendy bunch, drinks alone have cut costs - and I still do bullet proof coffee.

I find we also waste alot less food, and don't spend money on eating out. Cravings and hunger become something different all together (read ealachans journal, shes a great example of the transition) and you simply are find just noshing down on the leftover crockpot whatever.

### lioness7 — 2012-09-27T03:06:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-54645>

> **Jack Kruse;55421 wrote:** Let me ask another question........if you don't eat well how do you manage the cost of illness?

So true. My bil spends 500 per month in meds for him and his wife....and most of them are keeping them status quo and not curing them of course.. What could he do foodwise with an extra 500 bucks?

Stay out of isles. Find a local farmer/greenhouse operator...get to know them. Eat local/in season. Freeze, jar and cook . Plan your meals around what is on special. Once you get used to eating high quality nutrient dense food, you will eat less but eat better and the cost be less . People have been conditioned to think food is cheap. Processed food has been kept cheap for a reason....but people will pay the price eventually.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-27T03:11:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-54647>

Don't agree at all. It might be true yet that answer/question is a dead end. All it does is create more stress over the situation. It doesn't make your bank account bigger knowing the crappy situation you will be in if you can't make it change (can't obviously chosen here as W is asking for advice on HOW to make the situation better).

Withners, I think you asked the correct question. You obviously understand that it is necessary, and now you are looking for solutions.

My random tip: Seafood comes first. Whichever quality.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-27T04:33:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-54673>

We also struggle with the economics of things. What has worked for us is re-prioritising. There were/are a lot of things that seem like necessities (heat in winter being one ) that we've ditched. We buy all our clothing second-hand (except undies... I've drawn the line there) , we don't go out to eat (where would we go??), we frequent the local farmer's markets, we bulk buy the staples like coconut oil, we don't go on vacation... but we make sure we have a lot of cheap fun together as a family. My kids still have activities they do that cost money but that's about our one extra. This may sound dire and unappealing, but it actually has simplified our life and we are happy, as well as living & eating better. That isn't to say that we can always buy what we'd like to buy to eat (fish head smoothies aren't just good for you, they are dirt cheap... but I would rather have Dr K's sea bass with lobster ANY day of the week!), we can't. And I can't buy all the supplements I want either. This is a reality. A dollar only is worth a dollar no matter how many times you click your heels together. But there really are ways to realign yourself to this journey if you really want it.

### Integral_D — 2012-10-02T22:04:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-55926>

I think this is my first post, but I'll jump in with some (maybe) direct practical ideas. I agree that it seems crazy-expensive to buy "epi-paleo" food -- I've got three boys and two of them are teenagers. My wife is very focused on both good nutrition (as she understands it) and on not busting the budget. We are in the DC suburbs of northern VA, and while there are all kinds of food options, it's very expensive. We have a local farmer's market and can get local grass fed meats. (expensive). Same with dairy (although I've got the family almost entirely weaned from milk. The local pasture fed dairy also did not taste good, so that was easy.) Way too expensive to eat all the time.

First, I'd say eat a bit lower down the food chain... you don't have to eat Ahi tuna and wild salmon (or chilean sea bass/lobster) all the time. Dr Kruse recommends shellfish (both crustaceans and non-c) as the base. That can get very expensive if you're eating blue crab all the time. Think seaside forager -- (I highly recommend the old Euell Gibbons book "Stalking the Blue-Eyed Scallop" as well as his classic "Stalking the Wild Asparagus.") Mussels, scallops, clams etc (even oysters in some places) can be cheaper than fancy fish in season. Frozen often costs less than fresh, (and frankly, I trust it more than the "fresh" stuff that has traveled a zillion miles). Smaller sized ones are generally cheaper -- 40/lb shrimp are a lot cheaper than 16/lb -- and a pound of shrimp is a pound of shrimp. (As long as you aren't peeling them)

Second, hit up your local Asian/Latino supermarket. In SoCal it shouldn't be hard to find. One with a big seafood counter. In season (summer) the wild salmon will be cheaper there. But you can always find "less desirable" species for a LOT less money. Just a matter of finding good ways to cook them. On the east coast there are always croakers, bluefish, striped bass, mackerel, various tunas, etc. Same with the shellfish. Buy them whole and get the fish counter to clean them for you. Get the whole fish and figure out something to do with ALL of it. Same with cuts of other meats. Not all lamb is sold as perfect little chops.... If you are into the offal thing (ummm. .. I'd rather eat raw oysters) you can get all you can handle at these markets.

Third, I've discovered that the local Costco has started carrying a number of organic foods, including eggs, KerryGold butter (finally!), and some meats. MUCH less expensive than the "boutique" sources. The frozen seafood area has lots of wild caught fish, for reasonable prices. I stay away from the farmed catfish and tilapia. For veggies, especially greens, it can be good too. The fruit is not impressive, but the farmer's market or local supermarket are fine for whatever we need in season. On sale, it's as cheap as Costco. Also, cans of pink salmon, tuna, maybe even sardines.

Finally, cooking economically mostly has to do with simply learning how to prepare stuff that is not the standard cuts that are laid out in the meat trays. I have a theory that any cuisine that has survived for hundreds or thousands of years has learned to make damn near anything palatable. French, Chinese, Indian, Italian, you name it. People haven't always lived off of Ahi fillets and pre-snapped green beans. Hell, it must have been a very hungry French cook that figured out how to make snails taste good! I've seen Italian recipes for "Pie made of small birds". (Just like the nursery rhyme... Yechh!) Most of these things are made into soups, stews, scramble/stir fries, etc. It's pretty much how your great grandma cooked.

A couple of really good cookbooks include The Joy of Cooking (it contains ALL KINDS of recipes and instructions about how to prep and cook nearly everything), and Cuisine Economique by Jacques Pepin. Learning to cook this way is mostly about mindset -- it's the way people had to cook when every fish was either caught or bought whole, and a steak was something that you splurged on and then divided amongst all the diners. I'd also recommend a good Cajun cookbook-- all kinds of seafood dishes and it's country cooking so they tend to "use every part of the pig but the squeal." And they are NOT afraid of a little fat

I know that most families, including mine, don't have a lot of time to cook or unlimited money to spend. We save time by cooking big batches. That has been the situation for nearly every human since the Rift Valley Days.... Epi-paleo is how many people have always eaten throughout the world -- especially along waterways which is where most humans have always lived -- so there are countless recipes out there. If they are not quite "paleo" they can always be adapted.

It's doable -- the challenge for us is getting enough volume to satisfy growing boys without loading up on grain/carbs. And weaning kids off the prevailing cultural diet which is installed in every school, at every friend's house, every play date or group activity, and relentlessly via media. It's a process. Hope this helps a little.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-02T23:05:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-55945>

Hi Serotonizen, welcome. Great first post!

Thanks for the reminder about Stalking the Blue Eyed Scallop. I have the book somewhere, just haven't read it. I used to be more fixated on veggies than I am now.

I also look for old recipe books in used bookstores, anything pre 1960. There are some amazing recipes, and even better, some cooking techniques, like using fire and fileting fish, and butchering game.

### Glamazon — 2012-10-07T20:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-56763>

I am recently on the down turn for incoming finances so I have to seriously budgetize my life where as before I didn't have any worries. It has forced me to go to stores that I would not normally even think about going to.

I was very surprised to find at the Dollar Store cans of seafood for a dollar. I have bought kippers, sardines, clams, oysters all packed in water for a dollar. Sources range from USA to China to Thailand. I have also found decently priced canned salmon at Target and Walmart. It is also worth it to troll thru their frozen seafood. Raw, fresh is a luxury purchase right now. I can buy seafood offal (heads and stuff) at Whole Foods for a 1.99 a pound.

The Dollar store also has frozen veggies from the US of A that cost a dollar a bag. I don't eat much veggies anymore. Since it seems nitrates are off the no-no list I might be changing my sources of bacon.

Budget downsizing has forced me to become a more efficient hunter-gatherer.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-09T00:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-56965>

Here are my best tips for beginners. Of course, focus on the shellfish and seafood. Eat the whole animal if possible. In our mixed up society the "waste" parts are usually the best parts. Grow some of your own food in a garden; you can save a load from eating seasonally from a garden. If you can, get some livestock or start wildcrafting, fishing or hunting. I know that might be a bit much but usually we have to spend our time or our money if we want the best food for our families:

[http://eatkamloops.org/where-to-start-limited-time-and-budget/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/where-to-start-limited-time-and-budget/)

Just ignore the stuff about grains. This post is for the mass consumption. I am just trying to get the average person off industrial food and eating the real thing!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-09T03:16:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-57020>

Pasadena -- one of the loveliest climates in the world. Some plant foods should be extremely easy to grow, practically year round there. If you just choose the plants and herbs which are easiest to grow, that's a start. And you can't beat the convenience of walking into your yard, picking the herbs or veg you want when they are at their best, and never even needing a refrigerator for the greens and fresh produce part of your diet. Of course you have to learn timing. What you can have when, and what you need to wait for.

I remember when I lived in Stockton (not the nicest climate in the world!) and I had no money. I bought a two-in-one nectarine tree on sale because it had started as a three-in-one, and one of the grafts had failed. Maybe $8. Planted it, it thrived, soon I had incredible nectarines. I learned I could freeze them very easily. Cut them off the pits, put in a glass casserole dish with a glass lid. Microwave till sloppy looking. Spoon into freezer jars, leaving some room at the top. Top off with apple juice. They were incredible later in the year, and the cost was nearly nil, just some electricity for the freezer.

Today I picked bunches of blue muscat grapes off a grapevine I started from a donated cutting nine years ago. The vine is covered. They are seedless and totally delicious. Because they spike my blood sugar, I don't have them often, but just gave a bucketful to a friend who will juice and can them. I only watered that vine once this summer!

A careful choice of perennials like trees and bushes suited to the area (could you grow avocados??) will give the most food for the least work, and the most food security, all for pennies. Some herbs like dill can sometimes be planted once and then just volunteer from then on. You'll never beat either the freshness or the price.

I'm still learning how to cook fresh fish. So far my default tactic is to poach all the fin fish (with herbs like rosemary and oregano and some sea salt in the water), eat some right away and keep some in the fridge. I take the poaching water (used several times in one day for several piece of fish), add a few vegetables like onions and carrots, then I add a can or bottle of oysters, and at least a half pound or more of Oregon shrimp. Put into mason jars, freeze if necessary, but usually I can just warm it up and eat it before it goes bad.

"Proper" cooking will probably come later on, but this seems to work. The prime rule for cooking fish (which, sadly, my mother never learned) is not to overcook it. The second rule (should probably be the first) is to buy it as fresh as possible and cook it as soon as you possibly can. The most expensive food is what has turned and needs to be thrown out.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-09T07:18:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-57062>

Did somebody mention Pasadna, CA? We should meet up for some Epi-Paleo grocery foraging!

For my single person Epi-Paleo budget of $70/week (been that high ever since going paleo...just the price of good health, I guess), I've worked this out:

1 day/week total fast

3 days/week "surf"

3 days/week "turf"

Surf days:

\- 10 raw oysters from Seafood Paradise in Rosemead for $1/oyster (total 30 oysters/week)

\- maybe some seaweed (going into winter, not so much) from Whole Foods or ordered in bulk online for Mountain Rose Herbs, not factoring this into weekly cost right now

Turf days:

\- 1.5 lbs of grassfed chuck from Sprouts at $6.99/lb (total 4.5 lbs/week)

\- .33 lbs of grassfed or USDA Natural liver from Whole Foods at $6/lb (total 1 lb/ week)

This is a grassfed chuck heavy diet. I could get more fish (like mackerel) for the same amount of money, but I decided to maximize my seafood impact by maxing out on oysters, and I find that my body CRAVES grassfed chuck and does not feel satisfied without lots of it. Fish just does not satisfy in the same way; my body must need the Vit A or something else in it. YMMV.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-17T05:47:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-58669>

> **Destiny;59277 wrote:** That sounds like a very good eating plan. And $70.00 a week for good health - $10.00 a day is very cheap in my opinion compare to illness expenses!

Ironically, since I wrote that, I've gone a wee bit over that amount. For some weird reason, maybe the growing CT sessions and possible estrogen dump, I've been ravenous lately. Turf days see 3-4lbs of meat. And that just barely satiates me. Surf days are 10 oysters and and pound or two of oily fish.

I really, literally, can't afford to keep that up (part time income for a grad student life in Southern California just won't support that ravenous an appetite). But damn, I'm hungry and craaaaave that grassfed beef. Must be something in it that my body needs. Hopefully it will get its fill soon before I out-eat my means.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/knee-surgury-finding-blog-post-help.3949/post-54616>

Anybody want to share the link love and point me to the sites where Jack talks about preparing for this?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-27T01:14:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/knee-surgury-finding-blog-post-help.3949/post-54623>

### indigogirl — 2012-09-27T05:45:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/knee-surgury-finding-blog-post-help.3949/post-54684>

Mam, get his doctor to prescribe the ice machine that will Velcro around his knee and keep it cold. My insurance paid 100%. I had to ask my doctor for it, though, as they don't usually prescribe it for meniscus tear repairs. I slept with it on my knee the first 2 nights post surgery and used it as often as I could.

I also took Arnica a week pre surgery to help with bruising...I had almost no bruising.

# Thread: Heart Disease
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 45
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-27T12:47:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-54714>

What a coincidence! This was the topic on Jimmy Moore's podcast today, where he had guest Kent Rieske.

Mr. Rieske said that what the medical profession does is not "science", which as an engineer appalls him. I thought that was slightly unfair, because I don't think that, say, Ancel Keys equals "the medical profession". I'm also inclined to suspect that engineers have their own _dÃ©formation professionnelle - they think everything's about math. So Rieske said that a low-carb diet would halt the deposition of arterial plaque but could not reverse it. I suspect he's looked at some material on this and is probably right. His answer is medication to reduce LDL cholesterol below a certain number. The idea seems to be that you manipulate variables, by any means available, and the answer falls out. All about math. But what if you don't understand the aetiology fully? What if there are dimensions to the problem you haven't even dreamed of? How if, everything else being as it is, the LDL needs to be where it is?

I had an inkling of what was coming in BG13 - alhough it goes without saying no more than that. I'd seen the "French paradox is really an epi-paleo paradox" material and put a few things together.

So Key's line was that heart disease is caused by saturated fat. The low carb people think it's caused by carbohydrate. Paleo's current position seems to be that it's caused by saturated fat _plus_ high-glycemic load carbs.

But what if the problems is that it's not so much what you eat as what you don't_ eat? Where's the DHA in current diets?

Cordain has something about two Inuit bodies from medieval times that had been mummified in the ice. He says they have arterial plaque, and this shows that saturated fat will cause plaque to form (although, he says, that need not be a problem). I wonder if anyone has thought to do static-isotope analysis on the bones. It would be interesting to know whether they were eating predominantly land-based game (such as caribou) or predominantly sea-resources.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-27T14:22:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-54723>

I'll know where I'd lay my money.........

>

Anything that prevents autophagy would be a cause. Lack of sleep, lack of DHA, impaired hormone communication, inflammation, oxidation...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-28T00:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-54875>

I am realising more and more that the truth is a matter of both great simplicity and great complexity. It is what we do eat AND what we don't. It is what we do and what we don't do. It is what we think, what we don't think, and what we fail to even realise. It is our genetic switches, our epigenetic flipping of those switches and our knowledge of our own selves mixed with our current environment. There will never be one silver bullet or magic answer. The more you know, the more you have to learn.

I love that you are able to put all these sources together and question things. It helps me a lot in discovering my own learning edge.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-28T01:16:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-54885>

> **KiwiLauren;55704 wrote:** The more you know, the more you have to learn.

I totally agree and it drives me crazy! Lifelong learner, lifelong researcher, lifelong crazy person I guess!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-28T01:55:07+0300

what causes us to get fat is abnormal cellular signaling in the brain due to inflammation at the hypothalamus.....from there it all goes bad. you must have the brain injury first to get the disease.......and lots of things cause the injury.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-28T13:37:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-54965>

They don't have the hypothalamic injuries from their foods as we do........its not the foods that get us fat it is the brain injury in the hypothalamus that does. Modern humans have created those injuries at an alarming rate. They blame the food when all long it was modern life which caused their brain damage that altered how the account for electrons from food via their altered gut.

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-28T14:16:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-54971>

That really illuminates how distorted it is to think that we can just shop around the world till we find a culture that looks healthy...eat like them and voila...we're healthy too. To use a foodie term the _terroir_ of their brains is what makes the difference.

I'm still thinking about The Long Winter...the Wilder family in the 1800s was able to survive on wheat alone because they didn't have the brain damage from modern life...they were cold and dark in the winter and their signalling still worked.

> So Key's line was that heart disease is caused by saturated fat. The low carb people think it's caused by carbohydrate. Paleo's current position seems to be that it's caused by saturated fat _plus_ high-glycemic load carbs.
>
>
>
> But what if the problems is that it's not so much what you eat as what you don't_ eat? Where's the DHA in current diets?
>
>
>
> Cordain has something about two Inuit bodies from medieval times that had been mummified in the ice. He says they have arterial plaque, and this shows that saturated fat will cause plaque to form (although, he says, that need not be a problem). I wonder if anyone has thought to do static-isotope analysis on the bones. It would be interesting to know whether they were eating predominantly land-based game (such as caribou) or predominantly sea-resources.

I want you to channel what I said about the heart and brain in BG 13 with respect to how the Na/K ATPase is affected in metabolic terms in all tissues.......then think about the number one killer of all humans today: Heart disease. How does heart disease tie into this theory of whole body physiology?

If you begin to alter your blood glucose because of dietary or any other epigenetics signal (light or temp) and cause inflammation at a cellular level by eating badly or not being mindful of circadian cycles, you start secreting large amounts of insulin from beta cells to deal with it. This alters your Magnesium levels which activate the metabolically efficient Na/K ratio because of DHA as laid out in BG 13. When we have an altered Na/K (low) ratio, this in turn, simultaneously cause brain metabolism to ratcheded down because its function is directly coupled to the efficiency of the Na/K ATPase for our species. As the brain metabolism falters, it begins to shunt things to it at the determent of other organs. It robs Peter to pay Paul. This physiologic bio energetic "stealing" of energy alters functions everywhere in the body to satisfy the brains needs. As other organs fail slowly under this physiologic directive This further alters brain function from the excessive 60% energy boon created by the DHA/ Na/K ATPase, which results in your feeling less well with less energy and you have a chronic cognitive haze. Is this sounding familiar to anyone? It is the modern human condition. As this goes on chronically brain function is eroded (the real source of adrenal fatigue in the PVN) because there is an excess of Na ions which alter our homeostatic brain controls of the renin angiotensin system and this alters our BP. The renin angiotensin system target organ is the kidneys. The kidneys control the electrolyte balance of the serum and ultimate of the CSF (ultrafiltrate of the serum) to further alter the function of our brain. This slowly erosion of our kidney function goes on undetected and slowly undermines our electrical systems balance. This implies that our declining kidney function, directly afffects our electrical system of our heart (conduction system AV node and sinus pacemaker) and become less optimal or "foggy" too. This shows you why the declining brain function always correlates with declining heart function. They are chemically and electrically tied together by DHA and iodine at a foundational level physiologically in humans. This all results in the many types of cardiac dysrythmias we see clinically. This is an early sign of major bio energenic shifts in cells all tied to oxidation. When this goes on further brain function erodes and we can not sense it well. We in essence lose our primal sense of well being and eventually we develop an inability to sense vascular trouble before it happens clinically. This leads to a heart attack. Now think back to Inger.........she cant tell you any of this physiology........but she can tell you that she feels a sense of well being now........because her signaling and sensation in her brain is approaching optimal........this means the Na/K ATPase battery that is driving her Ferrari engine is working at the peak 60% efficiency I mention in BG 13. How did she intuitively find it? Look at what she eats..........MHS and fermented heart in a jar. She has tapped into her brain sensation because this is what it needs to sense its world to avoid illness. That my friend is understanding heart disease from the Quilt's perspective. Inger is a perfect example of where form has met function.

### Michael — 2012-09-28T15:20:49+0300

> **Jack Kruse;55874 wrote:** This is precisely what the paleo leadership has done to its followers with respect to the Masai and Kitavins........It has set back this movement more than they even know. They are blind to it. One researcher blogger in particular has done more harm in my view than any other and he remains completely ignorant of what he does not know while remianing quite confident all the bull\*\*\*\* he thinks he knows.

IMO, AHS 2011 caused big problems. AFAICT, there is a tiny coterie of people responsible - they could be counted on the hand of a dreamer in a sawmill. (And at bottom it was driven by a single person.) But what they succeeded in doing was driving a wedge between paleo and the low-carb people. That's all the wider ancestral health movement needed. It's damaged its momentum for a year or two, possibly longer. And such was the apparent plausibility, mainly down to force of the rhetoric, I guess - for I think the arguments were bogus - on this that it even took the confidence away from many low carb people. It's taken them about a year to get it back. I don't believe the low carb people are right on everything, but I think they're more right than wrong, and the movement could have done without a split anyway.

Kitava! As I pointed out here, apart from anything else there's no electricity on the island. No-one seems to have thought that important.

Thanks for the tip in post #12.

Their evolutionary picture is wrong. Their books neglect brain-chemistry. They've never laid out the theory Jack has.

It's not minor "semantic" differences. They're not even close.

### Michael — 2012-09-29T17:46:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55224>

I got sidetracked by the politics of the movement.

But I did read this:

> **Jack Kruse;55876 wrote:** I want you to channel what I said about the heart and brain in BG 13 with respect to how the Na/K ATPase is affected in metabolic terms in all tissues.......then think about the number one killer of all humans today: Heart disease. How does heart disease tie into this theory of whole body physiology?
>
>
> You just explained to me why heart disease happens, and it makes perfect sense. You've just told me something that perhaps no-one in paleo and few people outside it know. And it's huge. But that's not all, is it?
>
>
>
>
> > ... eventually we develop an inability to sense vascular trouble before it happens clinically. This leads to a heart attack.
>
>
>
> I'm thinking that with a brain not working as it should it's not only that we'd lose the ability to sense.
>
>
>
> Now think back to Inger.........she cant tell you any of this physiology........but she can tell you that she feels a sense of well being now........because her signaling and sensation in her brain is approaching optimal........this means the Na/K ATPase battery that is driving her Ferrari engine is working at the peak 60% efficiency I mention in BG 13. How did she intuitively find it? Look at what she eats..........MHS and fermented heart in a jar. She has tapped into her brain sensation because this is what it needs to sense its world to avoid illness. That my friend is understanding heart disease from the Quilt's perspective. Inger is a perfect example of where form has met function.

Am I right in thinking that Inger can probably expect to have every autonomic function working pretty much optimally? This would mean, for example, digestion. It could be all kinds of stuff: maybe if she walked across a room she'd be less likely to bump into something.

I'm wondering now why teenagers are clumsy. I was told as a kid it's because your limbs are growing, so that you've lost a sense of where they are. I'm not sure I buy that.

Getting a bit off-topic, but a couple of things.

Different kinds of bodywork are interesting. They seems to be attempts to fix what exercise can't - because you're dealing with pretty deep things not controlled consciously that have gone adrift. (It interests me that few people in paleo have done more than dabble their toes in this area. And these are people who are supposed to be interested in the body and its needs! Some are even openly contemptuous of yoga: it's sometimes implied that it's something women are often interested in - this apparently counts against it.)

Then there's neurofeedback. Interesting that the brain can self-regulate as soon as it can "see itself". (The only person in paleo who seems to have any interest in this is Nora Gedgaudas.) This is used, for example, with ex-military people stuck in a state of hyper-vigilance (gamma-waves up all the time):

[http://www.homecoming4veterans.org/](http://http://www.homecoming4veterans.org/)

[www.eeginfo.com/research/.../PTSD-NeurofeedbackRemedy.pdf](http://http://www.eeginfo.com/research/.../PTSD-NeurofeedbackRemedy.pdf)

Interesting to investigate more how all these things fit together. I'm thinking some of the tools we use to fix things, be it Tai Chi, be it sophisticated EEG machines, are probably needed more than they might be because of what the conditions of modern life have done to our brains.

### Michael — 2012-09-30T10:45:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55361>

> **prAna303;56281 wrote:** Yeah, dance have a very important aspect to human life.

I don't know of a single hunter-gatherer society that doesn't dance. One could find video on YouTube of Maori or Bushmen or Australian Aborigines pretty easily. There are enough paintings of Native Americans dancing, too.

And actually the same is true of agricultural civilizations. I don't just mean Iron Age ones like the Zulus. Everyone's surely heard of "country dancing" and what 19-century novel would be complete without a ball?

It's just us. For others dance has been something elemental and sacred or at least a pleasing way of moving in a synchronized way with others - enacting your social condition in a way - and of meeting the opposite sex.

At the turn of the century there was a huge move towards collecting folk songs and dances - something inspired by the Romantic Movement. People like Beethoven and Bruch set Scottish dance tunes. Dvorak and Janacek were doing something similar for Czechoslovakia. Cecil Sharp in England started a society for collecting folk songs and dances before they died out.

There was a kind of push among some educationists to incorporate what people like Sharp found in the curriculum. If you went to school in the 1960s in England you'd still have been taught some dances in a desultory manner. All gone now. All that they think matters is "the 3 Rs". And it never even occurs to them that kids who know how to move might learn better.

When the government in the UK set a national curriculum some 20 years ago now guess which were the last two documents to be drawn up and ratified. Music and Physical Education. Those are seen as decorations to the curriculum that fundamentally don't matter. Many schools have sold off their playing fields - I believe the same has happened in the U.S.

The bit about the people coming to buy sports equipment is interesting. Basically, their propioception is shot - even if they haven't got a specific condition.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-30T11:26:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55363>

Dance for me is primarily a way of connecting with my inner self, my true primal sense, then it is a way of expression, then processing, then communicating. Rarely does it have a social purpose per say, yet often do I meet people of my kind at dance. I love watching people dance expressively.

It's quite amazing what happens when you find that true connection with your Self, when your movement becomes unintentional expression of the things you have been looking for.... when you naturally realize that you just stepped on the brakes for your left hemisphere and the accelerator of your right, when those two are in true balance amazing insights will happen.... I'm not sure we were ever meant to have a dominant hemisphere.

When you dance, you become one with yourself, one with your environment, one with the music and the beat, one with the world around you. Yet you feel the boundaries.... you feel your feet in contact with the floor, you feel your body moving through space, things are real, things are now. Dance is primal sense. If anything, I danced my way out of anorexia.

Try drumming.... drum circles. Similar magic.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-28T19:43:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-a-and-night-vision.3963/post-55042>

Does it recover at all? My eyes have been getting worse since I was 5.... not just myopia, it's actual bad vision, I'm barely allowed to drive with correction.

# Thread: 23andme and the human microbiome
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-and-the-human-microbiome.3986/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-09-28T08:20:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-and-the-human-microbiome.3986/post-54942>

Ok. This might be a dumb question. I'm reading these days as much as my hurting head will allow me to. So I ran into this information about the human microbiome.

The human body does not only contain human genes but also microbial genes b/c the human body is a super-organism filled with microbes. Genetic studies can contain computer generated errors in SNPs if there is microbial DNA contained in the sample. Microbial genes need to be removed before human genes are analyzed.

For 23andme, we spit. Saliva is full of microbes last time I thought about it. I checked their website and no explanation of how they make sure that what they analyze is human DNA and not microbial DNA. This is where I get a bit confused.

**How do they make sure that the genes they analyze are not contaminated with microbial DNA?

Has anyone ever repeated the 23andme test? ... I'm strongly thinking no, since it would be expected your DNA will not change within your lifetime**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-28T13:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-and-the-human-microbiome.3986/post-54970>

by assaying mtDNA. Pretty routine.
