# Thread: Plains Indians
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-03T15:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56047>

I've been thinking about this even more since BG13.

The Inuit when they ate flesh rather than fish seem often to have eaten sea mammals - something people have been missing. Same went for Nansen and Johansen on the push "farthest north".

Another group often cited by the low carb people is the Plains Indians. Their meat was not intensively-raised grain-fed meat. Also they probably ate brains. Brains were used for tanning skin, but doubtless plenty were eaten, too. That's the DHA. Iodine? I bet they would have eaten the thyroid glands of game. If hunting peoples knew to eat adrenal glands against scurvy - Weston Price mentions that - then surely they'd know to eat the thyroid, too. It's a shame it's too late for really good documentation of exactly which parts of which animals they ate.

Not as good as living on the coast and eating plentifully of seafood, but would probably do, and better by a long way than what people do today.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-03T15:47:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56050>

“We have lingered in the chambers of the sea

By sea-girls wreathed with seaweed red and brown ...” — T .S. Eliot, Prufrock The last line.....

Till human voices wake us, and we drown.

Whole foods- eat it all. Eyes for your eyes, brains for your brain, bones for your bones, organs for your organs, glands for your glands, muscle for your muscle, fat for your fat.

### Michael — 2012-10-03T16:15:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56056>

> **Dali Dula;57275 wrote:** â€œWe have lingered in the chambers of the sea
>
> By sea-girls wreathed with seaweed red and brown ...â€￾ â€” T .S. Eliot, Prufrock The last line.....
>
> Till human voices wake us, and we drown.
>
>
> I have seen them riding seaward ...
>
>
>
> eat it all.

But this is just what hunting peoples _don't do - except perhaps in times of dearth.

Stefansson makes this point in _The fat of the Land_. IIRC, he says people assume they eat the bits we'd eat: they don't. Even more wrong, he says, is the assumption that they'd just eat anything indiscriminately. He then gives listings of what parts of which animals one particular northern people he stayed with ate - it varied from animal to animal.

One would really like to have more information of that sort. What was eaten, what was thrown to the dogs, what was discarded, what was thought not usually worth the labour of carrying back to camp and so on. Sally Fallon has some interesting information in an essay called Guts and Grease_ on which parts of the buffalo were thought best for men and which for women. I think there's a wealth of stuff here that it would be good to know more on.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-03T18:00:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56063>

> **Dali Dula;57275 wrote:** Whole foods- eat it all. Eyes for your eyes, brains for your brain, bones for your bones, organs for your organs, glands for your glands, muscle for your muscle, fat for your fat.

The benefits mingle between the organs, but I like this way of thinking about it.

### AKMan — 2012-10-03T22:43:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56110>

> **Michael;57272 wrote:** Iodine? I bet they would have eaten the thyroid glands of game. If hunting peoples knew to eat adrenal glands against scurvy - Weston Price mentions that - then surely they'd know to eat the thyroid, too.

And ponder this...where did the buffalo get his iodine? Ranchers raising buffalo in pens on grain need to supplement with 1mg of iodine for each buffalo. Horses are .6mg. It must come from the grass they eat, the dirt attached to the grass, and their drinking water. Need for iodine is dependant on size of the beast. Humans need for iodine is thought to be 150mcg/day.

Also, the thyroid hormones T3 and T4 are so named because they have 3 or 4 iodine molecules attached to them. T4 collects in the muscles and organs and is converted to T3 in the small intestine and liver. All those little T3 and T4 molecules contain iodine. The thyroid may concentrate iodine, but it is surely found elsewhere in an animal. I've seen thyroids of deer and other large game...not much to eat there, but as they boiled lots of what they ate it probably ended up in the soup.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-10-04T02:20:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56134>

Fresh water fish has iodine too, especially the skin. Not as much as marine sources, however, but I'm sure inlanders such as the Plains Indians ate 'em. [http://scialert.net/fulltext/?doi=jas.2008.1790.1792&org=11#t1](http://http://scialert.net/fulltext/?doi=jas.2008.1790.1792&org=11#t1)

### Michael — 2012-10-04T08:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56173>

> **AKMan;57361 wrote:** And ponder this...where did the buffalo get his iodine?
>
>
> Yes, an interesting question. What about from salt-licks?
>
>
>
> And I expect some from the grass, and soil that comes up with it, as they eat, as you say.
>
>
>
> There's an area of the U.S. where iodine is very low in soil - map here I found by googling:
>
>
>
> [http://curezone.com/forums/am.asp?i=1036622](http://http://curezone.com/forums/am.asp?i=1036622)
>
>
>
> But they covered massive distances, didn't they? So they'd pass into areas where it's richer in soil.
>
>
>
>
>
> I tried googling "salt lick".
>
>
>
> A mineral lick (also known as salt lick or natural lick) is a natural mineral deposit where animals in nutrient-poor ecosystems can obtain essential mineral nutrients. In an ecosystem, salt/mineral licks often occur naturally, providing the sodium, calcium, iron, phosphorus and zinc required in the springtime for bone, muscle and other growth in deer and other wildlife,

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mineral\_lick](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mineral_lick)

No mention of iodine there.

If I google "buffalo salt lick" there are a lot of references, none of which look particularly promising.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-04T16:58:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56221>

There's an interesting "discussion" going on in the horse world about free choice minerals. Instead of feeding all the minimum daily requirements for minerals in grain rations, there are now ways to feed minerals separately (salt, different ratios of magnesium/calcium, clay, seabed deposits etc) I've been convinced by my horses responses that they will eat what they need when they need it. Others feel animals don't have the intelligence to do this once they have been domesticated and changed by human interference.

I can see that once we get to a certain place of "broken" health are instincts are corrupted...we crave what we shouldn't eat. Our minds can help us change these habits and patterns even if part of us is still yelling for sugar and carbs.

Do animals retain enough of their survival instincts to go for the minerals when they need them? Insulin resistance in horses is a huge problem just as it is with humans. I think the key is to make sure they don't have an unhealthy option available. In other words, I wouldn't bet on my guys heading for the seabed minerals if there was a big bucket of oats nearby, but provide a healthy environment and I believe their brains start to rewire back to the old wisdom. And this would be more optimal than simply giving them standard rations of nutrients everyday...sort of like the cereal full of "complete" vitamins.

So I have all the minerals separated into feeders and it's been really fascinating to watch which horses eat what and when. I do hedge my bets and feed each of them magnesium regularly simply because there isn't enough magnesium in our soil to balance out all the potassium and phosphorus from fertilizers that is in their hay and pastures. We are reworking the fields at our farm to try and get the right combination of grasses to make optimal hay. The epi-paleo diet for horses did not include rich sugary forage.

### Michael — 2012-10-04T17:33:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56234>

Fascinating!

I wonder if "instinct" is the right word here - depends on what one means by it perhaps.

Would "feeling" be as good? The animal perhaps feels that such-and-such would be good now based on a memory of what eating it and afterwards feels like.

Interesting story. I had an aunt who said that she had been a vegetarian for some years. She was on holiday in the South Island of New Zealand. This was in some kind of nissen hut. She said she was in bed and she felt so cold, like she'd never felt in her life before. And she said: "I knew I needed some meat." So the next day she ate some and never looked back.

I didn't particularly know what to think when she told me this years ago. I probably thought: "Why wouldn't you eat meat? And I guess it would be comforting."

These days I would probably wonder about "thermogenic" effects - or simply whether all your systems run better when you get adequate nutrition. But she, I think, just "knew" from memory what eating the meat would FEEL like.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-04T19:02:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56248>

I have heard of chickens eating lots of their feed pellets (instead of grass and such) because they were wanting the salt in the pellets.

### diane — 2012-10-04T22:50:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56314>

There is definitely something to instinct in animals - and I don't think it's always based on previous experience. A few weeks ago, I noticed that my dogs were eating the soil from my potted plants - they had never done this before. I feed them a raw diet that is supposedly balanced (meat, organ, bones), but obviously there was something that they were missing. Reading Jack's blogs, I'm wondering if it's not some sort of bacteria from the soil.

One of my dogs also goes to bed in the bedroom when it gets dark. She started doing this about a year ago. And she goes later in the summer, started to go earlier in the fall. What a smart cookie!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-04T22:52:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56318>

BG 14 plays right into this thread.......just sayin

### Michael — 2012-10-05T10:57:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56433>

> **diane;57570 wrote:** There is definitely something to instinct in animals - and I don't think it's always based on previous experience.
>
>
> I expect some behaviour is just innate, although the more complex the behaviour the less I'd be happy thinking of it as _just that.
>
>
>
> One of the things I always think of is those birds that flutter along, mimicking having a broken wing, to draw a predator such as a fox away from their nest and nestlings. That always seems to me involve some kind of understanding of what's going on at some level. It also seems to me put a question mark against any very hardline system that want's to take "mind" out of the equation.
>
>
>
>
> > A few weeks ago, I noticed that my dogs were eating the soil from my potted plants - they had never done this before. I feed them a raw diet that is supposedly balanced (meat, organ, bones), but obviously there was something that they were missing. Reading Jack's blogs, I'm wondering if it's not some sort of bacteria from the soil.
>
>
>
> It's an interesting story. I'd think you could well be right, but it seems to me that you're smuggling two assumptions in there. The assumptions, I think, are that the dog (1) did need to eat the soil and (2) knew to do so.
>
>
>
> But maybe it just did it - maybe that's "just the kind of things dogs do". Maybe "eating stuff" (where stuff might be whatever was in its environment) would be a basic behaviour. Of course, if that were a basic innate behaviour, you would then be in a position to have some kind of memories as it were on top of that. You eat some "stuff" X that's around you, just because that's what you do, and _then_ experience what the effect of X is - and that might be why you go back for some more when in a particular situation, if you do.
>
>
>
> I suppose another possibility might be that there is some kind of pretty impressive signalling going on when the dog smells the earth that tells it to eat just this_ stuff here even though it's never done so before.
>
>
>
> One of my dogs also goes to bed in the bedroom when it gets dark. She started doing this about a year ago. And she goes later in the summer, started to go earlier in the fall. What a smart cookie!

That's interesting.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-05T14:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56451>

Innate behavior, instinct what ever you want to call it. I've seen it in all my dogs

I had a female lab who regularly used her hunting instinct to eat wild animals from gophers, mice, squirrels, chipmunks, & birds. Even in her old age of 14 she regularly would kill and eat what ever she caught.

When ever she wasn't feeling well she'd eat grass

Our other dog (male black lab) was a neurotic & metabolic mess. He'd regularly eat poop, sneak into the garden and eat all the kale, collards & tomatoes. He was the only black dog I've ever had that would bask in the sun on a scorching summers day (trying to get vitamin D or just warm adapted?) where as our female at the time was always escaping and heading down to the creek to cool off. At the age of 6 he came down w/diabetes

Looking back its all related & what I know now is that my mismatches drove his mismatches. He had a bad gut

Our current lab only wants to be cool. We discovered that we weren't getting any cool air in our bedroom b/c he was hogging the vent one smart dog

### Michael — 2012-10-05T15:07:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plains-indians.4087/post-56455>

> **Shijin13;57711 wrote:** Innate behavior, instinct what ever you want to call it. I've seen it in all my dogs

... and the other thing was that the example given didn't _prove_ the interpretation that was put upon it, because the assumptions, unnoticed, came in with the interpretation ... but, again, it may_ have been a correct interpretation.

### diane — 2012-10-06T00:47:54+0300

# Thread: Fasting Blood Glucose Levels
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-10-03T16:38:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56057>

I'm not sure where the best place for this is, so I, going to try here. I know you fine folks will probably have good info for me .

As per my consult with Dr Kruse I obtained a blood glucose monitor and have been checking my fasting levels ever day (except a few days that I've forgotten). Dr Kruse thought metaformin might be of benefit to me and I could use the info when I went into my Dr. My levels have typically been in the 70-84 range. I've had a couple in the 90's (those were after carb heavier days the night before -bad). This morning I woke up to an all time low -56. Seemed strange, so I tested again. Same. So, is that TOO low? What level constitutes too low?

### shilohman — 2012-10-03T18:25:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56073>

A FBG level below 70 is considered hypoglycemic. Anything below 50 can effect brain function.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-03T19:22:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56084>

If your FBG were too low, your body would be almost panicked, cortisol would be running like crazy, heart pounding...

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-03T19:54:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56089>

> **Nonchalant;57329 wrote:** If your FBG were too low, your body would be almost panicked, cortisol would be running like crazy, heart pounding...

I didn't have anything like that at all. In fact, I felt really good this morning!

### Momma28 — 2012-10-03T20:48:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56097>

> **Trisha;57337 wrote:** I didn't have anything like that at all. In fact, I felt really good this morning!

Trisha....have you already started the metformin? If so, then that could be dangerous because it could push you too low during the night while sleeping. You would have to adjust dosage to keep from having it go too low. When I worked with my Mother-in-law to show her how to eat for better health, after just a week and a half without carbs her meds were taking her morning sugars too low. We halved the nighttime dosage and eventually dropped it altogether. She just took her morning dosage after that. This worked for her.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-03T20:55:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56101>

I'd be suspicious of the meter, if you felt fine - even good.

My meter had me looking like I had adult onset type 1 diabetes, with FBGs of 110 - 125ish. I had it done at the lab, and while my meter said 110, the lab said 85. I discarded that meter.

Then, recently, during a 2 hour glucose tolerance test, my meter gave me 130 at 1 hour, and 136 at 2 hours. The lab gave me 134 at 1 hour, and 84 at 2 hours... Now I always take the numbers with a grain of salt, and watch more for trends.

### AKMan — 2012-10-03T22:34:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56107>

I bought an Ultra Mini OneTouch this weekend to play around with. My FBG was 136! i was freaking...then I read the book and it said you have to enter the number from the test strip bottle at the start-up of the meter. i did that and now I am a normal 90 again...whew!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-03T22:50:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting-blood-glucose-levels.4088/post-56113>

I didn't forget to do the number from the test strip. The meter is about a month old... how long I've been using it. Like I said, most of the my FBG numbers have been between 70-84, which I think is pretty decent. I sure hope the meter isn't off since I just bought it!

### Momma28 — 2012-10-04T03:21:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sulphur-and-cholesterol.4100/post-56142>

I posted this on the Vitamin D thread and thought it important enough for its own post. This is the link to Dr. S. Seneff's work on Vitamin D synthesis on/in the skin via the oxidation of sulfur and cholesterol. She believes that a high LDL means that our bodies are not producing the vitamin D because sulfur is deficient from our diets thus leaving the LDL to circulate. I have been spraying myself with DMSO and taking MSM for a while now for sulfur. I"m having bloodwork drawn this Friday so it will be interesting to see if my Vitamin D level is good and LDL lower than last time.

[http://www.ronpaulforums.com/showthread.php?316639-Dr.-Mercola-Interviews-Dr.-Stephanie-Seneff-(Part-1-of-7)](http://http://www.ronpaulforums.com/showthread.php?316639-Dr.-Mercola-Interviews-Dr.-Stephanie-Seneff-\(Part-1-of-7\))

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-04T03:57:04+0300

**Is Endothelial Nitric Oxide Synthase a Moonlighting Protein Whose Day Job is Cholesterol Sulfate Synthesis? Implications for Cholesterol Transport, Diabetes and Cardiovascular Disease.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-31T02:19:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparison-of-2011-labs-versus-2012-labs-feedback-requested.4102/post-61014>

Just to put a new thought out there. I have absorption issues (bypass) and the reset abolutely did not work for me. I was strict for 6 months. If you have absorption issues it may not be your best option.

It is not just about how much stomach holds, but also overloading the system with volume that wont be absorbed before it gets ejected - or worse used for energy storage but nutrients not absorbed.

I just got gi test and other than the expected excess fat in stools, I was in damn good shape considering the horror show I expected. I think Jack was even suprised.

I got there by doing a 14-16 hour daily fast betweenn dinner and bfast. I then ate rather frequently, 4 times, sometimes more. I ate small anounts and very fat heavy. I think the fast period gave my system a rest and supported autophagy.

My point is leptin reset doesnt always work - sometimes its a square peg, too.

After a few months of that, I finally got some peace from cravings and the like. When i followed Hcg with that routine - I finally felt I had established a relationship between food and my bodies energy needs.

# Thread: Improved eye sight anyone ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eye-sight-anyone.4105/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### somewhatchubby — 2012-10-04T11:13:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eye-sight-anyone.4105/post-56180>

hello ,I went for my routine eye test yesterday and had a bit of a surprise in that my short sightedness and distance vision had improved so much that I barely need glasses at all. I had been driving in my distance glasses and have been advise to stop wearing them as they are much too strong and I dont actually need any glasses for driving. My near sight is almost back to 'plain glass' , left eye slightly weaker.

Obviously I have been giving thought to the reason for this improvement and the only major thing I have done is the Leptin reset (now 9 weeks) and low carb in general since late December last year and the blue light blockers leading to vastly improved sleep ....although no sex at 3 am yet !

I just wondered if anyone else has experienced an improvement in their eyesight ?

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-04T16:09:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eye-sight-anyone.4105/post-56209>

### somewhatchubby — 2012-10-05T00:55:49+0300

I'm wondering if its Vit D as I have been taking 5,000iu daily for over two years and havnt as yet become 'big' into seafood although I am trying to love it. canned sardines and some regular fish is as far as Ive got. I once had a very bad reaction to mussels and its made me wary. Ive been taking fish oil for a few months so dont know wether ive been taking it long enough.

I wouldnt have believed this if I hadnt experienced it.

### Destiny — 2012-10-04T13:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/almost-there.4107/post-56186>

Eating Meat Made Us Human, Suggests New Skull Fossil

Fragments of a 1.5-million-year-old skull from a child recently found in Tanzania suggest early hominids weren't just occasional carnivores but regular meat eaters, researchers say.

The finding helps build the case that meat-eating helped the human lineage evolve large brains, scientists added.

"I know this will sound awful to vegetarians, but meat made us human," said researcher Manuel DomÃ­nguez-Rodrigo, an archaeologist at Complutense University in Madrid.

You can read more at:

[http://news.yahoo.com/eating-meat-made-us-human-suggests-skull-fossil-211048849.html](http://http://news.yahoo.com/eating-meat-made-us-human-suggests-skull-fossil-211048849.html)

# Thread: How long did it take YOU to cold adapt?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 43
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2012-10-04T14:16:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56190>

Hi everyone,

Just wondering how long it took people to cold adapt because I know it varies a fair amount.

Just post how long it took you to adapt or how long long you have been doing it without adapting.

I went straight to ice baths and have done 4 days full torso for 60mins in cold UK tap water (will measure temp when I can).

I have noticed slight headache, cold/sinuses, stomach ache and warmth at night but not cold adapted yet.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-04T14:55:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56193>

that's pretty aggressive. the CT protocol would have you ramp up slowly, but I would keep doing it if you can . You will be adapted in a few weeks. You're in good shape if you can handle what you have been doing assuming your water temp is >50f.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-04T15:28:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56199>

about 5 minutes for me........but I was a train wreck when I started. I knew I needed it and no matter how hard it was I was determined and disciplined to just get it done.

### seanb4 — 2012-10-04T15:30:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56201>

Yeah it takes some effort but it will be worth it. Hows your CT going Dali Dula?

### seanb4 — 2012-10-04T15:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56202>

Jack minutes or months?

### Optimalbound — 2012-10-04T15:57:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56208>

I only had about three face dunking sessions under my belt when, completely out of the blue, a friend of mine called and asked if I wanted to join him for a quick dunk in the ocean. This was back in March, and the sea temperature was 45 Â°F. My friends stayed in the water for just a few seconds, while I was amazed to find that I was completely comfortable with staying in and swimming around for several minutes. In fact, I liked it so much I got back in two more times.

_EDIT:_ I was up to 45 minutes in 50 Â°F one week afterwards.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-04T16:57:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56219>

I'm not sure what the gold standard is to know if you are 100% cold adapted or not.

But here's my experience. After following the protocol it took me about 2 months to get up to 50 minutes in 53F baths.

Since February, I've been taking 10 minute cold showers in the morning, and 25 to 40 minute baths in 53F water most days.

I'm still not sure if I'm adapted at this point or not.

but i have lost 20 llbs, 5 inches on my waist and still wearing shorts even thought it is getting cold outside.

### kathylu — 2012-10-04T17:14:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56230>

It took me about a month to start to like the cold baths. I stopped over the summer and now I am having to re-adapt. I still find getting in a shock, but I seem to be adjusting pretty quickly now.

### seanb4 — 2012-10-04T17:19:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56232>

@villjamur\_stevenson Sounds like you're adapted to me.

### ealachan — 2012-10-04T17:39:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56236>

I don't know if I'm technically cold-adapted or not, but I can go out in shorts and tank top in 40 degree weather now and be perfectly comfortable. I do take cold showers daily, but haven't gotten back into the cold tub yet.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-04T18:15:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56242>

> **ealachan;57491 wrote:** I don't know if I'm technically cold-adapted or not, but I can go out in shorts and tank top in 40 degree weather now and be perfectly comfortable. I do take cold showers daily, but haven't gotten back into the cold tub yet.

My kids starting playing hockey and I wear shorts and a T-shirt to the rink. the other parents huddle around coffee and sometime retreat to the heated locker rooms. They look at me strange for sure. When they ask me if I'm cold, I just say I don't get cold.

### ealachan — 2012-10-04T19:22:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56253>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;57497 wrote:** My kids starting playing hockey and I wear shorts and a T-shirt to the rink. the other parents huddle around coffee and sometime retreat to the heated locker rooms. They look at me strange for sure. When they ask me if I'm cold, I just say I don't get cold.

Yeah, it always freaks my non-cold-adapted husband out when I walk the dog in the morning in shorts and a tank top when it's 35-40 degrees out. He puts his coat on if it's 60 out, and cranks the thermostat in our apartment up if it gets below 68. Blows my mind. I love the man, but he's a complete and utter wuss.

### Endless — 2012-10-04T20:03:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56269>

I find it harder, as a former heat junkie, to enjoy cold air...walking the dog while underdressed is still a challenge for me. I am good with about 45min in 55F water though. If I'm cold to begin with (often at night...I get chilly when I'm tired) I can't force myself in.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-04T20:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56274>

> **Endless;57525 wrote:** I find it harder, as a former heat junkie, to enjoy cold air...walking the dog while underdressed is still a challenge for me. I am good with about 45min in 55F water though. If I'm cold to begin with (often at night...I get chilly when I'm tired) I can't force myself in.

There was a point in the summer heat where I did enjoy my CT times. But now that it is getting colder out, I can't say I enjoy it. I can tolerate it. At one ice rink I was standing by the cold air blower and some guy was like, aren't you cold? And I said my usual "no i don't get cold." But after 5 minutes I really had to try to fight my entire body from shivering. I couldn't let it show after I just said that!

But, I find I am not enjoying it as the days get shorter - but I can do it.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-05T00:13:00+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-05T19:02:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56497>

I don't know if I am adapted even now. I started in Feb and continued through May. Then stopped due to dental surgery and just couldn't get in the cold water. I've been back doing it for about a month and it seemed a bit hard to get started but now I am good in 53 degree water for 40 min. I actually like the lower temp. I only get pink in the spots where the ice blanket is touching so maybe I'm not quite there yet and need to lower the temp!

@dali:what "magic" do you ,( and others?),get below 50 degrees. Personally, I get a much more powerful energy boost than in an over 50 degree bath. ### Dali Dula — 2012-10-07T02:53:03+0300

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-08T04:11:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56856>

I shivered for the first time this season today. The pond has dropped a few deg. This was mild shivering. All last spring I would shiver intensely for 20 min after 25 min @50deg.

### freesia — 2012-10-08T09:36:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56871>

Hi, it took ME 3 - 4 weeks.

I started at the beginning of April (Fall here) at about 60F and got down to 45F over Winter. Water is warming up now it's Spring but I'm still getting a 48F bath most days.

Most of the studies I've seen suggest 2 - 3 weeks of frequent cold exposure will cause adaptation.

It took me a little longer because I paced myself due to my illness. I reckon I was "adapted" when my temperature started rising in anticipation of a cold bath or in response to very cold air on my skin.

Hope that's helpful

### QuantumRadical — 2012-10-08T18:43:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56903>

This is my experience (and subsequent speculation) with getting to a point of cold adaptation--your experience is more than likely different. It took me about two weeks to get past the "it's brutal" stage when I first started last February. If you look at the pace of biological change at the skin level, a cut takes me about two weeks to heal. A bruise usually takes me two weeks to clear up (varies, but a minor bruise from a minor trauma takes me about two weeks to clear up). So, my speculation would be that however long it takes you to "heal" things at the skin level (minor trauma) is about the time it will take for you to yoke your brain/body response to the "cold trauma" being experienced by your skin during CT. The body always seeks to adapt to its environment and depending on the "sensed" level of the required adaptation, the mind/body combo takes the appropriate action. Acute trauma may result in immediate mind/body response to include going into shock. But I think CT (as brutal as it may be at first) can only be considered a minor trauma (it's just cold after all) resulting in a mild level of response which in my case I define as about two weeks. But I am in pretty good shape, so someone who is in a different state of health may have a completely different response to getting to a point of cold adaptation. Release of toxins from burned fat is also something that undoubtedly factors in and makes the process problematic across a population, but very specific to each N-1.

As for shivering, I sit in a tub of about 45-50 degree water from anywhere from 30 minutes to 45 minutes. I begin slight shivering at about the 25 minute point. If I recall the CT webinar correctly, Dr. K. does talk about the benefits of getting into the shivering stage. When you shiver you are burning a relative boat-load of fat directly to heat--a good thing. I am in pretty good shape without much fat tissue, so I think I get to the shivering fairly quickly but am able to sustain a safe level for quite a while. I suspect I could endure 50 degree water for over an hour without threat of hypothermia based on my current conditioning. It still feels very cold but the body adapts and from about two minutes in until the mild shivering starts at about the 25-30 minute point the cold is actually very pleasant and relaxing. The mild shivering phase lasts another 20 minutes and is also quite bearable. The moderate shivering is just OK, nothing to write home about. I never, never, never let myself get into the violent shivering stage. As a young warrior in training, I got there, wear the t-shirt, and don't ever want to go there again.

I have not measured my core temperature before, during, or after any CT session, but I would bet another boat load of money that I get the furnace really fired up during these sessions. I am hot just thinking about a CT session.

When I have completed the CT session, I continue to shiver for quite a while (varies), on average about twenty to forty-five minutes depending. I don't try to artificially warm myself up, but let my body do its thing. With the continued shivering, I figure I am still burning another boat load of fat directly to heat, so all good.

I remember early on, Dr. K. talking about "first, one must control one's thoughts." The water is still very cold, and it still takes a level of mental toughness to "take the plunge." I now have a set of routines that I have adopted that let CT become a matter of habit. For starters, I never step into a hot shower anymore. I get into the shower and then turn the water on on full cold. Usually a bit of a shocker, then I gradually let it warm up a bit if I am just taking a shower. At the end of each warm shower, I turn it to full cold again so that my body remembers the cold. So, I start every shower cold, then go warm, then end on cold. Total cold is about four or five minutes, but it does give me an opportunity to "steel" my mind with the sensation of COLD.

When I do a real CT session in the tub, I get the water to between 40 and 50 degrees (measured with an IR thermometer). I do several face dunks holding my breath to start (I just lean over the edge and plunge the face and head right in.) I end the face dunks with a two to three minute plunge with a snorkel. I stop the snorkel session when it hurts, but it really sets the face on fire and really gets the furnace fired up.

I then start the music and a stopwatch, kneel in the tub, splash water over both arms, chest, upper legs, and down the back (worst part). Then I lay back slowly and immerse. From kneeling to total immersion takes about 30 seconds. I only keep my hands and head out of the water (but I usually put my hands into the water for the last five to ten minutes of the session). I can get everything into the tub except for my knees, and usually will hit those at the very end as the session ends along with my hands. The knees, nonetheless, get a bit at the beginning from kneeling.

Overall, it still takes a bit of will power to get into the cold tub, but for several hours afterwards, talk about being energized!

The quality of the CT for me also varies based on the quality of recent sleep and diet. But I am discovering that over time, these are of marginal, yet still perceivable, differences in the quality of the CT sessions.

I suspect that there are as many ways and processes to get to cold adaptation as there are people trying it. Dr. K.'s protocol should cover about 80% of it. It worked quite well for me, and still does. Along with a switch to a predominantly seafood-based diet (embrace sashimi!), and regular, frequent exposure to the sun, the combination of diet and seafood works for me. And I have the lipid profile changes to prove it.

Next step, MHS!!!! for breakfast!

Q-Rad Randy

### LinD — 2012-10-08T19:38:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56909>

I now have a set of routines that I have adopted that let CT become a matter of habit. For starters, I never step into a hot shower anymore. I get into the shower and then turn the water on on full cold. Usually a bit of a shocker, then I gradually let it warm up a bit if I am just taking a shower. At the end of each warm shower, I turn it to full cold again so that my body remembers the cold. So, I start every shower cold, then go warm, then end on cold. Total cold is about four or five minutes, but it does give me an opportunity to "steel" my mind with the sensation of COLD.

Ditto.

I think of the hottest summer day and how good it would feel to get in the ice-cold water. Then I step in...

Though our water is warm in the summer, this is how we've been approaching our shower time, too. The temperature and consequently water is starting to cool off. Perhaps this will prepare me/us for the cold bath--once we finally get into our new home.... Should be soon!

Don't you find it addicting? I mean, I get in and expose my legs and back side to the cold water first... For a moment I think, no way can I turn around and get my front cold, but then I am compelled to do so, plus dunk my head, too. Love it!

### shah78 — 2012-10-08T20:35:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56921>

I have only shirvered(slightly) once in over two hundred sessions. My first full body up ,to the neck session. Note:I always get out of the water once my temperature starts to fall from it's peak. I treat CT like a Body by Science workout. Short and intense. I get a temperature rise, ride the heat wave through it's 5 to ten minute plateau and get out. Total time in water 25-35 minutes.Then I repeat it one or two more times that day. On a good day I can get almost thirty three minutes of tempterature rise. My goal is to "get it up" for up to an hour.

> **Anna K.;58027 wrote:** Dali Dula, shah78 and others, do you still shiver when you get out of the water? I
>
>
>
> thanks.

### freesia — 2012-10-08T22:29:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56941>

I get a sustained temperature rise for an hour, though each time I nudged the temperature downwards I had to watch for signs I should cut the time short. Big muscle shivering was my cue....I only experienced it a handful of times. (Experiencing it in a cold shower can be dangerous BTW!) If I understand the process correctly this indicates my cold exposure had exceeded my capacity to heat via BAT activity. This always proved to be temporary..like any type of fitness using it builds strength.

When I first went into the 40's I needed to cut my bath time to 45 minutes. Within a week I was back up to an hour. Beautiful process.

**AnnaK**. I get out of the water (an hour @ 48F lately), put on a robe and refill my ice containers and transport them all to my freezers.....that takes a good 10 minutes. Then after I dry off and get dressed the shivering starts and lasts for 10 minutes. After that I get what feels like a resistance workout....I'm sorry, that's the best I can describe it! My body is working to maintain body temp but it doesn't involve big muscles past the 10 minute mark.

HTH

I agree it takes some mental toughness, or tricks. I had to line up something interesting to watch on my laptop and just get in fully fixated on the screen, feeling the cold at a distance and pretending it was happening to someone else. For what it's worth any time the reality of the biting cold intruded on my concentration I found it helpful to shout "Holy Suffering Seamonkeys!" Ealachan said this in one of her posts waaay back and it always tickled me .

I only needed to use those strategies in the first few months...then again each time I dropped the water temperature...and on a couple of occasions I came back to CT after a 4 or 5 day break.

### shah78 — 2012-10-08T22:32:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56943>

I hold my peak temp for 5 to ten minutes,but it takes me 15 to 23 minutes to hit my peak temp. So 20 to 33 minutes so far. I monitor my temps (I do not rely on my perception of the cold, I rely 100% on the thermometer)and get out once I get three staight readings .1 degrees off my high. Sometimes I can rebound after two straight low readings (sometimes even for three or four more minutes), but after three striaght low readings I've never rebounded, so I get out and" live to fight another day". I found out early that two shorter sessions a day are much better for me than one long one. I've been playing with this angle ever since April when Caveman asked Jack what was better: two short ones or one long one. That day I started two a days and never looked back.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-08T23:35:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-did-it-take-you-to-cold-adapt.4108/post-56957>

I know not everybody has access to swimmable cold water, but I have done CT sitting fully immersed in a tub, cold shower, and swimming. Swimming rocks! I am not looking forward to when i have to get back in the tub when the pond freezes over. Sitting in the cold tub listening to tunes and reading sucks compared to swimming. If you can find a place to swim do it.

# Thread: resveratrol side effects?

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-10-04T16:34:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-56213>

Has anybody else noticed an uptick in headaches since starting resveratrol? I'm not 100% sure that the supplement is what's causing it, but I've had a nearly constant headache for two days now, and it happens to coincide with when I started taking resveratrol. It's not a migraine-level event, but rather just a dull, annoying pressure in the front of my head and my sinuses. I'm debating whether I should take some Tylenol to get rid of it so that I can get some work done, or whether the Tylenol would do me more harm than good...

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-04T17:23:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-56233>

Sounds like what I get when I've tried a lot of supplements. I think the tylenol should be avoided if possible. I'm trying to take resveratrol too but do it every other day for awhile. I still haven't found pure sources of it or curcumin both have additives.

### ealachan — 2012-10-04T17:35:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-56235>

> **SeaHorse;57488 wrote:** Sounds like what I get when I've tried a lot of supplements. I think the tylenol should be avoided if possible. I'm trying to take resveratrol too but do it every other day for awhile. I still haven't found pure sources of it or curcumin both have additives.

I've been taking a bunch of other supplements for a while now and nothing else has bothered me, to date. The two things I've added recently were the resveratrol and magnesium malate. I've also been eating kelp every day for about a week and a halfish? Not TONS of it, but a good 1/3 cup serving at a time. Maybe I've over-iodined? I was going to lay off the seaweed for a few days anyway.

Honestly, the longer this goes on, the more it feels like a sinus thing. Right under my eyes and down into my cheekbones hurts, and that's usually a sure sign of sinus stuff starting up, at least for me.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-04T18:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-56247>

I threw out all the Tylenol in the house. If I'm desperate, I'll take Advil.

Iodine did give me headaches, so I had to back off to taking it 3x per week only.

Do your headaches respond to ice packs?

### ealachan — 2012-10-04T19:20:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-56252>

> **Nonchalant;57503 wrote:** I threw out all the Tylenol in the house. If I'm desperate, I'll take Advil.
>
> Iodine did give me headaches, so I had to back off to taking it 3x per week only.
>
> Do your headaches respond to ice packs?

I finally sucked it up and took some Advil and life is a little more live-able now...

re: ice packs - I don't know. I get headaches very infrequently, so I've never actually had cause to try the ice pack thing for them. If this persists I'll definitely try it, though.

### Destiny — 2012-10-04T19:41:57+0300

>

>
> re: ice packs - I don't know. I get headaches very infrequently, so I've never actually had cause to try the ice pack thing for them. If this persists I'll definitely try it, though.

Ice packs worked for my headache every time. I was skeptical the first time but was wrong.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-04T20:38:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-56277>

ealachan - try some peppermint tea or peppermint oil rubbed on your temples for headache relief. Works great and much less harmful than Tylenol.

Just be sure to dilute the oil in a carrier oil like olive or coconut if you apply it directly to the skin.

### Destiny — 2012-10-20T14:46:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-59315>

> **ealachan;57468 wrote:** Has anybody else noticed an uptick in headaches since starting resveratrol? I'm not 100% sure that the supplement is what's causing it, but I've had a nearly constant headache for two days now, and it happens to coincide with when I started taking resveratrol. It's not a migraine-level event, but rather just a dull, annoying pressure in the front of my head and my sinuses. I'm debating whether I should take some Tylenol to get rid of it so that I can get some work done, or whether the Tylenol would do me more harm than good...

I have been trying to increase my resveratrol intake but now I am getting obnoxious headaches too. I take Doctor's Best. There are the following - rice, silicon, magnesium stearate.

### ealachan — 2012-10-20T15:22:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-59323>

> **Destiny;60858 wrote:** I have been trying to increase my resveratrol intake but now I am getting obnoxious headaches too. I take Doctor's Best. There are the following - rice, silicon, magnesium stearate.

I think I either got used to it, or the headaches were due to something else, because it hasn't been an issue lately. I'm on 500mg with breakfast and 500mg with lunch right now.

### Destiny — 2012-10-20T17:37:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-59341>

> **ealachan;60866 wrote:** I think I either got used to it, or the headaches were due to something else, because it hasn't been an issue lately. I'm on 500mg with breakfast and 500mg with lunch right now.

That is good news! I usually don't have any headaches. I am pretty sure that my headaches are from it. I hope to adjust to it soon too.

### Destiny — 2012-10-21T19:53:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-59490>

> **Anna K.;61031 wrote:** I've seen on googles that resveratrol can mimic some of the same effects as estrogen supplementation.
>
> not sure if this is correct. probably a good question to ask dr K.
>
>
>
> "If your FREE estrogen is normal/high and your SHBG is moderate/low, resveratrol will BIND free estrogen and make it lower (so might be a good thing in your case if your FREE estrogen is too high).
>
>
>
> If your FREE estrogen is normal/high and your SHBG is high, your free estrogen can't be used by the body because it's all being bound by the SHBG. In that case (mine), the resveratrol is seen by the body as estrogen and it binds to the SHBG, thus freeing up your own estrogen for use by the body.
>
>
>
> If your FREE estrogen is low, resveratrol can act as an estrogen replacement."

That is interesting! Thanks, Anna! According to my tests from January and April of this year, all of my hormones were in a toilet.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-22T15:36:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-59604>

Emodin might be to blame for some of the side effects people are describing. It is a purgative resin found in japanese knotweed and rhubarb. The 50% resveratrol extracts have emodin in them accounting for the tan/brown color. Pure resveratrol is white. Some known side effects of emodin include nausea, liver damage, and kidney damage. There have been anecdotal accounts where people have blamed resveratrol for damaged heel tendons causing them to have trouble walking. Could it be the emodin or just a coincidental symptom developed by some of the already sick and aged people taking resveratrol?

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-10-23T05:22:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-59781>

I haven't had any headaches from the resveratrol (I'm using Biotivia brand). In fact, I love it and look forward to when I open up a capsule and empty it in my mouth for oral absorption (I do this with one capsule and I also swallow a capsule later). I don't know if I'm imagining it or if it's for real but it seems to give me a tiny buzz type feeling through my body.

### ealachan — 2012-10-23T17:04:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-side-effects.4112/post-59816>

>
>
>
> so I wonder what is high level?
>
>
>
> how much are you guys taking?
>
>
>
> thanks.

Interesting. I have 200mg caplets, and I take one with breakfast and try to also take one at lunch but I forget most of the time...so I'm probably not taking enough to amount to anything. I think Dr. K said he was taking like 3200mg a day for a while? It's in my EPCOTx notes, but they're at home so I can't confirm. In the webinar he did make it sound like the more resveratrol the better, though of course one must consider context lest one get yelled at...

# Thread: Meditation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-10-04T17:08:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56223>

I am looking for a good meditation source/program. I've never done any meditation before so I need all the help I can get.

Thank you!

### ealachan — 2012-10-04T17:11:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56227>

I really like Jon Kabat-Zinn - his guided meditations really helped me a lot when I was first learning. He also has some great books out that I've really enjoyed. Highly recommended.

### Destiny — 2012-10-04T19:39:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56258>

> **ealachan;57482 wrote:** I really like Jon Kabat-Zinn - his guided meditations really helped me a lot when I was first learning. He also has some great books out that I've really enjoyed. Highly recommended.

Thanks, ealachan! I'll look into it. Would you know of calming music CDs for a commute? I am asking since there are so many and I don't want to waste my money if I can go for a sure thing.

### PaleoCarol — 2012-10-04T19:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56263>

I learned TM and Mindfulness based stressed meditation years ago. I recently moved to a new neighborhood and if you google meditation and your city or zip code I bet you will find many places that have multiple weekly free sessions. That's what I did. There are also many books and CDs you can borrow from the library to get started. No one technique is best. Everyone has their own preferences. PM me if you want to come with me to my favorite place (it's free).

Carol

### Michael — 2012-10-04T19:57:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56265>

This might be something you could try. It's on iTunes, so you can just download it and put it on an iPod. I liked it:

[http://itunes.apple.com/podcast/mindfulness-breath-meditation/id267047723](http://http://itunes.apple.com/podcast/mindfulness-breath-meditation/id267047723)

### ealachan — 2012-10-04T19:59:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56267>

> **Destiny;57514 wrote:** Thanks, ealachan! I'll look into it. Would you know of calming music CDs for a commute? I am asking since there are so many and I don't want to waste my money if I can go for a sure thing.

I can't help you there, unfortunately. My musical tastes skew pretty far to the...erm...not serene.

### shah78 — 2012-10-04T20:36:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56276>

I have found that the cold tub itself is the best meditative tool I've ever known. I just sit there and breath. Seems to meet all the "requirements" of meditation. The price is really great.

### Michael — 2012-10-04T20:42:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56280>

> **Destiny;57514 wrote:** Would you know of calming music CDs for a commute?

A couple of suggestions - may not appeal, but I might as well post them.

Lute music could be fairly restful. The patterns could be soothing, but you probably wouldn't have to attend to them if you weren't in the mood. I like a CD from Nigel North just called Baroque Lute that Linn brought out for listening to, sometimes. But he's done a few, including one with the delightful title "Go from my window" (that told the lovesick swain were to take his strumming, I guess!)

You could sample some tracks at iTunes to see if that would appeal. Here's what Amazon have in CD format:

[http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb\_sb\_ss\_c\_0\_11?url=search-alias%3Dclassical&field-keywords=nigel+north&sprefix=nigel+north%2Caps%2C261](http://http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb_sb_ss_c_0_11?url=search-alias%3Dclassical&field-keywords=nigel+north&sprefix=nigel+north%2Caps%2C261)

Some of them probably come with vocals, which might make them less calm inasmuch as you might try to follow the lyric and external noise could then become annoying.

The second idea I have is a bit wacky, but why not?

I wonder about Sibelius. Maybe one of the symphonies (5 perhaps?). They can be exciting, but there are long passages more suggestive of darkly dreaming northern forests - just the thing to get one in the mood for CT.

Otherwise, I suppose something Japanese or Indian might be worth trying, because I think, to our ear at least, it would have less progression, so would feel less demanding. Something New-Agey would probably be undemanding but might get a bit boring.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-04T23:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56324>

Destiny, re:music, so much of this is what suits you. There is chanting meditation music (sometimes called kirtan) and several good artists out there who make lovely music.

And re: meditation, there are also many different kinds and traditions. Many are based in non-Western spiritual traditions. Some are Westernized (and I agree Jon Kabat Zinn has some great stuff). Some focus on watching the breath, others focus on particular practices (say, for example, metta (loving kindness)). Do you want teaching/dharma with your meditation or simply someone to teach you basic meditation practices? Thich Nhat Hahn is from a Buddhist tradition but has some of the most simple and beautiful meditations to teach (and ones that you don't need to do in a particular meditation time, but that you can do throughout your day). Pema Chodron has amazing teaching for those working with difficult circumstances. As does Jack Kornfield. Just some ideas.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-05T03:02:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56379>

Destiny...the mantras of Hein Braat a dutch singer are astounding. Listening to them can help take you into a meditative state. I've used music to teach how to quiet the mind and his CD is really wonderful. The online catalogue Isabella stocks his CD "Gayatri Mantra"

Also Andre Weil has a two CD set out: "Self Healing with Sound and Music." The Brainwave Symphony set of classical music put together by Dr. Jeffery Thompson is also great...music to fall asleep to, relax, energize and focus. Isabella stocks this.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-05T03:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56389>

time to get the chest submerged in some real cold water........and consider progesterone and the Epi-paleo Rx in a big way. Iodine on the areola too.........channel your inner doctor.

### kathylu — 2012-10-05T03:45:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56394>

I don't know where you are in New York, but if it is easy for you to get to the New York Insight Meditation Center, that would be an excellent place to learn Vipassana meditation. Vipassana is very easy to do, it focuses on the breath and relaxation. They work by donation only, so you give only what you can afford. They have free sessions for beginners and encourage the formation of local groups of like minded individuals who practice together (a "sangha"). I belong to a sangha in the Washington DC area, fun and helpful in encouraging the development of a meditation practice. Jon Kabat-Zinn and Jack Kornfeld are well known Vipassana teachers.

[http://nyimc.org/index.php/site/sitting\_beginners/](http://http://nyimc.org/index.php/site/sitting_beginners/)

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-05T04:29:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56404>

> **Destiny;57478 wrote:** I am looking for a good meditation source/program. I've never done any meditation before so I need all the help I can get.
>
>
>
> Thank you!

MindLight by silver raven wolf. Great book.

here's the link to the book on amazon - I picked it up b/c a friend had recommended it to me to use for child birth in conjunciton w/hypnobirthing (worked great) and the meditations are great.

you can take or leave the spirtuality if its not your cup of tea...but one thing that resonated w/me then - and now is that your thoughts can change things (sound familiar?)

there's a meditation in the book where you build your own "mindlight" - its a pretty cool excercise to do...

[http://www.amazon.com/MindLight-Secrets-Energy-Magick-Manifestation/dp/0738709859/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1349441381&sr=8-1&keywords=mindlight](http://http://www.amazon.com/MindLight-Secrets-Energy-Magick-Manifestation/dp/0738709859/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1349441381&sr=8-1&keywords=mindlight)

### maggie — 2012-10-05T04:31:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56406>

I enjoy listening to the talks by the Buddhist teachers on dharmaseed.com. There's lots and lots of stuff on that site and it's all free unless you care to donate. My favorite monk is Ajahn Sucitto, a British teacher who heads an abbey in the UK. But there are a gazillion talks available. Wes Nisker is a lay teacher who is very funny and witty; he talks a lot about evolution. There are also guided meditations. I often listen before I go to sleep; it stops the monkey brain stuff very effectively for me.

### Marccus — 2012-10-05T10:33:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.4115/post-56429>

I think that you should consider taking the advice of a yoga guru in your area. He will make you aware of the true meditation and its benefits and how you should do it.

### mafixit — 2012-10-04T17:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-take-a-look-and-give-your-opinion.4116/post-56240>

Still waiting for ASI and GI Effects. These are my results from Labcorp. Feel Free to Speak Up!!! FYI -I am on birth control pills, northindrone and ethinyl estradiol and ferrous fumarate 0.8/25 mcg. That is the only medication I take right now.

Issues I have that I am trying to resolve are migraine and other daily headaches, 24/7 dizziness/disequilibrium, internal shakiness and buzzing, trouble sleeping through the night, muscle weakness and inability to recover from any exercise (no matter how light), very high anxiety, some fatigue (I need short naps), overall stiffness especially in the mornings.

I am 48 YO female, 5'4", 139 lbs. Was exercising (mostly walking and weights) until about 2 months ago when I just could not take the dizziness and shakiness any more. Diet was low carb, no artificial sweeteners, no caffeine, no alcohol. I have move to the Paleo/Primal diet now. I was eating a lot of fruit (which I though was good for me!)

Did you fast for the blood test? Why are your trigs in the 300s? You can't have numbers that high whe you're low-carbing and also when your liver enzymes are in the teens and your IGF-1 is ~90 -- all consistent with low/moderate carbs and Paleo eating. Your trigs don't seem right. But then neither does your CRP and Ferritin. I would get ANA checked out given your morning stiffness and muscle weakness.

Low Vit D and you know how to address that. Rt3 a bit high. But your numbers are mostly good, except for some. So the area to look into is your migraine and muscle-related issues by testing for ANA --- they don't seem to stem from any subclinical hypothyroid issues. There is definitely inflammation somewhere.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T03:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-take-a-look-and-give-your-opinion.4116/post-57009>

I hope this helps:

_"ANA: Antinuclear antibody, an unusual antibody directed against structures within the nucleus of the cell. ANAs are found in patients whose immune system is predisposed to cause inflammation against their own body tissues. Antibodies that are directed against one's own tissues are referred to as autoantibodies. The propensity for the immune system to work against its own body is referred to as autoimmunity. ANAs indicate the possible presence of autoimmunity."_

[http://www.medterms.com/script/main/art.asp?articlekey=2232](http://http://www.medterms.com/script/main/art.asp?articlekey=2232)

.

It's frustrating that nearly 100% of Doctors NEVER mention **Sheehan's Syndrome (_Glinski-Simmonds syndrome**)Â to women who have given birth.Â  Any woman that has given birth more than likey has this disorder! Simply put giving birth kills your Pituitary Gland.Â  Once your Pituitary is toast - your health falls apart fast.Â  Since you'll never find one Doctor familar with this - you'll never get tested or treated.Â  Currently there's only one true Pituitary Endocrinologist in the entire United States.Â  All other endocrinologist treat from the neck down - which is of no use to the patient.Â  If you ask a Doctor about it or even google it - you'll find reports saying it's very very rare and nothing to worry about.Â  WRONG!

Even moderate stress has been proven to fry your Pituitary.Â  Just a moderate "jiggle" of your head and it can damage your pituitary.Â  Thanks to the research of Dr Erin Bigler, PhD out of Utah (the doc who proved head truama in the NFL lawsuit) has shown how deadly and common this condition is.Â 

Â 

**Men will also suffer a form of Sheehan's Syndrome** (pituitary necrosis**_**) - thanks to more than 80% of men getting Circumcised /wp-includes/images/smilies/icon\_sad.gif Â  ugh...

If you're Circumcised you've lost more than your Foreskin.Â  Early research is showing this maybe the primary cause of Erectile Dysfunction is found in so many men.Â  It's bad news but if treated you can regain some of your normal hormonal function.Â 

Â

# Thread: You go, Dr. Eades! AMPK activation, carb restriction, cancer, Metformin action
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-go-dr-eades-ampk-activation-carb-restriction-cancer-metformin-action.4138/>

### kathylu — 2012-10-05T19:53:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-go-dr-eades-ampk-activation-carb-restriction-cancer-metformin-action.4138/post-56501>

Great blog post from Michael Eades. He analyzes a recent feeding study comparing hi carb to low carb diets in caloric restriction/overfeeding. Finally, someone is looking at this!. Studies like this one are needed to convince the CW folks of the reasons for the inefficacy of hi carb/low fat diets. Of course, varying macronutrient composition affects cellular signalling, carb restriction increasing AMPK activation (which is a very good thing). Please read the blog, it contains a very nice explanation of how enzymes function and some of the effects of metformin. Entertaining as well.

[http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/inflammation/can-your-food-make-you-fit/](http://http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/inflammation/can-your-food-make-you-fit/)

Here's a nice overview of the history and mechanisms of action of metformin. It almost seems like you can't say enough good things about metformin...increases insulin sensitivity, lowers blood glucose (downregulates gluconeogenesis), increases fat burning, activates a major tumor suppressor gene (LKB1, liver kinase B1), enhances mitochondrial biogenesis, reduces mitochondrial leakiness, maybe enhances longevity...

[http://cancerpreventionresearch.aacrjournals.org/content/3/9/1060.full](http://http://cancerpreventionresearch.aacrjournals.org/content/3/9/1060.full)

I hate to say this, but maybe Big Pharma gets something right once in a while!

# Thread: After reading Brain Gut 13,   what fish are best to get in the fish market?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-06T04:38:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-56557>

Yo! so DHA now after reading Dr. Kruse, is the ultimate Brain food. You have to eat fish to eat it. But which fish are the ones that we need to buy that are loaded with Omega 3s? I read and it was mentioned by Dr. Kruse that deep dwelling fish and mammals are loaded with high DHA.

I am worried where to buy my Fish? Any Fish Market will do? I am concerned because we do pollute the ocean and mercury can be found on the fish.

### Michael — 2012-10-06T17:14:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-56595>

Any fish will be good. Oily cold-water fish will be a particularly good option - shellfish even better.

Pollution of the oceans is a worry, but Jack seems to be saying that our need for the nutrients found in the highest concentrations in seafood is so great that on balance better to eat it than avoid it.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-06T19:27:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-56611>

That is spot on........

### Integral_D — 2012-10-09T02:53:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-57004>

For most wild caught fish, the mercury scare is way over done. Ocean caught seafood is exposed to far less pollution than the great majority of land-based food, including organically farmed (which can still get substantial amounts of air/water pollution exposure, depending on where the farm is). If you eat a lot of fish, it's best to eat further down the food chain since metals bio-accumulate in the top predators. A large tuna or swordfish, for example, will have consumed hundreds of pounds of smaller fish (say, sardines) and if they are contaminated the metal concentrates in the predators flesh. So eating the smaller fish is going to give you a lot less exposure, more EFAs, and cost way less. Plus, take some pressure off those "desirable" species. "Farmed" catfish, tilapia, and to some extent salmon, are exposed to the same grain-feeding-antibiotic-swilling BS as other factory farmed animals, so be wary of those. Shellfish like oysters are usually "farmed" by simply setting out seed oysters in a protected coastal area, and waiting for them to mature. You can't really "feed" shellfish...

So unless you consume hundreds (maybe thousands) of pounds of swordfish, you're going to get more mercury exposure from your old dental fillings than from seafood. Other than metals (and crazy stuff like dioxins) other pollution doesn't accumulate in the flesh as much. I am hearing about some rivers with measurable amounts of pharmaceuticals in them (presumably from sewage treatment, which generally is aimed at breaking down poop and other straight biological pollutants, and is not equipped to handle the huge flow of Prozac in the wastewater of a big city....) but I haven't seen any data about it being found in fish caught in those waters. As far as I know, there are few commercially important freshwater species caught in those sorts of waters. As the posters above said, the benefits from seafood far outweigh the risks.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-09T05:49:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-57056>

antiboitics/steroids being used on fish too. i just bought some sardines from Shop Rite. I guess it is not best to buy fish from Shop Rite then correct or any local supermarket. Have to head to the Fish Market it seems but man, those fish are not cheap either.

I forgot to mention about Lobsters? Are these boys good in Omega 3s?

### Michael — 2012-10-09T11:54:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-57070>

> **x\_danny\_x;58358 wrote:** I forgot to mention about Lobsters? Are these boys good in Omega 3s?
>
>
> Here you go:
>
>
>
> H. gammarus populations inhabit sub-tidal eastern North Atlantic waters from the Lofoten Islands in arctic Norway to the Azores and Morocco, including the Mediterranean and western Black Sea, but not in the Baltic. ...
>
>
>
> The diet of adult European lobsters, who hunt mainly nocturnaly, comprises mostly of benthic invertebrates such as crabs, molluscs, urchins, starfish and polychaete worms, but may also include some fish, algae and zooplankton. Feeding is reduced in the winter as the metabolic rate slows because of the lower sea temperatures.

[http://www.nationallobsterhatchery.co.uk/education/lobster-biology.htm](http://http://www.nationallobsterhatchery.co.uk/education/lobster-biology.htm)

That's one particular species of many, but you get the gist? It does OK in arctic seas, which is a sign of the presence of omega-3 (helps it withstand the cold), and it's eating of the marine foodchain, so it gets those in its diet.

I don't know how they compare to, say, crabs or prawns or herrings. You might be able to find a table of values by googling something like "docasohexaenoic acid fish shellfish". I would simply reckon on eating all this stuff and varying for the sake of interest.

### Integral_D — 2012-10-09T13:00:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-57073>

> **x\_danny\_x;58358 wrote:** antiboitics/steroids being used on fish too. i just bought some sardines from Shop Rite. I guess it is not best to buy fish from Shop Rite then correct or any local supermarket. Have to head to the Fish Market it seems but man, those fish are not cheap either.
>
>
>
> I forgot to mention about Lobsters? Are these boys good in Omega 3s?

Sardines are fine, as Dr Kruse said. They are ocean fish, and low on the food chain. As far as I know the only fish that are commercially "farmed" are catfish, tilapia, and some salmon. Sardines, herring, and even canned salmon are generally going to be just fine, if not the most elegant thing. I found some 6-packs of wild Alaska pink salmon at Costco for cheap. There are lots of good recipes for salmon patties -- just substitute some non-grain for the bread crumbs. So if the ShopRite or any local market has decent stuff at a decent price, I say go for it. There are oceans full of marine species out there -- there's not one "right" answer. A fish market will simply give you a few more choices.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-10T03:39:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-57226>

Excellent dude! You just saved me some pain on my wallet. Going to shop for some can salmon and whatever fresh fish they have.

Thanks again.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-10T05:21:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-57242>

Just saw this article:

[http://nourishingourchildren.wordpress.com/2012/10/09/should-we-avoid-fish-because-of-mercury/](http://http://nourishingourchildren.wordpress.com/2012/10/09/should-we-avoid-fish-because-of-mercury/)

Echoes what Dr. K and others have been saying here: the packaging of nutrients that seafood gives you protects you from most of the bad stuff that comes along with it. Specifically, the high selenium levels in fish and shellfish prevent the buildup of toxic levels of mercury by binding to it and enable you to excrete it. In fact, in fish that are often advised against eating due to their mercury levels--tuna, for example--contain so much more selenium than mercury that people have observed \_lower\_ mercury levels in their bodies after eating the "high-mercury" fish.

Now when it comes to antibiotics, PCBs, and the effects of grain feeding of farmed fish.....I'm personally still skittish. I ate some farmed salmon today for the first time based on Dr. K's admonition that even "bad" seafood is better than the best organic/pastured land animal (reasoning being that the benefit outweighs the bad). We'll see if I drop dread or not.

BTW, a nice validation of Epi-Paleo:

"Dr. Weston A. Price who traveled the world in the 1930s to learn about the profound effect of traditional diets on human health found that populations who had access to fish and shellfish from the sea had excellent bone structure and integrity."

### Integral_D — 2012-10-10T19:11:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-57355>

While "Optimal" is what we're all after, getting there is a path that requires a first step (and a next, etc). The world is full of people who are waiting to take the first step until they have _all_ the steps and pieces perfectly laid out and understood. Optimal eating is one of those areas where "best is the enemy of good." Sure, it would be great to always have access to pasture-fed beef, but that's no excuse to eat KFC until the beef ship comes in  In the case of most seafood, the good news is that the benefits of the full package outweigh the risks. That info about selenium is great -- thanks for the link. So go for the farmed salmon!! I'm pretty sure you aren't going to keel over dead (unless you choke on a bone).

### Integral_D — 2012-10-16T18:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-58547>

As far as I know, feeding shrimp is a whole different matter than feeding fish. Although I've seen a few (unsupported) statements about farmed shrimp being "full of antibiotics and pesticides" it seems that the problems with farmed shrimp are more related to their environmental impact than to the substances they are treated with. The Rodale article below gives some recommendations about which seafood to "buy wild" and "buy farmed."

[http://www.rodale.com/wild-or-farmed-fish](http://http://www.rodale.com/wild-or-farmed-fish)

[http:/http://www.huffingtonpost.com/eatingwell/how-to-buy-the-right-shrimp\_b\_898454.html](http://http:/http://www.huffingtonpost.com/eatingwell/how-to-buy-the-right-shrimp_b_898454.html)
