**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-reading-brain-gut-13-what-fish-are-best-to-get-in-the-fish-market.4144/post-58676>

Our imagination is no competition for what we see in nature. Sometimes that imagination creates deadly diseases when we do not even realize it. All seafood, regardless of quality, exceeds most paleo foods any time on nutrient density when you consider the metabolism hog of the brain and heart. Should we strive for natural raw seafood not raised by aquaculture.......sure. But when you live in a world that creates 95% of all food you have to choose best and do it wisely. The Agra-food complex is transforming "things" into "foods" that our biology has absolutely no reference for and this is where disease begins for modern humans.

# Thread: Osmolality Thread
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-06T23:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/post-56630>

Since Dr Kruse posted about the Na pumps and 60 per cent (my lazy senile butt remembers right) DHA power being used by them in the heart and brain, I was hoping some folks besides me might have an interest. All of us with blood pressure and electrolyte issues are heavy players.

Betaine is the hcl that is the battery acid for our bodies. Just think about a weak battery and how things don't work so well. Thermostats, electrical gauges start responding to the surges and trickery. They do everything thing they can to keep your car safe, as long as the computer is right.

This is a little, but kind of complicated (to me) article I didn't want to pass by. It relates to magnetics.

[http://www.thefreedictionary.com/coulombic](http://http://www.thefreedictionary.com/coulombic)

> CouÂ·lomb (klm, -lm, k-lm, -lÃ´), Charles Augustin de 1736-1806.
>
> French physicist who pioneered research into magnetism and electricity and formulated Coulomb's law.
>
>
>
>
>
>
> [http://www.jbc.org/content/287/33/27813.abstract](http://http://www.jbc.org/content/287/33/27813.abstract)
>
> Impacts of the Osmolality and the Lumenal Ionic Strength on Osmosensory Transporter ProP in Proteoliposomes\*
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> H+ symporter ProP serves as a paradigm for the study of osmosensing. ProP attains the same activity at the same osmolality when the medium outside cells or proteoliposomes is supplemented with diverse, membrane-impermeant solutes. The osmosensory mechanism of ProP has been probed by varying the solvent within membrane vesicles and proteoliposomes. **ProP activation was not ion specific, did not require K+, and could be elicited by large, uncharged solutes polyethylene glycols (PEGS). We hypothesized that ProP is an ionic strength sensor and lumenal macromolecules activate ProP by altering ion activities. The attainable range of lumenal ionic strength was expanded by lowering the phosphate concentration within proteoliposomes. ProP activity at high osmolality, but not the osmolality, yielding half-maximal activity (ΠÂ½/RT), decreased with the lumenal phosphate concentration. This was attributed to acidification of the proteoliposome lumen due to H+-proline symport. The ionic strength yielding half-maximal ProP activity was more anion-dependent than ΠÂ½/RT for proteoliposomes loaded with citrate, sulfate, phosphate, chloride, or iodide. The anion effects followed the Hofmeister series. Lumenal bovine serum albumin (BSA) lowered the lumenal ionic strength at which ProP became active. Osmolality measurements documented the non-idealities of solutions including potassium phosphate and other solutes. The impacts of PEGS and BSA on ion activities did not account for their impacts on ProP activity. The effects of the tested solutes on ProP appear to be non-coulombic in nature. They may arise from effects of preferential interactions and macromolecular crowding on the membrane or on ProP.**

I want to believe that the phosphate is just waiting for magnesium. It seems like everything is just waiting for magnesium.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-06T23:57:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/post-56638>

This is the source of most cardiovascular issues like HTN. It is damage done to the battery. That battery is the extracellular matrix ion mix and the power loss at the mitochondria. Read Metabolic cardiology. My blog is metabolic neurosurgery.......and since the brain controls it all.........you better get the implications of that for your health

### chocolate — 2012-10-07T05:19:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/post-56669>

[http://www.heartmdinstitute.com/metabolic-cardiology](http://http://www.heartmdinstitute.com/metabolic-cardiology)

> Metabolic Cardiology
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Metabolic cardiology is a new field of medicine which concentrates on the prevention, management, and treatment of cardiovascular disease at the cellular level, through biochemical interventions that are native to the human body. Diseased hearts are energy-starved, which means they lack enough adenosine triphosphate (ATP) to fuel healthy cardiac function. ATP molecules are both synthesized and recycled within the body, and supply all of the body’s energy. Metabolic cardiology aims to build up and maintain ATP stores in the heart, and ultimately enhance heart health, by providing the body with the nutrients it needs to make ATP.
>
>
>
> Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10), L-carnitine, D-ribose, and magnesium, the staples of metabolic cardiology, are nutrients essential for ATP production. Dr. Sinatra refers to these nutrients as “the awesome foursome." While the body requires D-ribose to synthesize ATP de novo, or from scratch, it utilizes carnitine and CoQ10 to efficiently recycle ATP. Magnesium, which participates in over 300 enzymatic reactions in the body, also helps boost ATP production to prevent and treat cardiovascular disease.

I guess we just need to sleep and live keto. Then we can get the magnesium in and the other stuff as well. I think the d-ribose is glycine. I could be very very wrong.

Anyway, its all the same stuff. I guess I'll just toggle back and forth.

### chocolate — 2012-10-07T05:56:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/post-56671>

[http://www.traceminerals.com/news/PR-npa-vegas-2012\_release](http://http://www.traceminerals.com/news/PR-npa-vegas-2012_release)

> Electrolyte Stamina Shot was formulated with vitamin C, D-Ribose, Glycine Propionyl-L-Carnitine (GPLC), and over 72 electrolytes from Endure™. Vitamin C is an antioxidant that helps reduce the amount of oxidative damage caused by exercise and is a critical block in building strength and muscle. D-Ribose is a simple sugar in the body that is an essential part of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production. The body needs to keep its production of ATP at its highest possible level for maximum energy production, and peak physical performance. GPLC helps maintain stamina during workouts for better pumps and shorter post-exercise recovery while the over 72 electrolytes from Endure™ help efficiently hydrate the body and give the muscles the electrolytes they need to prevent painful cramps. Electrolyte Stamina Shot has a great berry flavor, is certified vegan and gluten free, and is available in natural and health food stores for an MSRP of $59.88 ($4.99 per bottle) for 12, 2-ounce bottles, which come pre-packaged in a box that converts into a counter or shelf display. TMR will be distributing samples of Electrolyte Stamina Shot in booth #501 during the trade show.

I guess the d-ribose is not glycine. Looks like these folks have been paying attention. Its some new product for sports and probably heart folks too. It seems all the fitness stuff really helps illness. Like creatine and sleep/memory. It seems like the sea salt would have everything else.

### chocolate — 2012-10-08T05:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/post-56860>

[http://aprovenyou.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2012/01/TMA-Testing-Results.pdf](http://http://aprovenyou.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/2012/01/TMA-Testing-Results.pdf)

Krill oil

> TMA as an Osmolyte
>
> In order for small crustaceans like krill and shrimp to survive the high salt environment of the ocean, they must possess certain charged molecules within their bodies. This ensures that the krill do not shrivel up from water rushing out of their bodies or swell up and burst because of water rushing into their bodies.
>
> One of the key osmolytes (molecules that control the osmotic pressure) in krill is called trimethylamine oxide (TMAO). Krill produce this in response to the varying salt conditions of seawater, so that they maintain the proper cellular water balance. For the krill, TMAO is not a problem, but a solution.

I was reading that choline could convert to this. IDK how, and give you fishy smelling breath. Its a long haul to fix, but worth it. Now, I swear, I'm having doubts about drinking little dabs of salt water. It has to be correct. You would get it if you were eating straight out of the ocean. TMA is removed from krill oil.

### chocolate — 2012-11-25T10:25:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/post-65548>

http://lifeinbitznpieces.wordpress.com/2011/04/01/mechanism-of-hypokalemia-in-magnesium-deficiency/

**Mechanism of hypokalemia in magnesiumÂ deficiency**

> http://jasn.asnjournals.org/cgi/reprint/18/10/2649
>
> I had summarized this article a while ago (before I started blogging) thought it was worthwhile to publish it, both for my benefit as for others.
>
> Hypokalemia is the most common electrolyte disturbance that nephrologists frequently encounter.
>
> Serum potassium (K) depends on intake, excretion & distribution between extra & intracellular spaces
>
> 50% of clinically significant hypokalemia has co-existing Mg deficiency and is refractory to treatment unless Mg is corrected. Causes includeÂ loop diuretics, thiazide therapy, diarrhea, alcoholism, barter & gitelmanâ€™s syndromes, nephrotoxic meds like aminoglycosides, amphotericin, cisplatin
>
> MECHANISMS
>
> Mg deficiency causes impairment of Na K ATPase. This decreases cellular uptake of K. {my interpretation: Since Na K ATPase is present in virtually all cells, serum K should rise. This in turn may send signals to the kidney to get rid of K}. Thus, increased gastrointestinal or urinary losses causes K wasting leading to a decrease in total body potassium. Since GI loss is negligible, kidneys play a vital role in this.
>
> EVIDENCE supporting this :
>
> Urinary K excretion is decreased and serum K increases when Magnesium(Mg) when given to hypokalemic hypomagnesemic patients (Bartterâ€™s syndrome, patients on thiazide diuretics) and
>
> even in normal pts, urinary excretion is decreased with Mg infusion
>
> In patients with Gitelmanâ€™s syndrome (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia & met alkalosis) Transtubular potassium gradient (TTKG) is decreased with Mg infusion
>
> Â 
>
> Â 
>
> I thought this might help some folks. I read the study, but this person breaks it down.

### chocolate — 2012-11-25T10:33:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/osmolality-thread.4155/post-65550>

continued from above

http://lifeinbitznpieces.wordpress.com/2011/04/01/mechanism-of-hypokalemia-in-magnesium-deficiency/

Â 

At physiological conditions Mg concentration needed for this is 0.1 -10 mmoles (median 1 mol). Since only 2% of total body Mg is in extracellular fluid, intracellular conc is 10-20 mmols, mainly bound to ATP. Only 5% is free

**In kidneys & heart 100% of Mg can equilibrate with the plasma in 3-4 hrs, but for brain it is 10%, muscle 25% Moreover it occurs over 16 hrs.

What this means is that when plasma Mg falls for any reason, the intracellular levels drop first in kidneys & heart. This damages the inward rectifying nature of the ROMK channel

In patients with isolated Mg deficiency, hypokalemia not seen why? Two reasons

**1\. Because the Na K ATPase is also impaired, this causes less Na uptake by muscle & kidney that will cause K to rise again & become normal..?

**{ Doesnâ€™t this also stimulate aldosterone secretion which in turn activates the ENaC in the DCT & CCD?}

2\. **Increase in K secretion (caused by Mg deficiency as not enough Mg is around to bind to intracellular side of ROMK) causes hyperpolarization of cells in DCT & CCD driving force for outward K flux is decreased and K secretion is limited.

**Hypomagnesemia alone is not enough to cause hypokalemia

**Additional factors needed are increased sodium delivery & elevated aldosterone levels.**

But these factors can independently cause hypokalemia. So How much does hypomagnesemia contribute? Are we back to square one?**

Â** 

Â** 

Â** 

just add fructose and there's your sodium!
**

# Thread: My favorit meal.. tasty and totally optimal! :-)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-favorit-meal-tasty-and-totally-optimal.4160/>

### Inger — 2012-10-07T11:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-favorit-meal-tasty-and-totally-optimal.4160/post-56684>

Ok I need to share with you what I love eating these days. I got mackerels again. After 2 weeks without..huh.

Then.. I always do the smoothie first, as a starter, with 1-3 heads and the liver and roe if there is any. I drink it fast - nose closed and rinse my mouth.

Then comes the good part!

I fillet the mackerel (2-3) and cut it into pieces. I add chopped organic onion, grated organic fresh ginger, a little raw wild honey, raw organic apple cider vinegar or white wine vinegar, celic sea salt, chopped dried seaweed, and on the top dried wild harvested mushrooms. I could eat this every. single. day. It is totally delicious!!! One could wrap the tartar in nori sheets too for sure.

What I have recognized since eating epi paleo rx.. my teeth does not go sensitive at all anymore when I eat wild sour berries! Before they got at one point. But now I can eat much much and they never get sensitive! How cool!

And my skin.. it is like velvet.

Guten Appetit!

# Thread: Early hominin diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/early-hominin-diet.4167/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-07T19:10:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/early-hominin-diet.4167/post-56738>

Not sure if we've had this one before:

> Here we report an in situ archaeological assemblage from the Koobi Fora Formation in northern Kenya that provides a unique combination of faunal remains, some with direct evidence of butchery, and Oldowan artifacts, which are well dated to 1.95 Ma. This site provides the oldest in situ evidence that hominins, predating Homo erectus, enjoyed access to carcasses of terrestrial and aquatic animals that they butchered in a well-watered habitat. It also provides the earliest definitive evidence of the incorporation into the hominin diet of various aquatic animals including turtles, crocodiles, and fish, which are rich sources of specific nutrients needed in human brain growth.

[http://www.pnas.org/content/107/22/10002.long](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/107/22/10002.long)

See also:

[http://www.pnas.org/content/107/24/10771.long](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/107/24/10771.long)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-07T19:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/early-hominin-diet.4167/post-56741>

More fuel for the epi-paleo fire.........that they said did not exist.

### AKMan — 2012-10-07T19:22:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/early-hominin-diet.4167/post-56744>

Really good find, Michael! You must have felt like Dr. Livingston when you dug that up! I've often remarked about stone age tools, that they find mostly arrow heads and tools, so conclude that they relied mainly on large animals, but the tools used for fishing would be more bio-degradable, nets, bone fish hooks, traps made of sticks, etc...

### Michael — 2012-10-08T08:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/early-hominin-diet.4167/post-56868>

> **AKMan;58046 wrote:** Really good find, Michael! You must have felt like Dr. Livingston when you dug that up! I've often remarked about stone age tools, that they find mostly arrow heads and tools, so conclude that they relied mainly on large animals, but the tools used for fishing would be more bio-degradable, nets, bone fish hooks, traps made of sticks, etc...

Dr. Broadhurst is interesting on this. You don't, of course, need equipment to collect shellfish, but, nor, she says do you need anything to catch an African catfish, the remains of which are sometimes found in large numbers at these sites. Presumably, a hominid could just scoop them out of the water.

The interesting thing is that the material evidence in the form of fishbones and shells has always been there, but people seem to have simply not seen it, because they'd assumed, for no good reason, that these creatures merely ate land-based food resources. Consequently, they assumed anything else had merely got deposited from the environment. You won't tend to get cut-marks on fishbones or shells, so there was often little overt to tip them off.

She says at one point in her talk that at one site there's a whole pile of _burnt_ shells - the obvious conclusion is that they were putting shellfish on a barbecue to steam them open.

[http://www.seafood.net.au/page/?pid=1438&nid=403](http://http://www.seafood.net.au/page/?pid=1438&nid=403)

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-10-22T15:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/early-hominin-diet.4167/post-59601>

I just love how easy the answers are, when we look for what is 'not' there. Why do we try to come up with complex and laborous explanations? Well...b/c of our ability to reason; and get into trouble.

Path of least resistance!! (You know what path of least resistance implies too? It still allows for active participation, not sit back and be 'lazy', but that the efforts put in fall into place better.

I feel lucky for this site and also to have an open mind and heart to read this site. The lessons that Jack teaches, beyond just health are life enduring. It's not so obvious for most people in modern society to understand these life lessons...so we must get it from internet...wow!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-07T21:14:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cunanne-videos-seafood-vital-for-brain-development-and-function-parts-1-and-2.4169/post-56777>

Change is a hard barrier for humans to break. It require fluidity of thought and that requires both a physical and mental motion to be used to get on in the environment. The new relationship helps you learn new ways of thinking about your situation and your life. Ultimately, you look at the world in a way that would have been so foreign to you that it wouldn’t have made any sense before you changed. I practice thinking this way and it opens doors to my brain that were previously chained shut.

### copychick — 2012-10-07T21:19:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-online-sources-for-oysters.4172/post-56779>

I need a good source of oysters online, since I live nowhere near any coast. They do sell small jars at Whole Foods but they are way too pricey. I would rather buy a large quantity and freeze them. Ideally, I could buy them already shucked and frozen so I can just take some out every couple days. Searching online, I am finding it is not easy to find a vendor!

Anybody have any resources? ~Thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-07T21:33:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-online-sources-for-oysters.4172/post-56784>

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-07T21:55:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-online-sources-for-oysters.4172/post-56789>

Is contamination of Gulf of Mexico seafood by the genetically modified oil eating bacteria a concern?

Have you tried Taylor Shellfish or perhaps Hilton's Shellfish. Hilton's is sold as Willapoint on the container. Both sell small and large size containers up to 1 gallon, but be mindful of shipping charges. I have priced oysters from my local Albertson's seafood counter here in Texas and its basically as cheap or cheaper to buy at the store rather than have them shipped. I spoke with the seafood manager and he says the oysters are distibuted to the stores fairly quickly after they are shucked. I intend to have him order me some larger containers such as the 16 oz. size.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-07T22:02:49+0300

# Thread: This Is My Life
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-10-08T06:46:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-56865>

I have been reading & thinking, thinking & reading; now is the time for DOING! Bit by bit, the message that I am in worse shape & condition than I even thought is becoming clear to me. I have been resisting committing to the Leptin Reset and the EPi-Paleo Rx, but I have run out of other options. It's a miracle I have not already been diagnosed with cancer or haven't had a heart attack. This is not the life I want for myself or my family. I just pray it's not too late.

I am 64 yrs old, weigh 324 lbs. I used to be 5'8" tall, now I'm maybe 5'4" . Where did 4 inches go? Part of that is the bone gone from my knees, but that can't account for 4 inches, can it? I think I'm collapsing in on myself. Do I have to add osteoporosis to the list of what is wrong?

So CW med has not helped so far. I think it's time for something radical. Loved the title of one of the blog posts, "Through the Rabbit Hole." That's where I'm going.

Step 1--light/dark cycles. Time to shut down iPad & TV, begin process of getting ready for bed.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-08T22:57:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-56948>

In bed 10 pm, slept most of night, woke up 2x to go to bathroom, once bc DH was up getting ready for work & had light on. Took meds, back to sleep for hour or so. Had to get up to get ready for plumber.

Breakfast-- leftover pork tenderloin, summer squash w cheese, green beans. Very yummy, but not sure enough protein. Green tea w 1 tsp coconut oil. Like the flavor of this green tea. I think quality matters in tea, evidently?

Read Darleen's post about flat spot on her back where cold water hits her back. Interesting! Spent all morning while plumber is here reading Dr. Kruse's FB posts & following links. Struck by the pic of 2 hands holding 2 pills, one blue, one red, one to keep things as they are, one to Wonderland to see how deep is the rabbit hole. So this is me, slowing heading for the rabbit hole.

Lunch-- sautÃ©ed sole in butter w lemon juice, parsley, capers. Will make enough for breakfast tomorrow so no excuses. Think I'll defrost some shrimp, too. Didn't make it to arthroswim bc of plumber. Wonder if exercising gently in really warm water cancels out any possible benefit from cool water showers?

Need to call & set up ultrasound Dr. N wanted me to have, then call his office for follow-up.

Also start writing medical history to send to Dr. K.

Also order blue light blocking glasses. Sure thought I had done that.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-09T00:12:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-56964>

Molliecat, the journey may be long and fraught with pitfalls but you're definitely in the right place!

Lucky you to be on coast where fresh seafood is readily available. I'm just getting going on the eli-paleo wagon and am finding it to be the best move I could have made so far.

The blu blockers are a must. I now find that when it begins to get dark the artificial light actually hurts my eyes. And I absolutely believe that because of them I am sleeping better than ever. Well, except for when the furbabies act up and wake me up in the middle of the night.

On the "lost" height. Here's my opinion, for whatever it is worth. You're probably slumping because of age, the weight (esp from your breasts) and generally being tired. Try this ... pull your shoulders UP and back (try holding your elbows at your side and move them back and down) then lift from your head as if you have a string attached and are being pulled upwards. I think you'll find those missing 4" real fast. If nothing else you'll feel an amazing difference in your breathing when you do this.

Good luck and I'm glad you're here!!

### Endless — 2012-10-10T03:57:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57235>

I second the idea that blue blockers are essential....I have a pair of BluBlocker sunglasses as well as a pair of the UVEX safety glasses. Sometimes I put them both on at the same time! lol

Do you have f.lux installed on your computer? I've found that really helpful too, and I went into my tv settings and turned the backlight down to zero. Funny, at first it seemed really dim, now I'm used to it and it seems bright again

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-10T05:29:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57243>

Thanks, Darleen & Endless! I don't know how you have the time to read, study everything Dr. Kruse writes, take care of a family/ house, and give the great feedback to other people, so I REALLY appreciate your time here!

Darleen, I know that slumping is part of my height issue. I use a walker & find myself walking bent over. I am making a conscious effort to do just as you said to break that habit. So many habits to change! LOL!

Thank you both on the encouragement to get the blue blocker glasses. Almost ordered some last night, but when I read the UVEX glasses don't fit so well over regular glasses I put it off again. Will do it for sure tonight.

Yes, installed f-lux on main computer, but 99% of time use iPad. Don't want to jailbreak it to install f-lux on it. Trying to turn off iPad by 8 pm, do none computer things til bedtime. Trying to avoid artificial light has made me very aware of just how dependent/addicted to it we are! Husband is already telling people how weird I'm becoming; wait til he sees me in the orange glasses! And when he finds out I ordered some for him! Oh, boy!

Dinner last night-- sautÃ©ed bacon, onions, mushrooms over ground beef patty. Made broccoli coleslaw with small orange, avocado, &

Best Foods mayo. When this mayo is gone, will try making my own. Avocado was a little ripe. Think coleslaw would have been better if avocado had been firmer. DH liked everything. Forgot & left the leftover squash & green beans in microwave, so threw them out this am.

Darn! Oops forgot I had a handful of Marcona almonds before dinner. Then spoonful of coconut bliss & cup of herb tea w tsp of coconut oil.

Enough to make me very satisfied.

Bed-- about 10 pm

Today (Tues). Up at 6:30, breakfast: leftover sole, leftover dinner from last night. Then fruit salad & little plain yogurt at Bible Study. EVERYONE (except me) brought goodies: pumpkin bread, banana bread, choc chip cookies, lemon pound cake, AND bruschetta! Resisted it all except bruschetta.

Lunch no, except then went by Starbucks & got 4 cake pops! Oh, dear! Then other errands, inc TJ's. managed to avoid buying popcorn, nuts, etc. bought flowers instead. Also got shrimp stir fry mix to have in freezer for emergency dinner.

Dinner: decided tonight was emergency dinner night so had shrimp stir fry. Not very much shrimp & ingredient list inc dextrose which I know is sugar, but I think tiny amount from the taste & better than the alternative might have been. Would have been pretty good day except for bread at Bible Study & cake pops.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-10T23:54:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57446>

Go into "settings" on your iPad and just turn down the brightness. I have mine set at a bit lower than 50% and at night WITH my blue blockers on it's still too bright. So I just turn it off. Oh well. It was supposed to be for studio use only. yeah right.

Have you read "Wheat Belly" by Wm Davis, MD? I pretty much guarantee that once you do you will never again have a problem avoiding anything with wheat in it.

Learning about the evils of sugar (glycation) keeps me in check when that devilish little 3yo inside me wants a blizzard.

Negative reinforcement is magical!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-11T02:02:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57472>

Hi, Darleen! Yes, I did read "Wheat Belly" which is why I decided to eliminate all grains. Most of the time, I can look at something "grainy" & now view it as close to poison. But 63 yrs of conditioning die hard, at least in my case. Doesn't help that I really like the taste of all those things! And I seem to have an issue with avoiding something at one point only to succumb later to it. Tonight will be another tough one as we have some terrific bakers in our group. BUT that's ne reason I decided to start keeping this journal, to keep myself accountable.. I don't want to come back here later & write that I ate something unhealthy for me.

Breakfast-- 3 eggs, leftover bacon mix, tea w co. Had to force myself to eat the eggs, don't really like eggs.

LUnch-- shrimp sautÃ©ed in ghee, old bay seasoning, 2 fresh shrimp rolls from TJ's (do not buy again, like them too much). Need to make my own version as salad w/o rice paper wraps. Pkg of kale chips. Yummy, need to make my own version of these also if I'm going to eat them.

Dinner-- have no idea.

Edit: sautÃ©ed red cabbage & onion in co, threw in sliced brats, heated it through with some dill flavoring & red wine vinegar. Done.

### Endless — 2012-10-11T04:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57509>

I am a wheat junkie...or rather, I WAS. I found that being strict for awhile, coupled with feeling better and a small downward trend on the scale has helped me stay on the path. Just like having an episode of AFib scared me into quitting smoking 8 months ago! Anytime my little 'demon' tries to convince me to go ahead and grab a slice of pizza I think of where I was, where I am, and where I want to be, and then I open my emergency can of sardines or salmon jerky

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-12T06:37:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57703>

Yes, Endless, having something available for emergencies is probably crucial.

No dessert at Bible Study last night! Yaay!

Breakfast-- eggs, bacon, potato.

Lunch (late, about 3:30 pm) salmon, salad, lemon-parsley dressing, a few pecans on salad

Dinner-- herb tea w co, pkg of TJ's kale chips, taste of DH's carnitas

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-12T06:58:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57706>

Slept better last night bc, i think because I added a light blanket. My legs didn't ache so much. Went to B&N, looked at HUGE fish cookbooks. Couldn't believe how tasty some of the recipes looked. Wonder if s&s can get in other varieties of fish/shellfish? Think tomorrow I will try Elachan's (sp) Mediterranean cod and add her recipe for wonton wrapper-less meatballs to the list of recipes to try.

Watched debate w DH saying "I wish I had some dessert. I wish I had some popcorn." :-(

Time to turn off electronics....

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-12T22:08:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-57836>

Again, last night, sleeping warmer helped the legs. Hard to get cold adapted when you like to be warm! Swelling in my legs and feet way down. Wonder if being much stricter on the no-gluten is the reason for reduced fluid retention?

Breakfast-- Mexican shrimp cocktail, not enough shrimp. Made Ealachan's roasted cod w tomato olive tapenade (used hake). Very good. Saved some for lunch, also roasted some shrimp for more cocktail, etc.

Lunch-- Mexican shrimp cocktail w cucumber, avocado. So good! So full!

Dinner-- spaghetti squash, pork, beef meatballs, my tomato sauce. K spending night. Hope he'll eat this. Made a few non seasoned shrimp for him to try again. Fingers crossed!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-17T09:02:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-58685>

I have got to have a plan for the weekends! Started out well, but then had two days of eating crackers with butter, then Cheez-its, then today fudge! Tonight was better, although I ate too much. Ground beef with mushrooms, onions, cheese on top, broccoli, prob the last of summer's tomatoes. At least I didn't feel any desire to snack after dinner. Tomorrow, shrimp, and if I need it, gr pork, for BAB. Stayed up too late watching tv w DH. Oh, no leg cramps last night after horrible ones previous two nights. Have no idea why or why not.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-17T20:30:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-58751>

Molliecat, I found a long time ago the best way to avoid temptation is to ban it from the house. If it isn't in the pantry, well, I can't eat it.

That's not to say I didn't used to tempt myself by strolling through the forbidden aisles at the store. But you know what? In the last year I have lost the need/desire to do even that.

You CAN conquer this. Baby steps and one at a time.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-18T11:09:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-58889>

Darleen, absolutely not having the stuff around is key for me also. Unfortunately, my husband is not totally on board with everything, & he bought the cheez-its when he was out with our grandson. I don't know why but I can stay away from this stuff during the week, but weekends are much harder for me. I am going to follow Mama Grok's advice, decide what I absolutely will not eat & crackers have to be on that list since they start me sliding down, down, down. I look forward to the day these foods don't hold any appeal for me.

Breakfast--6 oz gr pork, and a bunch of shrimp, homemade cocktail sauce ( sugar in the catsup & chili sauce), tea w co

Lunch-- no

Dinner-- brats, sauerkraut, 1 pear & some raw milk cheddar cheese.

Dessert at BS - no!

Snack -- 10 pm! Not good! Pecans & dark choc chips

Now up way too late reading on iPad, can't seem to wind down. Seems like if I don't go to bed when I first feel sleepy ( about 9 pm), then I get a "second wind" & then can't go to sleep. Swell! And I have to get up early tomorrow which is now today! Ugh!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-26T20:13:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-60344>

Okay, last 2-3 weeks not good. Back to a lot of my old habits, staying up late, too much blue light (computer, iPad, tv). Weather is cooler, so don't want cool showers, don't want to get up in am, don't want BAB, on & on. Only good thing is that swelling in lower legs & feet is still down, so thankful for that. Read reply Darleen posted in another journal about craving carbs when needing rest. I know this is an issue with me. Have to be really serious about light & get the decent sleep my body needs. Finally ordered the Unix (?) glasses, ordered a pair for DH also. Bought a bunch of battery operated candles at Costco. Think we'll get a bunch more for every room. They come with a remote, so very easy to turn on & off. K loved the "magic" of that! Maybe I'll get some for them too. Okay, it's 10:15 am, still haven't eaten breakfast, not off to good start, but will get back on the path.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-26T20:44:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-60346>

I love the idea of battery operated candles. I have to look into that. I sleep so much better when I turn all the light down at night, using candle light to read and relax. I don't use any computer or device after 7, that's my curfew.

You can do this, one thing at the time. I'd say the most important is to get your BAB. It also helps, with the sleep, to keep cravings at bay. I realise that it had become smaller lately, and might be one of the reasons I crave fruits. I will up it for a few days to see how it goes. Have a nice day!

### Endless — 2012-10-26T23:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-60360>

Have you tried taking a cold bath instead of a cold shower? I find showers much harder! With a bath you can sit in it up to your waist and if you feel ready you can immerse yourself a bit more. Just a thought.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T16:25:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-60553>

Your perspective matters a lot to your success. A boat only sinks when the water in the sea gets inside of it.......negativity is like that for a human brain. Once doubt or fear enter it sinks you.

Stop focusing on what you can do or what you struggle with and focus on what you can do well for you.

When you begin to do this you hear the magic music.........of health.

When you're happy you enjoy the music but when you're sad you understand the lyrics. Truth bomb alert.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-28T16:43:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-life.4182/post-60557>

Well said Dr K. That truth bomb is a keeper!

# Thread: Help please with understanding first labs!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-10-08T09:49:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56872>

Hi people,

I have recently had my first labs done and are having trouble understanding them. Please can anyone enlighten me? Lauren  I'm a 42 year old female... here goes:

Thyroid Antibodies: Anti-Tg antibodies 1 IU/mL (< 10)

Anti-TPO antibodies 8.0 )

Lipid tests: Cholesterol: 4.9 mmol/L HH

Triglyceride: 0.9 mmol/L

HDL Cholesterol 1.56 mmol/L

LDL cholesterol: 2.9 mmol/L HH

Chol/HDL ratio: 3.1

CRP: 10 mg/L ( < 5 ) H

Fasting glucose: 5.3 mmol/L ( 3.5 - 6.0 )

Sodium: 140 mmol/L ( 135 - 145 )

Potassium: 4.2 mmol/L ( 3.5 - 5.2 )

Creatinine: 67 umol/L ( 45 - 90 )

eGFR: 84 mL/min/1.73m2

Thank you to anyone that can help me to understand these.

### mambo — 2012-10-08T20:32:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56919>

Based on ferritin and CRP, you have inflammation. Do you have any thyroid symptoms? They look okay (unless you have symptoms) and you don't seem to have any Hashimoto's at least. BG numbers are good and lipids are excellent. Might want to spend some time to see where inflammation is coming from. If you post your height/weight, what your diet is like and what kind of symptoms you have, and what other issues you have, it will be easier for sleuthwork.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-08T20:46:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56928>

It appears there are no signs of Hashimotos. But you have some thyroid issue presenting in the labs. Both the FT3 and FT4 are low and should be near the top of the range. You should try to get to the root of what is causing this. Iodine (read the iodine thread if you haven't already) and lots of seafood and seaweed will help. I would also suggest getting a full hormone panel and an ASI test to see what is going on there. I would guess you are low in progesterone and maybe even pregnenolone.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-08T23:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56949>

Amber, you are getting great responses already (Patty Cakes is a thyroid expert... she totally knows her stuff). The one other thing I see is Vitamin D is very low. Remember, these units of measurement are not the ones used by Dr Kruse, so your Vitamin D translates into 28 in the units Americans reference. This is very low, even coming out of winter. Maybe think about some serious supplementation and a lot of naked sunbathing. Also, I'd suggest re-testing in January which still gives you a month of summer if you haven't raised your levels enough (they need to be high going into winter) and you can bump up your supplementation if that's the case.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-08T23:07:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56951>

PS re: your lipid panel, here are the converted figures:

Total 189.5

LDL 112

HDL 60

trigs 80

From this, I'd say your trigs are high and your overall cholesterol is lowish (many recommend to have total cholesterol above 200 for optimal hormone generation and health). As you get inflammation down (and tighten diet) you should see trigs come down and HDL rise I'd think. Others know more about cholesterol than I do.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-08T23:20:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56953>

Thank you Mambo and Patty Cakes.

Thyroid symptoms would be excess weight for one? That is me... my height is 5 foot 6, and I weigh 251 pounds, my diet - I only started paleo about a month ago and before that I had grains but have been gluten free for 2 years. I grow my own veges and eat them in season, eat meat from the farm (we live on a farm in New Zealand), but also eat a lot of chicken etc. I thought I ate a healthy diet, but obviously it wasn't that healthy at all being so overweight and having inflammation and thyroid issues. I'm endeavering from now on to start eating much more seafood. So with my second set of labs I'm hoping to see some changes. When should I do my second set of labs? I'll be adding in the full hormonal panel next time too. I started HCG yesterday to boost the weight loss and then I'll be going straight into leptin reset as soon as I come off of it. I met with KiwiLauren at the weekend who kindly answered many questions for me and connected lots of dots, but I have SOOO much to learn here.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-08T23:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56954>

great info from Patty and Lauren

I'd add that your LDL is a bit low you need LDL to make hormones.... and you T3 to convert it to pregnenolone... If you can get a full hormone pannel...that will help us fill in the blanks.

FWIW - my trigs go up when I add seasonal carbs.... and drop when they're out of my diet... you might not be ready for a lot of carbs.... My trigs in the winter run in the range of 34...in the summer they jump up to 56. yes your trigs could be better... you really want them = to or < your HDL.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-09T00:58:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56983>

Following what Gretchen said, Amber... if you can get even basic hormone info (so get doc to run blood tests for E2, P, DHEA-S and T (and anything else they can order... I know you can get SHBG (I did), maybe others - you can ask for pregnenolone, too) that will be really helpful. These should be drawn on day 20/21 of your cycle and with a doctor's script you can get them through MedLab. When you do this, get them to give you an hsCRP (not just CRP). Since you're doing HCG, I have NO idea when you should draw labs or if that affects things... perhaps someone on the HCG thread will know.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T01:33:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-please-with-understanding-first-labs.4185/post-56992>

If you can afford testing do it every quarter then you can track seasonal changes which will help if you are eating seasonally. That includes a full hormone panel like Lauren referenced.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-09T12:14:32+0300

# Thread: Nobel Winners Unlocked Cells' Unlimited Potential
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential.4190/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-08T19:01:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential.4190/post-56905>

[http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/10/08/162496684/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential](http://http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/10/08/162496684/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential)

> by Michaeleen Doucleff
>
>
>
> 09:05 am
>
>
>
> October 8, 2012
>
>
>
> Twitter (25)
>
> Facebook (104)
>
> E-mail
>
> Share
>
> Print
>
> Comments (11)
>
>
>
>
>
> John B. Gurdon (left) and Shinya Yamanaka will share the prize, worth about $1.2 million.
>
> AFP/Getty Images
>
>
>
> John B. Gurdon (left) and Shinya Yamanaka will share the prize, worth about $1.2 million.
>
>
>
> The two scientists who won this year's Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine discovered that cells in our body have the remarkable ability to reinvent themselves. They found that every cell in the human body, from our skin and bones to our heart and brain, can be coaxed into forming any other cell.
>
>
>
> The process is called reprogramming, and its potential for new drugs and therapies is vast. If neurons or heart cells are damaged by disease or aging, then cells from the skin or blood potentially could be induced to reprogram themselves and repair the damaged tissue.
>
>
>
> The winners — John Gurdon of the Gurdon Institute in Cambridge, England, and Shinya Yamanaka of Kyoto University in Japan and the Gladstone Institute in San Francisco — made their discoveries more than 40 years apart.
>
>
>
>
>
> In 1962, Gurdon proved that a cell from a frog's stomach contained the entire blueprint to make a whole frog. When he took the cell's nucleus and popped it into a frog egg, the egg developed normally into a whole frog.
>
>
>
> This method eventually was used to clone all sorts of animals, including cats, dogs, horses and, most famously, Dolly the sheep — the first mammal cloned from an adult cell. Gurdon, 79, continues to study reprogramming and was working in his lab when he received the call from the Nobel committee.
>
>
>
> But a major obstacle stood in the way of further development of these stem cells: Getting the frog's stomach cell to strip away its specialization and turn into one of the 200 or so cell types known to exist in animals always required the use of an egg.
>
> A question hung over the field for decades: Could a specialized cell reprogram itself all on its own?
>
> Shinya Yamanaka discovered a simple recipe for converting skin from a patient into many different cell types, including nerve and heart cells.
>
> Mattias Karlen/Courtesy of The Nobel Committee for Physiology or Medicine
>
>
>
> Shinya Yamanaka discovered a simple recipe for converting skin from a patient into many different cell types, including nerve and heart cells.
>
>
>
> **In 2006, Yamanaka and graduate student Kazutoshi Takahashi found the answer, and it sent shockwaves through biology and medicine. They demonstrated that any cell could be reset and induced to develop into another cell type. And, even more remarkably, that it took little to get the job done.
>
>
>
> Yamanaka and Takahashi took a few cells from the skin of a mouse and then switched on four specific genes. After a few days, the cells stopped looking like skin cells and starting looking more like embryonic stem cells. That meant the cells could then be manipulated to develop into just about any cell Yamanaka wanted. He could even get the cells to form heart muscle that beat inside a Petri dish.
>
>
>
> Yamanaka, 50, had essentially unlocked a cellular genie in the bottle. The discovery meant that a cell wasn't necessarily trapped in its fate to be, say, a skin, liver or heart cell. Rather, with the right instructions, a cell had the ability to strip away its specialization and become whatever cell the scientists chose. They called these new cells "induced pluripotent stem cells.**"

# Thread: Truly the brain does HEAL in Ketosis!  Testimony of a Two Year old!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Momma28 — 2012-10-09T02:50:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57002>

The story begins in January of this year when I am given custody of a 16 month old nephew lost to the system by a drug abusing, alcoholic mother. I'd only seen him once before this. He made no eye contact or any attempts at physical connection. He had jerky, palsy-like movements and seizures intermittently throughout the day. He weighed 18 pounds and was 28 inches tall; just 46th percentile on the growth charts. He would only eat bananas, white bread toast and commercial milk that was being microwaved and put in a bottle, oh, and of course, soda. He had by this time been given 22 vaccines.

His first week here I let him choose what he would eat to ease him in with the other 6 kids and let him acclimate. The second week it was on....nothing was offered but what I wanted him to eat. Three days later and lots of tantrums and shed tears and he was eating meats for the first time in his life. He was given watered down Welch's with D3, Iodine, a multivit liquid and probiotics in it throughout the day. He was also given a B12 transdermal patch. His diet was fresh veggies all loaded with organic butter or bacon fat. Meats were offered with each meal and he soon grew to love them. All seizures halted within the first week most likely due to lack of dietary fats. His palsy-like movements have gradually ceased as well and have become much more normal. In fact, his balance has more than improved, its incredible.

It took him 6 weeks to learn his name. Since then, he has learned so much more...body parts, animals, fruits and veggie names and songs. His cognitive function, that was about an 8 month old, is now just slightly behind his 2 years that he turned last week. He has been on a ketogenic diet for 8 full months now and today is 30 (77th%) pounds and 34 (87th%)inches tall. He has grown 6 inches and gained 12 pounds in 8 months! Unheard of! He makes eye contact, responds to facial expressions, simple commands and best of all, gives and receives LOVE! It is true...a ketogenic diet can heal the brain, even from something most docs would call AUTISM.

### kathylu — 2012-10-09T02:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57006>

I can only bow my head in respect and awe...thank you!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57021>

Made my heart feel full.

### Adria — 2012-10-09T19:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57117>

Momma28 what a smart and loving mother you are. Now your nephew has a priceless gift.. of a new life..so wonderful! Please keep us updated on his progress.

### Momma28 — 2012-10-10T00:12:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57174>

Thank you all for your words of encouragement...they mean so much. This little fella has had a hard first months of life. He continues to improve on the irrational fears that must have been part of his trauma. Ceiling fans and anything else that's spinning, in particular. We took the kids to the Dentzel Carousel last summer and he went bizerk. This was the first time we had seen him react in absolute terror. We use the desensitization technique with him to get him to recognize things he is afraid of as everyday objects or occurances. I turned the ceiling fans on all summer after that to get him used to the spinning above him. His other fear was the trash can. LOL He would get hysterical if you asked him to throw away his diaper. I was working with him on simple commands. We had to walk him to it, open the lid and have him release the diaper all with him in hysteria. After two months of screaming, he can now open the can and deposit it himself in triumphant victory! And he is so proud of himself when he does. He is really a very smart little boy. Most of these children are.

You all will get a kick out of this. Last night while reading him his little word dictionary that is full of colorful pictures in a to z order, we hit the B's. The word "breakfast" had pics of a bowl of cereal, pancakes and doughnuts...yeah. Then there was a pic of broccoli. I said, "oooh, I see something yummy to eat". At which he immediately reached for the broccoli and pretended to hungrily stuff it in his mouth. It was so cute. He didn't even know what the cereal or pancakes was. He has surprised me daily with what he knows even though he cannot talk yet. He is just now starting his first words and all of his wonderful baby babble!

### indigogirl — 2012-10-10T00:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57177>

Please keep us updated. This is so heartwarming and inspiring. Just makes me smile!

### Momma28 — 2012-10-10T00:27:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57180>

> **Adria;58419 wrote:** Momma28 what a smart and loving mother you are. Now your nephew has a priceless gift.. of a new life..so wonderful! Please keep us updated on his progress.

Adria,

What a sweetie you are. You would fit right in with the first seven kids....their names all start with "A". This new little fella's name is Jeramyah. I will certainly remember to update you all on his progress from time to time. He is a handful, but he brings great joy. I guess i learned to mother by what I lacked in one. I married and started a family, have homeschooled 22 years and in the process healed my own inner child by becoming the mother to her that I always wanted. I was very blessed to have married a man that allowed me that freedom and time to heal. My soul just delights in loving, healing and blessing children. Patty Cakes, I wish that I could take them all, but my house and my husband's wallet aren't big enough. For now, I am more than willing to continue to help other mothers in loving and healing theirs. I feel so blessed to have come across Dr. K's info and the wonderful people on this forum and to have a place to send these mothers so they can learn more and that there is now validity to what I'm telling them. This is the south and a Doctor's word is gold here, but without a credential they just look at me and walk away, though you have the answers to help their children and themselves.

### JoeBranca — 2012-10-11T00:49:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57458>

### indigogirl — 2012-10-09T03:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57023>

Can someone please explain gut microfilms?

When I google it I come up with stuff about German films!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-09T03:57:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57030>

think plaque on your teeth........it is the film that allows bacteria grow on it.......you got it in your gut too.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-09T16:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57096>

[http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html](http://http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html)

I liked reading this site. I still don't have a good solid grasp of the protocol though. Like how long to live with the diarrhea (I'm sure it's unique to each person). What signs to look for to tell you your films might be cleared out? And then there's the repopulation bit, which I think it pretty hormone dependant to achieve a good result - pesky hormones! I'll copy/paste what I wrote about it in the Hashimoto's thread:

_Jack talked in the last webinar about re-engineering the gut, and it seems to me, the starting place is getting a decent hormone profile, and then stripping out gut biofilms, and then re-seeding using the epcotx. He mentioned in passing about using Mg and/or coconut oil for "2-3 days" to remove existing biofilms. However, when I have done more reading about these biofilms, it seems they need far more treatment than just "a few days" of diarrhea. The bacteria create a polyssacharide (hard shell, I think) exterior and live happily underneath it. Antibiotics don't reach them in any measurable quantity, and they are very difficult to erradicate. I was reading the other night on another site about them (here: [http://coolinginflammation.blogspot....-diseases.html](http://http://coolinginflammation.blogspot....-diseases.html)). This author has a lot of similar ideas to Jack's, so of course I like a little consistency. But other places I've read about biofilm removal taking months (protease enzymes between meals can apparently do some decent work at cracking their shells).

The thing is, I think caution is needed, because without the right initial conditions after the clean-out (and this involves hormones, like the Pg/E2 ratio, for reasons I can't articulate, and would love if someone else could!, good cortisol profile, probably others), I would think you would cultivate something less than desireable.

So, these tidbits have been dropped, but without any real measurables (like how longshould you continue with diarrhea Mg loading? How do you know that they're cleared out and you're ready for the next step? Would CO be better, if once cleared you know it because the diarrhea stops - or does it stop?). BUt it seems imperative that hormones are step 1..._

### indigogirl — 2012-10-09T16:29:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57098>

Bigknitwit, I don't have any answers for you. I, too, would like more, specific info on how to do this. I do remember Jack saying ( during a Webinar or Q&A) that he had diarrhea for 3 weeks when clearing his gut microfilms.

### freesia — 2012-10-09T19:48:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57122>

If you google **Biofilms** you're likely to come up with a lot more. That's the most commonly used term to describe them.

I've done biofilm protocols over the past 4 years - destroying them can release a lot of stuff so a gentle approach (like Jacks) is highly recommended by me! Companies like Kirkmans and many others make formulas used by parent's treating their autistic children with a biomedical approach. Treating Biofilms can be very effective.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-10T02:20:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57205>

[http://bacteriality.com/2008/05/26/biofilm/](http://http://bacteriality.com/2008/05/26/biofilm/)

Super long read...

Talks about how biofilm bacteria can actually secrete something that binds to the VDR receptor to inactivate it, thus continuing to compromise the immune system...

### Momma28 — 2012-10-10T03:00:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57215>

The question to me is....do these bacteria enter the body through the teeth? Such as through the infection of root canals or decay under mercury amalgam? I have so many friends with serious health issues that have had one or more root canals and lots of AI issues.

Dr. Jerry Tennant says that these bacteria are dormant in the body until ph level drops low enough for the oxygen to fall within the tissues. They then wake up and begin to feed.....on us. They liquify nutrients in the cells and "drink" them up. Their waste products are actually what cause us to respond with inflammation. How the Body Works Part 2 on youtube.

Also, one of my friends who has suffered with fibromyalgia for years and has it well under control with a ketogenic diet underwent treatment with a Dr. Reezor out of Chicago by using something called Bellugi. This is supposedly an enzyme produced by a worm of this name. I will question her as to the results. It was supposed to clear biofilm. This was a year or so ago and had no clue what she was talking about back then. I'll keep every posted.

### indigogirl — 2012-10-10T03:29:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57221>

Momma28, you might be interested in this book: [http://books.google.com/books/about/Uninformed\_Consent.html?id=nNVpAAAAMAAJ](http://http://books.google.com/books/about/Uninformed_Consent.html?id=nNVpAAAAMAAJ)

"Uniformed Consent: The Hidden Dangers in Dental Care"

Dr. Huggins and Dr. Levy assert that a large number of disorders are, though often incurable, easily preventable. He proposes that multiple sclerosis, lupus, leukemia, chronic fatigue syndrome, Parkinson's disease, many mental disorders including Alzheimer's, and even major diseases like breast cancer are caused, in part, by the toxins we place in our bodies. Where do these toxins come from and how do they get into our bodies? You my be surprised to find that you have actually paid to have them put there. These dangerous materials--mercury, cadmium, beryllium, nickel, and others--are used in "everyday dentistry" to make up the fillings, root canals, and bridgework in our mouths, and are supposed to be "safe." But are they?

"Uninformed Consent" presents cases of toxic poisoning--of depressed immune systems and inexplicable illnesses--to toxins entering the bloodstream from the heavy metals in dental materials. The authors also discuss the hidden truths that the dental industry in America doesn't want to talk about, and the real reasons the dangers of these materials have been suppressed and ignored.

Drs. Huggins and Levy implore the reader: "Don't leave your health in your dentist's hands and assume that all will be fine. Become informed and take an active role in your health. Know what will be implanted in your mouth. You must decide at the outset what is more important to you--the life of a filling or "your" life." "Uninformed Consent" will give you the facts so that you may take responsibility for your dental--and complete--health and wellness.

### freesia — 2012-10-10T05:42:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57244>

> **indigogirl;58489 wrote:** Freesia, what kind of protocols have you done? How did you assess the results?

Hi Indigogirl.

I started a protocol using Kirkman's Biofilm defense (plus Vit C, bioflavinoids, activated charcoal and a few other things)...then I switched to something called Interfase Plus (Klaire Labs). The protocol crippled me! My understanding is that destroying biofilms too fast can lead to bacteria, their toxic byproducts and metals they're holding onto all spilling out into the gut. How well your gut is functioning, and the state of your general health will determine how well you cope with this. I had to step back the doses of everything but still really struggled with the protocol and I wouldn't know if it was a success. I guess what I determined was that I had a problem with biofilms?!

A year later I started with a more gentle approach using apple cider vinegar, coconut oil, occasional activated charcoal, and edta suppositories. I was doing the GAPS diet at the time so gut was in better shape I expect and I didn't have the same negative results, though fatigue and headaches increased temporarily. I followed the protocol for six months and I did improve over that time but it's impossible for me to assess how much any improvement was down to the biofilm treatment vs starting the GAPS diet.

Wish I could be more helpful!

PS should have clarified in my previous post that I have seen biofilm treatment mentioned by parents in my local biomed group as having had a very positive effect on their autistic children.

### chocolate — 2012-10-10T23:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57437>

I'm just trying to stay focused on the fact that bacteria need carbohydrates to build their homes with. If they don't have any, fresh, it seems that would be a positve.

# Thread: CT caused rapid weight gain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-09T13:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57077>

To start I am 32, female, and been paleo for +/- two years. Strictly so oct 2011-April 2012. 5'6", and at the start of CT, 160. Started leptin reset that all, about the time of the big ass thread at mad. BAB, etc. no weight loss seen, so I added CT.

I started with the face bath, the high fat/meal and ice water prior to, etc. When i moved into cold baths-45 min at about 58\*, things changed - gained 22 pounds of fat in two menstrual cycles. I stopped CT, suspecting that was the cause, as even while on 100% paleo, my weight was stable. (Another frustrating issue, but an aside, at this point.)

Fast forward to current, October 2013. The weight as not budged, though activity as increased dramatically (going from desk work to building fence and farm work-climbing bins, etc,) and per food journals intake is virtually identical.

I'm hovering at 182, which is far too heavy at my height. Walking an hour a day beyond what I do at work isn't changing anything, and its been 3.5 months since adding that.

Currently on 1.5 gr naturethroid (recently down from 4 grains) iodine, se, k2, a,d, bcomp, natural calm, DIM, 100 mg progesterone 12-25, and 10 mg DHEA. Suspect low estrogen and testosterone too, but had to start with a new doc who must reinvent the wheel since prev doc left practice.

In the interest of keeping this brief, I'm confused. I've followed instructions to a t, aside from quarterly labs, as I'm currently unemployed, with a mortgage AND a rent payment, (and oddly enough, the "recovery" hasn't hit my neck of the woods yet).

Suggestions on things to look at? I'm stumped, stress is lower than any point in th last 10 years even with the financial drought, and it doesn't appear to matter whether I eat 100% SAD or 100% paleo, the jeans fit the same. I feel better on paleo, which is th sole reason I'm sticking it out. I'm about ready to say to hell with it, however. Any suggestions of things to review/do differently would be most appreciated.

Laurel

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-09T14:30:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57088>

CT helps weight loss........if youre gaining you need a work up.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T18:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57108>

It sounds to me like you need a hormone panel, hs-CRP, full thyroid panel (TSH, FT3 and FT4 + antibody tests), and ASI at the very least. It sounds like you have a lot going on inside that only lab tests will reveal.

I'll tell you a little of my story as an example of weird loss/gain/loss that was easily explained in my labs. I lost 10# upon starting paleo. Started CT and BAM! 8 of those came right back on. I succumbed to the suggestions of getting labs. My hormone panel showed I was estrogen dominant after years of being low. I was quite surprised. I continued to CT to try and flush out the estrogen all the while doing other things to help reduce the high estrogen and supplementing my progesterone. I stopped CTing for three months in the summer and recently started back up. In the time period of stopping CT I lost about 5 of the eight I had gained back and it was S-L-O-W. After starting CT back up I am now dropping weight and within a couple of lbs. of my goal. This shows the weight gain and loss is not linear and can be attributed to more than CT. For me, CT was the catalyst that caused the weight gain while my body was trying to release that excess estrogen. Your labs will tell your story for you!

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-10T19:17:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57362>

All I have at this point are 24-hour urine labs, would be more than happy to throw them up for a second opinion if anyone is familiar with them. I have high 5alpha reductase due to a progesterone deficiency (as I understand it) so among other things, that is working against me.

Blood tests will have to wait until I can either find employment that pays, or sell my house, as right now I'm juggling bills, unfortunately. (My deep freezer died last week on top of everything, lost close to 250 pounds or more of good homegrown beef, pork, and elk, darn it. Thank heavens it's hunting season!)

Maybe in my next life i will get a body that reacts appropriately to what I do  in the meantime, however, maybe Ill add as much shellfish as I can, in the interest of nutrient density, until I can swing bloods.

Would it be prudent to restart CT as well, and tough it out, or just wait? Any thoughts? Thank you both!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-10T19:30:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57367>

Doing CT now or waiting is a contextual thing. Depends on your situation. Based on progesterone deficiency I would venture a guess that you are estrogen dominant, a club many of us belong to. This could be a partial reason for the weight gain. For me personally, I just went ahead and kept CTing. I'm not saying you do that but with my situation it seemed right at that time. Eating as much shellfish can only help. Do what you can because anything helps!

Sorry to hear about your lost meat. I would be so sad to loose the contents of our freezer....

# Thread: Speech therapy for Havoc
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/speech-therapy-for-havoc.4222/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-10-10T00:03:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/speech-therapy-for-havoc.4222/post-57172>

Havoc has been a bit behind in his speech/language development... we've been keeping an eye on this, and going through the different evaluation processes w/in our school district and through our pediatrician. LAst week she agreed its time to intervene.

Today Havoc had his speech evaluation... and we received confirmation his speech isn't where it should be. he scored 0% understand-ability on his speech.  his issues are also very different from most kids. he's mastered his vowels, (when he says a word its all vowels - very few consonants) he can't pronounce most of his consonant. & he'd probably pick up mandrin chinese pretty quickly!

however, the speech therapist has never completed an evaluation w/a 2&1/2 yo - at the speed in which he was moving, she actually had 2 hrs planned for his cognitive/speech eval and he finished it in 45 mins (Epi-Paleo anyone?) - she was astounded. He jumped right in to the eval and started yammering away, telling her what things were in his own words (even though she couldn't understand him and we understand about 10%) she was impressed w/the vocabulary he knows and can say/identify close to 150+ words as he hears them, even though he doesn't have recognizable speech.

The good news, his speech isn't cognitive or motor skill driven, its most likely impacted by his ear infections, and fluid in his ears, and how he uses his vocal cords. he starts speech therapy in 2 weeks. we're hoping that with the speech therapy, and management of his seasonal allergies - he'll get back up to where he should be, and he'll no longer be a frustrated little dude when he's trying to communicate, but we don't understand. we have home work to build him communication boards - that will help him communicate with us & dcp so he won't be frustrated while he comes up to speed

When she asked about foods, we replied what doesn't he eat.

### TheKid — 2012-10-12T14:38:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/speech-therapy-for-havoc.4222/post-57731>

Please keep us posted on the progress.

I can say my 21 month old is similar. She yammers away about a ton of stuff, and seems to understand us quite well (2 languages even). But she doesn't pronounce well and like to call things what she wants to call them.

She's younger than your child, so maybe by 2 1/2 that will work itself out. I hope.

I do know that if more than one language is spoken at home, onset of speech can be delayed.

we'll re run them again in Jan - to see where they're at. Chaos is who I'm worried about... If she drops w/supplementation, I'll be asking Jack -what we need to look at next... I'm worried she just might have some of the same Low T3 issues I have... her behaviour has tanked since the season/light changes really started to kick in. drop of the hat, she's crying, everything is setting her off etc... so we're watching it...

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-26T19:16:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/speech-therapy-for-havoc.4222/post-60336>

Havoc had his 1st speech therapy appt this week. It went really well. due to his delay, the speech therapist contacted FFX County Child Find program - b/c she thinks he needs additional sessions, and she wants to get the school district involved.

So I spoke w/the speech therapist who most likely will become Jacobs "special education teacher" and started the induction process. It looks like once we get the final report from our current ST - all I need to do is send in an electronic copy - to the school district ST -and then we'll schedule his sessions within the school district.

he's got an appointment w/ an allergist in 2wks, and then we'll probably follow up w/the pediatric otologists to make sure his ears are clear...

hoping we get him on track so we can start understanding him!

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T18:44:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/speech-therapy-for-havoc.4222/post-60596>

hi Gretchen.

+1 on Doidge's book as an intro.

Paul Bach-y-rita's work on sensory substitution is inspiring as well.

For example, when using accelerometer inputs to a woman's tongue to give her a sense of balance, her brain eventually managed to rewire past the bad signal to noise ratio in her compromised vestibular system and she no longer needed the accelerometer at all.

perhaps a similar approach would be worth a try, such as games that are used to teach deaf children to speak by using visual feedback of their voice.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-28T23:11:18+0200

+1 it's a great book and well worth reading.

Also probably worth investigating the sensitive issue that RiverNeighbor raised. The data on altered cortisol patterns and full-day non-parental care is pretty strong. (It's not an all-or-nothing choice, either... and there are daycare providers and situations that reduce this risk much better than others, so it's worth investigation IMO).

# Thread: Metformin help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-help.4224/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-10T06:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-help.4224/post-57251>

To all of you Metformin users, gurus and general smart folks, I have a question.

I'm trying to persuade my doc to write me a script for metformin. He says he's 'not opposed' but wants to know how I will evaluate that it's working. I do not have high fasting blood glucose (though have never checked it throughout the day). I do have a history of cancer and gut issues (and probably some early insulin resistance, though doubtful that's current). He wants me to convince him of a) how long of a trial I'd want and b) how I will know it's helping.

What is your advice?

### Destiny — 2012-10-10T13:17:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-help.4224/post-57284>

> **KiwiLauren;58557 wrote:** To all of you Metformin users, gurus and general smart folks, I have a question.
>
>
>
> I'm trying to persuade my doc to write me a script for metformin. He says he's 'not opposed' but wants to know how I will evaluate that it's working. I do not have high fasting blood glucose (though have never checked it throughout the day). I do have a history of cancer and gut issues (and probably some early insulin resistance, though doubtful that's current). He wants me to convince him of a) how long of a trial I'd want and b) how I will know it's helping.
>
>
>
> What is your advice?

Maybe this?

[http://cancerpreventionresearch.aacrjournals.org/content/3/9/1060.full](http://http://cancerpreventionresearch.aacrjournals.org/content/3/9/1060.full)

### kathylu — 2012-10-10T15:41:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin-help.4224/post-57297>

You could also start testing your blood glucose and give a diary to your doctor at the next visit (or just keep if for your own information). I'd suggest FBG, and 90 minutes post each meal to look for blood glucose spikes. This will also establish a baseline for you to compare to after you start the metformin. That way you will know if your BG is dropping too low in response.

The review article above says that even low doses of metformin (250 mg) reversed abnormal crypt cell formation in the colon (an epithelial tissue like the breast), showing anti-neoplastic activity.

### cjb — 2012-10-10T18:18:30+0300
