### [email redacted] — 2012-10-20T20:47:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59371>

> **differentstory;60913 wrote:** My post? Let's here it. I'm all ears.

Unless Jack has something to add other than a drive-by passive agressive comment (sitting in traffic and feeling annoyed maybe?). . .I say this:

We actually are not that far off. I threw out "yo yo dieter" as my term, but it was a lazy choice of words. I was operating under the Cavemam world view that of course anybody fat has tried to lose weight. . .at least anybody old enough and wise enough to be here! But of course, there are many people that have more reasons than YO YO diet. . .I guess what I \*meant\* was those that can't seem to lose weight doing 'tried and true' programs that work for other people.

I love you brought up the Taubes book. Mentally, that was a watershed moment for me. Prior, I had been stuck in my very deep rut of self-viewed failure for not being able to stop myself from eating volumes of bad food. For gods sake (my old world view) everyone else I knew was able to control themselves.

When in fact, it was that one teeny-tiny little seed that was planted, that blossomed and probably built a key neural pathway that aided in my re-invention. It was the simple idea of fat storage was my body keeping me alive and protecting me from something else going on. I wasn't getting fat because I was eating too much (which of course I was), but I was eating too much to support my fat which might have a different reason for being there.

I remember exactly what I was doing when I heard that (I was listening to an audio book and painting my sons room). Something clicked with that one idea - and the world has been different since.

I love opposing opinions, they force me to:

1\. Practice my objective muscle

2\. Think through my view

3\. Borrow and incorporate something I hadn't considered before - someone else's view/framework to add to my knowledgebase. Doesn't matter if you agree or disagree, understanding why someone has a particular perspective expands your own wisdom.

I am not sure about the hormone thing. I see it unfolding before my very eyes, and I'm not seeing what we all want to be true. What is unclear to me right now is what effects on your current body it will have. No argument on balancing them and being as healthy as possible. My question to explore is will it undo the harm (overfat) or will you have to make it worse before you make it better?

Will you need to get as healthy as possible. . .THEN take a few steps back on purpose to take care of some of the residual damage (over fat) and struggle a little more to get back to healthy?

I think that may be the answer for me. When I clean out a closet, the entire surrounding area looks like a bomb zone. But, when I'm done after all that hard work - it's container store photo ready. I think I may have to suck it up and face a closet cleaning day.

### differentstory — 2012-10-20T21:39:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59379>

Okay. We are on the same page then. I just took "yo yo" dieter to mean maybe extreme starvtion, mini thins and ED and i think hcg is for more than that.

Funny about your apocoleptic moment with Taubes. I had the very same moment and that has changed my whole life (while painting my sons bedroom - just kidding).

I dont know about the hormome thing either. I didnt mean to insinuate that I did. Only that I think my weight and hormone problems are related (for me) and it makes me wonder if it similar for others (my mothers family which has really struggled with weight and mental health issues for years).

i do find it curious based on old journals, that i have a higher sense of well being, joy and positivity when i have been thinner.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-20T21:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59380>

> **differentstory;60924 wrote:** Okay. We are on the same page then. I just took "yo yo" dieter to mean maybe extreme starvtion, mini thins and ED and i think hcg is for more than that.
>
>
>
> Funny about your apocoleptic moment with Taubes. I had the very same moment and that has changed my whole life (while painting my sons bedroom - just kidding).
>
>
>
> I dont know about the hormome thing either. I didnt mean to insinuate that I did. Only that I think my weight and hormone problems are related (for me) and it makes me wonder if it similar for others (my mothers family which has really struggled with weight and mental health issues for years).
>
>
>
> i do find it curious based on old journals, that i have a higher sense of well being, joy and positivity when i have been thinner.

I think this is a relevant factor - clearly that pharmacy in our heads is somehow effected by the outside world. Think about when you have a crush, and how you feel around them etc. Same thing with seeing a good old friend. Same thing (in a negative way) when you see someone you D R E A D.

One could argue that still, it's all things generated inside yourself and you just need to harness that. Having a child changed that for me. Any parent knows the sheer happy making chemicals just a hug or unsolicited smile from ones offspring can bring. I do think that others can and do influence us. . . thus when you are seen by the world as "lesser" and treated as such, how can that NOT make you feel bad.

The world simply reacts differently to better looking people. Fatter people are seen as less healthy, thus the tribe shuns them. It's really a viscious circle.

I to am genuinely happier when I'm feeling like my looks "fit in". It's seen over and over again in the human condition, like with wealth.

Once you have a baseline of met needs, then going over that doesn't necessarily translate into a happier state of being. I think the same thing for how you look. When you are in a state of being comfortable interacting with the world, then your baseline is met - and there isn't the same happy return on investment with being better looking.

Believe this - super wealthy people are no happier for their wealth than an average person, yet they are happier for their wealth than an impoverished one.

You could also say a super good looking person is no happier than an average looking person, yet they are happier because of their looks than someone very ugly.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-21T23:18:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59521>

i agree with you, differentstory. I LOVE hcg. Love it, love it. I tell everyone about it. It's changed my life. I could not lose weight any other way. No weight watchers, not low carb, not paleo, nothing else worked for me. You're right that hormonal problems are underlying all our problems. HCG fixes some stuff there. Then when you go forward and eat a good paleo diet, you can keep the weight off as long as you get your hormones in order.

I tend to see 3 problems when people regain. Either they go back to eating crappy food, or their hormones are messed up or they have a food sensitivity and keep eating that item (usually dairy if they are not eating grains.)

### colleencoble — 2012-10-21T23:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59522>

differentstory, on your dose, let's talk about it a bit. Have you skipped a day in the last 2 days? If so, how did you feel in the evening? If you haven't, go ahead and do that and examine how you feel after 3-5.

You may need to go up just a tad or down just a bit. And make sure you're not mistaking an empty feeling for hunger. I actually love that empty feeling.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-21T23:27:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59525>

vkiernan, I don't recommend hcg if you have less than 10 pounds to lose. It's the bomb if you have anything over 15 pounds to lose though. But you have to stay on it a minimum of 23 days and 30 is better. And you lose so quickly that you have to adjust things after the first week or 10 days.

You might take a look at Nora Gedgaudas diet which is pretty keto. That often works when you just need to lose a bit. Or try some intermittent fasting. If you read Pounds and Inches, the steak day Dr. Simeons recommends is basically intermittent fasting. You skip breakfast and lunch, then eat the biggest steak you can get down with an apple or a tomato. And you drink plenty of fluids that day. It often results in a loss of about 3 pounds. I know some people who have done 1 a week and lost just those few aggravating pounds.

I would get your hormones looked at if I were you though. Since you had to be on HC, you've got some other problems for sure.

### differentstory — 2012-10-23T00:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59722>

Thanks, Colleen for the advice.

My HCG life has been a bit of a roller coaster the last 3 days. On Friday, I threw the HCG in my purse after injection. Why? Who knows. Went to look for it Saturday and it took me over an hour to finally find it in my purse. Ugh. So I had to mix a new amount and now that I understand your mixing instructions better, I did it your way with more fluid. I also decided to go up 10iu to see if that would help. But. . .that was probably one too many changes. I totally wasn't thinking and injected 175iu instead of 135. Oops. Actually didn't cause much of a difference, but I did drop down to 135iu the next day (Sunday).

Then on Saturday, we made chili (tomatoes, broth, tomoto paste and spices), but something was wrong. After I ate it, I was uncomfortably full and sure enough the next day I was up 3 pounds. ( So on Sunday, no chili, but I used the same meat in a broth soup and the meat had been seasoned pre-chili. I ended up feeling the same full feeling again! So it's got to be the spices (chili powder, garlic salt, onion powder). I'm worried they had MSG. They were from an asian grocery store. All other ingredients were from Whole Foods.

Today, I'm swearing off all spices and eating clean so back to feeling mildly hungry. I've been on 135iu for 2 days. Thinking I should try 150iu and see if it gets better. I am slightly less hungry at 135iu than 125iu. Hungrier in the morning than at night.

I was very keto for awhile and it didn't make a bit of difference. I am truly thinking I wasn't on enough progesterone cream to knock out my estrogen but my doc doesn't buy it. Actually, she told me to exercise. 5 lbs in a month and exercise is going to fix it! What was your experience with progesterone initially?

Sorry K. to be hacking your thread.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-23T02:34:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59754>

I love progesterone. Totally changed my life.  No migraines now. I started feeling better pretty quickly on it. The swelling went out of my legs too. Great stuff!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-23T17:42:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59824>

> **differentstory;61307 wrote:** Hmmmmmn. . .need to switch to apples. Very bored with oranges. On another topic, is it normal to see my sex life go from 0 to 100??? I am out of control. My husband doesn't know what's gotten in to me.

it's not unusual no. enjoy!

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-04T16:24:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/name-five-things-you-would-do.4258/post-61974>

Today I'm dragging out the old college transcripts, my what a Bon-Vivant-Polymath experience my education has been. I currently have degrees or almost degrees or licenses (I'm a learner not a rule follower).

Trombone - full on degree in trombone playing. WTF was I thinking!

Music Education - full on degree, I got this after having the WTF was I thinking regarding trombone. I loved marching band and drill writing. To this day the only "sport" I can stomach is marching band exhibitions.

Philosophy

Physical Science

Chorale

Physics - not even close to a degree more of a set of classes with mediocre grades, I couldn't do maths

Art History

Elementary Education

Sociology

Real Estate

I did spend almost 9 years in school - not graduating. Until the financing plugged got pulled. Current day Cavemam would be outraged at her waste of tax $!

Anywhoo, I digress. . .it relates to this thread as the REASON I'm going down this memory lane is because I am going to take my first class in the spring towards some kind of medical thingy. This class could towards Paramedic or Nurse. I have had many of the classes before (25 yrs ago!) so I'm not sure what they will "accept" as completed so I'm not sure WHAT class will be my starter.

I'm being a bit of a princess on it, I don't want to take the night class or early class for circadian rythms - and I don't want to take one of the long ones because I do need to do my paying work, too.

### Huck — 2012-11-04T16:38:30+0200

Buy an RV {travel trailer) and spend half the year traveling.

Develop a suite of educational programs I designed in my mind about 10 years ago.

Go fishing on the Outer Banks every fall that I am physically able to.

Find a fantastic woman to travel with me.

Make $500/month on the Internet to cover entertainment expenses while traveling.

### AmyK — 2012-11-04T18:06:45+0200

# Thread: Anyone ever gain weight on Leptin Rx?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-gain-weight-on-leptin-rx.4281/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-10-13T00:24:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-gain-weight-on-leptin-rx.4281/post-57887>

So I am doing the Leptin Rx and have gained 5 lbs in 5 weeks. No biggie in a way, but I wonder if I am eating too much BAB? That is the one area where I am eating much more (don't usually eat 50g protein!) than before. No snacking, and I did have one screw up when I was out and left my protein part of dinner home (sardines) and had to do nut butter instead and got hungry at 11pm (UGH).

I'm sure it's hormone related, and yes, I'm waiting for Dr K to tell me what labs to get from my email consult, but I wondered if this has happened to anyone else....and if so, did you change anything at all diet wise? I wonder if I should lower my BAB to maybe 40g protein?

I'm now at 155lbs and 68 inches tall....

Thanks in advance!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-13T01:00:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-gain-weight-on-leptin-rx.4281/post-57902>

email consult????? I have not gotten a request for on in a long time.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-13T02:10:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-gain-weight-on-leptin-rx.4281/post-57910>

Hm....I submitted my history form to the email provided ([email redacted]) yesterday (this would be my first one as I just discovered you in August!) based on my membership etc. I did get a message received back....but nothing else (though I hadn't expected anything since it's been so little time).

Maybe it's called something else? It's the thing where you tell the client what labs to get. My brain is a little fried.

Is there a way for me to figure out if I should have done something else?

I sure hope it didn't get LOST! I am eagerly awaiting your response!!! so I can get pricked and all that and finally talk to you in a Skype consult after I get results!!!

### caroline — 2012-10-15T06:06:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-ever-gain-weight-on-leptin-rx.4281/post-58252>

> **hope2conceive;59208 wrote:** So I am doing the Leptin Rx and have gained 5 lbs in 5 weeks. No biggie in a way, but I wonder if I am eating too much BAB? That is the one area where I am eating much more (don't usually eat 50g protein!) than before. No snacking, and I did have one screw up when I was out and left my protein part of dinner home (sardines) and had to do nut butter instead and got hungry at 11pm (UGH).
>
>
>
> I'm sure it's hormone related, and yes, I'm waiting for Dr K to tell me what labs to get from my email consult, but I wondered if this has happened to anyone else....and if so, did you change anything at all diet wise? I wonder if I should lower my BAB to maybe 40g protein?
>
>
>
> I'm now at 155lbs and 68 inches tall....".k
>
>
>
> Thanks in advance!

I did initially gain weight too although I was eating a much bigger BAB. About 60 -70 g. But over the course of a couple of months I went down 2 sizes. I didn't even notice until my clothes started falling off - literally!! So I weight 2 k more but am 2 sizes smaller. Thanks Dr. K

you can have some carbs but if you blood sugar collapses after eating that much protein you need to immediately go re read \[BL\]Leptin part deux\[/B\].......because your liver is not working well and you likely are leptin resistant at this organ level.

Thanks. Printing and going into my learning binder. It appears I should start at the beginning and read everything forward not work myself backwards....

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-13T13:31:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-part-deux-anyone-know-which-blogpost-this-is.4283/post-57936>

I Re-read leptin part deux. Will be interesting to see what the next set of labs show at the end of this month. The breakfast before my bonk this morning was 4 free range eggs scrambled in grass fed butter, 2 scoops of whey protein, a handfull of walnuts and a cup of green tea bulletproof style with grass fed butter and MCT oil. Wonder if there was an insulin response to the whey that dropped the blood sugar.

### Jude — 2012-10-15T05:30:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-part-deux-anyone-know-which-blogpost-this-is.4283/post-58249>

> **T Field;59261 wrote:** I Re-read leptin part deux. Will be interesting to see what the next set of labs show at the end of this month. The breakfast before my bonk this morning was 4 free range eggs scrambled in grass fed butter, 2 scoops of whey protein, a handfull of walnuts and a cup of green tea bulletproof style with grass fed butter and MCT oil. Wonder if there was an insulin response to the whey that dropped the blood sugar.

Just my 2 cents worth-should your breakfast selection include whey protein and walnuts? We found during our reset and the during the 4mths following, that we needed to basically keep with a reset ideology. So, large BAB with all kinds of protein/fat, & eggs always with a protein- eggs solo just didn't provide the sustinance between meals that Dr K recommends we achieve.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-15T06:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-part-deux-anyone-know-which-blogpost-this-is.4283/post-58254>

Eggs 6 gm protein each for 24 gms of protein, scrambled in 2 tbsp Kerrygold butter. Whey protein 19 gm/scoop for a total of 38 grams of protein. for a total of 62 gm of protein. Not counting the walnuts. Nuts to move the 06:03 ratio back towards 4:1. Also a cup of green tea with 1 tbsp of MCT oil and 2 tbsp butter. Seems like enough fat and protein to me.

### Jude — 2012-10-15T06:56:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-part-deux-anyone-know-which-blogpost-this-is.4283/post-58264>

cycloset

I've been taking cycloset immediately upon awakening per suggestion. I noted a pre lunch glucose of 77. This morning I nearly collapsed in my first case. Glucose after a can of oj was 84. Breakfast is usually 50 to 70 gm of protein and

fat. What about carbs for breakfast?,

10-13-2012, 05:55 AM #2

Jack Kruse

Administrator

you can have some carbs but if you blood sugar collapses after eating that much protein you need to immediately go re read Leptin part deux.......because your liver is not working well and you likely are leptin resistant at this organ level.

OK. Just because I'm intrigued. How do you explain your collapse? and what measures have you/are you, going to implement to avoid that happening again?

### BenG — 2012-10-15T08:33:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-part-deux-anyone-know-which-blogpost-this-is.4283/post-58270>

I had signs of fatty liver, raised alt levels, etc. after 3-6 months of LRx, everything normalized.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-15T21:19:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-part-deux-anyone-know-which-blogpost-this-is.4283/post-58386>

My guess is that I had an exaggerated insulin response to the whey protein. I'm going to pick up a glucose monitor and repeat the experiment with the same meal and see what happens to the BG. I'm not at all symptomatic at 77 or 84, so I suspect it went much lower. Breakfast is usually eggs and bacon, eggs and smoked salmon, left over fish, etc. Whey came into play because the fridge was empty.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-part-deux-anyone-know-which-blogpost-this-is.4283/post-58450>

# Thread: Dd (3.5) seems to be very cold adapted...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dd-3-5-seems-to-be-very-cold-adapted.4286/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-10-13T02:33:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dd-3-5-seems-to-be-very-cold-adapted.4286/post-57914>

Here I am shivering all day long in my cooling house (65 in here today) wearing an extra sweatshirt, and my youngest has been walking around for days still insisting on wearing her beloved sundresses. Today's high was 53 and it was windy. She spent lots of time outside like this, barefoot. Then she comes in and doesn't complain about being cold (because she isn't), and goes to play in the (unheated, window open) sunroom with her dolls for great lengths of time (still wearing only the sundress). How did she so effortlessly achieve what I've been striving for (and not achieved, at least not like this!), for months??! At night (she still sleeps with me), I keep the window open and try to minimize my covers (it's not going well so far, and I try to prioritize sleep over cold for the time being), and I spend a great deal of time fighting the covers back on because she's kicking them off! Maybe her Armour dose is too high, but I don't think so (she's up to 1.5 grains now)...

Seriously, how did this happen so transparently for her? I have NOT been pushing any of this, I just haven't been blocking it (insisting on a coat, etc). Because she's so young? She really does seem to be seeking the cold (my other kids aren't, and have varying degrees of tolerance). Oh, and when the sun goes down, she asks to go to bed... To be young again!!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-13T02:59:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dd-3-5-seems-to-be-very-cold-adapted.4286/post-57919>

she has a normal HPA axis and momma dont. Pretty simple

### AKMan — 2012-10-13T05:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dd-3-5-seems-to-be-very-cold-adapted.4286/post-57930>

How long has 3-1/2 year old daughter been on Armour? Just curious.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-13T14:33:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dd-3-5-seems-to-be-very-cold-adapted.4286/post-57938>

She's been on it since February - so 9 months. She started acting not quite right - a little more cranky, a little more tired than usual. I had her full thyroid panel done, including antibodies. She was positive for TpO antibodies, and her thyroid panel showed reduced output.

> **AKMan;59253 wrote:** How long has 3-1/2 year old daughter been on Armour? Just curious.

### AKMan — 2012-10-13T17:59:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dd-3-5-seems-to-be-very-cold-adapted.4286/post-57963>

> **bigknitwit;59263 wrote:** She's been on it since February - so 9 months. She started acting not quite right - a little more cranky, a little more tired than usual. I had her full thyroid panel done, including antibodies. She was positive for TpO antibodies, and her thyroid panel showed reduced output.

That's scary! Good thing she has you as a mom! Wonder what Jack's take is on a kid having those thyroid disorders?

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-13T21:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dd-3-5-seems-to-be-very-cold-adapted.4286/post-57987>

> **AKMan;59289 wrote:** That's scary! Good thing she has you as a mom! Wonder what Jack's take is on a kid having those thyroid disorders?

It's factor X, playing out in my own family  I was diagnosed at age 23, my father was diagnosed in his 50s, and my mother had Grave's in her 30s. And then it switches over to the paternal side of my mother's family. Her father had Graves and had a thyroidectomy in his 20s. Earlier than him, I'm not sure...

Now the question is, how will her own children (if she's able to have them) fare?

# Thread: Excema - 8 year old daughter - need HELP!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-10-13T12:26:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-57935>

My 8 year old daughter has had excema since she was a baby. She is the youngest of 4, and I had a water birth with her. She is not vaccinated, she is gluten free and we live a pretty healthy lifestyle (or so I thought until I discovered this site). I have tried so many things to rid her excema. It settles down in winter, but in late winter/early spring it comes back with a vengeance coinciding with the pollen from the trees. I have just put her on a paleo diet in the last 2 weeks, and in the first few days it improved rapidly, but then stopped improving and then got slightly worse again. I just don't know where to go next with this. She is determined herself to clear it up and has been very strict paleo, always asking me what she can and can't have.

Can anyone make any suggestions?

### kathylu — 2012-10-13T14:28:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-57937>

This suggestion won't help the underlying cause, but might help the symptoms a bit. Try rubbing coconut oil (I use organic refined because I don't enjoy the coconut smell from virgin coconut oil) on any irritated areas several times daily. It seems to be anti-inflammatory. I've used it on several people, myself included, for eczema and the problem clears up within a few days. Works well for diaper rash and mild yeast infections too.

I think you are on the right track by looking for dietary triggers.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-13T15:45:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-57946>

she has an imbalance of 03/6's Likely a low LA and DHA level. Testing and replacement with diet.

However, you can start giving her lots of seafood and fish asap (and get rid of all the chicken... I'm pretty sure you said kids don't eat any grains or industrial oils (canola, soy, seed oils, etc)... but make sure those are gone, too). Good luck!

### PrimalPam — 2012-10-14T05:41:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-58041>

I had nasty excema that is completely gone now, having rid myself of the above. That, and adding coconut oil daily to my diet, plus following all the other epi-paleo protocols. But the very first thing to go when I first rid myself of all grains and sugars was the excema. It flares up if I "cheat".

### nonchalant — 2012-10-17T21:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-58775>

I've had a rash on the back of my right hand for over 6 months. I'm not too familiar with excema, but when it gets puffy, red, scaly, and dandruffy, I think it's excema. I used coconut oil for 5 months but it didn't really help much. I changed to O6 oils, like olive oil and grape seed oil, and it helped dramatically, reducing the redness and scale and flakes. But it didn't heal all the way, and stopped improving after a week or so.

Recently I vacationed at the beach, and often went in the water. The next morning I noticed my hand wasn't itching. It looked pretty good. We left the coast, and I figured that soaking in epsom salts would also help. I'm not sure that they did, however. I stopped after a couple days of intermittent soaking.

Anyway, it's been about a week since we were at the coast, and my hand looks really good. I'm continuing the olive oil, and all the scabs have healed and dropped off. The skin is light-colored, not tanned, but it is smooth and only thick in a couple spots at the knuckles.

If my hand doesn't completely heal, I may try soaking in some sea salt water next, made with filtered water. And maybe with some seaweed added.  Hope this helps.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-17T23:55:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-58810>

Shijin - I wondered about detoxing, am making her drink more water from today to try and flush it out.

Seahorse - her excema was much worse before I gave her the chowder

Nonchalant - interestingly her excema shows much improvement when she swims in chlorinated water?! Sea salt would probably be really good - we live an hour from the sea though, might take her for a dip and see what happens. Can children do CT?

### nonchalant — 2012-10-18T00:09:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-58824>

Children can definitely do CT, but tossing someone in the Spring ocean without preparation might be a bit drastic. She could wade in the water as far as she wants to go out, and then you could check her legs for improvement. I wish I were only an hour away from the ocean!

I remember when my children were playing in a mountain stream. They had a fabulous time moving rocks to make a dam. I had to practically pry them from the water, after a couple hours of play and after I noticed their lips were turning blue!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-18T00:09:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-58825>

> **Amber Tyrrell;60347 wrote:** Shijin - I wondered about detoxing, am making her drink more water from today to try and flush it out.
>
>
>
> Seahorse - her excema was much worse before I gave her the chowder
>
>
>
> Nonchalant - interestingly her excema shows much improvement when she swims in chlorinated water?! Sea salt would probably be really good - we live an hour from the sea though, might take her for a dip and see what happens. Can children do CT?

Absolutely children can do CT. I had mine at the beach in winter!

Amber, I think this is an area where many variables are connected. You address one thing via changing diet and other things change, too. If it's skin we're talking about, the gut is probably also centrally involved. Everyone has detox in different ways. Everyone heals at different speeds. The problem is that she is a child and it may be hard to tolerate things getting worse before they are better. Perhaps instead of telling her you 'know' what the problem is and this 'will' fix it, treat it like an experiment. Give it x amount of weeks with a certain protocol and see what happens.

Re: diet, it's not just eliminating things, but it's also make sure she has certain things in her diet (eg lots of good fat). Maybe she could have a version of BAB (my kids typically do) with lots of egg yolks and bacon/meat and some sort of shake/sauce with coconut oil (good for the gut and good fat for the body/brain). I sometimes make my kids a version of Dr K's smoothie (which, since we're going into warmer weather, would be great)... 3 egg yolks, a couple of Tablespoons of barely melted (so it's not too hot) coconut oil, coconut cream (if you do dairy, she could use organic dairy cream, but I wouldn't do milk), cinnamon, some cocoa powder (unsweetened) if she wants it chocolatey, and some organic berries or even a frozen banana (this makes it really creamy). Blend it up and serve.

If you search Chris Kresser's site/podcasts, he has some good info just on excema, too.

Also, get her out in the sun as much as she can be.

Last bit of advice (from a mother who knows this reaction well), try not to panic. In some ways, her body is still showing you that it's not happy with the external signs of a skin issue. I think many of us were children with skin issues that 'went away' over time because they went underground.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-18T02:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excema-8-year-old-daughter-need-help.4289/post-58845>

Amber, look at this comment from Dr Kruse from the "I need help with eczema' thread"... (there might be other good info in there for you, too; btw my kids both eat one organic Brazil nut every morning along with seafood 5x/week + and my Se was just over the top of the reference range before I started epi-paleo by eating one Brazil nut per day)

Lauren - thank you for all of your wonderful advice - I finally feel like I have support in doing what I am to get rid of Katy's excema. So many people wonder why I just don't use steroid creams. She was in tears this morning as she was so frustrated with it all. I made a promise to her that we will find a way to get rid of it, and it may take some time but we WILL get rid of it. I'll be buying some brazil nuts tomorrow!

I read Jack's blog about Vit D last night - also wondered about the correlation there with lack of vit d and excema. Thanks again for your support. And thank you to everyone else too!

# Thread: Native American Diet: Guts and Grease
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/native-american-diet-guts-and-grease.4296/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-10-14T07:11:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/native-american-diet-guts-and-grease.4296/post-58058>

While researching pemmican recipes, I came upon this:

[http://westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/628-guts-and-grease?qh=YTo0OntpOjA7czo1OiJkcmllZCI7aToxO3M6NDoibWVhdCI7aToyO3M6NToibWVhdHMiO2k6MztzOjEwOiJkcmllZCBtZWF0Ijt9](http://http://westonaprice.org/traditional-diets/628-guts-and-grease?qh=YTo0OntpOjA7czo1OiJkcmllZCI7aToxO3M6NDoibWVhdCI7aToyO3M6NToibWVhdHMiO2k6MztzOjEwOiJkcmllZCBtZWF0Ijt9)

From the fermented section: The Indians also enjoyed fermented, gamey animal foods. The Coahuiltecans, living in the inland brush country of south Texas set fish aside for eight days "until larvae and other insects had developed in the rotting flesh.24 They were then consumed as an epicure's delight, along with the rotten fish." Samuel Hearne describes a fermented dish consumed by the Chippewaya and Cree: "The most remarkable dish among them. . . is blood mixed with the half-digested food which is found in the caribou's stomach, and boiled up with a sufficient quantity of water to make it of the consistence of pease-pottage. Some fat and scraps of tender flesh are also shred small and boiled with it. To render this dish more palatable, they have a method of mixing the blood with the contents of the stomach in the paunch itself, and hanging it up in the heat and smoke of the fire for several days; which puts the whole mass into a state of fermentation, which gives it such an agreeable acid taste, that were it not for prejudice, it might be eaten by those who have the nicest palates."25

From the remarkable health section:

The early explorers consistently described the native Americans as tall and well formed. Of the Indians of Texas, the explorer Cabeza de Vaca wrote, "The men could run after a deer for an entire day without resting and without apparent fatigue. . . one man near seven feet in stature. . . runs down a buffalo on foot and slays it with his knife or lance, as he runs by its side."7 The Indians were difficult to kill. De Vaca reports on an Indian "traversed by an arrow. . . he does not die but recovers from his wound." The Karakawas, a tribe that lived near the Gulf Coast, were tall, well-built and muscular. "The men went stark naked, the lower lip and nipple pierced, covered in alligator grease \[to ward off mosquitoes\], happy and generous, with amazing physical prowess. . . they go naked in the most burning sun, in winter they go out in early dawn to take a bath, breaking the ice with their body."

From the whole animal section:

According to John (Fire) Lame Deer, the eating of guts had evolved into a contest. "In the old days we used to eat the guts of the buffalo, making a contest of it, two fellows getting hold of a long piece of intestines from opposite ends, starting chewing toward the middle, seeing who can get there first; thatâ€™s eating. Those buffalo guts, full of half-fermented, half-digested grass and herbs, you didnâ€™t need any pills and vitamins when you swallowed those."15

I definately need more work on my perception of nutrition.

### Michael — 2012-10-14T09:04:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/native-american-diet-guts-and-grease.4296/post-58067>

Yes, that article's fun, isn't it?

I think they've mixed up their sources somewhere along the line. I'm pretty sure the man who could run alongside a buffalo and run a lance into it comes from Catlin. Still that's a small matter.

I think at a time when even people who had expressed interest in hunter-gatherer diets had thought they must have been low-fat diets that article made the point that certainly not all of them were very well.

I knew it is all conditioning! We can change what we like for sure. It will take time but hey, just start today! It helps huge to connect the foods with how we will look and feel in ten years when eating it.. all of a sudden it becomes almost... delicious..lol

>
> But, not any less healthy I guess Michael? That's the good part!

That bit was the Plains Indians, wasn't it? I wonder if they supply something that would otherwise be lacking in what seems to have been a very high meat diet (except for some seasonal berries and grapes and maybe a little in the way of roots like prairie potato or traded grain).

I've just read Piers Paul Read's _Alive_, which is on Jack's list of recommended books. I don't know whether it interests him because of the cold, or the altitude, or as a survival story, or at lots of different levels. It's interesting in this context, though, to note that most of the crash survivors gradually got used to their gruesome diet and that they were also driven to seek for other things and eat what they'd previously avoided (like intestines). Presumably, that was a reaction to not getting everything they physiologically needed in what they were eating.

# Thread: Jugged Kippers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jugged-kippers.4297/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-14T09:58:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jugged-kippers.4297/post-58068>

I love these. It's a very traditional way of cooking them - and doesn't make a kitchen smell, like grilling them does.

You take a tall jug and warm it, then fill it a fair way up with boiling water. Drop the kipper/s in, top the water up, cover with a saucer, and leave for six minutes.

I had one of those for breakfast today along with a grilled tomato, a large flat mushroom cooked with garlic butter, broccoli, a couple of Dublin Bay prawns (langoustines), and some coffee. I still felt that I wouldn't mind a bit more, so I had a few nuts.

### kathylu — 2012-10-14T15:31:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jugged-kippers.4297/post-58080>

Why do you cook your kippers? I just eat them out of the can. Is it to reduce the salt content?

### Michael — 2012-10-14T17:38:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jugged-kippers.4297/post-58113>

> **kathylu;59498 wrote:** Why do you cook your kippers? I just eat them out of the can. Is it to reduce the salt content?

These are "fresh" ones - I mean, they're smoked but not canned bought off a wet-fish counter. Canned ones will have been cooked in the can.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-14T20:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jugged-kippers.4297/post-58144>

Paleo is a clear step in the correct direction but it is no destination for Optimal health when you have a Ferrari engine in your head. That one species twist requires you to look for deeper biologic truths. Kippers are part of the Epi-paleo Rx.

# Thread: Yaghan Natives of South America
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-10-15T15:25:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58291>

We typically tend to look north when we want to examine traditional cultures living cold climates; but the south gets pretty cold too. I don't know how I found this today. I surely wasn't looking for it.

Anyway, before the Europeans wiped them out (there literally was a genocidal campaign), the Yaghan people lived a very interesting life. This from Wikipedia:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yaghan](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yaghan)

Despite the extreme cold climate in which they lived, early Yahgan wore little to no clothing until their contact with Europeans.\[5\] They were able to survive the harsh climate because:

1) They kept warm by huddling around small fires when they could, including in their boats to stay warm. In fact, the name of "Tierra del Fuego" (land of fire) is a name given to the island cluster by passing European explorers who witnessed these fires burning.

2) They made use of rock formations to shelter themselves from the elements.

3) They covered themselves in animal grease.\[citation needed\]

4) Over time they had evolved significantly higher metabolisms than average humans, allowing them to generate more internal body heat.\[6\]

5) Their natural resting position was a deep squatting position, which reduced their surface area and so helped to conserve heat.

### Michael — 2012-10-15T18:45:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58328>

Yes, certainly cold in Tierra del Fuego! It has been suggested that some of the people on the southern tip of South America may not have come via the Bering Strait and may have been related to Australian Aborigines - mainly on the grounds that old photos show people who look a little like that and that there's a rock painting that shows darker and lighter skinned figues at war. Whether DNA testing could sustain that ... If there are many people left known to be descended from them.

Charles Darwin wrote a little about Fuegians he met in The Voyage of the Beagle.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-15T19:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58337>

notice how polar people are darker than most expect.........CT6 and the alpha MSH link again. Inuits, the Outback natives in aus are Antartica natives and these people.........all have a tie if you look for it.

### Michael — 2012-10-15T19:56:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58352>

Tried a google image search and interestingly the first one that came up was on a page that has an extract from Darwin's diary.

Not much in the way of clothing despite the cold:

[http://2.bp.blogspot.com/\_9oTslXzpqec/SaT\_MAFr1bI/AAAAAAAAFGM/t4ekRJUS-iE/s1600-h/Yamana-Family.jpg](http://http://2.bp.blogspot.com/_9oTslXzpqec/SaT_MAFr1bI/AAAAAAAAFGM/t4ekRJUS-iE/s1600-h/Yamana-Family.jpg)

He says they eat shellfish, fish, and "sea eggs" (?)

> To look at the Wigwam; any little depression in the soil is chosen, over this a few rotten trunks of trees are placed & to windward some tufts of grass. Here 5 or 6 human beings, naked & uncovered from the wind, rain & snow in this tempestuous climate sleep on the wet ground, coiled up like animals. In the morning they rise to pick shell fish at low water; & the women winter & summer dive to collect sea eggs; such miserable food is eked out by tasteless berries & Fungi. Jerking out little fish out of the Beds of Kelp

[http://darwinbeagle.blogspot.co.uk/2009/02/25th-february-1834.html](http://http://darwinbeagle.blogspot.co.uk/2009/02/25th-february-1834.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-15T20:00:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58358>

People are afraid of going outside their comfort zone in so many ways.........

Every time I have stepped outside my comfort zone in my life and into vulnerability it is clear that there is a risk in my current world falling apart. The interesting part is that it never seems to occur in the way that you originally feared it might. And sometimes, it reveals your own opportunity costs of your current reality that you were blind too for decades. Change is often better than good.

### Michael — 2012-10-15T20:14:17+0300

He saw them, their lack of clothing, the possible insecurity of their food supply, and experienced fear perhaps? And that would colour his view of them? The truth is they probably just don't need many of things he thinks of as absent.

[http://www.amazon.com/Patagonia-Penguin-Classics-Bruce-Chatwin/dp/0142437190/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Patagonia-Penguin-Classics-Bruce-Chatwin/dp/0142437190/)

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T19:22:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-60602>

there was some discussion of the indigenous peoples of Tierra Del Fuego back in April in the What the H-- thread.

Jack has said they would be part of a future blog.

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?43-What-the-H-is-Factor-X-Post-your-guesses&p=19983&viewfull=1#post19983](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?43-What-the-H-is-Factor-X-Post-your-guesses&p=19983&viewfull=1#post19983)

they are fascinating, as their metabolic processes respond differently than the Inuit, the Kalahari bushmen, and Korean Ama.

their metabolic rate actually falls as the temperature decreases.

Favorite quote from Darwins notes..

“It was marvelous the way they could stand the cold. One woman who was suckling a baby came out to the Beagle in a canoe, and she sat there calmly in the tossing waves while the sleet fell and thawed on her naked breast. On shore these people slept on the wet ground while the rain poured through the roofs of their crude skin huts”. It was obvious that this tribe had adapted physiologically in some manner to the extreme climate with very little in the way of protective garments."

### Michael — 2012-10-16T15:46:22+0300

So Dr. Frahm is probably right. And to connect prostate cancer? And perhaps osteoporosis, too?

This looks even bigger than I'd thought.

And if so paleo has really missed this boat. They mostly recommend land-based food when iodine (and DHA, etc.) are as big as this? And in the U.S. of all places where there's this broad "Goiter Belt" of iodine-deficient soil.

And as you've said before Cordain's own tables should have tipped him off.

And what's almost the first thing he says? Cut your salt. That's crazy advice unless you're getting iodine from seafood. Government mandates that to be iodinated for a reason.

Professor Cordain is a smart and hardworking researcher. I think the problem is he's too "conservative". I don't mean that in a political sense - he may be on the left for all I know. I mean he goes along with the consensus in the research community - vide his attitude on salt. Taubes has torn that salt-consensus apart. If someone like Jimmy Moore questions Cordain on changing his opinion on something, he says loftily that he goes by research and that the research changes. I don't think he sees that sometimes "the research" is just professional groupthink. Gary does. Gary breaks windows. Cordain doesn't break windows. I think Peter at Hyperlipid is the same: Peter never saw a paper whose assumptions he didn't question. He's not credulous of research.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-16T15:54:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58515>

IMO, Iodine in salt is a bad joke. Most of it evaporates before its even used, and the balance is so miniscule, it just prevents frank goiter. Subclinical goiter is still very common...if your pcp will palpate the thyroid. That "if" is the $64,000 question, at least around here.

but then, I've never been afraid of iodine...got started taking it when I saw Dr.Fitt...made me sicker than crap at first...bromine, but now, we take lugols with relative impunity. First sign of a bug of any kind...congestion...ten drops of 5%.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-16T18:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58535>

Pulse dosing..ramp up, 1, then two, then three (etc) to ten iodoral every other day, lots of sea salt and lots of water.

I had what felt like a bad flu for about ten days...achy, snotty, congested, felt like hell. Weird acneform rash, don't recall what else. Pushed thru and it ended. My sis was way sicker than I, and had to back off to every third day for several months. I had a lot of yeast too, but that's pretty well gone I think.

I take between 10 and 20 drops of lugols 5% now, with Se and C daily. More if it sounds good.

# Thread: Just started cytomel and have questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 51
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2012-10-16T21:59:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58583>

First off my dosage is 25mcg and like an idiot I followed the instructions and took one whole tab. Now I'm wondering if that was a mistake.

Also, my cortisol (am AND pm) are both low. Any help in fixing that? Should I stop the cytomel and fix adrenals first? I am so confused.

And my T3/RT3 ratio is 3.8

Oh yeah, just got a new digital thermometer and have hit a new low. Temp was 94.8

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T23:43:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58597>

I'm sure others will chime in here, but I'll offer what I can.

If one tab = 25mcg T3, then yes, I would definitely break it up into 2-4 doses throughout the day. T3 has a short life and is high maintenance for taking orally.

Next, I'm not sure what your labs looked like to begin with, but you may really need to ramp up the T3 slowly. Otherwise, after a few days, your adrenals may not be able to handle the sudden increased metabolic rate. Maybe a good starting dose would be a total of half a tab (12.5mcg) broken up throughout the day? You may need to ask for more tabs in a lesser dose, unless the doc gave you time-released T3? You will need to monitor your temps (basal morning), and even throughout the day, to get a sense of how you're responding to the T3. I have a metal (non-mercury) thermometer that I used to use all the time (got it at the pharmacy for $8). I'd hold it under my armpit for 10 minutes. I found 10 minutes would produce a steady reading. Some prefer under the tongue readings, but I found it more doable under the armpit. HTH!!

There's a lot written up at the stopthethyroidmadness website about how to monitor your temperature for adrenal and thyroid management.

I have a metal (non-mercury) thermometer that I used to use all the time (got it at the pharmacy for $8). I'd hold it under my armpit for 10 minutes. I found 10 minutes would produce a steady reading. Some prefer under the tongue readings, but I found it more doable under the armpit. HTH!!

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T02:12:37+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-17T03:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58647>

Your rT3 ratio should get better in 3-4 months on t3 only. The dose should be divided up into 3 to start. You can ramp up one of those three doses every 5-7 days (maybe sooner just depends on your body). Definitely read stopthethyroidmadness.com for more info on T3. If you have low cortisol you may get to a point where the body cannot take any more T3 and you will appear hyper: HR increasing too much, temp too high, heart palps, weird blood pressure, etc. That may or may not be enough T3 for you but it's all the adrenals can handle. You will know if you still have hypo symptoms as well.

I'm not a fan of the digital thermometers. I have three of them and all can give different readings. I have a Geratherm basal non-mercury thermometer that seems to work well as long as you don't drop it or keep it in a hot car! You should be monitoring the HR, BP, and temp at least two times a day to see what the T3 is doing to your body.

You can ramp up one of those three doses every 5-7 days (maybe sooner just depends on your body). Definitely read stopthethyroidmadness.com for more info on T3. If you have low cortisol you may get to a point where the body cannot take any more T3 and you will appear hyper: HR increasing too much, temp too high, heart palps, weird blood pressure, etc. That may or may not be enough T3 for you but it's all the adrenals can handle. You will know if you still have hypo symptoms as well.
>
>
>
> I'm not a fan of the digital thermometers. I have three of them and all can give different readings. I have a Geratherm basal non-mercury thermometer that seems to work well as long as you don't drop it or keep it in a hot car! You should be monitoring the HR, BP, and temp at least two times a day to see what the T3 is doing to your body.

Patty, dumb question but if one is still cold does that mean it isn't enough? Or, is the only way to really know by testing the things you mentioned? I have to buy a thermometer as my doc told me to take it 2 times a day along with my pulse; so thanks for the tips.

I will say I felt great today. Like I woke up and suddenly felt more alive. Missed that.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-17T14:20:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58693>

I'm thinking that by having the sr, if I forget for an hour, I am not completely running out and it should help smooth things over.

### fitness@home — 2012-10-17T18:20:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58722>

Iâ€™ll be watching this thread, as I started the STTM T3 protocol in October.

In June, my FT3/RT3 ratio was 11.3. Went through two doctors and my endocrinologist in an attempt to have just one of them understand and then treat. Seriously shook up my endo with the request â€“ he did not react well to it (firmly entrenched in the TSH only viewpoint). I did not succeed with the docs, so I ordered T3 myself.

About a six weeks ago, I began preparing by adding supplemental iron as my Ferritin and IBC levels leave a lot to be desired (very low). Also tracked my temps and BP for a â€œbaselineâ€￾. Currently adding and increasing dosages slowly as I work my way through the protocol and still tracking temps/BP. This isnâ€™t something you should do in a haphazard manner and I respect that fact.

Will get thyroid + iron labs in early January and post results in the Labs section. If successful in raising the FT3/RT3 ratio and I am feeling **good**, the next decision will be whether to stay on T3 only or transition back to NDT. Crossing that bridge laterâ€¦

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-17T18:51:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58725>

> **vkiernan;60183 wrote:** Patty, dumb question but if one is still cold does that mean it isn't enough? Or, is the only way to really know by testing the things you mentioned? I have to buy a thermometer as my doc told me to take it 2 times a day along with my pulse; so thanks for the tips.

Here is my rule of thumb for someone with thyroid disease, although I'm sure there are "other" factors that may play into this. Simply put if your temp is low you need more T3. If you temp is erratic (averaged daily) from day to day it's most likely the adrenals.

As far as testing goes, with T3 the lab result will depend on when you took (or didn't take) the meds. My last T3 lab was at the bottom of the range but I hadn't had any T3 for over 14 hours...I'm sure the T3 is long gone. I'm not sure what the best timing is.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-17T20:07:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58739>

>
>
>
> As far as testing goes, with T3 the lab result will depend on when you took (or didn't take) the meds. My last T3 lab was at the bottom of the range but I hadn't had any T3 for over 14 hours...I'm sure the T3 is long gone. I'm not sure what the best timing is.

So how long do y'all wait before testing to see if the T3 is effective? How soon do you increase your dose if you don't feel like you're getting results?

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T20:19:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58742>

I wonder about timing taking the T3 and measuring in the lab. Seems we can produce dramatically different results depending upon when we last took the T3. We say we want the T3 in the "top quartile," in the labs, but for those of us supplementing it, what does this mean? Top quartile when our T3 dose is at it's highest concentration (and then we try to maintain that level steady state throughout the day). My guess is that ideally, this is what we're after. A reading close to 4.2 for free T3 where the range max is 4.2, given that I just took T3 roughly 3 hours before the draw, and that my regimen allows me to maintain that level throughout the day. This allows us some "wiggle room" when dealing with a worried doctor. We can always take the T3 much farther away from the blood draw, to minimize the chance of the doctor seeing levels that are "too high." I don't like playing these kinds of games though...

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T20:21:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58744>

I would think your T3 labs would be reflecting your T3 dosages almost right away. But the TSH takes much longer to respond fully - like 6 weeks. Most doctors are so entrenched with the TSH that they usually make you wait that long.

Stopthethyroidmadness has excellent info on increasing T3 (or Armour, which has T3) dosages, based on tracking your temperatures in the mornings, and throughout the day. It makes it look so easy!! In reality, I found it a little trickier, but at least it's something to work with.

> **BJK77;60275 wrote:** So how long do y'all wait before testing to see if the T3 is effective? How soon do you increase your dose if you don't feel like you're getting results?

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T20:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58748>

Last winter when I switched from synthroid to ARmour, I was tracking my progress by monitoring my temperature. What I found was that my temps stayed nice and even throughout the day, even while I was becoming more and more hypoT (as the T4 levels dropped, and weren't replaced adequately). I was on 2 grains of Armour, and figuring it was more than enough to replacing the 137mcg synthroid plus 1/2 grain Armour I had been taking prior. I was wrong. And my temps didn't really reflect it. I was still averaging 98.5ish on my metal thermometer during the day. Anyway, my take-away lesson from that experiment is that temps are not solely influenced by thyroid hormone levels. I think the hypothalamus also has it's say here in other ways.

> **vkiernan;60229 wrote:** I am doing a hybrid mix of sr and reg. I take my sr t3 40 mcg at 6 am and I am adding reg. t3 6.25 mcg every 4 hours and slowly titrating up from there. Doing the protocol on STTM. So far, all is ok. I'm thinking that by having the sr, if I forget for an hour, I am not completely running out and it should help smooth things over.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-19T17:14:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-59172>

> **Jennifer;60699 wrote:** Yes kiwi. I am trying it. Starting to think rT3 is my major problem with years of low iron and thyroid destruction (from eating gluten). I had low iron for 20 some years. That is one cause of high rT3. Getting some advice from a yahoo group on this. It's most interesting but confusing as taking T4 can cause high rT3. Just chewed my Armour this morning. I might even start a night dose. It was suggested I stabilize Adrenals and get iron higher before trying just T3 no NDT. I wAs surprised to find that the Armour tasted good - sweet even. Kind of scary. Wonder whats in it. I will be interested to see my labs on Monday.

Forest Labs who makes Armour, added a sweetener and changed the filler in the tablet a few years ago. I think most of the tabs, unless compounded have some type of "sugar" added to them.

Thyroid Hormone in Armour Thyroid

The active ingredient in Armour Thyroid is derived from pig thyroid glands. It is a combination of the thyroid hormones T4 (levothyroxine) and T3 (liothyronine). According to Forest Laboratories, the maker of Armour Thyroid, these hormones are in a fixed ratio in each tablet of 4.22 parts T4 to 1 part T3. The prescribing information for Armour Thyroid states each 60 mg tablet of Armour contains 38 mcg of T4 and 9 mcg of T3.

Lubricant in Armour Thyroid Tablets

Calcium stearate is an inactive ingredient used as a lubricant in Armour Thyroid tablets. According to Pharmaceutical Formulation Databases and Resources, lubricants in medication tablets help keep the medication powder from sticking to the equipment used to make the tablet.

Fillers in Armour Thyroid Tablets

Dextrose and microcrystalline cellulose are inactive ingredients used in Armour Thyroid tablets. According to Pharmaceutical Formulation Databases and Resources, these act as fillers and compression aids to improve the overall characteristics of the tablet powder.

Other Inactive Ingredients in Armour Thyroid Tablets

According to Pharmaceutical Formulation Databases and Resources, sodium starch glycolate is used to help the Armour Thyroid tablets dissolve once a patient takes the pill. Opadry white is a coloring agent that helps keep the color consistent in each batch of tablets.

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/43912-armour-thyroid-list-ingredients/#ixzz29kpFWRpy](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/43912-armour-thyroid-list-ingredients/#ixzz29kpFWRpy)

### vkiernan — 2012-10-26T14:35:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-60296>

> **DarleenMB;61820 wrote:** Doesn't Armour have both T4 and T3? and yes, I'm trying to clear the RT3.
>
>
>
> As for the dosage I could, indeed, as her to change the scrip to smaller amounts used several times a day. But I would like to know if that's necessary before I do it. IF you're right and the T3 only lasts about 4 hours then I definitely need to do that.

Yes, Armour has t3, my point was you might not swell up on Armour but that is only my opinion based on my n=1. I've been thinking a lot about you lately. I think I remember reading you have adrenal fatigue going on as well and that is probably why you have the rt3 level you do. There are a few protocols out there that can help with this.

1.) The one that has been around the longest is the t3 only that is mentioned in STTM. Basically you use only t3 but you have to keep titrating this up slowly. You start with a small amount first thing in am and stay there for a few days, then add it another dose 4 hours later and stay there a few days, and when you finally are taking it 4 or 5 times a day and doing fine, no heart palps etc. you can add another dose to the first thing in the am. And the cycle continues over a course of 2 to 3 months until your rt3 clears and you finally get the t3 into your cells. You have to watch for heart palps, etc. the whole time you are doing this. You need to back off if you get them. Once your rt3 clears and t3 does flood your cells, you are supposed to hyper pretty quickly and then back off. That should be your sign of clearance from what I understand.

2.) The second is newer by Paul Robinson. He has a book called Recovering with t3 and the main difference is you dose in the middle of the night (2-4 hours before waking) to supply t3 to make cortisol before you rise. Then you can take NDT or t3 the rest of the day. This is supposed to work very well with people with adrenal fatigue. I have not read it yet as I am waiting for my book to arrive but there is good info out on STTM for his protocol. If you read the success stories, some of them tell you exactly what they went thru and how they dosed. PattyCakes out here is the one who has informed me about this and it worked to heal her adrenals and clear the rt3. She has written about in several places and probably out here too. Given that my HPA axis isn't in balance yet, this is what I am trying now too. Since you are sleeping so well, not sure you want to do this though. My sleep for now is crap until my body finds the sweet spot and I adjust to it. I find myself waking all hours and wondering what time it is and is it time to take the t3.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-60326>

> **FarmersDaughter;61938 wrote:** I'm voting for increasing your dose. Have you considered splitting a cap and adding 1/2 at noon?

Do you mean take a full dose (25mcg) first thing in the A.M. and then another 1/2 at noon? I was thinking of trying that and wondered if I should talk to the PA first about this. Get her on board, as it were.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-26T18:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-60330>

Yup.

IME, begging pardon is easier than getting permission, and that way can say

I was just desperate, I had to do X, and it just helped so much...blah blah blah.

$0.02, but I've had to self treat for several years, between my good doc quitting and finding a new one who knew that TSH was a pit hormone, not made by the thyroid...

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-26T19:02:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-60332>

OK, well I"m game. Esp since the full tab seems to just do nothing now. I sure did like the way I felt that first day. It's like I've been living in a fog for years and it suddenly lifted.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-26T19:22:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-60339>

> **DarleenMB;61948 wrote:** OK, well I"m game. Esp since the full tab seems to just do nothing now. I sure did like the way I felt that first day. It's like I've been living in a fog for years and it suddenly lifted.

This sounds like you do need more. If you felt better with the 25 mcg, even for only one day, then felt worse, it usually means your body is craving more. If you do the two doses as you described above, you'll know if it's too much. You could also be very safe and just take 6.25mcg for the second dose. If you have stressed adrenals you will want to titrate up slowly.
