Here's a general description of meat canning (which includes fish). Lots of pictures...knock yourself out!

[http://www.fao.org/docrep/010/ai407e/AI407E22.htm](http://http://www.fao.org/docrep/010/ai407e/AI407E22.htm)

### JanSz — 2012-10-23T00:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59723>

On canned seafood.

I hope the can lining will do no harm.

Seafood with water is probably ok to eat.

Seafood with anything else than water needs study.

I am looking at my can of oysters in cottonseed oil.

Why do I eat oysters--- because of omega3, specially DHA + good incidentals, zinc, etc.

but

cottonseed oil is devoid of Omega3, it is pure Omega6 (how could they do that to me).

When I open the can, I will drain all oil, replace with olive or coconut oils.

\---------------

When I think seafood, I think predators and their place on a food chain.

Salmon is too high for my taste. Also other bigger fish.

Not really sure how this thinking would fare with bottom feeders, flounder, halibut.

Probably biggest would be a cod, sea bass, that would be ok to eat.

Yest to mackerel no to salmon.

Anyhow, saltwater fish is a secondary food, primarily we should concentrate on shelfish.

......................................................................

healthy ratios of n−6:n−3 range from 1:1 to 1:4 (an individual needs more n−3 than n−6)

Typical Western diets provide ratios of between 10:1 and 30:1 (i.e., dramatically higher levels of n−6 than n-3)

The ratios of n−6 to n−3 fatty acids in some common vegetable oils are:

**this three oils are acceptable

canola 2:1

olive 3-13:1

flax 1:3

this oils should not be eaten**

soybean 7:1

sunflower (no n−3)

cottonseed (almost no n−3)

peanut (no n−3)

grapeseed oil (almost no n−3)

corn oil 46:1 ratio of n−6 to n−3

.

### kathylu — 2012-10-23T04:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59773>

Sorry I wasn't clear. Broiling, not boiling. Steam under pressure can get much hotter than 100 degrees C. Commercial canning is done in large autoclaves with steam under pressure. The temperature for commercial canning processes was chosen in order to kill the spores of clostridium species.

We old folk still use centigrade and celsius interchangeably.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-23T21:07:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59887>

I think canned seafood is great as an introduction to eating seafood regularly. It was by eating canned sardines, salmon and tuna (with frozen shrimp and an occasional frozen fish fillet) that I learned to start liking seafood more than once a month or so. And I can send sardines in the mail.

I still eat canned sardines about once a month, and the other day I actually had tuna (but it was expensive tuna in a bpa free can and soy free.)

But now I'm starting to crave raw oysters once a week, though part of me still wonders why. I don't particularly love the taste. But they're ok.

### Jude — 2012-10-29T03:26:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-60664>

My n=1 since Sept 1:

Daily has been 75% canned sardines/salmon, 20% fresh lightly cooked fish and 5% grass fed lamb. So, almost 2 months on tinned, wild Atlantic Canadian fish. Decided to go with Dr K's recommendation that it won't hurt you, and just did it. During that time I did one week exclusively on tinned sardines, the second on tinned and fresh cooked. I'm alive and well, in fact I completely stopped my ubiquitous palpitations with that week of tinned sardines! So for me, tinned fish has helped me have a fantastic win health wise.

Anna K: Only get fresh fish weekly here, no MH. all expensive..prawns and salmon 33-36$ kilo but I supplemented with these.

### Jonathin — 2012-10-30T19:59:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-60972>

Regarding cottonseed oil:

Cotton is not a food crop. This means that the chemicals used on cotton are not regulated as with a food crop. This means cottonseed meal (used for cow feed) and cottonseed oil can have residues not found in food crops. Also there is a natural component of cottonseed oil that is not healthful for humans. Sorry I don't have the info on the details.

BPA is not good but it is everywhere. Touched a cash register receipt lately? Non-BPA substitutes for cans are not necessarily better just because they are not BPA. BPA has been around since the '30s and just now a problem is perceived! There are a thousand things to be concerned about regarding the food processing industry but I am not sure that any of it is stopping us from getting healthier but probably optimally healthy.

### JackStapleton — 2012-10-31T00:58:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61010>

Regarding intuition: everybody has it, in some it is underdeveloped. Because of the way I was brought up, mine was completely atrophied, but I trained myself to get it back and now I am considered by friends to be some kind of psychic genius. It just a matter of trusting that everything you need to know is instantly available and accepting the guidance when it comes. Also, be assured that if anyone else can do it, you can too. We are all the same at our core. Sometimes you can get more intuitive just by imagining that you are "borrowing" the powers of someone who has them. The answer will always be provided. The only impediment is trusting it when it comes. In most cases, people who try will get an answer but then immediately shut it out. Sometimes that is because if you open yourself to this kind of guidance, you will get messages you don't want to hear and a defensive reaction is to shut out all such info (as a way to maintain the illusion of control of your life).

Inger is correct in saying that someone with a history of a junk food diet could experience amazing benefits from canned fish if they cut out the bad stuff at the same time. So that would seem to indicate that canned fish might be great for them. But it is also clear that the fresh stuff is way better. It really doesn't matter what the researchers and scientists can prove. You just know, don't you?

If you don't trust that you know, hold some fresh fish in one hand a canned fish in the other. Your body will speak to you about the relative benefits of each. But you don't really need to hold the actual fish, you can just imagine doing so. Same results, unless you haven't learned to trust your intuition.

Anna, I am not Jack but my intuition tells me quite clear what I would do if I had only these options..

I would eat both. Of course. And the grassfed beef only raw and lots of different organs. Brain too. All raw. Additional canned fish. The best quality I could afford of course. And then I would go out and look for nettles etc. and wild berries for free and eat lots a lots of seaweeds too. Seaweeds raw if possible (avoid to heat it), to get some viruses in from the sea..  And then to get some dirt in like dirty raw carrots. I think I would feel great!

### Jonathin — 2012-10-31T17:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61095>

> **Anna K.;62683 wrote:** Jonathin, how non-bpa substitues are worse then bpa? what do you mean? thanks.

I didn't say they were worse. The fact that they are not BPA does not mean that they are necessarily more benign. One would have to test and confirm the properties of any new manmade material that is in intimate contact with food or contained within (ie - ag chemicals, pollutants). Don't hold your breathe waiting for that to happen in a timely manner!

### Inger — 2012-10-31T19:18:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61120>

> **Anna K.;62800 wrote:** thanks Inger, what do you do with nettles?

Anna, you can just crush them and eat them plain raw, or make a smoothie from nettles and water and drink it (that is what I like to do once in a while, I keep forgetting though..), or use as spinach! Fry in butter or whatever you'd like. One could make nettle soup with an cooked egg as condiment, we ate that as kids often. Just cook and puree nettles with water and onion, add spices you like and some cream or butter or other fat and enjoy!

You can use the "flowers" too, very healthy fats in there!

### JackStapleton — 2012-10-31T19:37:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61126>

Anna,

Sounds like Dr. Inger has some good advice for you. My intuition says that you have a very active mind, always asking very many questions. Quiet and meditation would help you to still your mind and take you to a place where you could hear messages from the silence. From the depths of your true self, you might find that fresh beef is good one day in the summer and something else for a different day in the winter.

I agree with you about eating the frozen thawed fish raw. I guess I am lucky to have fresh wild-caught fish available every day and sushi in every grocery store. If I didn't, I am sure my intuition would lead me to move to where I did!

Anna, I am also sending you a message that you will feel. It is not with words, so you will not be able to understand it with your mind. You will have doubts that you got it - that is your mind - but you will get it. When you feel it, even if your mind isn't sure you did, the feeling will take you to the place of your intuition. Enjoy it. It is a fun place. The more you visit, the more it will feel like home. It feels a bit like love.

JJack

>

>

>
>
>
> JJack, Should we eat canned fish or grass-fed beef? what's your intuition is telling you?

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-31T22:51:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61157>

for the record, I don't think there has been anybody yet to disagree that Fresh fish is best.

What isn't clear is - is canned always the better choice. Let's take a forum favorite - canned sardines. The issues we have are:

1\. Heat- it's not superheated it seems, but it is cooked which in theory does degrade the epi goodness.

2\. Canning cooties, bpa and whatever else if it's in oil. If you can't find non bpa fish, might there be better choices?

3\. Profit. (hehehe internet joke for the cool kids

Jack put forth the idea that all seafood no matter what is always better than any other food. To me, that seems like a generalization to make a point rather than a hard/fast rule to live by.

Can we suss out how much fishy goodness we are gettin from a sardine? Same as 2 shrimp? Same as 1 shrimp etc. . .

### kathylu — 2012-10-31T22:58:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61160>

I definitely nosh on shrimp more often than sardines. Shrimp taste better imo and rank #2 (crustaceans) on Jack's list of epi-paleo wonderfulness.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-01T16:37:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61281>

> **Inger;62994 wrote:** Lol.. sometimes I wonder what is the purpose with this speculations..
>
>
>
> There are many roads to Rome.
>
>
>
> And then.. lol.. I wonder if I totally talk past you now Cavemam..or misunderstood what you intended to say. I apologise if it so.

I think Inger, that your obvious and genuine happy levels are an inspiration - although I do not think everyone is built to have them the same way. Check what I posted over on the happy thread.

There are two things for consideration in understanding happy and how it relates to meditation. (and the incorrect assumption that everyone can/has the ability to meditate and respond spiritually).

1\. [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoamine\_oxidase\_A](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A) This is ONE discovery, fairly recent, I'm sure there will be more.

2\. VMAT2 is a vesicular monoamine transporter that regulates the flow of mood-altering chemicals in the brain - commonly refered to as either the god gene or the meditation gene. See this interesting page and video: [http://www.thoughtsinperspective.com...c-warfare.html](http://http://www.thoughtsinperspective.com...c-warfare.html)

So the expression of these genes that one has are a very large influence on if you are religious, spiritual, meditative etc and recieve the positive brain reactions to those things. Some get it more, some get it less. Meditation only works on those that have a high expression of these.

In addition, It's likely many people have differing levels of damage to HC neurons.

I observe that nature has not made us all the same, and this surely applies to how we think and how our brains work. I bet there is a steady mix of homosapiens with both the bliss capability as well as the ones who's brains are wired to analyze and walk down the analyzing path.

Just like with an addict who goes over the edge to keep having their bliss, I'm sure there are some analyzers who go over the edge to their detriment. I think we've seen some of those on here, too.

I am at a place where this analysis is NOT debilitating - it's more information that my mind needs to keep moving forward on my health path. It's energizing, not draining.

### Stavity — 2012-11-01T16:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61286>

Cavemam, are you saying that moving forward with the best information is energizing? I agree to a degree (hehehe!!) I have been studying nutrition for 20 years now and most of the information is garbage and I hit many walls.. What amazes me is the information I have gotten from this blog in a short time has been exhilarating, but there comes a point I need to just "do" instead of analyzing everything. Now is the time to use my intuition and feel with all my senses in order to move on. Someone once told me to "keep it simple stupid." Wow how that fits me. But, no joke, in order for me to move on I will have to take the time to meditate so that not all is lost and I am able to develop what I have learned so far. I feel disease runs skin, organs, mind/spiritual and in order to heal it's the reverse. If my mind heals/clears then I can work on the rest when the time comes.

I love your mind and read most of what you write with enthusiasm because of the person you are. I would take nothing away from your experience and only learn from having taken the time to read what you and others have to say. So personally, while information is power, I am kind of tired and just need to fully implement what I have learned thus far. I am probably way off base in my observation but I just wanted you to know you are appreciated here and I wish I had your mind at your age.

But for me, it makes my spirit a bit less light.. it is totally hard to describe but a certain heaviness appears that is not there had I had it raw. Lightness is like happiness for me too. The happier I feel the lighter I feel. IDK.. sometimes I feel like almost flying. When I feel real good and I have been strict with my food choises. Now.. I feel that way when in love too..lol But it also happends with right foods when not in love.

When eating certain cooked seafood the difference is small, I can not really notice any, with one cooked seafood meal/day. Also, if drinking HWC, or too much coffee.. or anything else that is not optimal for me like many not good quality almonds with butter (I love this combination..). I just feel less happy/vibrant. I know it because I have tried it many times you see. But I am not so willing to "try" it often anymore because I prefer to feel better.

Sometimes I think happiness feels like.. power too? It feels like, if the whole world fall into pieces around me it would not matter at all. It is kind of peace and power together. Hmm.

Why I think I am broken.. to me as long as something is not optimal it is broken. Easy...that is how I think. Maybe it is weird? IDK.. I am far from optimal, I know many things that I can improve on. My mind, my body.

There is so much to improve still! And I have the amazing days and I have the days when I feel a bit less good. The amazing days shows me where I am going. But not every day is like that yet.

Now.. I am wondering if the "crazy healthy foods" in a raw state makes my body/mind open up in a certain way, and that is what makes the joyful feeling? I am thinking it could very well be so? This happiness do feels very much like openness.. like sucking in life and beeing a part of everything. I just enjoy the people around me immensely, the nature too.. it is weird.. lol

Maybe it is all in my mind. Maybe I have made up my own rules about what makes me happy lol. But fact is, all raw stuff have lots of live viruses and bacterias in them still.. and more of certain vitamins.. and raw heart is the bomb for Q10. Q10 is sensible to heat...

All this just confirms what I am feeling.

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-01T20:02:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61349>

Believing something makes you feel good will make you in fact feel good. Placebo effect.

Satisfaction is a very very nice feeling. You know how great I feel after cleaning the entire house spot-less? Accomplished. Satisfied. GOOD! Did the action of cleaning my house do anything particularly good for the health of my body? Probably not other than a bit of a workout maybe. And some sort of zen effect about having a spot-less environment.

BUT - I feel terrific when it's done. I do it just for that purpose. In fact, I love cleaning house for the sole purpose of feeling great. After. A lot of the time, the more undesirable the task itself, the more satisfaction after it has been completed. In my experience. I feel even more terrific if others admire my accomplishment of my super-clean house.

### ealachan — 2012-11-01T20:41:17+0200

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-01T20:52:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61374>

I'm saying that there technically could be an entirely different reason for you feeling great when you eat the way you say makes you feel great than the actual nutritional effect of the food.

The thing that doesn't feel good is that no one wants to have their bubble burst. Yet there is many many people who are entirely happy on a different than Inger's diet ("sub-optimal" diet). Which disproves your theory of your food choices being the sole/major factor in your happiness.

Equating food choices with happiness - that's the part that makes my gut act up. Look at the monks that Cavemam cited - they eat a veggie cooked diet and they live in bliss. B/c they don't equate their food choices with happiness. They equate it with time spent in mediation and prayer.

Cognitive dissonance is one of the most stressful internal states of humans. Remove it and you will be happier. Remove it and you will be healthier. You have removed the stress of knowing you eat junk by eating foods you believe healthier - you feel less stressed, you feel happier. Now if those foods actually made a difference or if it was your belief in those foods - that I yet need to see answered.

Not saying you should stop doing whatever you are doing - I believe every one person should do what makes them happy. Whether that is eating Inger's diet or cleaning house - who really cares?

Monks lived a very healthy life all their life Soul. You forget a lot of things here. And of course they are not stressed like normal people working long days and having destroyed their health/brains with modern luxuries like Jack said. They are in a totally different place.

I FOR SURE CAN TELL YOU, WITH COGNITIVE DISSONANCE NOT EVEN THE HEALTHIEST FOOD WILL HELP YOU!

You are absolutely right here. But to remove the dissonance, AND eat healthy foods.. that would be the most healing thing!

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-02T00:22:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61421>

It seems to me that Souldanzer, you get an oxytocin release from doing things that make you feel good, like getting your house clean. So what you're saying is, regardless of what food you eat, when you do certain things, they make you feel good? Again, I believe it's oxytocin.

And Inger, I believe you're saying that it IS the food you eat that makes you feel good. That by eating a SAD diet, no matter what you do, you do not feel good. But when you eat epi-Paleo, you feel good, which inspires you to do other things that make you feel good.

Maybe a chicken-egg thing?

For me, I do not feel good on a SAD diet. Period. Doesn't matter what I do in my life, how many friends I have, how well I'm doing professionally, personally. When I eat garbage, I feel like garbage. And literally, almost the moment it goes down my throat.

But if a person's BG axis is well, and their circadian rhythms are aligned, etc., then sure, they can eat some garbage and do some things that make them feel good, and they will be happy. Not for most of us who find these forums, and definitely not for me. I have to eat good, then I feel good, then I want to do good. But for each his own.

### JackStapleton — 2012-11-02T04:03:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61478>

> **Souldanzer;63174 wrote:** maybe it would be a good idea to stick with "I" statements.......

Souldanzer,

I don't know you but I notice you seem to post a lot on this forum.

I would guess by your comments that you have a personal history of bullying, either you were bullied yourself, or maybe you are a bully, or maybe you come from a family or environment in which bullying is seen as normal and acceptable.

I come from an experience in which bullying is to be called out for what it is and addressed. This usually leads to attacks from the bullyer. In other words, more bullying. Still, it is important to do.

I read Jack Kruse's comment as affirming and encouraging. Very positive and beautiful. You chose to nitpick about a pronoun. This comes across as an attack on both Jack and Inger. I am guessing that you cannot see that. As far as accuracy goes, I am in complete agreement with the feeling that Jack expressed, so his use of the "we" is accurate since it includes more than Jack himself and therefore the "I" usage is not necessary. I assume many others may agree with Jack's sentiment. It would be helpful if you could try to feel what he means instead of criticizing insignificant details of his sentiment.

Your comment adds nothing to the discussion, but since you have made 3,764 posts since March, I am guessing that you do not censor your impulse to reply very much. It is likely that you do not care how your comments come across and will lash out at anyone who tells you what they feel about them, as I am trying to do, and as you just did in your reply to Jack's request that you not be so quick to add snarky comments to heartfelt ones.

This comment is meant to be helpful.

JJack

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-02T04:36:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61483>

I want to believe that there is room for everyone on this forum. That we don't have to agree... and that we are all entitled to our 'off' moments. I think it's vitally important that we treat each other with kindness. We are all different - and different versions of 'broken' to quote Quelsen's great description - and we know that this can be an uphill battle for many, myself included. I know I am capable of a post that I later regret. I really hope that someone would be kind enough to point that out to me; I also hope that there would not be shaming involved, nor outing of sensitive issues in a public way.

Whether or not I agree with Dr Kruse is not the point. I have learned a great deal from Inger and I adore her honest, open ways of discussing what she has discovered. She has inspired me. I have also learned a great deal from Soul and I adore her ability to persist when the going is tough, to keep questioning even when the entire world doubts her. She has also inspired me.

It was suggested by Dr Kruse to 'gloss over' things one does not like. That door swings both ways. Both JJack and Dr Kruse missed an opportunity to gloss over what they dislike. This feels very unkind, not helpful.

Optimal (and the road thereof) is not perfection... none of us attain that no matter how good our labs look. We all get it wrong sometimes. (I'm guessing someone is going to explain why I've got it wrong now.) I suppose what I'm really saying is that this community, comprised solely of people I don't know, is important to me. I'd like to find a way for us all to share and learn, question and challenge, fall down and get back up, and still treat each other well.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T15:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-62195>

> **Stavity;63018 wrote:** Cavemam, are you saying that moving forward with the best information is energizing? I agree to a degree (hehehe!!) I have been studying nutrition for 20 years now and most of the information is garbage and I hit many walls.. What amazes me is the information I have gotten from this blog in a short time has been exhilarating, but there comes a point I need to just "do" instead of analyzing everything. Now is the time to use my intuition and feel with all my senses in order to move on. Someone once told me to "keep it simple stupid." Wow how that fits me. But, no joke, in order for me to move on I will have to take the time to meditate so that not all is lost and I am able to develop what I have learned so far. I feel disease runs skin, organs, mind/spiritual and in order to heal it's the reverse. If my mind heals/clears then I can work on the rest when the time comes.
>
>
>
> I love your mind and read most of what you write with enthusiasm because of the person you are. I would take nothing away from your experience and only learn from having taken the time to read what you and others have to say. So personally, while information is power, I am kind of tired and just need to fully implement what I have learned thus far. I am probably way off base in my observation but I just wanted you to know you are appreciated here and I wish I had your mind at your age.

For me, I think epi-paleo is the most intuitive thing and is the best thing to work for me in all my life (whether I was looking at food as health or not). I know Jack was hinting at seafood for a while leading up to July...and I ate some seafood throughout for sure. But when July hit and I decided to eat seafood every day and also eat seaweeds, it felt like a miracle. I can't see myself eating any other way ever again. I don't like looking at hunter gatherers from paleolithic era...when they were deciding to branch out. Sure that works, but for me seeing how our brain evolved in the FIRST place then apply that to healing...it's as easy as 1 2 3. So if seafood 'built' a brain, then it can 'restructure'/heal a brain that might have been damaged in some ways by epi-genetics or even life events. I honestly think the nutrients provided in this plan (and the circadian rythms) allow for the brain to re-grow and/or set down new pathways so that you can override possible errors from before. You don't need to 'fix' the broken at all...you can just 'fill on top' of it. Make new neurons....

So this is why it's simple and easy for me and I don't get overwhelmed about canned or frozen or fresh. I eat mostly from frozen and even in expensive too. I eat canned if I want sardines and oysters. Easy. Seaweeds I've come up with many good ideas. In the Fall/Winter I decided to eat apples b/c I felt it's good for my digestion and for resveratrol, and the fact that they are harvested in Fall and keep so well over winter easily w/o modification. Just keep in cool dark room. So I put salt, curcumin and cinnamon on the apple slices (usually before breakfast). Very tasty. I eat carrots, onions and kale too. Of course I love my coffee and I put coconut oil in it b/c I want the ketones for my brain. For me it's important to get a decent amount of coconut oil in me despite it being from the tropics. I eat nuts and seeds but not like a million (and I feel OK on them) b/c I don't have AI. I eat eggs b/c of no AI. I don't eat dairy tough, b/c the way I feel on it. Goat butter is the only dairy I seem to tolerate. Cow butter...I don't know...I just don't like it anymore. For me my health is #1, so I use food to be prescriptive and within that context I overlay that with seasonality. So if I eat coconuts all year that are not normal for winter, well that's the prescriptive side of things. I mean, technically coffee is not from my region either, but I choose to drink it, and in this case mainly b/c I truly truly enjoy the complexity in each cup of coffee and the literature from where the coffee bean comes from. Just fascinating. (And b/c also it makes a great carrier for my coco oil lol).

I just feel like life is so much easier now b/c I know to go to bed early and wake up with the sun (well in winter it's before the sun due to going to bed early...but that's different). I obey the circadian cycles. Period. I don't worry about food or what to eat b/c I have my seafoods and I have the fats which I know are good. ie. bacon, duck fat, goat butter coconut oil, avacadoes. Easy. How can it get any easier?

That's MY n-1.

### EWO — 2022-08-07T23:18:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-314925>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=314910)
>
> please tell us more ......how intriguing.
>
> I spend way too much time thinking....

There are lots of different formal practices (like "sealing of the five senses")...

with a similar intention...

of helping a person organize and "seal" up their energy field to avoid taking in undesired energies in from others/the world around them, or leaking personal energy reserves.

Doesn't have to be formal or complicated or esoteric though.

One thing I am practicing right now is "not having an opinion" rather than "being nonjudgemental." Being nonjudgemental is more active/requires more energy output, whereas having no opinion is more passive/requires less energy output. I've noticed, for example, I tend to go out and about seeking people and things that irritate me. Even just looking over at the driver next to me and "catching" them on their cell phone. Or noticing who is or is not wearing a mask. Or who is or is not plastering their cars with what bumper stickers. Then I go through being judgmental about those people and things. Then I pull my attention around to "trying to be non judgemental," telling myself things like "everyone is just doing the best they can." But I've already stressed out about stuff that doesn't really matter, for the tiny little bit of egoic boost that came from feeling momentarily self righteous, then the tiny boost that came from exercising the spiritual elitism of "being nonjudgmental."

Setting the intention to "have no opinion," I stop looking around for stuff to irritate me. If I happen to notice something that would otherwise irritate me, I catch myself and say "I have no opinion about that." I spend way less energy wrapped up in what other people are doing or not doing, and have way more energy left for whatever...

It's also making me more aware of all the things I waste my time having an opinion about when I can never really know why anyone is doing what they are doing.

Become your own best friend and guard your energy inputs and outputs, whether or not you follow any specific practices.

> Philadelphia physician Dr. Colin Champ from the â€œCaveman Doctorâ€ blog and the â€œRelentless Roger & The Caveman Doctorâ€ podcast is currently involved in studying a topic that is very near and dear to his heart that happens to be what weâ€™ll be discussing in this weekâ€™s ATLCX podcast. [http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2012/10/31-dr-colin-champ-ketogenic-diets-and-cancer/](http://http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2012/10/31-dr-colin-champ-ketogenic-diets-and-cancer/)

### Inger — 2012-10-23T22:26:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-caveman-doctor-on-cancer.4465/post-59889>

I am thinking.. or my logic is saying, a cancer diet should be ketogenic but then add in therapeutic carbs like wild berries because they are known to have anti cancerous properties. That is why I eat lots of wild picked berries as my only carb source really (except of some wild raw honey as a "spice") I can imagine the body and the cancer cell would then suck up the carb and with it the nutrients that kills it..huh clever, or? No idea if it is right but I have figured it sounds logic so I eat all the wild berries I want without any issue. I cannot eat apples from the garden or strawberries or domesticated raspberries without getting in troubles but wild grow berries do me only good! I figure then they must be great for me too..

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T04:18:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-caveman-doctor-on-cancer.4465/post-59933>

> **Inger;61464 wrote:** I am thinking.. or my logic is saying, a cancer diet should be ketogenic but then add in therapeutic carbs like wild berries because they are known to have anti cancerous properties. That is why I eat lots of wild picked berries as my only carb source really (except of some wild raw honey as a "spice") I can imagine the body and the cancer cell would then suck up the carb and with it the nutrients that kills it..huh clever, or? No idea if it is right but I have figured it sounds logic so I eat all the wild berries I want without any issue. I cannot eat apples from the garden or strawberries or domesticated raspberries without getting in troubles but wild grow berries do me only good! I figure then they must be great for me too..

I can do blueberries, and I do cucumbers and iceberg lettuce. I haven't gotten my hands on wild berries, other than blue. Even apples get to me.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-24T04:21:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-caveman-doctor-on-cancer.4465/post-59934>

Ketogenic diets and cancer are ideal. We need better long term studies done but the data already is pretty stout supporting them.

### Inger — 2012-10-24T09:47:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-caveman-doctor-on-cancer.4465/post-59951>

> **chocolate;61522 wrote:** I can do blueberries, and I do cucumbers and iceberg lettuce. I haven't gotten my hands on wild berries, other than blue. Even apples get to me.

Chocolate, are you sure the blueberries were wild? They rarely are selled anymore in shops.. The wild ones are smaller and one gets very blue lips/mouth when eating them. At least the ones we have growing here. Not sure if you have the same over there?

I took some pics from the berries I am eating these days growing around here;

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

The rose hips I love too. I eat them whole with stones and all. I guess that could only be good for the gut microfilm to get brushed..lol

It taste great, I feel great and my digestion works great so I figure it is fine!

Now.. some of the berries I eat are planted like rosehips, but they grow like wild, just from themselves without any assistance and I can digest them so well, very different from apples from the garden. Strange.

You know.. before I did not saw much berries at all around.. and now I see them everywhere?

Huh.. it is amazing what one can see when start to looking for something!

### Inger — 2012-10-24T10:01:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-caveman-doctor-on-cancer.4465/post-59952>

I am thinking wild herbs and mushrooms are therapeutical too.

People here in the north have used Chaga mushrooms and Birch ash too, to fight cancer for very long.

I am wondering what is in Birch ash that is so good? When I put the ash leftovers from the sauna out for our sheeps to taste on they love it. If we have burned some threes or bushes on their pasture thei love to lie down in the ash at times...

I also read about children in the second world war when people were so poor and the children ate ash too. I am thinking there must be some important minerals in it?

### Michael — 2012-10-24T10:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-caveman-doctor-on-cancer.4465/post-59957>

> **Inger;61541 wrote:** You know.. before I did not saw much berries at all around.. and now I see them everywhere?
>
> Huh.. it is amazing what one can see when start to looking for something!

Do you get cloudberries? Those are supposed to be very nutritious. The vikings supposedly took those on sea voyages, and I think Nansen and Amundsen used them.

### Inger — 2012-10-24T12:28:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-caveman-doctor-on-cancer.4465/post-59959>

> **Michael;61547 wrote:** Do you get cloudberries? Those are supposed to be very nutritious. The vikings supposedly took those on sea voyages, and I think Nansen and Amundsen used them.
>
>
> Oh yeah. You can buy them here in shops too. They are so expensive. Like gold.. but so delish! The most delicious berrie ever I guess.. together with wild raspberries and wild strawberries... They grow further north though, not around the corners where I live (in southern Finland). I buy lingonberries the most, they are very cheap here and you can buy them frozen in every shop year around. I like them too for sure. Here some nutrient info on them;
>
>
>
> Nutritional properties
>
> Ripe lingonberries
>
>
>
> The berries contain plentiful organic acids, vitamin C, provitamin A (as beta carotene), B vitamins (B1, B2, B3), and the elements potassium, calcium, magnesium, and phosphorus. In addition to these nutrients, they also contain phytochemicals that are thought to counteract urinary-tract infections, and the seeds are rich in omega-3 fatty acids.
>
> Medicinal
>
>
>
> In folk medicine, V. vitis-idaea has been used as an apÃ©ritif, astringent, antihemorrhagic, anti-debilitive, depurative, disinfectant/antiseptic (especially for the urethra), a diuretic, a tonic for the nervous system, and in various ways to treat breast cancer, diabetes mellitus, rheumatism, and various urogenital conditions.\[12\]

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaccinium\_vitis-idaea](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaccinium_vitis-idaea)

# Thread: European Union Public Service Review: Growing link between diet and brain function
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-23T19:22:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59843>

> Just as drugs can affect brain function, evidence shows that what we eat may influence not only our bodies but our brains and minds. Eating plenty of oily fish, olive oil, and fruit and vegetables has been linked to lower risks of age-related cognitive decline and mental illnesses such as depression, as well as conditions like heart disease and diabetes. These foods are rich in important micronutrients: vitamins, minerals and the unsaturated fatty acids EPA and DHA. ...

[http://www.publicservice.co.uk/article.asp?publication=European%20Union&id=590&content\_name=Research,%20Innovation%20and%20Science&article=21023](http://http://www.publicservice.co.uk/article.asp?publication=European%20Union&id=590&content_name=Research,%20Innovation%20and%20Science&article=21023)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-24T05:01:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59943>

Europe is way ahead of us with respect to food and the industries around it.

### Michael — 2012-10-24T08:11:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59949>

The public services in any country ought to be interested in this, because besides the problems and costs with regard to healthcare there's this:

> ... raises hopes of reducing levels of **educational failure via a relatively simple intervention. Secondly, evidence that violent behaviour** can be reduced by dietary supplements ...
>
>
> Education and also crime. If it were just people getting a little thicker around the waist; if it were even no more than people hitting a range of health problems as they entered old age ... But it's not. In the end, a denuded food supply is even a public order problem.
>
>
>
> The road seems rocky, though:
>
>
>
>
> > Government's role is essential, and here there is room for improvement. For example, a recent attempt to replicate the UK prison study was seriously disrupted when the prison service decided to move many enrolled participants elsewhere. Long-term science does not always fit well with short-term political and administrative priorities.
>
>
>
> BTW, this is coming from the IFBB. It says at the top:
>
>
>
> The Institute for Food, Brain and Behaviour's Professor John Stein and Dr Kathleen Taylor report on the growing link between diet and brain function…

Stein, along with Professor Michael Angus Crawford is one of the science advisors to the IFBB. Interestingly, so is someone from the NIH in America:

[http://www.ifbb.org.uk/science-advisory-council](http://http://www.ifbb.org.uk/science-advisory-council)

Gives a link here:

[http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/JosephRHibbelnMDbiosketch.pdf](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/JosephRHibbelnMDbiosketch.pdf)

### Michael — 2012-10-24T09:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59950>

Isn't that interesting? I've just noticed that the biography of the American expert is hosted at an Australian website:

[http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au)

Poking around there I found a news item on a lecture by him:

> Captain Joseph R Hibbeln, M.D. voiced this opinion when delivering the prestigious Black Dog Institute 2010 International Lecture on Friday, 12 November. Captain Hibbeln is a scientist and psychiatrist who was **the first to identify a link between reduced seafood consumption and depression** and has led an incredibly broad amount of research on the subject. ...
>
>
> [http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/2010InternationalLecture.pdf](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/2010InternationalLecture.pdf)
>
>
>
> So he's the first person to note that particular link.
>
>
>
>
> > “Avoid junk, especially deep fried foods. Avoid mayonnaises, salad dressings and margarines. Avoid soy, corn and vegetable oil. Use olive and flax oils,” he said.
> >
> >
> > Thank you, Captain Hibbeln.
> >
> >
> >
> > He said that in the U.S. expectant mothers had been warned against eating fish because it contained small amounts of mercury. Captain Hibbeln questioned the wisdom of the advice ...
>
>
>
> The report finishes:
>
>
>
> “Seafood contains a host of balanced nutrients and if we have a seafood deficient diet it means the brain isn’t getting enough nutrition,” he said.

All this stuff is out there. And all these people I never heard of ... Hannah Russ in archaeology, Captain Hibbeln in depression ...

See also:

[http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/search/index.cfm?q=hibbeln](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/search/index.cfm?q=hibbeln)

### Inger — 2012-10-24T10:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59956>

You know, it is strange to think about the culture here. There is an Swedish culture in Finland too, they are a minority but here everyone must learn Swedish in scool and on every food and anything you can read the text in both languages. Now, the Swedish minority also is the richer and have better health and better education.. their culture have many different seafood sermonies like crab parties, lots of herring.. Seafood is a strong part of their culture. Also tanning and swimming in the sea, many have cottages on own small islands and are there a lot in summer. My best friend in scool, they had own an island too and her mother was the psychologist in town. They were the whole summer on their island and the were tanning and swimming naked! Yeah.. talk about paradise..

People are a bit more "wild" here. Now.. here are only around 5 millions in quite a large country. That might be a reason too.

That is why I love to live here quite a lot.

# Thread: what time of day do you like to do CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-10-23T23:30:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59896>

This may have already been covered previously in past threads, but I'm curious: what time of day works best for you with regards to CT (either cold-water or cold-air)? Do you have a set schedule, or just do it when the fancy strikes? Did you try it at one time of day and decide it was all wrong and switch it up until you found what worked best?

Inquiring minds want to know!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-23T23:33:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59897>

Mid afternoon works best for me...but w/working on site w/a client it isn't as feasible so I'm back to ice packs in the am while making breakfast... I'm trying to get my butt into the tub after the kids go to bed...may actually hit it this evening... definitely need it considering I've had a migraine for 3days...and the 2nd one in a week... effing hormones...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-24T00:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59904>

Mid afternoon is best for me too. Did a 50 degree bath last night at 7:30 and it slayed me. I was so cold. I had the room dark w/a candle and was very relaxed but cold. It took me hours to feel warm again. Yah, I guess I should feel cold for a while. I don't know maybe the dark had something to do with it since most of my CTing has been during the day.

Mornings right after BAB are good too!

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-24T02:50:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59919>

I CT mostly in the am after bab, but occasionally i do a sunset swim. This evening i swam in the rain at sunset. Very calming. I would CT before bed, but I am cold for hours and this freezes my wife so i don't. I like to CT in the am because I feel good all day.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T03:22:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59925>

I do it whenever I can...would love to be organized enough for a schedule, but alas...

Maybe in my next life.

I seem to fare better in the am, temp rises quicker and steadier, but the BAB is larger then dinner tends to be as well, so that may be why? With the advent of winter, I may start doing cold air CT in the am, we'll see.

### kathylu — 2012-10-24T03:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59926>

AM is the only thing that works for me. I CAN'T fall asleep if I'm cold! I know the CT jocks here probably sleep naked on an ice slab, but I still use a blanket and wear socks to bed. I don't see that changing any time soon, so I need at least 6 hours minimum between the bath and a comfortable sleeping temperature.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T03:51:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59927>

> **kathylu;61514 wrote:** AM is the only thing that works for me. I CAN'T fall asleep if I'm cold! I know the CT jocks here probably sleep naked on an ice slab, but I still use a blanket and wear socks to bed. I don't see that changing any time soon, so I need at least 6 hours minimum between the bath and a comfortable sleeping temperature.

Yeah, we won't discuss bedclothes....cold water I can do...cold sleep equals no sleep.

Not there yet.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-24T03:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59928>

Evening. I'm just fine afterwards, unless I dry off. And once I get dressed I start to tremble. When I touch the sheets the shivering begins. If I could stay up for a couple hours, I'd have no issues.

Lucky for me DH doesn't mind a little second-hand CT.

### furface — 2012-12-12T01:01:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67180>

Since I can only CT using my shower, or face dunking...which I did for the first time today after lunch, I take my cold showers after my regular warmish shower in the morning, then again at night after dinner.

Â 

I'll probably face dunk before dinner.

### endless — 2012-12-12T03:02:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67182>

Morning if I can, but anytime up until about 3-4pm (this time of year, anyway. After that I start to feel colder and have a lot of resistance. I can't sleep when I am cold either.

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-12T08:02:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67190>

I love doing a sunset swim this time of year. The low light and the cold go nicely together.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-12T10:39:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67202>

im doing it now.......22 degrees of frigid

### nonchalant — 2012-12-12T12:50:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67219>

> **Jack Kruse said**
> im doing it now.......22 degrees of frigid

I'm guessing you don't mean 22C.

What are you doing?Â  Adding alcohol to the water?Â  Oh, it's probably a freezer, cold air.

### Awainer1 — 2012-12-12T16:40:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67250>

I was gonna say 22 degrees wow that is cold water! Oh wait. I do it at night in a dark bathroom before bed. Sleep like a log. I understood it as that being the best time to do it.

# Thread: what type of seasoning do you use on your fish, beef, etc?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-24T15:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59964>

Im just starting this Epi Paleo and eating a bunch of fish and shellfish, mainly oysters. The taste is kind of "bleh"

since I am used to eating meat from cows, chicken, pork, etc.

I need to put some seasoning into my meat/fish so it will be more enjoyable to eat. The thing is, I don't know what type of seasoning I can use that would be acceptable to consume during the fall/winter.

What do you guys use?

### BJK77 — 2012-10-24T18:19:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59990>

I'm using tons of garlic lately, much to my husband's dismay  I have a small herb garden that's still going strong, so I'm also using lots of fresh rosemary, oregano, thyme and tarragon.

Those are my favorites.

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T18:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59994>

chimichurri sauce is really awesome on salmon. There are a lot of ways to make it, but mine is usually lots of fresh parsley and cilantro, olive oil, raw garlic, salt, pepper and a splash of vinegar.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-24T19:01:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59999>

sea salt, black pepper, turmeric, cumin, garlic, ginger, cinnamon, nutmeg, thyme, rosemary, basil, oregano, sage, mint, cilantro.

it really depends on what I'm making - but the above are my go to seasonings and herbs.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-24T19:14:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60000>

thanks guys, i will disregard this portion about eating seasonal and use some seasoning on the fish. got to get used to eating fish first.

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T19:41:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60002>

Eating seasonally isn't necessarily as black-and-white as you seem to want to make it. The over-arching intention, as I understand it, is that we should look around where we live and notice what kind of carb-containing stuff is growing at certain times of the year, and take our consumption cues from that. In other words, no bananas in January unless you live in Ecuador. Fresh and dried herbs aren't used in such vast quantities that you'd be consuming significant carbs from them, so they probably wouldn't fall under the "seasonality" rule, unless one was taking said rule to a fairly fanatical degree.

### diane — 2012-10-24T19:46:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60004>

Lemon or lime, cumin and cilantro. I like mine with a bit of a mexican twist.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-25T08:20:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60093>

I sometimes use Paul Prudhomme Seafood Magic or Meat Magic depending on what I'm cooking.

### freesia — 2012-10-25T10:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60101>

I often use use Maori "bush pepper" or horopito\* in fish dishes. Porcini salt on beef. Thyme on either. Rosemary when I cook a leg of lamb. And LOTS of garlic.

\*An anti candida herb, also known as Kolorex. Available here as a seasoning.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-27T23:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60471>

> **ealachan;61595 wrote:** Eating seasonally isn't necessarily as black-and-white as you seem to want to make it. The over-arching intention, as I understand it, is that we should look around where we live and notice what kind of carb-containing stuff is growing at certain times of the year, and take our consumption cues from that. In other words, no bananas in January unless you live in Ecuador. Fresh and dried herbs aren't used in such vast quantities that you'd be consuming significant carbs from them, so they probably wouldn't fall under the "seasonality" rule, unless one was taking said rule to a fairly fanatical degree.

Im in new york, the fruits and veggies that I eat dont grow during the fall and winter. i kind of figure that that the amount of herbs would not be significant to make a difference, though wasn't 100% sure. thanks for that.

i have to say these responses are beautifull. now i have things to buy.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-27T23:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60478>

> **x\_danny\_x;61556 wrote:** Im just starting this Epi Paleo and eating a bunch of fish and shellfish, mainly oysters. The taste is kind of "bleh"
>
> since I am used to eating meat from cows, chicken, pork, etc.
>
>
>
> I need to put some seasoning into my meat/fish so it will be more enjoyable to eat. The thing is, I don't know what type of seasoning I can use that would be acceptable to consume during the fall/winter.
>
>
>
> What do you guys use?

Sirachi is a favorite

### Stavity — 2012-10-28T00:14:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60480>

Yummm!!! homemade mayo with Sirachi together. I often use ginger, cilantro, or dill in salmon tartare and ceviche. My Ana test was negative so lots of hot peppers it is. Mignonette Sauce is great on oysters, if you can handle vinegar (I can't). It's just shallots and some type of vinegar.

### Primal Dream — 2012-10-28T02:26:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60488>

One of my favorites for lightly cooked fish or scallops: I lightly saute each side in bacon fat. I add minced garlic, ginger, and rosemary, plus sea salt and black pepper. I add in some lemon juice and a tablespoon or so of water that my seaweed has soaked in. Then I let it reduce and pour it over the fish/scallops.

This is so good!

On raw oysters or other raw seafood: I squeeze lemon juice and add some tabasco. I love tamari (gluten free) and wasabi with raw sashimi fish (but I'll bet the tamari is not optimal!).

I (and my body) so love this way of eating.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T03:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60491>

Just made epipaleo stew with sirachi and grand padano stew........posted a FB pic. WOW was it good.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T21:58:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60632>

why not try what Michaelangelo's David would have used as a condiment?

Garum was a highly prized and precious(expensive) sauce of the ancient Greek and Roman empires.

Ancient Garum Recipe

Use fatty fish, for example, sardines, and a well-sealed (pitched) container with a 26-35 quart capacity. Add dried, aromatic herbs possessing a strong flavor, such as dill, coriander, fennel, celery, mint, oregano, and others, making a layer on the bottom of the container; then put down a layer of fish (if small, leave them whole, if large, use pieces) and over this, add a layer of salt two fingers high. Repeat these layers until the container is filled. Let it rest for seven days in the sun. Then mix the sauce daily for 20 days. After that, it becomes a liquid.

or just use Thai fish sauce if 35quarts of homemade garum is too much.

# Thread: I'm not making this up
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-24T20:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60009>

I was listening to a podcast interview today ...

Anyway, the interviewee says that a biochemist at the NIH decided to qualify in medicine and pursue that instead. So this doctor, as a new doctor, is going the rounds with a more senior doctor - a registrar or something. The senior doctor says to him that he's got two men who are dying, because they've been amateur mushroom collecting and made an identification error. Go and sit with them till they die and make them comfortable, he says.

So the junior doctor does. But after a while he just can't stand it. He telephones old colleagues at the NIH and says, Have you got any suggestions? Is there anything you've found that may bear on this? An old colleague tells him that it looks like alpha-lipoic acid can help. The junior doctor gives that to these men, and both survive their ordeal. But get this.

The senior doctor tears him off a strip. He says, I already told the family they'd die and there's nothing we could do, and what you did wasn't sanctioned. _Don't ever do that again!_

In other words, a doctor was actually telling another doctor that he should have let people die rather than embarrass him.

Honestly, he said that:

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio/2012/10/24/complicated-cases-naturally-with-dr-tim-gerstmar-1](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio/2012/10/24/complicated-cases-naturally-with-dr-tim-gerstmar-1)

### Optimalbound — 2012-10-24T21:21:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60020>

That's just incredible, Michael. When a doctor is more concerned about not losing face than about saving lives, something is horribly, horribly wrong.

That particular story is told at 70:05 in the podcast, by the way.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-24T23:41:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60036>

Sadly, I am not surprised one bit.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-25T00:55:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60047>

I am appalled. We really have to remember that we are ultimately responsible for our own health.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T01:16:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60051>

Considering my own n=1 not surprised.

### Destiny — 2012-10-25T03:09:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60066>

I guess I am naive. I would never consider that something like this is possible. Good to know!

# Thread: Artificial light - 1924?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T08:53:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60272>

Jack has mentioned a few times now, and again in the October webinar, that the first time humans were faced with artificial light at night was Paris streetlighting in 1924.

I can't figure this out. This picture shows Paris streetlighting in 1878 [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov\_candles\_illuminating\_Avenue\_de\_l%27Opera\_ca1878.jpg](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov_candles_illuminating_Avenue_de_l%27Opera_ca1878.jpg)

Inside a Paris music hall in 1880 [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov\_candles\_illuminating\_Music\_hall\_on\_la\_Place\_du\_Chateau\_d%27eau\_ca\_1880.jpg](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov_candles_illuminating_Music_hall_on_la_Place_du_Chateau_d%27eau_ca_1880.jpg)

And here in London, again in 1878 [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Jablochkoff\_Candles\_on\_the\_Victoria\_Embankment,\_December\_1878.jpg](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Jablochkoff_Candles_on_the_Victoria_Embankment,_December_1878.jpg)

Wikipedia, Street light:

> The first street in the UK to be lit by electric light was Mosley Street, in Newcastle upon Tyne. The street was lit by Joseph Swan's incandescent lamp on the 3rd February, 1879.\[3\]\[4\] The first in the United States, and second overall, was the Public Square road system in Cleveland, Ohio, on April 29, 1879.\[5\] Wabash, Indiana holds the title of being the third electrically lit city in the world, which took place on February 2, 1880. Four 3,000 candlepower Brush arc lamps suspended over the courthouse rendered the town square "as light as midday."\[6\] Kimberley, South Africa, was the first city in the Southern Hemisphere and in Africa to have electric street lights - first lit on 1 September 1882 .\[7\] In Latin America, San Jose, Costa Rica was the first city, the system was launched on August 9, 1884, with 25 lamps powered by a hydroelectric plant.\[8\] Timişoara, in present-day Romania, was the first city in mainland Europe to have electric public lighting on the 12 of November 1884. 731 lamps were used. On 9th December 1882, Brisbane, Queensland, Australia was introduced to electricity by having a demonstration of using eight arc lights, erected along Queen Street. The power to supply these arc lights was taken from a 10 hp Crompton DC generator driven by a Robey steam engine in a small foundry in Adelaide Street and occupied by J. W. Sutton & Co. The lamps were erected on cast iron standards, 20 ft in height. In 1888 Tamworth, New South Wales, Australia became the first location in Australia to have electric street lighting, giving the city the title of "First City of Light".\[9\]

So, what was different/what changed in 1924?

### Michael — 2012-10-26T11:42:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60280>

I expect it was a slip of the tongue - 1924 for 1824.

The first major lighting with gas would have been around that time:

> By the use of Argand burners in 1821, the lighting was improved. The first gas lamps were placed in the Place du Carrousel in 1829.

[http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v132/n3345/abs/132888c0.html](http://http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v132/n3345/abs/132888c0.html)

Lighting doesn't get really prolific or really bright anywhere till you get into the 20th century, though. And it couldn't except on the back of the incandescent lamp:

[http://americanhistory.si.edu/lighting/history/patents/ed\_inc.htm](http://http://americanhistory.si.edu/lighting/history/patents/ed_inc.htm)

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T12:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60281>

The Westminster bridge was lit in 1813.

If you're right, that it was a slip of the tongue, that would add 100 years of artificial lighting to flip epigenetic switches.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T12:34:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60282>

From your second link:

> However, he departed from this experimental path even before the patent was granted. His demonstration lamps of late December used bristol-board filaments cut in a single arch, horse-shoe shape. The bamboo filaments used in commercial lamps from 1880 to 1893 also featured a single arch. Filaments with a tight spiral did not become common in commercial lamps until Irving Langmuir developed the gas-filled tungsten lamp in 1913.

So perhaps it's the filament that Jack was referencing, rather than gas or electricity as I was thinking.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T12:44:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60283>

This is interesting: [http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm](http://http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm)

> 1882 The City Of London is the first to light streets with the Edison incandescent lamp when Holborn Viaduct and neighbouring streets are lit.

### Michael — 2012-10-26T13:14:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60284>

> **Coriander;61894 wrote:** This is interesting: [http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm](http://http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm)

Thanks.

That figures. I think London would have been the first to do something really stupid with technology by the 1880s.

By that time Napoleon had damaged France pretty badly - something like a million men lost in the failed invasion of Russia alone. And then there was the Franco-Prussian war:

[http://francoprussianwar.com/](http://http://francoprussianwar.com/)

With France exhaused, the baton for doing stupid things with techology would have been passed to Britain.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-26T13:57:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60285>

I was referring to the entire city being simultaneous electrified during all night time hours. That first happened in 1924. Light made an appearance in spots earlier.......for sure. I should have been clearer about it.......but what I am pointing out to you was when modern night time became infected with fake light and became the primordial condition that biology would begin to battle against

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T14:56:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60299>

Ah, thankyou. The can't-get-away-from-it kind of light which became the new way of life.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-26T21:26:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60350>

I'm glad you asked this Corriander. I was a bit puzzled myself while watching Downton Abby on PBS set in the early 1900s (season 1) and they had lighting in the house.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-27T02:08:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60385>

Now I've found some more confounding info.

[http://www.jstor.org/stable/2141397](http://http://www.jstor.org/stable/2141397)

1888

> ...Paris was behind her sister capitals in Europe and very far behind the larger cities of America in the public use of electric light.

The first contract to supply electricity for public lighting and power in Paris covered 1888 to 1907. There were six power companies, plus the municipal supplier, covering the city. Then in 1907 there was a new contract, in effect until 1940, during which the municipality may purchase the private infrastructure of the other six companies any time after June 30 1924.

The only significance I can ascribe to Paris/street lighting/1924 is the fact that the local govt could have owned all of it after that time.

### Primal Dream — 2012-10-27T02:30:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60389>

I live in Schenectady, NY, where Thomas Edison founded General Electric -- in the 1890s. But my guess is that the more widespread use of electrical lighting happened after WW I, which would be much closer to Dr. K's dates. think of what it took to do the infrastructure for street lights, wiring for homes, etc. I know that early street lights in many places were gas or lantern.

My house was built in 1926: the electrical system has been upgraded, but I still have a couple of rooms with just one outlet each. Which is now out of code.

But the fluorescent light did not come into widespread use until the 1930s. I think this is a more destructive form of lighting than the incandescent lightbulb.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-01T19:01:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61319>

> **Primal Dream;62005 wrote:** I live in Schenectady, NY, where Thomas Edison founded General Electric -- in the 1890s. But my guess is that the more widespread use of electrical lighting happened after WW I, which would be much closer to Dr. K's dates. think of what it took to do the infrastructure for street lights, wiring for homes, etc. I know that early street lights in many places were gas or lantern.
>
>
>
> My house was built in 1926: the electrical system has been upgraded, but I still have a couple of rooms with just one outlet each. Which is now out of code.
>
>
>
> But the fluorescent light did not come into widespread use until the 1930s. I think this is a more destructive form of lighting than the incandescent lightbulb.

For all intents, isn't an incandescent bulb the same as a candle? It's light is from burning?

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T21:16:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61382>

> **Cavemam;63053 wrote:** For all intents, isn't an incandescent bulb the same as a candle? It's light is from burning?

not burning, but you are right both incandescent and candle light are "black-body radiatiors" and are a continous spectrum.

candle color temperature is around 1900 degrees Kelvin though, and incandescents are over 3000K, meaning the have more blue.

[Colour Temp](http://http://www.3drender.com/glossary/colortemp.jpg)

you can put an incandescent on a dimmer switch and until the bulb is the same as candle light though. at 20watts, you are at approx. 2400K, about the same temperature as sunrise/sunset.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-02T01:38:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61449>

> **Entelechy;63116 wrote:** not burning, but you are right both incandescent and candle light are "black-body radiatiors" and are a continous spectrum.
>
> candle color temperature is around 1900 degrees Kelvin though, and incandescents are over 3000K, meaning the have more blue.
>
> [Colour Temp](http://http://www.3drender.com/glossary/colortemp.jpg)
>
> you can put an incandescent on a dimmer switch and until the bulb is the same as candle light though. at 20watts, you are at approx. 2400K, about the same temperature as sunrise/sunset.

ooooh, you seem to know some things. I've been pondering the effects of being in big windowed naturally lit room (by sun only) during day. Do the windows filter out anything beneficial you think? I wonder if the same melatonin effects are being had.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-02T02:14:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61458>

don't we all?

I can't say about the health benefits, just the facts as we know them.

Different coatings on glass affect transmittance, but you are safe to say plate glass blocks out all light below 350nm

UVB is 315 – 280 nm, and this is what is required for VitD production. Your window would block it.

as for melatonin.. the SCN is reset by UV at approx 470nm (or any visible light of sufficient intensity), and this frequency passes freely through glass.

I'm personally of the belief that the UV benefits us in other ways too, such as preferential microbial control, and molecular effects such as ["enzymatic photorepair"](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?43-What-the-H-is-Factor-X-Post-your-guesses&p=22496&viewfull=1#post22496) ....longer wavelengths, can counteract damage by repair of DNA.

### Adria — 2012-11-02T16:53:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61544>

Our town had electric street lights installed in 1895. Photos from that time show electric lighting indoors also.

# Thread: I hope Jack has not gotten burned out :-/

### Inger — 2012-10-26T09:27:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-jack-has-not-gotten-burned-out.4507/post-60275>

I wonder how Jack is.

He usually post every day. He have given some hints he has rough times. I hope he is well and taking some time for breathing. He have done so much this last year. I sometimes have been silently thinking I hope it is not too much for him all this. Sometimes... people are not easy to help when they are acting like you are a God too. Taking every sentence into investigation.. forgetting that Jack is also a human, asking same questions over and over again.. not bothering to look for the answer themselves in the blog etc. Demanding more and more without really listening to his core message. I think this cannot be easy. I think it must be real hard.

I wonder why people want to fight. I wonder what will it bring? I wonder if we sometimes forget we are all human and we all fail at times, fail at doing things perfect. It does not matter. In the end we are all one big family. Today I fail, tomorrow maybe you. But we could always encourage each other and love our selves and each other anyways. And learn, all the way long.

IDK I just needed to get this out of my mind.. might be a bit confusing.
