# Thread: Yellow teeth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-10-27T01:24:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60380>

Any thoughts on what causes teeth yellowing other than the typical plaque build up, coffee/tea, smoking, etc? My 12-year old daughter has never had problems with her teeth. They're strong and she has yet to have a cavity (unlike me and her brother). She's great about brushing, eats a good diet - no grains or refined sugar at all. She does have some fruits here and there and a bit of raw honey once in a while, but her diet is primarily meats, seafood, eggs, fats and veggies.

We use a homemade toothpaste made from coconut oil, olive oil, baking soda and some essential oils. I was hoping that would help, but it's been over a year that we've been using it and I see no difference.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-27T02:07:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60384>

I was told that my teeth had a yellow cast to them (before whitening) that it was from antibiotics as a child. May not be your daughter's issue but I thought I would post it here in case it might ring a bell with anyone else.

### Destiny — 2012-10-27T02:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60390>

You are right, Patty, Tetracycline antibiotic can stain teeth in children.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-27T13:53:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60421>

She has only been on antibiotics (amoxycillin) twice in her life, still more than I would have liked, but not too bad considering society's gross overuse of them.

### Glamazon — 2012-10-27T15:27:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60427>

fluorosis? too much fluoride in the tap water, etc. irreversible I think. It is interesting to note that the color of your teeth sometimes is a reflection of the color of all your bones which would indicate a systemic problem.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-27T15:54:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60430>

> **Glamazon;62046 wrote:** fluorosis? too much fluoride in the tap water, etc. irreversible I think. It is interesting to note that the color of your teeth sometimes is a reflection of the color of all your bones which would indicate a systemic problem.

I appreciate everyone's responses and hate to keep knocking them down, but I'm pretty sure it's not fluorosis. I've never given her the fluoride drops from the dentist and we've had our well water tested and it was fine.

You do have me a bit concerned about the relation to the color of bones and a systemic problem though. I did some googling and can't find much about this. Do you happen to have any links to info so I can research some more?

12 year olds are so critical of their appearances. I really wish I could figure out what was causing this and fix it for her, especially if there's some underlying cause that I need to address.

### Destiny — 2012-10-27T16:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60432>

BJK,

Not everybody has white white pearls in their mouth. Could it be that it is her natural teeth color especially when her teeth are healthy?

>
>
>

No links. I heard this as a passing conversation while working with some docs.

Sorry about the fluorosis statement. I missed it.

### janagram — 2012-10-29T02:09:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60653>

My teeth are yellow too, as were my dad's....I hate it but I drink coffee, wine and tea....which I'm sure your daughter does not. I've heard that tea is the worst. I have sent away for neem sticks which claim to do a good job cleaning and whitening. We'll see. I've had them whitened by the dentist but they turned back yellow pretty quickly. Arghhhh.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-29T02:48:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60658>

I couldn't afford the whitening treatments they offered. Recently I've noticed that they have become quite white, though. It surprised and delighted me. I believe it is the seafood I've been eating daily. I read somewhere on this site that with good nutrition, teeth whiten by themselves.

It sure isn't because I've been brushing or flossing more often. My coffee/tea consumption hasn't changed.

### Dextery — 2013-06-27T19:22:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-89078>

I have been using a two step process. First 1 tablespoon of hydrogen peroxide and 1 tablespoon of non fluoridated water and swish for a minute. Then mix equal parts Tom's non-fluoridated toothpaste and baking soda and brush for two minutes...AM and PM. Whitens up the teeth quickly. Not all Tom's toothpaste is non-fluoridated.

### fitness@home — 2013-06-28T02:33:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-89099>

> [Dextery said:](/goto/post?id=89078)
>
> I have been using a two step process. First 1 tablespoon of hydrogen peroxide and 1 tablespoon of non fluoridated water and swish for a minute. Then mix equal parts Tom's non-fluoridated toothpaste and baking soda and brush for two minutes...AM and PM. Whitens up the teeth quickly. Not all Tom's toothpaste is non-fluoridated.

This is _exactly_ what I just finished doing! I keep a shot glass on the counter and combine my hydrogen peroxide & water in it. Tom's non-fluoridated is my choice too.

### Dextery — 2013-06-28T03:39:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-89104>

**Yello Teeth**

One thing I would like to point out is that Tom's Toothpaste as well as practically all other brands contain sodium laural sulfate or Sodium Laureth Sulfate. Both seem to be poisons in quantity. And they seem to mimic estrogen.

"A report published in the Journal of The American College of Toxicology in 1983 showed that concentrations as low as 0.5% could cause irritation and concentrations of 10-30% caused skin corrosion and severe irritation. National Institutes of Health "Household Products Directory" of chemical ingredients lists over 80 products that contain sodium lauryl sulfate. Some soaps have concentrations of up to 30%, which the ACT report called "highly irritating and dangerous"."

I was looking at amazon and there are toothpastes that have Neem in them that do not have SLS and that is a selling point the manufacturers like to tout.

I also understand that SLS being a poison, must have a poison emblem on the tube. I have my Toms in front of me. There are two emblems but who knows what they mean. I plan on calling Tom's tomorrow.

Look on your shampoo bottles. Most contain SLS.

This is why we use no shampoos or soap or conditioners as there are unpronounceable ingreients in those products. Baking soda works fine....even for under arm.

# Thread: The future of education is going to meet epigenetics this way:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 78
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-27T22:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60469>

Teenagers learn best by doing things, they learn best in teams and they learn best by doing things for real â€” all the opposite of what mainstream schooling actually does. Education needs to work by pull, not push. Education should be a self-organizing system, where learning is an emergent phenomenon. The teachers union does not want us to know that. Education is great, to a degree â€¦ but it's really my creativity that's taught me that I can be much more than what my education told me I am. Creativity now is as important in education as literacy, and we should treat it with the same status. We don't because of the system of education in this country. Very many people go through their whole lives having no real sense of what their talents may be, or if they have any to speak of. We are educating people out of their creative capacities. We need to stop it and I think the internet will do just that soon enough. I know my website is going that route in healthcare.

[http://bigthink.com/collective-intelligence/higher-ed?utm\_source=Big+Think+Weekly+Newsletter+Subscribers&utm\_campaign=83ba574a7b-Sat\_10\_26\_Nantucket\_Project10\_26\_2012&utm\_medium=email](http://http://bigthink.com/collective-intelligence/higher-ed?utm_source=Big+Think+Weekly+Newsletter+Subscribers&utm_campaign=83ba574a7b-Sat_10_26_Nantucket_Project10_26_2012&utm_medium=email)

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-27T23:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60476>

I don't think I'd be so quick to blame the teachers' unions for this. Our educational system has been formed over the last couple of hundred years by trial and error. There's no real blueprint for anything at all in this life. We just make it up as we go along.

While the very basics need structure so that as the child grows and matures they have a solid foundation on which to build the rest of their education, I think you are on the right track with adolescents. By the time they reach 9th grade their brains have matured enough that what you're talking about ... allowing their own creativity to build their education ... seems like it might be a viable addition to regular "book learning."

I'm not sure how this next would fit into your scenario but on the surface I find it more than appealing. The editorial in the latest Mensa Bulletin mentions "chaordic organization" which is a "combination word melding of 'chaos' and 'order.'" Rudolph says it is a phrase that "... I believe describes our Mensa organization and a concept that, once embraced and implemented, is the path to success." She goes on to state that "Our wild diversities (opinions, backgrounds, styles) and the need for some scaffolding for those wild ideas to take form are what make Mensa a chaord."

Now that resonates with me somehow and I can see applying it to your scenario. But I need to really think on this some more. It's intriguing anyway.

â€” Count Leo Tolstoy, "Education and Children" (1862)

A government run public education system is incompatible with the values of a free society, as it's interest by natural emergence is not education but in social change and cultural conformity.

### Huck — 2012-10-28T02:04:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60486>

I owned a training company for about 10 years and my classroom strategy was controlled chaos. The more senses that a student uses, the better they learn, the better they retain what they learn, and it also gives them more "hooks" to pull the information out when they need it. There is a time for a quiet and controlled environment, but it should be as minimal as possible.

One comment that I got over and over again from students was that they didn't know learning could be so much fun.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T03:52:18+0200

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-10-29T05:37:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60678>

### Huck — 2012-10-29T14:18:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60688>

> **Nonchalant;62326 wrote:** Way back when, slaves were not sent to school. The people in power found it easier to control those who don't know much.
>
> Currently, the people in power have taken another tack. Send them all to school, but water down the curriculum. Same result.

Let me piggyback off this to give an example.

When I was teaching at my own company, I would tell students that there job was to learn and mine was to present the information in a way that they could understand it. If they didn't understand something I said, their responsibility was to ask me to explain it again and my job was to explain it in as many different ways as necessary for them to understand. If they didn't understand my 2nd explanation, then tell me so and ask if I can give an example, an analogy, tell a war story, etc.

So at 55, I'm in grad school working on a Ph D. I have a newly minted Ph D instructor that was a lousy teacher. I didn't understand something and asked her to explain it a different way. She immediately challenged my prerequisite skills and said to see her outside of class if I needed special assistance. The rest of the class was also having trouble with her, but they just shut up and played the game.

I decided that getting a Ph D wasn't for me as I didn't want to work in an environment where learning was not the highest priority. In universities, teaching is a low priority, at least in my experience. Meetings, publishing, government contracts, consulting, all seemed to be a higher priority.

I was taking 3 classes, and 2 out of 3 instructors were new Ph D's with little to no practical experience. They offered nothing I couldn't get out of a book. So that is what I did. The head of the department agreed to allow me to withdraw with no penalty even though it was after the drop date.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-29T16:59:26+0200

I just had to add that I have a sister who is a teacher, and a very good one. She gets all the "problem" kids and she really makes a difference in their lives. She expects them to learn much more than the regular curriculum. They all end up loving her, and learning that they can do anything with their lives that they work for.

That said, she constantly has to battle the principal, other teachers, and parents. I think it is a losing battle for her, but I admire her.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-29T18:18:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60733>

I have been mulling this over for 2 days trying to clarify what I wanted to say in my first post.

The point I was trying to make was that before you can allow children to frame their own educational/creative experiences they have to have a solid foundation (the scaffolding) on which to build it. The educational process provides that basis with the "basics" of reading, writing (grammar) and arithmetic. Add to that world geography, history and art and you have a nicely rounded BASIC education.

200 years ago a 6th grade education was more than sufficient for children to make it or break it out in the world. Today it's a different story. I would also like to point out that there is nothing inherently wrong with specialization. Let's face it, neurosurgery, for example, is, of necessity, a highly specialized field.

Now this is just my personal POV and you can take it for what it's worth. School's are not a free, public day care system. I am OF THE OPINION too many parents just dump their kids at school and forget about them until 3PM. They forget, or simply don't realize, they are part of that educational process. If that person thinks their child's school is falling short or stuffing their child into a (non-creative) box they have the option to put that child into a more creative-friendly environment or to GET INVOLVED in the schooling process.

Most children and adolescents need structure. And most of them will never have the necessary creativity to just dump it all and head out into the world and make a fortune. Which is why I disagree heartily with the article linked to in Jack's original post. When I went to CSU there was something like 17,000 of us crowding the campus. Out of that number I'd bet money that less than 1% would have had what it takes to follow the suggestion to quit college and just go for it and to be a success. And I don't think it has anything to do with creativity or ability. I think that's just the way people are. (side note ... I don't watch "reality" TV but I do know who Snookie is. Need I say more?)

Now about the crack about teachers unions. That one really stuck in my craw. One, my husband belonged to a union and is still a member although he's retired. So color me biased from the get-go. And he'd have been a union member even if he hadn't gone to work for the railroad because after he graduated from CSU he was offered a position as ... wait for it ... a school teacher until he found out what the pay was.

Secondly, one of my dearest friends is a school teacher. I've known her for about 25 years going back to when she just had a BA and was teaching out at Clearmont (which is so small it can't even be compared to a pimple on a flea's backside). There were times she had to go on HORSEBACK to get to some of the small schools out there. THAT'S dedication.

She decided to go back to school and went to CSU for her Master's and CU for her Ph.D. It was a proud moment and one we got to share it with her. She came back to Sheridan and COULD NOT GET A JOB even working at the local bagel store because now she was over-educated. She finally got lucky and landed a teaching position out at Big Horn which she still holds. I get to see her MAYBE for 20 minutes after Christmas and MAYBE for 20 minutes right after school let's out in the Spring. Why? Because the rest of the time she is busting her backside working at her teaching job. She travels all over the state with her students, she is so creative she takes my breath away and her students love her and several have gone on to attend major universities at her urging to follow their passion. What does she teach? MUSIC. She lives it, breathes it, eats it and shares that passion with her students.

And I bet there is not ONE person here who would work the hours she works for the pay she gets.

Blaming the teachers is like blaming the victim. And blaming the teachers' unions for your children's failings is even worse.

We pay athletes and movie actors and celebrities millions of dollars to entertain us. But we balk at paying our teachers a barely living salary. It infuriates me.

The median income in this country is around $50,000 and teachers most places make way less than that.

We expect our teachers to train and teach our children so they can make their way in the world but we don't want to pay them anything. and then some people have the nerve to blame the teachers because a federal directive aka EVERY child left behind, has added a huge burden to their already heavy workload.

I have read posts on other forums from teachers who are buying school supplies for their classrooms out of their own pockets.

But hey, let's blame the teachers. They all have such an easy life. Who wouldn't want to get paid all that money and get their summers free.

Sorry if this is not real coherent. My thought processes are not what they were before 30 years of poor nutrition, insomnia and hormonal misfires derailed them.

But that's my story and I'm stickin' to it.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-29T18:35:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60738>

Right on, Darleen. Public schooling is only a fraction of the education children need to be getting. Too many get next to no learning assistance at home (can you say TV and video games instead of math & spelling help) and then society railes about "the schools are failing." A teacher friend recently earned her master's and would you like to guess how much more she is earning every month? Yes, hold your breath - a whopping $50. Starting salaries for teachers here are around $33K. Yeah, maybe that sounds like a lot if you work in retail but it's a pittance for a professional we expect to help us produce the next generation of workers. Teachers can't bear the full responsibility of educating children but somehow that seems to be what many people expect these days.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-10-29T20:58:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60771>

> before you can allow children to frame their own educational/creative experiences they have to have a solid foundation (the scaffolding) on which to build it
> You mean define the box that they are allowed to live within? Make sure they're good at standing in lines, only allow them to socialize with other children their age, set arbitrary time limits for doing work, shame them if they are uninterested in the subject at hand, and drill and assess them on facts that they may or may not be able to see in the context of their lives at that moment?
>
>
> > the "basics" of reading, writing (grammar) and arithmetic. Add to that world geography, history and art and you have a nicely rounded BASIC education
> > Who decides which "basics" are the important ones? Who decides what perspective the story of geography, history, and art are told from? How is it decided that all kids should be ready to read at a certain level and process mathematics at a certain level at a certain age?
> >
> > I see my children learning the "basics" every day through living their lives. Working on reading when they see signs they are interested in or when they are navigating games. Learning math by playing with tape measures and measuring cups. Learning geography by looking at maps of the zoo or our town. And they're not even school age yet.
> >
> >
> > > nothing inherently wrong with specialization. Let's face it, neurosurgery, for example, is, of necessity, a highly specialized field.
> > > Ahhh, yes it is a specialized field. The problem is if one loses competence in solving problems by integrating information that lies outside their specialization. How many neurosurgeons are thinking about the gut? Or hormones? Or evolutionary biology? When specialization means adhering to a closed set of ideas or rules that serve some institution rather than the individuals involved then you're losing, rather than gaining, from specialization.
> > >
> > > Most children and adolescents need structure. And most of them will never have the necessary creativity to just dump it all and head out into the world and make a fortune.
> >
> > Do they? Or, Do adults feel the need to create structure for children and adolescents because it makes their lives easier? Less questions to answer, less time helping them get access to things that interest them, less discomfort when they challenge "authority". I think children need parental guidance from the aspect of being a partner... someone to help them understand behavior within a social context, and someone to act as a docent for the world both by helping them get access to things they're interested in and introducing them to new and interesting things. Some people will grow up to choose to go after high salaries, some will have passions that leave them with empty bank accounts but full hearts, and some will create in imaginative ways the world has never seen before, and unfortunately.... some will be told by a teacher, or counselor, or principal, or parent, that they don't have the necessary smarts or creativity to make anything of themselves in the world and they'll believe it, and give up.
> >
> > Now about the crack about teachers unions. That one really stuck in my craw.
>
> There is nothing inherently wrong with workers joining together to use a common voice. The problem is that many unions have their own political, monetary, and institutional objectives that have nothing to do with the teachers, students, or families.
>
>
>
> And I bet there is not ONE person here who would work the hours she works for the pay she gets.

I left a six-figure job to stay home with my children. I work harder now than when I had a full time job. So what. If money is important to her I'm sure she could make the decision to find a trade or career that the market would offer her more money to perform.

>
> Now this is just my personal POV and you can take it for what it's worth. School's are not a free, public day care system. I am OF THE OPINION too many parents just dump their kids at school and forget about them until 3PM. They forget, or simply don't realize, they are part of that educational process. If that person thinks their child's school is falling short or stuffing their child into a (non-creative) box they have the option to put that child into a more creative-friendly environment or to GET INVOLVED in the schooling process.

My tax bill to pay for the public school (that we don't use as I think the whole system is rubbish!) says differently. I'm fortunate that I can choose to be involved and go an alternate route. I would totally homeschool if my son were not such a social butterfly. So I get to pay for school twice.

I do think the vast check we pay the school district SHOULD give me a say and it certainly gives me an opinion on the matter. The teachers are a single symptom in the raging inflammation cascade that is public school. I think a daycare would probably do abetter job with the little ones, as they wouldn't interfere with their natural play as much.

This is one area where I think some old school anarchy might actually work. I think we should absolutely be able to put our $ somewhere else if we so choose. FREE MARKET BABY! Let the public school collapse!

The current system is never going to treat/pay teachers as if they are the professionals we want them to be. Unions are a zit that need popping. I want vouchers and I want teachers salaries cut, I want riots in the streets and a collapse of the system. Nothing short of that is going to see a material change.

AND, I think the opposite about who is serving who. We are paying a lot of $ to the school - the school should be serving the children and familes that created it, NOT the other way around. Policy, procedure, required homework, and all that institutional blather and requirements put on the families is ass backwards.

RISE UP FAMILIES!!! RISE UP CHILDREN!!! Now is the time.

(hmmm, my testosterone may be high still).

Darleen, i see you are very passionate about this topic, and you give some personal reasons for why you think as you do.

I have not attacked you, your ideas, nor people you know. I too have carefully and thoughtfully derived opinions on this subject, and wish to share them as well. The point in doing so is to question them, to test them, to evolve them to a more refined state, or to identify weaknesses in their accuracy and effectiveness.

I can only respond to bits and pieces of your post, if there is point made from the sum of the whole I am afraid I've missed it.

> **DarleenMB;62428 wrote: The educational process provides that basis with the "basics" of reading, writing (grammar) and arithmetic. Add to that world geography, history and art and you have a nicely rounded BASIC education. .
> there are numerous examples of systems where basic education is achieved without a system of classroom conformity. One very common one here is Montessori. According to many studies (J.Piaget, et al. and supported by fMRI scans) would indicate that teaching 'reading/writing/arithmetic as early as we do, is a net detriment to overall brain development. (over develops some networks) all children are of course different.. but on average the most appropriate age seems to be 11 or 12.
>
>
>
>
> > **DarleenMB;62428 wrote: there is nothing inherently wrong with specialization. Let's face it, neurosurgery, for example, is, of necessity, a highly specialized field..
> > this is specious. It is quite obvious that being a neurosurgeon requires an incredible breadth of knowledge, from a wide and disparate range of subjects. to "specialize" in medicine, one must first 'generalize'. Specialization only would be an accountant, who calls an electrician to change a light bulb, or a fry cook who can't run the range.

> >

It's getting young minds to buy into the idea of a Social Contract, before they even know what one is. As most parents don't either, because they were never really "educated", it's difficult for most to even know there is a limitation in the first place. They never question that there might be another, even superior way.
> >
> > This was my fundamental point, and the subsequent quotes by myself and others. It's self fulfilling. Those that question are mavericks.

> >
> >
> > Dare to question....
> >
> >
> >
> > DarleenMB;62428 wrote:** Most children and adolescents need structure. And most of them will never have the necessary creativity to just dump it all and head out into the world and make a fortune. .........

>
> DarleenMB;62428 wrote:** Now about the crack about teachers unions. That one really stuck in my craw.

Should comments on the internet that you don't agree with, affect you that way?

The truth is that trade unions ARE modelled after communism. In the US, it was the Communist party between 1937–1950 that drove the Labor Movement.

Right, wrong, ethical, moral, or other subjective opinions are not the point. The question is whether they are beneficial or do they stifle?

And he'd have been a union member even if he hadn't gone to work for the railroad because after he graduated from CSU he was offered a position as ... wait for it ... a school teacher until he found out what the pay was.
> I'm was also a union member for over 20 years, but this is irrelevant. One should still question whether it's optimal or even effective. Of course the Ministry of Buggy Whip manufacturing is going to support it's own interests, to the end.
>

Great teachers would receive great pay, (relative) and the poorest teachers would not have their incompetency protected by tenure. The damage caused by an incompetent teacher, multiplied by 30 or more students, is quite substantial.

>
>

I've been a guest presenter there numerous times on specialized courses for free. numerous people volunteer. I don't think we can or should speak for others on the forum.

Personally I believe it's our scientists that are the most undervalued in our society. What's a Nobel prize worth?

No one is "blaming" the teachers.. no one is blaming anyone, I don't think. Question everything..

quote by Epictetus..

To accuse others for one's own misfortunes is a sign of want of education. To accuse oneself shows that one's education has begun. To accuse neither oneself nor others shows that one's education is complete.

>

after 20 or 30 years, it's not really a delusion anymore. All those people have made it their very own reality, to their own detriment.

I recognize that your lot in life is highly influenced by luck and happenstance, and that not everyone can earn a \*\*\*\*load. What everyone can do is realize not all is equal nor just - and $$$ isn't the only measure of a life. Some people get health, some people get wealth, and some people get \*\*\*\* on. . .but rarely does anybody get it all, and some people really do get screwed. The worst part is those that 'play along' with the system never have the realization that they do not have to play the game.

I want my son to reaaaallllyyyy believe that he can actually and in reality go out in the world and not stop himself with imaginary limitations. He can influence is life, dependent on what he's willing to do FOR it.

I figure he will learn to stand in line or drink out of the water fountain in an orderly fashion should that be a requirement on the way to getting something he wants. Hell, look at all the biology and crap we are learning here and now? I would bet most here know more about how the body works than almost any public school graduate. . and we are old and sick and slow.

Imagine what things our kids can learn when we let them. I figure there are going to be millions of other school children who know the core curriculum to testing standards, and my son can hire them when that is needed.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-29T23:13:38+0200

No one said multinational corporations are OK, but they are the present reality we live with at the moment. Why so argumentative Darleen?

The Corporations are a result of the very systems that are in place. Obviously, they exist in the environment that has created them. Throughout history, every type of political organization has eventually migrated towards accumulation of capital. This is just the form that the concentration takes in a Capitalist one. Again, this in not an assessment of right or wrong. Just a description of what is. Organization of the 'common man' to 'protect themselves', via trade unions is just another part of that same beurocracy. We are a long way from 'shoveling 16 tonnes to get deeper in debt', at which time the trade unions were necessary. now, thy no longer protect but rather offer another level of protection to the very corporation itself. (much like supposed individual privacy legislation is for corporate protection, not yours... but that's another issue)

I would imagine RiverNeighbor must have worked smarter in addition to harder for >100k. I'll bet she didn't 'fit the average mould, learn her R's, get a college diploma, and join a trade union"

I would have guessed the vast majority of people here are free thinking, or they probably would not be here.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-30T05:53:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60870>

This is a very interesting and important discussion. I've worked connected to school systems in 3 different countries. My personal take is that the key to authentic education is authentic relationship. When control becomes the overriding principle, learners have two choices: rebel or comply. When authentic relationship is cultivated, the adult takes responsibility for the child but not for the learning. In other words, the teacher meets the child where s/he is and works in unison with that child to help create opportunities for understanding themselves and the world. All schooling has some sort of structure, but structure can be fluid and responsive or it can be oppressive. The Steiner/Waldorf system frames structure as rhythm. Montessori frames it as an internal order that then allows children to work as they wish. Structure, when done with the child's best interest at heart, is about creating a safe platform in which to jump (as well as a place where you can rest when you don't want to jump). Where you jump and how your rest are driven by who you are and who you find yourself unfolding to be, not be a pre-determined idea of what you should be doing. It is also a function of how good the relationship is.

I have seen 'mainstream' schools allow children a great deal of personal freedom, expression and assumed creativity. I have also seen mainstream schools stifle every ounce of creative humanity. I believe it is possible to have a curriculum while also allowing freedom, but this requires conscious attention. I want my children to learn freedom and to know freedom: intellectually, emotionally, interpersonally, spiritually. I want my children to know real connection and to realise that real connection supports freedom... and that those who seek to shut you down or make you like everyone else are not working toward real freedom and connection. And, like the serenity prayer famously says, I want them to have the wisdom to know the difference.

The public school system I see is an indoctrination institution that also teaches kids information they need to pass a test. Short of breaking it down and it collapsing the system, what do you think will change it in a non destructive way? Me? I want to edit the process so I don't pay taxes to something so ridiculous.
>

>
> A known aspect of a content and happy life is having basic needs met. After that your return on happy does not go up with income. If he can make his way in the world as a ballet dancer and be happy, then I will feel I did a good job as mom. I suspect his world will have little room for artists as a way of providing for yourself. We don't have to do just one thing. He can be prepared to make a happyness level income and also dance unrelated to that.
>

>
>
>
> Paradox;62669 wrote:** find peaceful ways of change and growth and ways to build their community.

What I observe is widespread change to a system like a corporation or public school system just does not come without destruction of the original system. I am not trolling here, I would love to consider/study examples of large systems changed without the destruction of the original. I see that peaceful change can evolve - but it's with such little swiftness, that for my lifetime and the world I leave my child in, it's theory not practicality.

I'm not saying take a sword and destroy all the teachers in a bloodbath. Many systems have been destroyed and the cogs can move onward and upward or sideways or whatever they want.

My assumption is that the world at large and abroad isn't a peaceful place, and never has been, so to expect it to behave different in the future isn't practical. I personally can not stand real conflict, but I do my best to teach my son there will be conflict - probably destructive conflict - and he's going to have to navigate that, too.

The peace we can have is from building our own world that suits us.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-30T23:05:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60997>

> **Cavemam;62679 wrote:** Many systems have been destroyed and the cogs can move onward and upward or sideways or whatever they want.
>
>
>
> The peace we can have is from building our own world that suits us.

By design, we are cogs in a machine that moves on without us. In this machine, the cogs can choose to move on without the machine being destroyed. It will attempt to recruit more cogs, it will attempt to force cogs into position where they will not function.. but cogs that can reason and think can opt out of turning. No wooden shoes required, the system will evolve to suit it's new environment. It already has. I've had similar discussions to this for decades, and never before have I seen so many with common questions, normally I've been attacked or looked at like I have two heads. Even called silly and ridiculous. The internet has allowed us to educate freely, without limits, chaotically. without someone guiding what we should think, and hoarding the information as being "too complicated/technical/over your head" This alone will spawn new ideas that have never been considered before. Before now, to access this information one would need to have spent years.... decades... following a curriculum.. being taught... even forced to think in a certain way. (isn't that what grading is?) DaVinci and company weren't famous because they thought the same way everyone else did. That should be the norm, not the exception.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-30T23:10:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61000>

> **Entelechy;62707 wrote:** Which 3 countries? it would be invaluable to get a perspective from people who have not been biased... or more accurately biased differently.
>
> most public educations systems come from common roots.

the USA, Germany and New Zealand. In Germany, I worked most closely with the US military primary & secondary school system (DODDS). I've found New Zealand to be the most open-minded, flexible and workable... it is also where my children have gone to school. We are currently facing the institution of 'national standards' for the first time in the country's history (following the US model, sadly). However, almost every single principal has come out against this change and eloquently spoken about it. It's going to happen (cogs in the machine that move without us....) but it will not be swallowed whole as the government wants. We also still have parent-run preschools that are nationwide and government funded like all other early childhood options. Lots of food for thought (most of it not epi-paleo).

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T23:20:45+0200

Love it. The biggest issue I have with the current state of public education is that not only are the kids being taught how to take a test bit they're also being taught to the lowest denominator I imagine that many good teachers are frustrated by their inability to step out side "No Child Left Behind" to help those that need help but don't qualify b/c they don't have a learning disorder or b/c they're not quite smart enough to qualify for accelerated learning. ### diane — 2012-10-30T23:36:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61003>

> **Shijin13;62715 wrote:** These days I see kids who when confronted with a real life challenge/problem can't manage to figure out how to even consider what to do and immediately seek adult guidance We got to reengage active thinking.

While I don't have children (and therefore am not familiar with the current state of the education system), I do work with and teach adults. And I see the exact same pattern in adults. They expect to be handed the answer, and don't like when the answer is "figure it out."

I have changed careers a couple of times, and one required me to take a course outside of a school or university. This was taught by a person who wasn't university educated - but by far, taught me the most. The biggest thing I learned was how to learn - and how to figure out things by myself. How to observe and use observations and informations to design action plans. And how to communicate to clients.

I wish I would have learned this more as a child - but am glad that have done this as an adult as it's never too late.

But public education system or not - I think that parents can have a huge influence on whether a child is curious and will be out of the box thinkers. Mine valued following the rules, getting good grades, and following the accepted path (and my mom was a school teacher - but definitely one that went above and beyond for the kids). So it's not just an institutional change, but cultural change as well.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-01T18:36:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61311>

Regarding Jack's link above.....

Yes, the internet is a wonderful tool. But, it's only a tool and just because someone can translate a classroom course into an online course that is available to anyone, not just those capable of attending a university, just means that the information is more accessible. Don't get me wrong, accessible is good, but the writer of the article seems to miss something that I think is important - that the value in having the information accessible is what is done with it. It's mentioned that when snail mail correspondence courses were first offered that there was a low completion rate and this is a problem to be grappled with. Who cares about completion rate? Isn't it more important whether people found something useful in the material? What if I only need a portion of the course? Sure, there will be folks who are able to improve their income or job situation because they are able to access training that an employer seeks. Yes, that is a good thing, but I'm thinking bigger. The real explosion of thoughts and ideas without constraint. Where we don't think of learning and knowledge in the context of courses, and degrees we've completed, but where we seek to maximize our cognitive potential (ugh, I'm going to have to eat fish, huh?). Where things like this are more common: [http://cosmiclog.nbcnews.com/\_news/2011/09/18/7802623-gamers-solve-molecular-puzzle-that-baffled-scientists?lite](http://http://cosmiclog.nbcnews.com/_news/2011/09/18/7802623-gamers-solve-molecular-puzzle-that-baffled-scientists?lite)

I think that the type of learning experience that one can participate in here is where a tool like the internet really shows its value. Jack isn't a teacher because someone hired him into a job to perform that function, he's a teacher because he has thoughts, and information, and experiences that people are interested in. And lots of folks aren't just interested in what he says, they're interested in the style of thinking he uses. We are all in a position to choose whether we view him as a teacher. No one is here under any form of compulsion. We have the liberty of asking ourselves, "Is there anything I can gain by studying the information this person is offering?" We don't have to play any games so that we can obtain a piece of paper that will allow us to practice a profession. This site isn't important because there is a course or coursework to complete, it is important because it moves beyond formulas and procedures. There's no need to have quizzes or tests to see if one can regurgitate answers or facts, the "test" is life. The information isn't important because someone else thinks it is, it's important because it fits within the context of a need in our real life.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T20:04:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61351>

> **KiwiLauren;62713 wrote:** the USA, Germany and New Zealand....

USA and Germany have a similar roots based on Prussian ideals of centralized schooling and national uniformity. I'm unfamiliar with NZ, but google shows that the details of NZ Education Act 1877 follows a similar path and timeline. This is to be expected, as a natural outcome of 'democracy' by majority as it has been implemented throughout the world in parliamentary and republican systems.

What I found telling in the original accusation of vilification, was that I was attacking the US system although I am from Canada, and was quoting a Russian. The US was formed at a time and by a people who where escaping the very systemic control that is being discussed. Early colonial America was the first time in the "civilized world" that a persons character and capacities could determine their social standing, rather than lineage and wealth.

They had shaken off thousands of years of subservience and created a nation of free men, whose worth was not based on a caste system of privilege. Individuality and independence were core principles or of these early Americans, and look at the great innovative and practial thinkers this period inspired..... Franklin, Paine, Jefferson, Thoreau... all at a time when there was no compulsory education. That's fine for them, but what about the lazy masses? the 1840 census figures show that there was a never before seen revolution in reading and self directed education, people wanted to, had the freedom to learn, and did so.

It was later that compulsory govt. directed public "education" was enforced, based on systems in place in Prussia and India, for very specific purposes that created what is in place today.

Villify the US system? not at all. I think you had it right... you just messed it up by copying someone else.

and of course, I must leave it with a quote.

"only here in America were common folk given a chance to show what they could do on their own without a master to push and order them about" Eric Hoffer.

### Huck — 2012-11-02T03:57:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61477>

Finally read the article. The biggest problem I see is, to a large degree, they are simply computerizing what colleges already do. The only one in the article that is doing anything really new is Sal Khan ([Khan Academy](http://http://www.khanacademy.org/)). Schools that use his classes have the students use Khan Academy outside of class as homework. This flips the typical education around by having the lecture done as homework and labs and exercises does as classwork. The teacher has a dashboard which shows her which students are having trouble and what they are having trouble with so she can give individual help.

What I dislike about college structure is that you have to take course 1 before course 2, when only a small amount of the information in course 1 is actually required to do course 2. Courses need to be structured so that I can jump right into the course I want to take with the prereq's built into the course as options if needed. With online courses there is no reason this can't be done.

The other thing I don't like about the direction is the reliance on statistics. In my former career, I had to maintain several computer certifications that were earned through computerized testing. Over the years, companies such as Microsoft, Novell, and Prosoft began hiring Ph D's in Education and Statistics to develop courses and tests. Prior to that time subject matter experts developed the courses and tests. The courses and tests developed by subject matter experts were far superior to those done by the Ph D's, but the Ph D's had statistics to show a high correlation between passing the test and learning the material. The problem was that the tests often tested on material that wasn't covered in the courses. So the high correlation was based on the prior knowledge and experience of the tester as much as the actual mastery of course material.

The other problem I see being ignored is Instructional Design. Having a fancy delivery system doesn't make up for poor design.

I earned my M Ed through an online university. The course design was pretty bad, the instructors were average at best, but I learned a tremendous amount. I was already sitting at the computer, so if I didn't understand something, I would just look it up online and then go back to the course. I started a doctorate program where the courses were broadcast from a live classroom and it was terrible. There is minimal interaction, so it would have made more sense to video tape the lectures and put them online. This was from a major state university in VA.

Some questions I have.

I earned my BA degree in early 70's. That means I had to physically go to library and type papers on a typewriter. Today you look everything up online and use word processing software with spell check and a thesaurus. If you have a question you email a classmate or look it up online. The point is you don't have to wait for help. So why are courses the same amount of time they were 40 years ago?

Why are courses 1 hour, 3 days a week or 3 hours 1 day a week? Why not do it like a real job and take 1 class for 2 weeks straight?

If you did 1 course in 2 weeks, you could take 10 courses straight (20 weeks) and then 4 weeks off, then another 10 courses followed by 8 weeks off. That would be 20 courses or 60 credit units in 1 year or a Bachelor's degree in 2 years.

You could also do this with classroom courses. You could reduce tuition costs by about 1/3rd plus you wouldn't have to build new buildings since students would only be on campus for 2 or 3 years, not 4 or 5.

Why not find the best 2 or 3 English speaking instructors on a given subject and build an online course based on their lectures? Videotape them and build the rest of the support structure. Hire some graduate students to answer online questions.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T05:33:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61924>

> **Huck;63225 wrote:**
>
> Why not find the best 2 or 3 English speaking instructors on a given subject and build an online course based on their lectures? Videotape them and build the rest of the support structure. Hire some graduate students to answer online questions.

This was the idea of the guy who started that company Great Courses (I think that's the name) - to hire the best instructors he could on a subject, video / record it and publish it. Anyone can get the course, it's inexpensive, and you can do it on your own time table. I don't think there is support to answer any questions though.

### Huck — 2012-11-04T16:43:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61988>

> **RiverNeighbor;63698 wrote:** This was the idea of the guy who started that company Great Courses (I think that's the name) - to hire the best instructors he could on a subject, video / record it and publish it. Anyone can get the course, it's inexpensive, and you can do it on your own time table. I don't think there is support to answer any questions though.

If universities adopted this model. they could get rid of a large number of their faculty and replace them with lower cost educational assistants who answer questions and grade tests.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T16:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61990>

# Thread: Anyone making progress with thyroid issues doing CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Claudia — 2012-10-28T00:45:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60482>

I am diagnosed hypothyroid and on Cytomel only. I have a conversion problem, my t4 is turning into rt3 instead of t3. I have lots of hypo symptoms, but my numbers are still in range, but t3 barely in range. I don't have Hashi's according to doc's but autoimmune runs in my family (mom had rheumatoid arthritis).

Just treating with Cytomel, but only 10 mcg once in the morning. I haven't been doing my CT, but I swam a lot every day all summer and into fall. I'm in NY. I plan on continuing to swim when I can. I have maintained my cold adapting from the spring, though. I'm never cold (interesting for hypothyroid), and I often still wear sandals and don't use a jacket when others clearly are very cold. That's why I think I didn't lose where I got to when I was doing really cold baths before summer.

I am gaining weight, probably due to low thyroid, and it's upsetting. I know it's not my diet because I eat paleo. I'm wondering if anyone has been able to lose weight or reverse thryoid doing CT. I was hoping not to be on Cytomel long term. I normally don't take any medications, not even tylenol. If I have a headache, I just deal.

I'm only about 5-10 lbs overweight right now and would like to keep it there and not keep gaining .

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-28T17:04:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60566>

I have Hashi's, and I'm interested in the replies as well.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-28T19:40:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60606>

One's inability to convert FT4 to FT3 is often caused by low iron or adrenal dysfunction. Have you checked your adrenals? Issues there often cause weight gain and inability to lose. If you are barely in range for FT3 you are operating on too little FT3. 10 mcg is a very low amount and may not be enough for you. The FT3 range should be near the top.

Once I cleaned up my adrenal problems I was able to lose the last 7#. CT also helped with that and the depressed appetite so I ended up eating less.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T19:55:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60609>

When you eat Epi paleo you are never missing any brain or thyroid specific nutrients. Conversion becomes natural.

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-28T21:01:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60621>

> **Jack Kruse;62268 wrote:** When you eat Epi paleo you are never missing any brain or thyroid specific nutrients. Conversion becomes natural.

Does this apply to someone with Hashi's as well? I take 6 mg Cytomel along with my synthroid each day. 6 mg is the max or else my T3 shoots over range, and I get hyper symptoms. Skipping the Cyomel makes me feel fatigued and sluggish.

### Claudia — 2012-10-28T22:06:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60633>

My ferritin is up high so I think my iron is good. I cook with cast iron pots every day, for years now. Not sure if that matters. I haven't tested adrenals yet but I'm on adrenal support anyway. I didn't have the money for the saliva test that is not covered by insurance. It is a priority for me to test. I have a depressed appetite anyway from doing high fat low carb and from CT I think so I'm already eating less but I'm gaining weight (slowly gained 5 lbs in the last few months).

Patty Cakes, how did you clear up your adrenal problems? You make it sound easy .

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-29T01:34:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60652>

Claudia I worked on my adrenals for 18 mo with some supplementation, enough thyroid meds, no exercise except walking, etc. I felt a little better but still had fatigue especially in the morning. Along came CT and watching light cycles and looking into the sun in the morning. At this same time I switched from NDT to T3 only and started the Circadian Method of taking T3. Six months later I tested and my adrenals were good and actually a little high in the morn which I have fixed. I am continuing the CT, lights out at night and looking at the sun in the morning, epi-paleo, etc to keep things right so I don't go back to where I was before. These are the things that helped me, YMMV. Sounds like your adrenals are your Achilles heel and once you get that healed you will be able to tolerate more T3 and hopefully feel less fatigued and just better all around!

### Claudia — 2012-11-08T00:47:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-62724>

Sorry I lost power during the recent hurricane.I'm in NY. We didn't have power for 7 days, it was so cold. I wonder how that helped my cold adapting. It was 50 degrees in the house.

Anyway, I do tolerate the 10 mcg of Cytomel I take very well and I'm sure I could take more. My doc thought I perhaps don't have adrenal issues since I tolerate it fine. That's what she said. I am taking the adrenal support anyway, she said ashwanga and rhodiola are good for thyroid, too, anyway, and can't hurt me.

She won't give me more Cytomel and says I should do 5 in the morning and 5 later in the day. I am still doing 10 in the morning. I originally started with 5 in the morning for a couple of months and that became too little so I increased it to 10. I have heard of Circadian Method of t3 (Paul Robinson????). I am scared to do it on my own. My doc said it was dangerous for the heart but she is open to hearing more about this method and clearing the rt3, which in my case is high. This doc also tested me for the MTHFR gene mutation and I am homozygous for c677T. So, now I am getting b12 shots and have to take a Rx called Metanx, which is methylfolate, for life. She also said baby aspirin. I have a high risk for stroke, heart attack. Patty Cake, I'm glad things are going better for you.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-09T03:46:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-63059>

Claudia, I have just started struggling with this myself. I'm finding that I can only tolerate about 6mcg of the cytomel (1/4 of a 25mcg tab) daily. After about a week or so I then start getting hyper and have to drop it for a day or two. I don't really feel all that much better when I do take it. My energy level still is in the basement and if it does come up a bit it disappears just as quckly.

My cortisol tested low both A.M. and P.M. Did you read Jack's latest post on adrenal fatigue? As usual it's chockablock with great info. You might also want to read the iodine thread (over in the monster thread forum). I've been upping my iodine slowly, very slowly. So far no detox symptoms.

### BenG — 2012-11-09T14:04:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-63105>

My TSH was 7.87 last December (PCP said I had subclinical hypo). Started LRx in January and CT a few weeks later. TSH dropped to 4.23 by March (PCP was amazed). I stopped CT over the summer months (and ate post-leptin reset foods (continuing LRx with BAB/no snack/no night eating)), and TSH was in the 2's in September. (Started Doing CT again.) I still have about 1/2 the weight to lose, but my internal labs (including fatty liver) all normalized except for Adrenal fatigue/DHEA didn't improve from March to Sept. (as it did from the CT) As soon as I got back in the ice, sleep improved, and I'd bet DHEA is improving. Detox took 2-2.5 weeks after the 6 month lapse (as opposed to 6 weeks last winter). I only get labs every 6 months, but things are improving again.

### MartiD — 2012-11-09T19:22:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-63157>

> **DarleenMB;64904 wrote:** Claudia, I have just started struggling with this myself. I'm finding that I can only tolerate about 6mcg of the cytomel (1/4 of a 25mcg tab) daily. After about a week or so I then start getting hyper and have to drop it for a day or two. I don't really feel all that much better when I do take it. Current major supps include Naturethroid 1.25 grains once a day, pregnenolone 75 mg twice a day, 7 keto DHEA 50 mg once a day, 25 mg DHEA once a day, progesterone cream 20 mg twice a day days 12-26 (for last 2 months), 10,000 iu vit. d3, magnesium, milk thistle, melatonin, vit. b12, vit. b complex, coq 10 and zinc.

Thinking of increasing Naturethroid, progesterone, DHEA, switching to sublingual vitamin d at 10,000 IU/day, and adding curcumin. What do you think? Any other suggestions?

# Thread: Palpitations- finally gone.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jude — 2012-10-28T08:57:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60523>

I thought I would like to share my success at finally being rid of dreadful palpitations. Noticed them 5 yrs ago and they gradually increased, especially with strenuous activity. Started Leptin reset last Nov and they hit me with vengeance. Started to ruin my life, little walking & climbing stairs for a couple of mths...disaster for me as I'm very active.

Dr K assured me it was a lack of DHA/iodine causing them and to eat more seafood.(this is before the epi paleo release). I started adding 2 to 3 meals a week and I think I noticed some improvement, enough to want to keep going. Then epi paleo was released, and I finally decided to try it and go all out on Sept 1.

I really hit the tinned fish twice daily,sardines and salmon mainly, went through heaps of lemons! We can buy fresh fish once a week here. Prawns are 33-36$ kg, salmon 36$ ! Lamb consumption halved instantly-hubby watched in awe. We are both going to try prawn smoothies soon!...Discovered I could eat the fresh salmon very lightly cooked, but haven't been game to try Inger's raw recipe yet.

Once I noticed that the palpitations really were decreasing I did 2 weeks solid- fish only. This was intentional/accidental sort of, as I was away with sick friend in city hospital-hopeless cafeteria and no local shops close by, so canned sardines was my fare for a week!. I noticed at the end of the week that I could climb the stairs quite well- surprise, surprise. So, back home, kept up the fish only for another week. That was the big kicker for me as there was an amazing downturn and complete disappearance by the end of the second week. I kept testing myself with exercise to prove to myself that I was improving, running up and down stairwells when I could find them.

Long story short-in ER late Aug with severe palps -now late Oct, with epi paleo and basically they're gone. Yes!.

So the question is... am I fully tanked with DHA/iodine or just out of the woods? I'm not sure- I note that I do need to keep to 2 lots of fish daily because on days away and only having 1 fish meal(breakfast) I can notice I'm inclined to develop a faster pulse the following day.

I put in 3 huge, strenuous days in my garden this week and worked like I could 20yrs ago, with no palpitations-WOW..I haven't been able to do that for 5 yrs........wonderful.

Anyhow, it's great that such a 'simple' food change can bring about such an amazing difference in ones' life. What a great result and a big thanks to Dr K and his protocol.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-28T09:15:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60524>

That's a brilliant outcome Jude! Good on you for sticking with the sardines for that first week; what a breakthrough!

### Jude — 2012-10-28T13:22:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60528>

Thanks Coriander, was really looking for a change in seafood by the end of the week. Fellow residents at hostel I stayed at, watched me eat like this for the week and wanted to know why. one of the supervisors heard all about epi P and the Leptin reset and started it while I was there!!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T14:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60531>

Jude you just gave proof of concept to what I wrote in BG 14. Once your brain is filled with its brain specific nutrients it wont have to usurp nutrients from the heart to function well and cause its dysfunction. You must have the brain injury first to get the disease. Moreover, lots of things cause the injury to begin with.” The injury must exist in the brain before the illness appears. This is a foundational concept in my theory

**For those who dont understand how brain function can cause heart rhythm issues here is the playbook again for you to review:**

The brain allows us to sense the epigenetics alterations in our environment well. I want you to now channel what I said about the heart and brain in Brain Gut 13 with respect to how the Na/K ATPase is affected in metabolic terms in all tissues. Then, I want you to think about the number one killer of all humans today: Heart disease. How does heart disease tie into this theory of whole body physiology?

If you begin to alter your blood glucose because of dietary or any other epigenetics signal (light or temp) and cause inflammation at a cellular level by eating badly or not being mindful of circadian cycles, you start secreting large amounts of insulin from beta cells to deal with it. This alters your Magnesium levels which activate the metabolically efficient Na/K ratio because of DHA as laid out in BG 13. When we have an altered Na/K (low) ratio, this in turn, simultaneously cause brain metabolism to ratched down because its function is directly coupled to the efficiency of the Na/K ATPase for our species. As the brain’s metabolism falters, it begins to shunt energy to itself at the determent of other organs. It robs Peter to pay Paul. This physiologic bio energetic “stealing” of energy alters functions everywhere in the body to satisfy the brains needs. As other organs fail slowly under this physiologic directive this further alters brain function from the excessive 60% energy boon created by the DHA/ Na/K ATPase, which results in your feeling less well with less energy and you have a chronic cognitive haze. Is this sounding familiar to anyone?

It is the modern human condition that most of us exist in. As this goes on chronically, brain function is eroded (the real source of adrenal fatigue in the PVN) because there is an excess of Na ions, which alter our homeostatic brain controls of the renin angiotensin system and this alters our Blood Pressure. The renin angiotensin system target organ is the kidneys. The kidneys control the electrolyte balance of the serum and ultimate of the CSF (ultrafiltrate of the serum) to further alter the function of our brain.

This slow erosion of our kidney function goes on undetected and slowly undermines our electrical systems balance. This implies that our declining kidney function, directly efffects our electrical system of our heart (conduction system AV node and sinus pacemaker) to become less optimal or “foggy” in its function too. The relays in the hearts electrical system is sticky and this cause all kinds of unusual heart rhythms. This shows you why declining brain function always correlates with declining heart function. Modern medicine has never made this link but they know it exists. These organs are chemically and electrically tied together by the action of the Na/K ATPase, DHA and iodine at a foundational level physiologically in humans in our cell membranes. This all results in the many types of cardiac dysrythmias we see clinically. This is an early sign of major bio-energenic shifts in cells all tied to oxidation.

When this goes on further brain function erodes and we can not sense it well. We, in essence, lose our primal sense of well being and eventually we develop an inability to sense vascular trouble before it happens clinically. This leads to a heart attack/palpatations/or heart failure.

### kathylu — 2012-10-29T21:36:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60780>

Thanks for this thread everyone! My husband recently (six months now) has been noticing a heart rhythm irregularity. The palpitations occurred mostly at night and would wake him from a sound sleep. Went to the "best" cardiologist in DC, who told him he was having premature atrial beats, and that no one knows what causes them. The doc suggested that he give up all caffeine. Since my hubby infrequently drinks anything with caffeine, he wasn't too hopeful there would be a positive effect and there wasn't. Nothing changed. I suggested that he start taking magnesium, Lugol's iodine and eat more seafood. All based on Dr. K's ideas of course. About 3 weeks later he mentioned to me that he hadn't had any night time palpitations for a few days and was sleeping straight through the night. The problem seems to be completely gone now. Hallelujah!

### Michael — 2012-10-28T10:27:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/john-jewitt.4535/post-60526>

_The Adventures of John Jewitt_. This short narrative is available in several formats from Project Gutenberg.

It's interesting from the anthropological point of view, but among other things there are some descriptions of food. Basically, in this area it seems to have been almost all seafood, including a lot of roe, and everything - even the berries (and by the sound of it even the blubber!) - was dipped in train oil.

[http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/38010](http://http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/38010)

# Thread: Hands and feet always very cold except for after 7-9pm?

**Posts:** 8

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-and-feet-always-very-cold-except-for-after-7-9pm.4551/post-60684>

Hi guys,

Like the title says may hands and feet are always really cold throughout the day (I have to wear gloves all the time) except when it gets to around 8pm they seem to warm up somewhat. I have noticed this more so since CT.

What is causing this phenomenom? I suspect thyroid/ T3 issues but I dont understand why my thyroid would suddenly switch on at night.

Could gut inflammation during the day play a role on this (from constant bloating after eating?)? Maybe blocking T4 conversion or something?

What do you think?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-and-feet-always-very-cold-except-for-after-7-9pm.4551/post-60694>

### ColdBren — 2012-10-29T22:56:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60791>

I just started taking prometrium and it seems to be lifting my mood and obliterating my anxiety, just like zoloft did, but without the side effects. Does progesterone actually increase seratonin? If so, why doesn't anyone talk about this when discussing ways to raise seratonin. It seems so easy.

### shah78 — 2012-10-29T23:08:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60794>

dear bren, please remember the seratonin-depression connection is a unproven fantasy concocted by Big Pharma in order to convince allopathic physicans to hand out ssris like candy. My depression went away when I started eating 60% of my calories from animal fat. It took less than one week.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-29T23:25:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60800>

I think that when the brain gets what it has been lacking, you will get a 'high'. I sure had one when starting paleo, and I had it again when I started eating seafood daily. In fact, I get a charge out of CT also, just in a different way. Glad you are seeing some relief!

### ColdBren — 2012-10-29T23:45:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60801>

My issue has never really been depression, but as for anxiety, there is no doubt ssri's have always relieved the symptoms.....though I don't know the mechanism and I know from the info on here it is not a good drug. I just can't believe how fast prometrium worked. BTW, the creams have never had this kind of effect. So again I am just wondering, does progesterone increase seratonin? Because we can all agree seratonin does influence feelings of well being correct?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-29T23:49:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60802>

### shah78 — 2012-10-29T23:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60809>

Taking (ZOLOFT)ssri does,, did influence feelings of well being. but so does TCH.But they both just mask some other issue that is causing a low seratonin issue.Low Vit d, low cortisol, low thyroid, low "anything". Sort of like the statin- cholestoral issue. High cholestorol is a sympton of something else. In Paleoland it would be inflamation.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-10-30T00:00:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60811>

### ColdBren — 2012-10-30T00:05:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60812>

Dr K- I think you are right about my issue always being my pg/e2 ratio, at least in terms of chronic anxiety....shocker, you are right again  But a side note to anyone who may see this with nocturnal panic attacks, after testing sistergroks theory for many months, salicylates are the cause of these nocturnal central sleep apnea episodes.....turns out they weren't really panic attacks at all.

>
> Melinda;62607 wrote:** Thanks for the real time update. It's hard to picture what it's like to be in the way of such a massive storm.

having been through it several times before - you can't imagine it unless you've lived through it. the difference is when I lived in Guam and we had 11 Super typhoon (Cat 5 hurricanes) in one season, the island was always prepared. the houses were built in such away that the winds weren't an issue, and while we may have lost power for several weeks, we didn't loose water. they knew how to deal with it on a regular basis b/c they were a part of daily life for 9mths out of a year, you knew you were gonna get hit. In NJ and NY they've never seen anything like this in the past 60yrs (no one alive remembers the last time a hurricane - blew threw and brought the city to its knees) the infrastructure isn't built to withstand a direct hit... add a Cat1, plus high tide and a full moon - and that's a lot of water going into a city that's surrounded by water on 3 sides...

the best you can do is prepare for the worst, and hope for the best, and get out of the way if you're in low lying areas if you can. That's whats different about a hurricane hitting the mainland of the US - you can get out of the way if you choose too. when you live on an island that's 9 miles long, and btwn 3-6miles wide at given points - you can't go anywhere - you can't afford a $3K plane ticket to the Philippines, or Korea, or Japan - and most like the typhoon you're trying to get away from will just end up their any way - so you ride it out...

This is mother nature's way of reminding us we don't control the environment to the extent we think we do...

# Thread: Celiac and seasonality
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lyndra — 2012-10-30T00:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/post-60827>

Just saw this at NYT - [http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/10/29/really-celiac-disease-is-influenced-by-season-of-birth/?smid=tw-nytimeshealth&seid=auto](http://http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/10/29/really-celiac-disease-is-influenced-by-season-of-birth/?smid=tw-nytimeshealth&seid=auto).

Reportedly, babies born in spring/summer are more likely to develop celiac disease. They theorize it is because when babies are typically weaned at 6 months and start eating gluten, it will be wintertime when colds & flu are more common, thus depressing the babies' immune systems. No mention about what the mother is exposed to when she's pregnant during fall, winter & early spring when she is probably not eating based on seasonal food availability.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-30T01:01:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/post-60830>

I tweeted this story this afternoon..........

### Lyndra — 2012-10-30T01:46:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/post-60838>
