### TheKid — 2012-10-31T17:09:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61089>

My 22-month-old is due for some vaccines in the coming couple of weeks. I was wondering if anyone has a protocol they use in order to help immediately chelate out any toxins.

I was thinking to do a week of cilantro smoothies before and after.

Any suggestions?

### Jude — 2012-11-01T05:17:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61215>

In Dr Sherri Tenpennys' book Saying no to Vaccines" on page 295 there is pre vaccination prep given for infants & toddlers up to 30lbs, toddlers from 31 t0 50lbs & for children 51 to 100bs, plus whole lot of other helpful hints.

### Lanie — 2012-11-01T10:20:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61229>

> **caveman;62945 wrote:** Quick Question?.....
>
>
>
> Why would you want vaccines?

You sound young.

Do you know what an iron lung looks like? No, me neither. Your grandmother might though. You should ask her why we use vaccines.

### TheKid — 2012-11-01T10:57:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61230>

Yeah, I'm all for vaccines. I'm not trying to let my daughter get polio. I just want to keep them spaced and I want to take every precaution to minimize negative outcomes. That's why I'm interested in any types of protocols that can be used Pre and post vaccination to help in this regard.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-01T11:58:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61235>

there is good and bad in people like there is in medicine.......but when you view everything as an extreme you really lose perspective of the gains you can make.

### Inger — 2012-11-01T12:51:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61246>

> **Jack Kruse;62965 wrote: there is good and bad in people like there is in medicine.......but when you view everything as an extreme you really lose perspective of the gains you can make.**

I loved this..!

I myself do like this according to vaccines; I would never take one only because my doc tells me to, but I go and search all about the certain desease and then I do a sober desicion. Until this day this made it very easy for me to know what to do. I am not telling what my desicions was in particular situations, everyone should search for themselves.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-01T15:03:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61262>

And it isn't just about the vaccine itself, it's about the state of the body it is going into.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T17:17:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61293>

To each their own...but I'd rather risk the childhood disease (look up polio and arsenic) and treat the treatable, rather than inject unknown quantities. Among other things, the introduction of a respiratory or GI pathogen intramuscularly has to affect the response. (Water is necessary when introduced via the GI trac but injected IM, well, you wont appreciate it).

The recent whooping cough epidemic in the PNW affected primarily vaccinated kids.....hmmm. My fathers oncologist, when asked about SV40, said his cancer did show hallmarks of it...but it was never tested. No point.

Yes, a loss of one horse in a 1000 horse stable may be acceptable, but when that one horse is your only one...and each child that reacts has only "one horse".

Feed your kid well, skip the vaccines.

My $0.02.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-01T22:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61395>

> **TheKid;62808 wrote:** My 22-month-old is due for some vaccines in the coming couple of weeks. I was wondering if anyone has a protocol they use in order to help immediately chelate out any toxins.
>
>
>
> I was thinking to do a week of cilantro smoothies before and after.
>
>
>
> Any suggestions?

This is obviously a contentious issue, but I don't hear you asking for our opinions about vaccination, only about suggestions to reduce the toxins associated with it.

If I were going to vaccinate my children, I'd be thinking not only about natural chelators, like cilantro, but also about methylation and what supports it. So I'd probably be thinking about upping B vitamins and including PRP's, especially if your child is not still breastfeeding. I'm guessing your child is healthy... gut health would also be essential (but you're probably already consuming bone broth and the like regularly). I know our paediatrician gives homeopathic support to those parents who choose to vaccinate, but I'm not sure what that protocol is (I'm guessing it would be easy to google.)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T00:27:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61426>

I allow this extremism.......but I dont advocate it 100%. There is limits to every therapy good or bad. Some vaccines are vital.......other not so much. Build your knowledge to discern the difference. If you dont only you and your family loses.

### Jude — 2012-11-02T01:57:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61455>

So the question I have is,

Is there any risk to a brain which is optimal,ie full of DHA/Iodine, + circadian rhythm OK, and perfect diet ?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T02:31:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61463>

depends upon what vaccine we are talking about.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-02T02:35:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61464>

> **Jack Kruse;63211 wrote:** depends upon what vaccine we are talking about.

Ok here's 3 I'd love to hear your thought(read your thoughts)

Gardasil

Smallpox (original Wyeth vaccine that's been in cold storage for the past 40 yrs)

DTAP

### Jude — 2012-11-02T02:52:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61465>

> **Jack Kruse;63211 wrote:** depends upon what vaccine we are talking about.

So having the context of optimal brain health(DHA/Iodine), no leaky gut ,great circadian rhythm, in other words having a totally optimal baby/child; there is still a perceived need to be careful?..........so these pre and post vaccination schedules almost become a requirement then?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T04:17:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61481>

Gretch.......small pox is now just about eradicated because of the vaccine. The other two I would pass on........Would I pass on Hep B vaccine? nope. How about polio? nope........context. Not all things we do in medicine is bad........In 1900 more people died of infectious diseases than most anything else. Now that is not true. Vaccines have helped in many cases. In other cases they do not help and can hurt. You have to find your personal ground with respect to them.

### kathylu — 2012-11-02T05:31:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61489>

> **TheKid;62808 wrote:** My 22-month-old is due for some vaccines in the coming couple of weeks. I was wondering if anyone has a protocol they use in order to help immediately chelate out any toxins.
>
>
>
> I was thinking to do a week of cilantro smoothies before and after.
>
>
>
> Any suggestions?

I'm actually going to try to answer your question without slipping into diatribe.

Before my son receives any vaccination I make sure that:

1\. He is well rested and well nourished.

2\. I supplement with vitamin D and vitamin C (based on weight) and make sure he gets 2 egg yolks per day (vitamin A) for a week prior to vaccination.

3\. I make sure he is showing no signs of illness before the shot.

4\. I choose vaccines that are mercury and aluminum free if possible.

5\. I make green smoothies for detoxification for several weeks afterward (continue vitamin supplements for several weeks too)

I hope this is helpful.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-61490>

>
> 3\. I make sure he is showing no signs of illness before the shot.
>
> 4\. I choose vaccines that are mercury and aluminum free if possible.
>
> 5\. I make green smoothies for detoxification for several weeks afterward (continue vitamin supplements for several weeks too)
>
>
>
> I hope this is helpful.

It is to me!

### BJK77 — 2012-11-05T21:31:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-post-vaccine-protocol.4594/post-62244>

> **Jack Kruse;63229 wrote:** Gretch.......small pox is now just about eradicated because of the vaccine. The other two I would pass on........Would I pass on Hep B vaccine? nope. Vaccines have helped in many cases. In other cases they do not help and can hurt. You have to find your personal ground with respect to them.

Jack - what is your opinion of the meningococcal conjugate vaccine? Anybody else have thoughts on this one? It's the only one that has me somewhat second guessing my decision.

# Thread: Evolution of vision
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-vision.4609/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-01T08:44:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-vision.4609/post-61225>

> All known visual pigments in Neuralia (Cnidaria, Ctenophora, and Bilateria) are composed of an opsin (a seven-transmembrane G protein-coupled receptor), and a light-sensitive chromophore, generally retinal. Accordingly, opsins play a key role in vision. There is no agreement on the relationships of the neuralian opsin subfamilies, and clarifying their phylogeny is key to elucidating the origin of this protein family and of vision. We used improved methods and data to resolve the opsin phylogeny and explain the evolution of animal vision. ...

[http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2012/10/25/1204609109.abstract?sid=1016fb84-2c79-4871-94db-72128eccd02e](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2012/10/25/1204609109.abstract?sid=1016fb84-2c79-4871-94db-72128eccd02e)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-01T12:08:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-vision.4609/post-61237>

Nobel was recently awarded for G coupled protein science..........This protein family exploded after the great death of the Cambrian explosion......molecular O2 was the key missing ingredient and plant re growth was the defining moment for life's rebirth and the re evolution of eyes.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-09T00:20:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-vision.4609/post-63017>

The Cambrian explosion was due to oxygen and also DHA.

"Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) provided the basic

membrane backbone of the new photoreceptors that converted photons into electricity

laying the foundation for the evolution of the nervous system and the brain."

So vision was the foundation for the creation of the brain? Eyes developed and the brain followed?

So I guess Jack's "48% of the brain is tied to light" might even be an understatement, if you consider indirect ties.

[The Role of Docosahexaenoic and Arachidonic Acids as Determinants of Evolution and Hominid Brain Development](http://http://www.londonmet.ac.uk/library/a61437_3.pdf)

I was re-reading BG5 which led me to the above document.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-09T00:46:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-vision.4609/post-63021>

bingo.........Nonchalant.......BG 5 is an epic post with massive importance.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-09T01:07:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-vision.4609/post-63027>

So electricity in the brain is not just along the nerves and synapses? DHA (and its gamma coherence) is everywhere in the brain and eyes. The eye converts the light to electricity.

I bet scans of people who eat lots of DHA (and AA) really light up. And not just the brains.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-09T17:16:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-vision.4609/post-63127>

So if the brain started with vision, it really makes sense to base its timing on lightness/darkness. Light is the original 1, darkness is the original 0. Let there be light.

# Thread: Study suggests antibiotics might be another suspect in honey bee die-off
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-01T08:51:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/post-61226>

> The gut bacteria of honey bees have acquired several genes that confer resistance to tetracycline, a direct result of more than five decades of use of antibiotics by American beekeepers and a potential health hazard for bee colonies, a new study by Yale University researchers show. ...
>
>
>
> Moran speculated the new drug \[Tylosin\] may have disrupted beneficial gut bacteria in bees, contributing to the health problems of the bee colonies.
>
>
>
> She also noted that antibiotic resistance genes are able to jump between unrelated bacterial species and that the genes found in the gut of honey bees were identical to those found in bacterial pathogens in humans and isolated from livestock.

[http://news.yale.edu/2012/10/30/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-honey-bee-die](http://http://news.yale.edu/2012/10/30/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-honey-bee-die)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-01T11:59:45+0200

### Inger — 2012-11-01T12:25:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/post-61241>

I believed for long already, this is the main reason for the honey bees dying.

I buy only wild honey, what means the bees get no sugar fed in winter. The beekeeper takes away only a part of the honey and let the rest for the bees to feed on in winter. This makes superior and very healthy honey and healthy bees too. This honey is expensive though but I use very little anyway. Even Organic honey is given sugar in winter today. It is very hard to find "wild honey" anymore that is fed nothing else then their own honey.. because most beekeepers wants to have the most possible honey from their business and think little about the poor suffering bees. I personally know beekeepers and they have bees only for their own use and they feed the bees sugarwater too! They all feed their bees antibiotics and stuff too, because the bees get sick otherwise. No wonder, by pure white sugarfeed all winter long...?!

Sometimes I really cannot believe the stupidness posessing people. It is like.. people don't THINK.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-01T12:40:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/post-61243>

no Inger........they really do not think well when their brain is slowly destroyed by modern day luxuries.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-01T12:43:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/post-61245>

Recently we've been getting local honey at a farmers market my mil goes to. It's the same farmer she gets eggs from for eggs. She brought us the last delivery of eggs for the season about 3 wks ago Honey us only available from may -aug. the farmer leaves them alone from sep -April he only moves the hives to probed shelter during bad weather. We get a big gal jar. What we have us what we eat (I actually don't eat it except when I make Ingers graved lachs)

### Inger — 2012-11-01T13:02:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/post-61247>

@ Jack.. yes.. it is sad.. The good thing is, no one needs to destroy their brain but can heal it instead if they choose another path then the common.. The holy trinity! I tell my parents this again and again, and always when I do something for them like MHS or seaweed chocolate or whatever healthy stuff they love it! And have candels on when I do it.. but when I am not there, they fall into their old patterns FAST! Like they have no own power at all... OMG it almost drives me crazy it is the same with my sis and brothers. They all love to hear and do when I am there, they are so very intrested and gets really motivated... but when alone? That is the issue. They have not motivation alone without me. But I can no way be by them day and night. I have given it up and take care of me. Once in a while I do something for them but only when I have the power to do so. I need to be more aware of my power resources and use them wisely.

@ Shijin, have you asked if the beekeeper feed his bees sugar in winter? If, it is not healthy honey really.. you could ask and then tell him he do not need to do that, but could leave half of the honey in, and then take the double price instead! Tell him you will buy more then.. That way you would get really healing honey!

I am thinking, maybe we need to tell them how bad the sugarfeed is, that there is another way to do it, maybe they start to think.. and soon the honey-bee problem is solved! Yes!

### Michael — 2012-11-01T13:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/post-61248>

And the healthiness of the honey aside, there's the huge issue of bees as pollinators.

Everywhere you look there's short-term thinking.

I was interested to hear that biologists had tried to breed some of the "antinutrients" out of a crop. I can't remember which - quinoa perhaps. Anyway the (predictible) result was that the pests were all over the crop.

Of course they would be: what are "antinutrients" to us are chemical defences to the plant. But then extrapolate that to agriculture in general and ask yourself: If plants come armed with their own chemical defences, why are artificial pesticides necessary? The answer to that is likely to be monoculture and poorly designed growing systems in general.

IIRC, Joel Salatin says he has only had to use vetinarary treatments on animals twice - and in both cases he realized later it was his own fault; it was mistakes to do with the living conditions of the animals or their diet.

### Michael — 2012-11-01T13:11:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-suggests-antibiotics-might-be-another-suspect-in-honey-bee-die-off.4610/post-61249>

It makes an interesting comparison with the woman who gave herself a load of problems with a weight-reduction plan who was on Abel's show. IIRC, Jack commented that everyone is looking for a quick fix. It's the same here.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-01T16:11:46+0200

# Thread: Changing hunger  food consumption
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/changing-hunger-food-consumption.4617/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-11-01T17:45:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/changing-hunger-food-consumption.4617/post-61302>

Anyone finding their hunger / food consumption is changing.

I'm finding that I'm not able to eat as much in one sitting any more... my BABs have increased in Fat, and decreased in protein. yesterday I could barely eat 3 eggs for breakfast, and some BPC. For Lunch I ate a can of smoked oysters, 1 hardboiled egg, some seaweed salad and avocado, and for dinner I had baked cod w/cabbage.

What's interesting is suddenly w/in the past week or 2, my meals have suddenly become smaller and not by design. its just happening, there are days, that If I don't pay attention its close to 2pm and I haven't had lunch

I'm trying to figure out what's going on. has my body discovered "latent" fat stores? is it finally re-wiring?

My CT of recent has been limited to using Ice packs around my torso, either when I first get up and I'm making breakfast, and packing lunches or after work usually after a work out or during dinner, its not every day yet but I'm working back to that.

I did just bump up my Armour from a 1/2 grain to 1 grain. I'm still having the periods of HOT, but since upping the Armour I've also started having periods of cold especially at night.

I've also upped my Pg to 10% gel - currently I'm taking 100ml/day (currently finishing off my 5% gel so 1.0ml am and pm)

is this a convergence of changing meds, or is this a convergence of my diligence over the past year and change? or a combination of both? instinct says its all diligence - I doubt I'd have this type of response from upping thyroid and Pg in a weeks time

anyone have an idea or similar experiences?

### ealachan — 2012-11-01T18:10:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/changing-hunger-food-consumption.4617/post-61309>

I definitely can't put away nearly the same amount of food I used to be able to. I used to be able to easily eat a pound of meat in a sitting, along with sides. Now I'm usually FULL after about half a pound. Like, verging on uncomfortably full.

I'm not on any kind of BHRT or thyroid stuff, so I can't speak to that...but I definitely started to notice the difference after I started making myself eat lunch again (even though I'm not usually that hungry for it). I went for several months eating just a BAB in the mornings and a big dinner at night, and while I could comfortably make it through the day without eating, I was definitely eating a LOT more at breakfast and dinn,er AND I was getting hungry a lot more. I've adjusted my breakfast to more fat and less protein, and added a small protein lunch (can of olive-oil-packed tuna, sometimes some seaweed), and now I find that I get full much more quickly not only at dinner time, but at breakfast as well.

edited to add: this is with no significant change in activity level at all either, though come to think of it, the timing DOES coincide with my starting to do cold-air CT regularly.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-01T18:30:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/changing-hunger-food-consumption.4617/post-61310>

I've noticed the same thing the last few days, Gretchen. Decrease in appetite and the amount of food I can put away at each meal. Oddly enough, I'm also on NT and started compounded T3 and oral Progesterone a few weeks ago.

I thought my appetite changes might have been due to just starting a cycle for the first time in 8 months! Though if I remember right, I actually used to get MORE hungry with my cycles before. I'm still feeling hot most of the day too, even at night. We're keeping the house around 62 and DH had to add an extra blanket to the bed the last 2 nights. Before that we just had one light blanket and the top sheet. Both nights I've had to kick the extra blanket off. I'm burning up!

I had one morning where I couldn't finish my BAB, but for the most part I can still get that down no problem and it's still averages 60 gm protein.

Just not sure what's going on . . . but I did have new labs run last week so I'm anxiously awaiting them to see what they show.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-01T19:35:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/changing-hunger-food-consumption.4617/post-61334>

DH and I used to eat a steak apiece. Now we split one. A can of sardines is perfect for lunch these days. Food is getting simpler and simpler. I'm expecting to start tearing into a raw fish with my teeth any day now instead of going for sushi. We went to our local Mexican restaurant the other night and I polished off a cereal bowl full of shrimp ceviche for dinner. Yum! Mostly seafood nowadays and I love it. Thanks to Jack, et al.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-02T00:28:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/changing-hunger-food-consumption.4617/post-61427>

It's so funny that you say this, because as I type, I'm finishing my dinner of shrimp sauteed in olive oil with spinach. About 4 oz. of shrimp and a cup or so of spinach, a tbsp. or so olive oil. But when it got to be "dinnertime" (these days, around 4:30! lol), I thought...you know what, I've been really good on this epi-Paleo thing, I'm gonna go out & buy myself a huge steak, a huge salad, etc.

But when I started to leave my house, I thought, well let me check my house...yeah, I have shrimp, spinach, garlic, basil, sauerkraut. That sounds good. And it's about 1/3 of the amount of food I was GOING to eat. And I'm completely satisfied, till tomorrow morning's BAB.

So yes, it's happening to me too.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T00:31:02+0200

# Thread: Pituitary adenoma - mri findings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-11-01T19:09:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61325>

So...after my last thyroid labs my doctor realized there has been a trend in my labs since I've been seeing him (about a year) in that despite continued low freeT3 and low freeT4 labs I also have VERY low TSH. He had me go in for an MRI to rule out a pituitary adenoma. I saw him today and got the results. He ordered a bunch more labs and is also referring me to an endocrinologist. So, just wondering if anyone has any input about this. I'm going to copy the MRI findings below.

Findings: There is abnormal superior convexity of the pituitary on the left side. There is well circumscribed area of hyper intense T1, hypointense T2 signal within the left side of the pituitary measuring approximately .9 x .8. x .5 cm. The floor of the sella is nondepressed and overall size of the sella appears normal. Signal in the clivus and sphenoid sinus are unremarkable. The optic chasm and pituitary infundibulum are nondisplaced. Normal vascular flow voids are visible in the internal carotid circulation, without evidence of cavernous sinus mass. There is no suprasellar mass. The imaged portions of the brain show no evidence of hydrocephalus or mass effect, with preserved gray-white differentiation and normal brain volume. On post-contrast portions of the examination, there is slightly heterogeneous appearance to the enhancement within the left side of the sella. No other abnormal enhancement is seen within the brain.

Conclusion: Abnormal signal, morphology and enhancement within the left side of the pituitary concerning for a microadenoma or small area of prior hemorrhage.

So now I'm just wondering if there is more info I should arm myself with before seeing the endocrinologist? Anyone have any experience reading these things?

### vkiernan — 2012-11-01T19:44:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61337>

No experience here, just wishing you good luck!

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-01T19:51:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61341>

> Abnormal signal, morphology and enhancement within the left side of the pituitary concerning for a microadenoma or small area of prior hemorrhage.

It means that spot in your brain is not working correctly and he suggests that it's either b/c of a tumor that's not actually brain tissue or b/c you had a brain bleed there at some point that damaged the area of brain tissue. Everything else in your brain appears normal.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T19:52:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61342>

Good luck as well. As a neurosurgeon, I'd expect Dr K to be THE man to ask...hopefully he will see this.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-01T19:57:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61344>

Trisha, Last fall I had a pituitary MRI too. I don't have any comment on their findings, but I can share with you what my plan was if there had been anything remarkable on my MRI.... Okay the kids are pulling me away so I'm going to have to come back later but my quick summary is that my plan was to call Dr.K and plead to find a way to talk to him about it (this was before he was offering educational consults). Low TSH isn't just dependant on the pituitary - don't forget hypothalamus. Who read your MRI? Radiologist, or Neurosurgeon? You might also want to be considering a second opinion on the reading of the MRI.

I'll be back later...

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-01T21:38:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61387>

Thanks for the input. The physician that interpreted the MRI is a DO in neuroradiology.

I wish I had this when I had a couple consults with Dr Kruse a couple months ago!

I just hope these results can help the doctors get me feeling better. So fatigued, achey, and blah (not to ,ention the weight gain) despite a clean diet, periodic CT, and minding my light cycles. I have 4 little boys to be taking care of and can't afford to be feeling like this.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-01T22:10:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61397>

This sounds like a microadenoma......but if it is driving your TSH down they will likely do medical management with the endo and monitor you will clinical exams and MRI's If it has bled then you need to watch for Headaches and visual changes but based upon what you wrote you it appears it is intrasellar and not suprasellar with the nerve to the eyes are located.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-01T23:22:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pituitary-adenoma-mri-findings.4619/post-61409>

Trisha, there you go!

I hope you get feeling better too...

# Thread: Botox for Migraines
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mafixit — 2012-11-01T19:58:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-61346>

My headache neurologist at the Mayo Clinic has suggested and approved Botox treatment for me for daily chronic migraines (which mostly manifest with 24/7 dizziness). He had migraines and swears by it. My insurance company has approved it, so I would be covered. This is in lieu of taking a calcium channel blocker (which only made me sick to my stomach) or the suggested medication Pamelor, an anti-depressant (which I have never taken), or Topamax and/or Imitrex (which I have never taken). I prefer to stay away from anti-depressants.

Any thoughts on this? I am so tired of feeling like I am on a roller coaster physically that I am thisclose to trying it, while of course I change my lifestyle and get my testing done to try to find and get rid of the root cause of this migraine problem.

Karen from Arizona

### Lyndra — 2012-11-01T20:38:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-61370>

I think migraines are complex and what works for one person won't for another. I tried imitrex for a while but couldn't stand the weird way it made me feel. It took the edge off the pain but just felt so unnatural. I worried about what was really happening to my brain. My migraines have abated significantly and I chalk it up to dietary changes and hormones. Going grain-free made a big, big difference. From trial and error, I have found that coconut oil or butter will invariable give me a headache (sigh). Hormones are strongly implicated in my headaches. I was on HRT for years after my hysterectomy and had headaches off and on. I stopped the HRT and headaches went away (but hot flashes were murder, killed my sleep, messed up cortisol & thyroid) so I started on BHRT early this year. Headaches are generally under control except for the last week when I've been experimenting a bit more with diet and cutting supplements (I reduced CoQ10 which I think does help me). I've thought about trying botox but feel like I have other avenues. Good luck!

### mafixit — 2012-11-15T17:25:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-64496>

I had the Botox done yesterday. We will see how it works. My Mayo Headache Neurologist says I should know in 2 to 4 weeks.

Karen from Arizona

### mafixit — 2012-11-19T14:55:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-65308>

So far no relief and because I ended my birth control pills, my headaches are more intense than ever. Never ending pain and dizziness. Here's hoping the Botox kicks in soon. Not sure I can take this discomfort much longer.

Karen from Arizona :-(

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-19T17:46:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-65362>

Migraines are mostly about substance P and the trigeminal nerve. Once your trigeminal nerve becomes overstimulated as it happens in chronic migraines, you get overly sensitive to Substance P/trigeminal nerve stimulation, which means that a normal level of substance P will give you a vicious headache. The oversensitivity has been shown to last for 28 days in some rat study. That is why it is so hard to get rid of chronic migraines.

There is a variety of reasons for elevated substance P including hormones, thyroid, dental malocclusion (the trigeminal nerve passes through your TMJ). And probably others that I don't know of yet.

### freshveggies — 2012-11-19T17:51:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-65363>

> **mafixit;67325 wrote:** So far no relief and because I ended my birth control pills, my headaches are more intense than ever. Never ending pain and dizziness. Here's hoping the Botox kicks in soon. Not sure I can take this discomfort much longer.
>
>
>
> Karen from Arizona :-(

HI Karen, have you looked into Dzugan ---The Migraine Cure. There seems to be a hormonal connection for some.

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-19T17:59:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-65367>

Substance P is a strong vasodilator. Estrogen has vasoconstrictive/dilative effects. Estrogen could be the cause of chronic migraines potentially. Or, the vasodilation could have a different cause and BHRT, while effective, is used as a patch instead of a fix. One needs to find the true cause of elevated substance P to find the fix.

### colleencoble — 2012-11-19T19:12:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-65380>

migraines are hormonal. I tried everything known to man for 40 years, including getting checked for the PFO in the heart. I actually had one and was getting ready to get it fixed when I read The Migraine Cure by Dr. Dzugan. I just KNEW when I read it that hormones were the answer. Now here I am 6 years later and I can't even remember when I last had a migraine. I had them daily for 15 years and 2 a week for the 25 years before that.

Your progesterone in particular is likely very low and the thyroid is often low. You need a good doctor who understands hormones for sure. Good luck!

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-19T21:59:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-65422>

I forgot neurotransmitters. Serotonin is involved in vasodilation/constriction as well (this is how estrogen/hormones affect migraines - low estrogen, for many women just prior or during menstruation, will lower serotonin, and low serotonin makes you much more pain sensitive).

> Estrogen has several important actions in the central nervous system that may account for its association with migraine \[4\]. The predominant effects are facilitation of the serotonergic and glutamatergic systems. Serotonergic tone in women is positively correlated with estrogen levels. When estrogen concentrations decline, serotonin concentrations also fall due to a decline in production coupled with an increased rate of elimination. The resultant decline in serotonergic function is associated with release of calcitonin gene-related peptide and substance P from trigeminal nerves, and may lead to vasodilation of cranial vessels and sensitization of meningeal afferents of the trigeminal nerve \[5\]. Estrogen may also affect other chemical mediators, such as nitric oxide, magnesium, or prostaglandins, which may also modulate the balance between excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmission \[4,6\].

Low magnesium can be a cause b/c it affects your blood vessels ability to relax.

Estrogen rapidly induces nitric oxide production, a potent vasodilator (migraines are usually induced by cerebral vasoconstriction, which is followed up by too much of vasodilation and the whole process is very painful and produces all sorts of yucky neurological symptoms, but there's atypical migraines as well that don't follow this pattern).

### colleencoble — 2012-11-19T22:05:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-65424>

The entire migraine protocol I follow addresses all those things. I take 5htp to help serotonin. Magnesium, pregenolone, DHEA, and bioidentical hormones.

### mafixit — 2012-12-19T19:39:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-67997>

Ok - so 5 weeks in I still have daily headaches - Botox did not work for me.Â  I guess I am one of the 25% who get no reliefÂ  { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I am being placed back on a short course of steroids and am eliminating all the foods that came back positive on my intolerance labs (which were many things in my daily diet).Â  Between that, and the new hormone therapy I hope to be reporting back soon that I am feeling better.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-05T20:11:27+0200

### mafixit — 2013-01-15T17:28:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/botox-for-migraines.4620/post-70918>

I do not see Botox has a cure - I see it as a short term symptom reliever while I get to the root of the problem. The first round did not work, so Mayo Clinic wants to go another round and increase the dose next month. They also want me on Nortyptyline, which I have not yet started and do not want to start. In the meantime, 1 night in the ER and then 3 days of IV treatments of Toridal, Dexamethasone, Ativan and Benedryl did not work, (the headache came back the next day) and the Arthotec I have been placed on daily ("until the Nortryptyline kicks in" they told me) is barely helping, but is helping me to be able function somewhat. They also gave me Vicodin, which I filled but have not taken, and will only take as a last resort. What's wrong with this picture?

My hormone doctor has added DHEA and Pregnenalone to my Estrogen compound cream. I am also on Progesterone 1x per day, at night, and a Lifepak Supplement Pack 1x per day. Everyone says 'back on Lipitor' because my small LDL is is so high. Even my hormone doc, who is dead set against statins, is worried about my small LDL. She also said to increase my magnesium (blood work shows its low). Her and her colleague are recommending Nystatin therapy - he says I have an overgrowth of yeast that could be causing some of my symptoms.

Exactly one month later with hormone therapy I am really not feeling any better at all. I still don't sleep well, still have head pressure and dizziness every day, and a headache/backache/neckache to go with that most every day to some degree, and as a matter of fact, now I have this strange buzzing feeling that comes and goes, like I am sitting on my cell phone

on low vibration. And the left side vibrating/weakness is back in my hip, leg, left shoulder and left arm - which strangely enough seems to coincide with the increase in my magnesium supplement. Absolutely no relief from the shakiness I feel when I try to lift light weights at the health club. Also still having a lot of digestive issues, even on this restrictive diet (Paleo and eliminating food allergies and intolerances). The headaches start in the back of my neck and work their way up to my forehead and into my temples. They hurt and nothing helps. The good news is I am dropping about 1lb per month. Bad news is all my pants are too big and I need to go shopping

I just had blood drawn to check for Lyme Disease, Lupus, Epstein Barr and Valley Fever. I am also starting a probiotic.

I was checked for TMJ a couple of weeks ago - it's not that.

I am a hot mess.

Karen from Arizona

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-official-im-crazy-aka-the-square-peg.4623/post-61403>

As I sit here waiting for my bus ppl walking by keep looking at me hehehehe I'm the square peg!!

### AKMan — 2012-11-02T00:04:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/its-official-im-crazy-aka-the-square-peg.4623/post-61418>

Slight thread hijack:

I just spent the afternoon in a warm doctor's office. Probably at least 75 degrees. We all left totally overheated (kids all red-cheeked, lethargic, etc). But then what happened afterwards surprised me. We all started feeling cold at temps that are normally fine. Even now, a few hours after leaving the office, I'm still chilly, and hungry. I'm not doing any hard-core CT right now, just going with the cooler weather outside and indoors. I wonder how long until I re-adjust back to the regular me?

# Thread: ok,  I'll make you uncomfortable.   Let's break some social taboo
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 292
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-11-02T02:59:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61466>

For goodness sake people, we can talk about bodily discharge etc - but we seem to shy away from politics.

I'll open.

I hate both candidates. I voted for Romney in mail in for the sheer sake of change. I would have voted with enthusiasm for Ron Paul. I think our elected officials are are utter failures for not fighting to change the electoral and party system.

Our system prevents the best leaders and candidates from rising to actually be on the ballot.

I also think you vote does not always count. I probably haven't voted in most elections as objectively speaking my vote was pointless. Why bother.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-02T03:00:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61467>

Oh, and Obama care is a cluster-f.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-02T03:11:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61468>

I'm not uncomfortable, but I don't see either as an option. The RNC thinks we will vote for anyone who is slightly to the right of the Democrat running. And we have, for decades.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-02T03:15:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61469>

That's why I went third-party. As my guy said, the only wasted vote is for someone you don't believe in.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T04:12:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61479>

The real story of election day is not the idiot in the oval office.......it is for the idiots who actually write the checks and run the country. Until we control them........we are screwed. The president is like the Queen of England except with a penis........and I got a feeling she is more of man than either one of these two candidates.

### Huck — 2012-11-02T04:14:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61480>

I'm a Palinista, but since she chose not to run, I'm voting for Romney.

Two Out of Three Ain't Bad by Meatload describes how I feel about Romney:

> I want you (I want you)
>
> I need you (I need you)
>
> But-there ain't no way I'm ever gonna love you

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-02T13:45:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61506>

Ok I'll bite. As I've gotten older my conservative views have skewed more toward the center and I suddenly have found my self sitting in the libertarian camp. However I can't fully back a libertarian candidate b/c my job is tied to supporting the DOD & military.

So considering there's a high probability ill be in a precarious job position in the next 6 mths courtesy of Obama's proposed budget for the next 4yrs (yes I've seen it b/c we're programming for it right now) and the proposed personnel cuts in the DoD the dc metro area is looking at a jump in unemployment if govt contractors by close to 40%. Yeah 40%. What ppl don't realize is that there is a 3rd economy that's getting ready to take a nose dive & it impacts all 50 states & the 4 US territories

I do not like Romney at all but he's my best chance for keeping my job

Johnson & Obama have nothing to offer me & my family economically so I'm resigned to vote for some one who I don't like or respect. But then again I feel the same way about POTUS & Johnson

If I weren't in a precarious economic position I'd write in John Gault & Howard Roark

### BJK77 — 2012-11-02T14:06:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61507>

Politics never makes me uncomfortable. The fact that some people can't carry on a conversation with differing opinions about issues shows how clearly arrogant many in our society are.

I consider myself to be an independent. I refuse to tow any party line. I think it forces you to be narrow minded. Anyone who is a staunch party member will overlook any fault of their candidate because it's all about winning, not about choosing the 'best' person. And when I say 'best' I'm referring to an individual's character, not only their position on the issues. There are issues in which I agree more with the position of the Democrats and there are issues where I tend to side with the Republicans. I have voted both Democrat and Republican in my life and have never made my decision based on one or two issues alone. How someone can vote for a pro-life / pro-choice candidate purely because of their position without looking at that person's honesty and integrity is beyond me.

There are, of course, certain issues that rank higher for me, but overall I base my decision more on my assumption of where the individual's heart is - their honesty, integrity, character and the people they choose to surround themselves with. Unfortunately that eliminates 99% of the politicians out there from being a good choice for me

I would have loved to vote for Ron Paul. He's the only one that has the balls to truly take a stand and stick with it. And the only one with the balls to say we screwed up this country and need to go back to the traditional values we were founded on. Since my vote for Paul wouldn't mean squat, my next choice would be Johnson; again, that vote won't mean squat. I'd love to say I need to vote for him just because the other two are dimwits, which I believe, but based on the latest Benghazi incident and many others, I can never in good conscience vote for Obama. He has yet to show me that he has even the capability of being honest and forthright, let alone the intelligence to surround himself with good people.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-02T14:12:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61508>

Since I live in NH, I was proud to vote for Ron Paul in the primary  First time I've cast a ballot and felt GREAT about it, despite knowing my candidate was probably going to lose. Now, I'll be voting for "not Obama."

### Michael — 2012-11-02T15:17:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61515>

> **Jack Kruse;63227 wrote:** The president is like the Queen of England except with a penis........and I got a feeling she is more of man than either one of these two candidates.

I should think Prince Charles would be pretty much in favour of the ethos of Epipaleo.

He has a strong and abiding interest in the countryside and in organic farming and gardening. The socialist TV satire puppet show _Spitting Image_ back in the 80s used to satirize him by having him talk to plants, which tells you something about his interests.

He's also an acomplished watercolourist, but his interest in the countryside isn't prissy in the way that might imply. He's the kind of bloke who'd spend hours stalking stags on the moors in bad weather; he also broke his nose playing rugby; and his skiing approaches foolhardy.

He currently has a considerable business selling organic food through the Waitrose supermarket chain (part of teh John Lewis partnership). I don't think the venture's primarily about making money, or even about making the food he thinks we should all eat available, so much as it's about ensuring that the land is farmed in the way he thinks it should be.

He also got together with architects to build a "model village" at Pundbury in Dorset, using local materials and traditional designs of building in harmony with the landscape. He's taken a fair bit of abuse for that from the people who make their living selling brutalist architecture to the rest of us. Someone tried to click the classwar button by saying he wanted "neo-classical for the nobs and pitched roofs for the common folk". That kind of rhetoric writes itself, doesn't it? I think one can probably buy it by the yard somewhere.

Charles also seems spiritually open - he's the kind of man who would be a deep and sincere member of the Anglican communion and happy to take part in the traditional forms of worship of his church, but who would also be interested in what the Dalai Lama was saying. He was a great friend of Laurens van der Post, who was interested in Jungian psychology and ethnography - in fact Charles is, like Laurens, something of a defender of the Bushmen.

### ealachan — 2012-11-02T17:21:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61551>

I hate politics. The whole system is completely \*\*\*\*ing broken, and nothing short of a major revolution will ever change it. I vote 3rd party out of principle, even though it doesn't matter because my state is already decided as a blue state due to the utter bull\*\*\*\* that is the electoral college. I am a socialist at heart, which means I will forever be disappointed, because pure socialism relies on people being responsible and not greedy, and that's not what America is about.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-02T17:42:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61557>

Voting is tricky and involves some interesting ethical dilemmas. Do you do what is good for your personally or for society in general? Do you vote for short-term impact or for solutions to long-term issues? The biggest problem I see with our system (at least president/congress/senate) is that once they get in office, they will do anything to stay and be re-elected. Any desire to work for the good of the country goes out of their heads.

I don't think it really matters whether Obama or Romney is elected this go-round. Neither one will be a true leader who gets us to focus on long-term problems (climate change, national debt, widespread human-caused poor health, social security/medicare) which are going to destroy us in a matter of years. To fix those problems will require someone to give us the straight, unvarnished truth and persuade us to sacrifice. Ain't nobody gonna want to sacrifice, especially if they think some other guy is getting a better deal.

The only solutions that will work are local. [http://www.ellenlaconte.com/life-rules-the-book](http://http://www.ellenlaconte.com/life-rules-the-book)

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-02T20:11:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61593>

I am ridiculously, painfully conservative and a capitalist, and don't recall ever voting FOR someone, as opposed to AGAINST someone else.

I haven't found a third party candidate that I can really sink my teeth into, so once again, I'm voting to stem the bleeding. Obama is a bloody disaster, and a train wreck anyone with their eyes open could see coming a mile away. Why anyone is surprised he failed the way he did is beyond me. Small government is a necessity, and why people can't see that WE CAN'T AFFORD much of what we spend money on is, again, beyond me.

And the race card. Oh for the love of BOB, can that PLEASE just die? I don't care what color you are, just don't be lazy, don't have an entitlement complex, and dont expect to be perpetually babysat.

Oh, and I'm highly opinionated. It's a personality flaw. That said, feel free to speak your own mind. IMO, you're just as likely to think I'm full of BS as I am to think you are, so we're even. Ain't it great how that works?

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T14:49:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61752>

I voted "Not Obama" too. I'd have voted for a 3rd party candidate, but my problem in doing so is that I'm afraid it just takes away votes from the main parties. Since the likelihood of a 3rd party getting elected is so slim, I stay within the 2 major parties so that one of them at least has a clear chance of winning.

I really wish there'd be a greater depth and breadth to the Libertarian party though...

In any event, I do feel blessed that we CAN vote.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-03T17:05:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61781>

Cavemam...i thought nonchalant was banned too! I was totally perplexed as to what transpired that I couldn't see on my phone.

I could be wrong but I really think the tide is turning. So many people my grandfathers age were hard line repubs or dems, no in between.

I know a few holdouts my age who claim democracy, but the large majority are libertarian or constitutionalist. They may vote repub for lack of a third option for certain things, but I think the days of the two party system really are gone. We just have to get the mechanics caught up to the population. How the mechanics would be designed for that tpe of "no party system" is to much for my little brain at the moment.

This may be a regional thing, it's just my observation. We are very rural here, and the recession didn't really hit as hard as it did elsewhere, mostly because we were already depressed. Once it did hit though it was with a vengeance because nearly everyone is or works for small business. Family farms and the like.

On a totally unrelated note...went to see Argo last night....once again, just like after watching The Stoning of Soraya M, I am so very, very thankful to live in this country. I worry about getting enough seafood and too much light after dark...they worry about far more. It's a good show though. Not really an Affleck fan, but he did a good job.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-03T18:07:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61791>

> **PrimalPam;63518 wrote:** I voted "Not Obama" too. I'd have voted for a 3rd party candidate, but my problem in doing so is that I'm afraid it just takes away votes from the main parties. Since the likelihood of a 3rd party getting elected is so slim, I stay within the 2 major parties so that one of them at least has a clear chance of winning.
>
>
>
> I really wish there'd be a greater depth and breadth to the Libertarian party though...
>
>
>
> In any event, I do feel blessed that we CAN vote.

IMO, the way the country is going won't change much whether we elect a democrat or republican. Neither will work on the big, gnarly, long-term problems. Voting third-party sends a strong message that the electorate is dissatisfied with the "two" choices. Imagine, if all the people who don't like either major candidate voted third party, we might actually get some attention. There won't be revolutionary change at first, but if we could get 10-15% this election, then more people would be emboldened next time around. However, if we keep choosing the lesser of two evils, that's what we'll end up with -- evil.

> **kathylu;63632 wrote:** Yeah, but I can't figure out where Romney is going to get the bucks, or even what he's going to cut or spend on...and I'm a smart girl.

It's all smoke and mirrors, that's the thing. It's just like selling cars. There's the absolute bottom line of what the car actually cost the dealer, but everything above and beyond that, all the financing and "rebates" and "incentives" (oh hai, stimulus anyone?!) are just creative accounting. I am an accountant for a living (dealing with the government, funnily enough). You can bet your sweet bippy that when the gov't comes calling and wants to audit, we have to have all our i's dotted and our t's crossed...but when it comes to the American people wanting to know where our tax dollars are going? Oh, it's far too complicated. We could never understand. \*eye-roll\*

edited to remove a bunch of specifics about my job that would support my argument but that might get me in trouble, and since I need my job to, you know, buy fish and bacon and 99% Lindt bars, I figure it's better to not run my mouth. For once.

I'm pro-life & I support a woman's right to choose abortion

I support looser gun control

I support gay rights

I'm a staunch supporter of Israel and their ability to defend themselves against Iran and other extremist Islamic states

Climate change - not sold when you look at the geological record - Mother Nature will correct it when she feels it is time

Reducing the deficit will require ppl to give up entitlements, cutting programs & some tax hikes

Equal pay will never happen by force in the private sector. The only way it's going to happen is when more women shun traditional rolls and break the glass ceiling. Where it is a big problem is at lower level jobs that aren't governed by minimum wage

There are some issues that the moral right & moral left will never agree upon and from my perspective should be left out of politics completely

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-04T15:34:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-61956>

> **Anna K.;63725 wrote:** Agree, that's the problem, nobody wants to compromise. We need both, increase the taxes and reduce the spending.
>
>
>
> And military spending and military jobs is like and entitlement program too. We still have soldiers in Europe and Japan. Why? We don't need to spend more then the next 10 countries combined on military, especially traditional military like tanks, for example.

Economic security is tied to national security, which is tied directly to military strategy - which includes overseas bases. Here's the interesting thing - in anticipation of the withdrawl from both Iraq and Afganistan the DOD has initiated a draw down of all personnel - this hasn't been directed by POTUS - this is the DOD seeing the changing environment. and the need to change how it operates. additionally several of the overseas bases have been slotted to close w/in the next couple of years - as a result of the last BRAC in the mid 2000's. If you want to talk about tanks - the Army has requested permission to get rid of thousands of tanks - which would save $Billions in storage, and maintenance costs - but congress wont let them - and these aren't even the current tanks in use - they're Korean WAr and Vietnam area tanks. The Army is the only service that doesn't have a major weapon system upgrade since their last effort was canceled and rightly so - Yet the air force has purchased 2 different fighters - made by the same company that do the exact same job? Yeah Fraud, Waste and Abuse IMO -and I'm former Air Force. Is all of this necessary? probably not

but can we pull back to the isolationism of pre - WW1? unfortunately not - we entered a slippery slope when Clinton established the US Military as a police force for the world back in 1993. Whats sad is the politicians haven't studied history - we've just relearned the lessons of Vietnam - and only one man understood what we're doing: Gen Patreaus. We continue to make the same mistakes - because we forget to look to our past. you cant have a successful and secure economy w/o a well thought out national security strategy, that includes military. Are there ways to do that w/o DOD being a bloated beast - yes - but you have to remove the entrenched leadership that's use to running DOD on contingency budgets....

Regardless of faith - there will always be people attempting to convert you. that's their intent/focus - you can't let that rule your life.

as for the SCJ - these men and women - while they may have certain political leanings, their job is to evaluate a case against LAW, not impose their personal morals on a case. while yes those morals may influence their perspective - it all comes down to wether a law stands within constitutionality or not. The SC is responsible for keeping both the President and Congress in Check - while we may not like their decisions - they are made w/impartially based upon where something stand w/respect to constitutional law

### Michael — 2012-11-04T18:12:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62020>

> **ealachan;63614 wrote:** I did say I was a socialist...that's about as liberal as it gets, innit? LOL

Not as different as it might seem. LOL

Just flicking around seeing what was on the site wasting time. I'm not going to argue about U.S. politics, which mean nothing to me. But here's something of more general interest.

So these are both modern ideologies and both based on abstract absolutes, right? In the case of the first it's _liberty; in the case of the second, _equality_.

Get a mental image - a load of people standing around. Each has a circle around him. That's the area of his individual liberty: no-one can step inside that without violating his freedom.

But hang on ... each circle must be the same_ size or each person's liberty can't really be guaranteed, so the concept of liberty already contains the concept of equality.

### SueC — 2012-11-06T21:46:47+0200

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-06T22:03:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62420>

I don't think it's simply a woman's rights issue. It's a woman's symbiotic lifeline to another person. There is not clarity with the majority or science on when "life" starts and if or if not that organism is as valuable to the society as a POTENTIAL sentient being vs an actual sentient being.

I think it's re-opening as an issue, because all the children of the first generation living with legalized abortion are now having children. For many, that simply changes the value they put upon that potential sentient being. It did for me, and it's a very common switch of POV. I was all for abortions right up until I saw that 1st ultrasound.

It's not a question on IF society is ok with eliminating a sentient being (war), but simply a fight over the circumstances in which that is o.k.

Until we perfect the electronclouderator that can pinpoint if or if not a mass of cells is hooked up to that thing called life, arguing if it's life or not is a straw man.

We are ok with taking life, so that is irrelevant.

We need to determine if we are ok with doing it to protect the freedom of a singular woman.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-06T22:06:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62421>

> **Anna K.;64233 wrote:**
>
>
>
> Remember the mandatory ultrasounds that you have to do before the procedure?

This one doesn't seem that draconian to me. Either it is life or it isn't. If you are getting an abortion, it's because you are either of the mind that it isn't - or you understand that you are taking a life to preserve yours. Educating people (the mothers and fathers) is something that society does everywhere for the sake of the social order. God knows we have to sit through all kinds of emotional drivel growing up as the price to pay for admission to the tribe.

What is it about this that seems so out of line?

And, to add - I'm not asking because I already KNOW an answer and just looking to give it. I'm asking because you are a very reasonable, cogent and logical poster and I'm very interested!

### ealachan — 2012-11-06T22:16:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62423>

> I don't think it's simply a woman's rights issue. It's a woman's symbiotic lifeline to another person.

I'm sorry, but if that's NOT a woman's rights issue, I don't know what the hell WOULD be.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-06T22:34:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62425>

> **ealachan;64237 wrote:** I'm sorry, but if that's NOT a woman's rights issue, I don't know what the hell WOULD be.

If the government were trying to take away your ability to vote because you are a woman.

If the government were trying to prevent you from driving because you are a woman.

Society gives the above by it's wholly man-made, sometimes arbitrary, fabricated construct. Nature, not society gave women the child bearing end of the stick. The ability to have a clean and safe abortion is part and parcel of the fabricated societal construct - thus subject to it's whims.

I'm against it for me. I would do everything I could to educate friends and loved ones on what they are doing - but I think we sacrifice potential all the time for much lesser causes than a particular woman's freedoms, so it doesn't make sense to prevent this procedure.

Of course, before I had kids - I used to think "the damn things don't even have a consciousness until about age 6, so I'm for 12th trimester abortions". Thankfully, one of the wisdoms life has taught me is there is rarely a black and white answer, and it's the fool that doesn't learn what those who blazed the trail before them are willing to share. When I have not had a particular experience, the next best thing to do is learn from those that have.

I went to bed very early last night because I knew what was coming. Instead of fighting against the grain in medicine the die is now cast. The world in which I play is forever changed and it matters little what I think about it any longer. The people have spoken. But one thing is crystal clear now that no party can deny based upon what is coming in 2014. We must rely on us.......and our ability to adapt now that the country has spoken. The ability to attain greatness and make forward progress with positive transformative change dies on the vine the moment we try to emulate someone else. Be you, the world needs your insight. Since our country wants mediocrity in healthcare........we need to strive for optimal in ourselves to overcome that obstacle. The rut for patients just got deeper. So it means your personal education about health is now paramount to avoiding what is coming.

An example: Our childhood is remembered through a multi-sensory combo of feelings plus the rare random photo. ### kathylu — 2012-11-07T18:12:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62569>

Jack, the paradigm is not shifting fast enough to prevent the oncoming health disaster that awaits most people. Our children and grandchildren may stand a chance. The current generation...not so much. That is independent of proposed changes in the heath care system. It's not like the entire world will wake up tomorrow and realize that "Gee, it sounds good to go for a swim in the frozen lake after my brekkie of 2 dozen oysters in seaweed and duck fat. Then I'll check my supply of candles for tonight because I gave up all artificial light since that 2012 AMA position statement that no one else paid attention to. Then after an hour or two of meditation, yoga and my twice weekly sprints, I'll walk (barely clothed) through the snow (checking my game traps along the way) to my 4 hour low-stress creative job hand carving dulcimers. Boy, I'm glad I gave up that 80 hour per week investment banking job to adopt an ancestral lifestyle!"

Flippancy aside, managing the results of the rampant neolithic diseases occurring may be the best most people will be able to hope for for the next 30 years. Until what you are telling us slips into conventional consciousness, if it ever does. Those of us who know will survive and teach our children, families and friends. Unfortunately, I fear it will be painfully slow.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-08T00:51:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62727>

I believe it's the American Medical Association that regulates licensure of physicians, not the govt? most professions have their own regulatory bodies, and new ones are free to form at any time. (Naturopaths, chiropractors, voodoo practitioners, Eleachan's Kitty Litter treatments, Jack Kruse's optimal life) and to attempt to convince people that your version of reality is the most beneficial..

With socialized medicine, everyone gets treated the same... for instance, everyone ends up on statin medication. we tax fat, and we extol whole grains.

people are responsible for themselves, and fully capable of making their own decisions. Why do some insist on making the choices for others?

peer regulation is far more effective than state control, as well as being broader in scope.

### Inger — 2012-11-08T01:17:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62739>

It is just like in Finland.

Here you will need to wait half a year to get you caries filled in your teeth if not doing it privately and paying all by yourself. The same with other health care. In comparison to Hamburg (Germany) where I lived before, the Doctors here know very little. And you need to wait... long. Very long.

To me all this matters little, because I took my health in my own hands long ago.

It only makes me more aware, how important it is not to be dependent on the heath care. So I do all I can not to be.

The Russian, that often come to eat/visit as tourists in the restaurant I work in, they are a different species. They are used to have to rely on their own. I can sense how different they are. I like it. They are used to have to take care of their own.

One Russian friend of my parents told me visiting here a week ago.. His mom grew up in Sibiria (Northern Russia). And when she had a cold or was sick, she always got into the ice cold lake, submerging her whole body. The next day she was healthy again. He/she did not knew anything about CT or the science behind. They just knew out of experience, it worked.

The Russian people have a lot of old wisom still. Because they have no other choise. No hospitals or doctors around.

I have seen where that leads to, especially when doctors are not professional (wich is too common here sadly). One family friend died last spring of lyme desease. A number of doctors have stopped accepting Medicare patients. My bro-in-law had a heck of a time finding someone. The doc we saw (who we paid cash) said Medicare pays him 25% of his fee. Every year, Congress threatens to cut Medicare spending, but backs down. Ain't gonna happen. The best part is they tell us they have suddenly, magically, found $500 Billion (that would be $500,000 million to those playing at home) in fraud that they will now do something about. Why did it have to come to this before they decided to tackle fraud? Because it is the government and government generally does nothing efficiently. For proof, visit your local DMV and try to get in and out in 30 minutes. More like 3 hours around here. How in the world can the same number of doctors serve 60 million more patients?

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T02:09:49+0200

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T02:15:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62777>

> **Anna K.;64586 wrote:** how is that possible?
>
>
>
> they will force you to take statins?
>
>
>
> ban you from eating fish?
>
>
>
> what exactly are you afraid of?

Ask the people in New York who can't order their own health tests. People in Canada can't get a full thyroid panel. Doctors few and far between in New Zealand. With 60 million more patients with "insurance" and no more doctors, I foresee a crisis. More docs are already declining to take Medicare patients, others are fully self-pay and you're on your own to file a claim with your carrier. We are creating a divide in health care - the wealthy will simply pay out of pocket and get immediate service and everybody else will get in line and hope/pray to see someone.

Part of the problem is that people have lost the meaning of insurance. They now want "health maintenance" - pay a monthly fee and get all the care & services you want at no cost. No incentive to take care of yourself when you can just go to the doc and get loaded up with free meds.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T04:14:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62809>

> **Anna K.;64646 wrote:** my point is republicans blame the Federal gov all the time, saying local will be better, well, state is local

Certainly state is more local than federal but I agree with the premise that lobbying your local agency (city, county) is far easier than battling the state which in turn is easier than going up against the Feds. That's all playing out here. A local group of citizens got a referendum on the city election which banned fracking within the city, going against state regulations which determine where it will be permitted. State voters also voted to legalize pot which of course goes against federal law. I'd much rather take my chances lobbying my friends and neighbors than trying to get through to a bunch of career politicians in DC.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-08T04:27:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62813>

> **Anna K.;64630 wrote:** how is obmacare = no longer have control over my health???
>
>
>
> they will force you to take statins?
>
>
>
> ban you from eating fish?
>
>
>
> what exactly are you afraid of?

I have options today b/c I could leave the VA and find a progressive doc who still takes insurance in the DCMetro area. Before Obama care passed close to 60% of docs in our area stopped taking insurance - I noticed this migration began back in 2004. It's even harder now to find docs that take ins those same docs are also not taking in new patients

So if no one take your insurance or will take you on as a new patient w/o insurance . You get kaiser or Tricare /VA medical care where they only run basic tests & if your labs look normal even if you have symptoms of say hypothyroid you get told your ok and move along If you don't fit in their box of how they are told to practice medicine - you will find its hard to get appointments you'll be flagged as a problem patient If you do get a dx your only medication options are synthetic hormones. If you have asthma they won't try to determine what causes it or how to help prevent attacks but send you home w/inhaled steroids & tell you you'll die if you don't take them. If you have migraines they give you triptans even though your medical records indicate you don't tolerate the drugs.

Im guessing you've never been seriously ill Or have bad health in general I hope you stay healthy, never have to have a medicalized birth (b/c in a medicalized l&d ward in the us the average c-sect rat is close to 40% and climbing) or have a disease that kaiser/Tricare/VA determine its too expensive to consider a drug to treat it or too outside the box

Come see me in 20 yrs after you've run the gaulent of Obama care. We can compare notes about our experiences in a system that doesn't value n=1.

Your sadly mistaken if you think the government dictating health care will lead to better care and greater market options.
