**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62838>

> **Lyndra;64616 wrote:** Ask the people in New York who can't order their own health tests. People in Canada can't get a full thyroid panel. Doctors few and far between in New Zealand. With 60 million more patients with "insurance" and no more doctors, I foresee a crisis. More docs are already declining to take Medicare patients, others are fully self-pay and you're on your own to file a claim with your carrier. We are creating a divide in health care - the wealthy will simply pay out of pocket and get immediate service and everybody else will get in line and hope/pray to see someone.
>
>
>
> Part of the problem is that people have lost the meaning of insurance. They now want "health maintenance" - pay a monthly fee and get all the care & services you want at no cost. No incentive to take care of yourself when you can just go to the doc and get loaded up with free meds.

Upfront confession: I have not read the ObamaCare document and I'm not going to.

From far away on the other side of the world, it seems that the problem isn't 'socialised' medicine vs 'capitalist' medicine but that you've voted for a monolithic mindset about medicine. Everyone will get the same thing, in the same way. Free thinking outside the system - by patient or doctor - will not only be discouraged (as it already was) but will be mostly impossible. Doctors are shifting into job-keeping/a\*\*-covering mode and patients must follow along if they are going to get treatment at all. So 'everyone' gets healthcare, but no one gets healthy.

Scoliosis will cause arthritis in the future, you should try to correct it now if possible. And no, it's not covered by my insurance, I have to pay for it. But I feel it's certainly worth it.

Agreed. My grandfather was a doctor. He started practicing in the 1930's and ended in the 80's. Doctors back then weren't millionaires. They lived comfortably, making more than the general population, and were highly respected, but were not the extremely wealthy people they are today. When did it become a given that doctors should expect to make huge salaries?

It's even harder now to find docs that take ins those same docs are also not taking in new patients
>

>
> So if no one take your insurance or will take you on as a new patient w/o insurance . You get kaiser or Tricare /VA medical care where they only run basic tests & if your labs look normal even if you have symptoms of say hypothyroid you get told your ok and move along If you don't fit in their box of how they are told to practice medicine - you will find its hard to get appointments you'll be flagged as a problem patient If you do get a dx your only medication options are synthetic hormones. If you have asthma they won't try to determine what causes it or how to help prevent attacks but send you home w/inhaled steroids & tell you you'll die if you don't take them. If you have migraines they give you triptans even though your medical records indicate you don't tolerate the drugs.
>
>
>
> Im guessing you've never been seriously ill Or have bad health in general I hope you stay healthy, never have to have a medicalized birth (b/c in a medicalized l&d ward in the us the average c-sect rat is close to 40% and climbing) or have a disease that kaiser/Tricare/VA determine its too expensive to consider a drug to treat it or too outside the box

My DH goes to the VA for medical care. He's had 2 heart attacks, and has injuries from Vietnam that still bother him today. VA care is not perfect, but he is very satisfied with his care there. Most of what you mention happens with civilian doctors, too. The majority of all doctors are narrow minded, not wanting to do full tests, not knowing how to interpret them anyway, prescribing meds for symptoms instead of finding the root cause. VA care varies greatly from hospital to hospital. Ours in Phoenix has improved greatly in the last few years, because of a new director.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T19:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62939>

> **Anna K.;64665 wrote:** while I agree that USPS sucks and need to be changed, the question is why do you go there? you have competition, fedex, ups, etc... why use the government run agency that sucks???
>
>
>
> I disagree with the premise that everything gov run automatically sucks, and private rules. I've had many bad experiences with private enterprises as well.

Only USPS will deliver letters to my house. USPS is the only option when sending to a PO box. I won't say all government is bad and all private enterprise is good. Nothing is that black & white. However, after spending 25 years employed by the state, I saw all the bad that comes with a highly regimented system. Hiring & firing, procurement, all with ridiculous layers of bureaucracy. Try to hire someone in less than 3 months (unless it's an emergency temp). Try to fire someone unless you have undisputable cause (theft caught on video tapes or child porn on the computer makes it easier). What comes with government is layers and layers of red tape. That can be true in private companies but they generally end up going under (unless of course they get a nice bailout and aren't forced to make necessary changes).

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-08T20:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-62961>

All I can say is I hope everyone who thinks Obama and Obama care is just wunnerfull is right, and I'm wrong.

I havent seen anything the govt has taken over as better, more effective, or safer (hello fda safety record, I'm looking at you, among others). Regulation is more expensive. Period.

The fact is, we STILL have "KL" capsules being administered, it's just patented KL with a fancy name and a few studies showing that it was marginally more effective than a placebo.

It might kill you slowly and painfully after 8 weeks, but hey, the studies lasted for six weeks, and there were no side effects, so rock on!

Universal health insurance is NOT going to fix anything. Until people buy into their own N=1, and quit expecting a Ferrari performance on Cheetos and Mountain Dew, nothing will change. It will cost more, and be less effective. But when the majority of the country can't explain the difference between a democracy and a republic, and don't know which the US was intended to be, and why, I guess it isn't that surprising after all.

### Stavity — 2012-11-09T13:32:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63101>

I am seeing two specialists and actually finding my experience with them to be better than ever before. No more than 5 minutes wait time all four visits. They are better then average doctors, very efficient and most of all respectful. One doctor even called me to explain one of my tests more thoroughly. Though, I find it strange they look more like business men than doctors--no more white coats. I like it!!!

But, as much as I like them and respect their opinion, I won't be using their treatment. No way, a year of being sick when I am actually starting to feel better than I have in 4 years? Now, how to handle explaining my position without shooting myself in the foot since I am not sure I can turn health around.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-09T13:34:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63103>

Today's thought: Analogy of you and your healthcare: You are a conductor and there has to be, between you and the orchestra, an unshakable bond of trust, born out of mutual respect, through which both can spin a musical narrative that both believe in. This is how beautiful music and health is created.

### kathylu — 2012-11-11T00:12:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63437>

Yes, but only if you qualify due to income. If you are over the limit (125% of the poverty line?), no coverage. I know, it happened to my sister some years ago (accident, surgery, long recovery, no insurance). She didn't qualify for medicaid, but didn't have enough money to pay. She had to declare bankruptcy because of the unexpected medical bills.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-11T01:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63476>

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-11T01:32:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63477>

The number one reason for bankruptcy is medical expenses. The number one reason for short-sales on houses is medical expenses causing a loss of house.

What I see is although on the surface, looks awful - and to most going through it, it IS awful because of both bankruptcy and losing your house have such social stigma, but in reality it's such a lifesaver of body and spirit for those that go that route because their medical expenses are too high.

I've seen too many people, once they get to the other side of that adventure end up experiencing both a new found sense of value for what's important in life and a place of freedom without any debt on their plate. I've been fortunate enough to help many people through having their house foreclosed or shortselling and almost exclusively a year later after the bankruptcy or foreclosure they are at such peace. It's inspiring really.

so what I see with our current system is this:

1\. we have something in place to deal with trauma and emergency medicine

2\. we have something in place to absolve the individual for the responsibility of that expense. Yes, it's more injury and insult on top of the trauma, but all in all it's better than not having it.

It seems that insurance premiums are really a tax to fund the socialized nature of our current system - and yes the haves get a better experience and benefits.

I do not think medical care should be something given only to those who can afford it. . .I think our country is wealthy enough to cover medical care just as we cover infrastructure items.

The problem with our current system is administrative expenses. . .

So I have not read obama care stuff. .. and all I know of it is the headlines I read. It does look to me like it's just adding yet ANOTHER layer of administrative expense and "pork belly" if you will, which doesn't seem like the right direction to me.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-11T01:36:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63478>

Also, does anybody really expect every person that is a US citizen to get EQUAL quality of care by right? Nothing in nature works that way. No matter what the system, some do better than others. I guarantee every single person reading this is benefiting from being a "have" in the scope of all the humans on earth.

In light of that, I think the 'ideal' would be to provide a base level of care through taxation and let the rest go as nature intended.

### chocolate — 2012-11-11T01:49:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63485>

> **kathylu;65308 wrote:** Yes, but only if you qualify due to income. If you are over the limit (125% of the poverty line?), no coverage. I know, it happened to my sister some years ago (accident, surgery, long recovery, no insurance). She didn't qualify for medicaid, but didn't have enough money to pay. She had to declare bankruptcy because of the unexpected medical bills.

Your sister was discriminated against. The money should be repayable over a long term. My son was dealing with a high deductible for fat blob the size of a bing cherry on the back of his neck. He couldn't believe as a certified bean counter that he couldn't just get a few prices from a few docs about how much it would cost to remove it, at least a ball park (especially since he could send the picture and size on the iphone. We were afraid that he would go to a garage doctor. He didn't pay for anesthesia to get his wisdom teeth out.) Prices should be knowable.

In California, if you have nothing but kids... you are covered and covered very well. If you have insurance, it dictates your care and your copay is non negotiable. They run an asset check before they talk money in collections. i.e. if you piss away your money and time and get injured, you pay nothing. If you have a house or two, they'll take one and let you live in the other till you die. Then they'll take it. It's as messed up as student aid. In other countries, any one can borrow to go to school. In this country, some people borrow, some people free ride. Some people's parents borrow and lose their house. One of my son's friends went to a prestigious art school and finally understood what was wrong with liberalism when he lost one of his friends to just that situation. He was riding on needs based scholarships and grants. His friend was riding on loans and had to quit school to help repay the loans on the house after his dad lost his job. People live in government housing and get a free ride to school as well. If it was a matter of getting what you paid for, maybe people would get really involved. We get what what we pay for. Humans fed like livestock getting the illnesses livestock get when they outlive their growth. And we pay to sustain those illnesses.

### stacyj — 2012-11-11T02:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63497>

> **SueC;64811 wrote:** The main thing I want out of universal health care is that if I have a car accident, I can be operated on and not go bankrupt afterward. Conventional medicine has never been great at treating chronic disease, that's up to us to take responsibility for our lives. But I sure would like to be able to get trauma care without being worried about losing my home.

If you want that, then it is your responsibility to carry enough insurance to provide for a catastrophic event. Check your car insurance med pay, and adjust it accordingly. Universal health care is not the issue here. As an accident victim- most of my care has been covered by my medical insurance, and what has not been has been covered by liens against future settlement. Mine, and my family's medical care is well in excess of 6 figures, not coming close to bankruptcy yet.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-11T02:40:45+0200

> **Cavemam;65350 wrote:** .............. to provide a base level of care through taxation and let the rest go as nature intended.

sounds good. Now let the argument of what 'base level' means.

Trouble is, the US is the victim of it's own success as a Republic. Our (and I mean the entire worlds) medical care today is so much better, we're living better and longer, because of innovations in drugs, implantable cardiac defibrillators, vagal/neural stimulator's, surgical procedures, stents, ... things that didn't even exist 10 years ago. well over half of all the advances in medicine in the past decade have come from the US, and I can't help but think it would be far more with less regulation and interference. Treatment options in the US exceed what is available to in countries where the Govt. holds the purse strings and decides what and how much each person should be entitled or limited to.

### Glamazon — 2012-11-11T02:58:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63514>

I was recently dropped by my health insurance when I lost my job. I am considered uninsureable due to previous co morbidities like high blood pressure and sleep apnea. So I go to the state. I can get very low cost insurance from them with a 10k deductible. Insurance won't cover my cutting edge health needs anyway(epi paleo labs/meds are not covered). All states have plans for the uninsureable. Some won't cover for cancer but just about everything else goes. I just need it for catastrophic reasons. I can get some medicaid coverage for traumatic brain injuries after discharge.

RE: medical payment coverage. In CO, the primary insurer pays first and then sues the auto insurance co. for re-imbursement. I can't remember the name of the particular state statute. Auto ins. generally covers about a week or less in the ICU. People can creep up to a 500k bill in just a few days. We used to have to notify the cost analysis dept when we hit the first 100K. Now, in a short time people can easily peak at 500k. Miracles can happen in the ICU and they are expensive.

I have had patients who have stuck around for several months in the ICU awaiting to meet acute rehab requirements for transfer to a lower level of care. Sometimes the hospital can't transfer because a rehab center won't take a patient until they have medicaid in place. This is after all the other kinds of benefits have run out. It's a mess to navigate. It is horrendous stress on the patient and their family.

We used to be a no fault state but that was overturned several years ago.

>
> Wrens teach their eggs to sing
>
>
>
> Teaching embryos the password for food helps parents avoid having to feed imposters.
>
>
>
> ZoÃ« Corbyn
>
>
>
> 08 November 2012
>
>
>
> Baby fairy wrens won't get fed unless they sing the secret password they learned in the egg.
>
>
>
>
>
> Mothers usually set about teaching their offspring the moment they're born. But the females of one Australian bird can't wait that long.
>
>
>
> Superb fairy-wren (Malurus cyaneus) mothers sing to their unhatched eggs to teach the embryo inside a 'password' — a single unique note — which the nestlings must later incorporate into their begging calls if they want to get fed.
>
>
>
> The trick allows fairy-wren parents to distinguish between their own offspring and those of the two cuckoo species that frequently invade their nests. The female birds also teach their mates the password.
>
>
>
> Fairy-wrens were known to discriminate against cuckoo nestlings on the basis of their foreign begging calls1, says Sonia Kleindorfer, an animal behaviourist at Flinders University in Adelaide, who led the work. But it wasn't known that wren nestlings learned the passwords before hatching.

[http://www.nature.com/news/wrens-teach-their-eggs-to-sing-1.11779](http://http://www.nature.com/news/wrens-teach-their-eggs-to-sing-1.11779)

If you want to see a hateful racist, you should look at the picture of that fairy wren.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-11T05:09:17+0200

Anarchism, the other end of the spectrum.

### chocolate — 2012-11-11T20:44:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63664>

The argument I was trying to make is that a few of us pay for everyone, no matter what. The prices are not knowable because it is based on your assets. Why isn't there a menu with a price list? The poor doctors try to make up for being cheated by medicare on our backs. Its all craziness. Why can a medical supplier at a hospital get 100 bucks for a cane that costs twenty at Walgreens and a doctor has to fight to get 50 bucks for an office visit while paying a staff of 7?

Why do I get the feeling that the 100 buck cane is still going to be around but the staff of 7 isn't? Its all broken. The best hours for clinics are kept by the pot clinics in California. They are scheduled for the working class. The doctors are pretty good and they are glad to take time with you (so I hear). The professionalism and thoroughness is something talked about. Why is that? Is it because they are actually getting paid a fair price for their time without the huge overhead? Little hard to come up with harm over herb and malpractice.... but then, the something for nothing crowd isn't going to those docs.... Well, actually they are. They are just paying because thats the way the game is played.

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-11-11T20:49:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63666>

Most people on this forum agree that our "health care" system in the US is severely broken. ObamaCare (whether you support or oppose it) is as John Boehner, Speaker of the House, said "is the law of the land".

Most people resist change, even when stuck in a bad rut. ObamaCare represents change. So the logic should be if a system is broken, then change is needed.

Many people who oppose ObamaCare are predicting what will happen. In reality, nobody knows all of what will happen. One of the things that makes the US a great country is the ability to change and move on.

I agree with Cavemam ... we need to accept the past, and move on to the future. **What our health care system will look like in 2014 or 2016 is a story yet to be written.**

I predict lots of creativity with a greater emphasis on alternative methods .... HEY, that sounds like the creative ideas shared on Dr. Kruse's blog and forums. Starfish march on.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-12T01:59:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63737>

> **Joann;65624 wrote:** hmmm you are right Kaiser I think is original HMO? widespread HMO after Clintons I believe.

I agree with Joann, the HMO was the beginning of problems with the healthcare system. I thought it was great, at first. I didn't have to pay $10 to see the doctor, the visit was "free". And I didn't have to fill in the forms to get reimbursed for expenses. Now there is no way I could afford to pay for most of the costs in the first place, nevermind reimbursements.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T02:12:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63738>

I think many people see "broken" as meaning unaffordable, either in terms of paying for insurance or getting coverage if you have some pre-existing condition and can't get in under a group policy. In my view, it's more a matter that the people are broken. So many people have neolithic diseases that need regular monitoring & medication and are not going to be cured by any CW doctor or pharmaceutical company. It looks to me like we're on a collision course with bankruptcy. It's estimated that caring for one person today with diabetes costs $7K/year. By 2030, it's expected that 1/3 of Americans will be diabetic.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T02:20:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63741>

> **Nonchalant;65652 wrote:** I agree with Joann, the HMO was the beginning of problems with the healthcare system. I thought it was great, at first. I didn't have to pay $10 to see the doctor, the visit was "free". And I didn't have to fill in the forms to get reimbursed for expenses. Now there is no way I could afford to pay for most of the costs in the first place, nevermind reimbursements.

Now that I have a high deductible plan, I am woefully aware of the cost of care. A couple of years ago, I had a UTI and went to my doctor's office for a test and Rx for antibiotics. I paid my $30 co-pay and a few weeks later, got bills for over $600, a combination of the doctor's fee, facility fee (they bill separately), and the urine test itself. Had I known the cost in advance, I would have gone to some urgent care place where I bet it would have been way cheaper. I know of no way to get a cost estimate in advance, though.

### LinD — 2012-11-12T03:42:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63758>

This is a FB post from a doctor friend of mine:

Today I attended the opening session of the 2012 AMA Interim Meeting in Honolulu, Hawaii. These opening sessions are largely ceremonial with awards and speeches. Always a bit too self-congratualory for my taste, but some egos need to be stroked.

The two main addresses were from AMA President Dr. Jeremy Lazarus, of Colorado. He emphasized the achievements of the AMA since the last meeting and called on members to continue to stay the course of healthcare reform initiated but the Affordable Care Act. He went on to talk about how physicians should be selfless and consider the greater good of society.

Dr. Lazarus was followed by Dr. Madera, the Executive VP who outlined the strategies of the AMA going forward. He emphasized the need for physicians to consider the economic implications of their actions. At one point he was attempting to explain how better health would improve the economy, something that no one would argue with. However, he challenged physicians with the task of ensuring both individuals' health and the nations economic wellbeing. Near the end of his remarks he used the phrase "physiologic benefit" referring to our patients and "our nations GDP" in the same sentence. He might as well have been reading from the Communist Manifesto. When he concluded his remarks, as is the custom, the Delegate dutifully stood and applauded. All but a few, who like me, sat silent, stunned at what we had just heard.

To cap off this afternoon session the Speaker of the House, Dr. Andy Gurman outlined some specific things that would be coming forward soon for the House to consider. One was the need to "revise" AMA's current Code of Medical Ethics, to make them more relevant to the changing world in which we live.

It seems pretty clear to me that the AMA recognizes that many of their actions actually run counter to the time honored ethics of the medical profession, so rather than reconsider those actions and policies they have simply decided to change the rules to fit their agenda.

Even before today, I was planning on this being my last AMA meeting, but after what I heard this afternoon I'm finding it difficult to even return tomorrow to fulfill my obligation to represent the American Society of General Surgeons. The only thing I can hope to accomplish at this point is to gather more ammunition that can be used by other organizations like Docs4PatientCare and Association of American Physicians and Surgeons to fight back against this obvious assault on American values of Freedom and Liberty.

Just yesterday I started rereading Ayn Rand's "Atlas Shrugged" but I have put it down. Today, I lived it, and many of my colleagues figuratively said "Who is John Galt?"

### Entelechy — 2012-11-12T04:18:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-63770>

2\. people come to the US when they can't get services in their own country?

true. people drive from here to the US all the time paying out of pocket for MRI's rather than wait months to get one. Cancer treatment protocols are limited here, and people pay out of pocket to go to the US for treatment. (i.e. 10 times as many options in neoplastic medications) then of course the infamous Shona Holmes fiasco.

3\. the 2000 WHO index is written with a strong political ideology as a bias?

was run by Gro Harlem Brundtland, the prime minister of Norway.

She is clear about the ideological factors she thinks are important: “Where health and responsiveness are concerned, achieving a high average level is not good enough: the goals of a health system must also include reducing inequalities, in ways that improve the situation of the worst-off. In this report attainment in relation to these goals provides the basis for measuring the performance of health systems.”

4\. the US tops out in most categories once the biases are adjusted?

A WHO discussion paper states, “we consider only the distribution, not the level, as there is no consensus on what the level of health spending should be.” Equal results, not necessarily good results, are the focus.

### Glamazon — 2012-11-14T06:36:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-64174>

> **Nonchalant;65748 wrote:** I tried to find the website but failed. I read an account where a woman needed an x-ray, I think for an arm, and she comparison shopped. It took persistence, because the hospital admins didn't really know what to tell her, but she finally got a price, in the ten thousand range I think. Then she called another, said the price that she got, and they quoted a lower price. Like bargaining for a car, she called another and another, and got it down low, like $100 or less.

Hospitals will give discounts, sometimes substantial ones, if you pay in cash up front. Always ask. I have done some auditing for hospital charges. I was amazed at varying prices charged. One day I came across $15k for snake antivenom vial and a particular patient used 10 vials. Also hospital bills are notoriously inaccurate. I have seen more than a few times men charged for a urine pregnancy test and it wasnt because the doc was trying to rule out cancer. Even basic supplies are all screwed up. I have seen hundreds of thousands of dollars of missed or inappropriate or wrongly assigned charges as well.

### ColdBren — 2012-11-14T06:49:36+0200

This works for MRI's Ct scans etc....good intentions just doomed everyone.

### Endless — 2012-11-14T17:10:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-64255>

How different is 'ObamaCare' from what we already have here in Canada? I am low income, so I pay nothing for my medical insurance. I pay nothing for any testing or treatment unless it is considered cosmetic or not medically necessary. I do pay for prescriptions. The downsides I can see are difficulty getting certain tests unless your doc is proactive, and sometimes long waiting lists for certain surgeries, notably joint replacements.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T06:29:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-ill-make-you-uncomfortable-lets-break-some-social-taboo.4627/post-64421>

Look everyone, government intervention will ruin every market that it touches. Why? Because human behavior and all the inputs of all the things going on in the world are too complex for them to predict and control. This is assuming that politicians are well intentioned and have your best interest at heart. In reality, they're in someone's pocket and they ain't close to being on your side. If you're a socialist or communist look at China to see how central planning works out. Right here, right now, is where we all need to unite (and I'm not talking geeks and non-geeks). Without government intervention we would be able to form our own networks and associations (if we so chose) to contract for healthcare. In fact, why not start now? Act boldly in the face of this mandate. Get together with your co-workers, family, friends and find the doctors in your area that think this is an abomination too. If you're scared about being able to afford healthcare out of pocket figure out a way to contract with them, as a group, for their services.... This isn't a new idea. If you need inspiration see here: From Mutual Aid to the Welfare State: Fraternal Societies and Social Services, 1890-1967 [http://www.amazon.com/From-Mutual-Aid-Welfare-State/dp/0807848417/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1352952990&sr=8-1&keywords=david+beito](http://http://www.amazon.com/From-Mutual-Aid-Welfare-State/dp/0807848417/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1352952990&sr=8-1&keywords=david+beito)

You know what... I wasn't quite fair in my post above. I forgot to admonish all the fascists on the left and right too.

It boils down to this: I don't want the state running my life or my affairs on behalf of you (individually or the collective you), some corporation or other institution, or some mis-guided philosophical ideal. I own me. You do not own me, or my labor, or the fruits of my labor. Therefore, I should decide how I spend my money, who I associate with, and I should be able to contract for the services of others as I deem suitable for myself. Seem fair?

# Thread: Dietary intake of fish and PUFA, and clinical depressive and anxiety disorders
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-intake-of-fish-and-pufa-and-clinical-depressive-and-anxiety-disorders.4632/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-02T09:12:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-intake-of-fish-and-pufa-and-clinical-depressive-and-anxiety-disorders.4632/post-61501>

Australian study just published in the _British Journal of Nutrition_:

> ... those in the second tertile of DHA intake were nearly 70 % less likely to report a current depressive disorder compared to those in the first tertile. The relationship of DHA to anxiety disorders was linear; for those in the highest tertile of DHA intake, the odds for anxiety disorders were reduced by nearly 50 % after adjustments, including adjustment for diet quality scores, compared to the lowest tertile. Those who ate fish less than once per week had higher GHQ-12 scores, and this relationship was particularly obvious in smokers. **These are the first observational data to indicate a role for DHA in anxiety disorders**, ...

[http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=8711172](http://http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=8711172)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T15:25:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-intake-of-fish-and-pufa-and-clinical-depressive-and-anxiety-disorders.4632/post-61521>

The military is taking a lead on this in the USA......with the looming budget cuts in the DOD they need to do something to make soldiers more fit for duty when there is less of them around.

# Thread: 11/2 Tip of the day to save some money and get nutrients:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-02T15:18:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61517>

Fish cheeks! Get some. I think they are part of the secret sauce of Inger's MHS. They are awesome and generally dirt cheap in oriental/asian stores for making soup. I have been chowing them religiously for 5 years in recipes. In my opinion, it is the best part of meat on the entire fish. Any fish has them but a bigger fish has the bonanza of size. Cheek sashimi rocks.

How to do you get to the cheek? Once the fish is cooked, it will be found in a cavity exactly on the side of the face of the fish where a cheek would be! You can peel back the skin, and there you will find a fish medallion of meat there waiting for you to eat.

### ealachan — 2012-11-02T17:11:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61550>

I learned the beauty of fish cheeks on a beach in Belize 10 years ago. Fresh-caught red snapper roasted over a fire pit dug in the sand, with coconut husks thrown on the coals as the fish cooked. It was in the ocean literally 20 minutes before it was in my mouth. Best fish I've ever had in my life.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-02T20:36:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61600>

Fish 'wings' are popular in New Zealand and very cheap... they are basically the bigger fins of the fish (often salmon) which are then typically smoked.

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-02T21:40:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61612>

Don't forget the tongue. One of the best seafood meals I've ever had consisted of cod tongue and cod cheeks that had been expertly marinated and lightly cooked by a top chef. Divinely delicious!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61617>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-61634>

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T03:01:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/11-2-tip-of-the-day-to-save-some-money-and-get-nutrients.4633/post-62121>

For now I'm sticking to oysters, clams, mussels, squid, crab, and then whatever fish (loveeeee salmon) and usually basa. These are easy for me to get and I make sure I get the shellfish as the primary source for my meals---since I know these are on the top of the epi-paleo Rx. Since I love the taste of all seafood it's not really tough or difficult to choose one over the other, so going by nutrient density is easy for me to follow. I must say though, I think my favourite fish is salmon (if cooking/preparing it on my own). If out on vacation and have better access, I love grilled snapper or -insert any Caribbean fish-. Mmmmmmm....fish!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T03:03:04+0200

O, and I won't forget my love for seaweed. Lately I've been enjoying it as toasted. Must be a winter thing, feels more comforting as opposed to during the summer I preferred it as a salad (cold). lol

# Thread: Great Rift Valley Kids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-11-02T15:37:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61524>

reposting from the future of education thread, I think it deserves discussion in it's own right.

The goal: to see if illiterate kids with no previous exposure to written words can learn how to read all by themselves,

â€œI thought the kids would play with the boxes. Within four minutes, one kid not only opened the box, found the on-off switch â€¦ powered it up. Within five days, they were using 47 apps per child, per day. Within two weeks, they were singing ABC songs in the village, and within five months, they had hacked Android,â€￾

[Link](http://http://www.technologyreview.com/news/506466/given-tablets-but-no-teachers-ethiopian-children-teach-themselves/)

Ironically.... One village is called Wonchi, on the rim of a volcanic crater at **11,000 feet; the other is called Wolonchete, in the Great Rift Valley**.

The question is, would SAD kids fair as well in such an experiment? or have we evolved to the point where only training can take place, rather than education?

### nonchalant — 2012-11-02T16:58:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61545>

That sounds like a great experiment! They also fixed the camera that had been disabled, and also customized the desktop, though OLPC had installed software to freeze the desktop settings.

### Melinda — 2012-11-02T18:14:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61567>

Thanks for linking to that. What a world changer giving tablets to the unschooled. As a homeschooling family, my goal is to have my children be self learners. I'm going to have them read that link.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-02T21:40:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61611>

This is unschooling at its finest. kids learning on their own

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-02T22:42:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61621>

Great post and wonderful to think about with our own, 'modern' children. What I was struck by was the belief (or at least the suspended belief) that this learning was possible. How much do we limit our children's learning (and our own) by thinking something isn't possible. We self-limit in so many ways. And we then share those limitations with those we are responsible for. I remember, when I was in grad school, a professor saying to me that her favourite interns were the students. "They have so much hope and they don't know enough to be certain of anything... so they have the best results." Buddhists call this 'don't-know mind.' It is considered a very intelligent state of being. There is so much pressure to 'know'... yet if we already know, then we are by definition limited to what we can find.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-02T23:03:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61623>

OH NO, they're getting too much screen time and we must put those tablets away until after they can do 100 multiplication problems in 10 minutes!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T00:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61626>

LOL.........I thought it.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-03T00:09:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61629>

my thought was this is a way to keep the poor man down! Give them the magic boxes that only teach them what we want them to know. . . .why not just hand out a monsanto gift bag with those

### Entelechy — 2012-11-03T03:47:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61696>

Not sure I understand Cavemam.. do you think it 'improper' to provide them with tablets and internet access?

I suppose there is an argument to be made for non-interference, as that really is the overarching base principle being discussed in regards to education, but I have difficulty imagining why voluntary access to information would ever be construed as a negative in any culture. (Propaganda and intentional misinformation aside)

What am I missing?

### Entelechy — 2012-11-03T03:53:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61700>

thinking about the 'wow' moments with my kids...

they aren't the times they demonstrate mastery of anything i have shown or told them.

They are the times when they have discovered things on their own, beyond what I could ever teach them.

Like playing chess with my son, I look up and see him grinning at me... he has me in three moves, and I never saw it coming. He was 12.

now at 14 he thrashes me consistently, and has never read a Chess book. The things he comes up with, wow moments.

now, if he would only eat fish..... lol

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-03T12:05:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61737>

I didn't think they had internet access. They had preloaded games etc to allow them to learn to read without a teacher. I understand what Cavemam is alluding to. Those kids only have whatever information has been provided. What they learn depends on who's providing the information.

Reminds me of the schoolbooks for Afghani kids. [http://supportdanielboyd.wordpress.com/usa-printed-textbooks-support-jihad-in-afghanistan-and-pakistan/](http://http://supportdanielboyd.wordpress.com/usa-printed-textbooks-support-jihad-in-afghanistan-and-pakistan/)

### Entelechy — 2012-11-04T04:05:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61910>

I see, and agree that there is always bias, and will always be bias. (with or without Internet access)

This fact is what inspired my statement in the preceding thread, that

"a government run public education system is incompatible with the values of a free society, as it's interest by natural emergence is not education but in social change and cultural conformity",

My childhood Canadian textbooks tell a different version of the war of 1812 that the US version, survivors write the history books.

The advantage of OLPC versus a corporate or govt. agency, is they don't appear to have an overtly ulterior motive other than their expressed mandate.

OLPC bases it's model Seymour Papert's theory of constructionism, the idea that knowledge is best constructed through the act of sharing and collaborating. The tablet is a tool for engaging in creative, intellectual work.

Whether this method of guiding a childs intellectual development in a prescribed direction is beneficial or not, is up for debate, but I'm still enthralled at the capacity of these kids.

Anyone who has modified an app, rooted an android device, or even just explored the file structure can attest at how arcane it can be even to someone familiar with other operating systems.

Now Imagine not being able to read at all. not understanding the structure, and being handed a tablet in Chinese characters or Arabic abjad.

5 monrhs later you have hacked the safeguards put in place by professional developers.

Beneficial, or deleterious... it's impressive, and I can only imagine the exponential development to master such a wide variety of disparate skills in so short a time.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T05:11:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-rift-valley-kids.4635/post-61918>

Seymor Papert! I forgot that he was part of OLPC.

I owe a lot to the man.... when I was little I did a lot of turtle logo and it layed a great foundation for the higher math I did not to mention computer programming.

Edit: Actually, I think "laying the foundation" is the wrong thing to say. By having unstructured access to the computer I was able to learn ways of thinking that allowed me to do well in math and programming.

# Thread: Oysters in Denver

### Glamazon — 2012-11-03T00:40:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61645>

For Happy Hour, Fresh Fish Company has a half dozen raw for $3.00. Same price goes for the shrimp/crab cocktail. That's in the Hampden and Tamarac mall.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T00:54:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61648>

I just shucked a dozen.......I should post the pic on FB. I think I will.

### ealachan — 2012-11-03T02:27:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61670>

6 oysters for $3 is a screaming deal! My local fish place gets $1.35 each for them, and that's not even shucked!

### Lyndra — 2012-11-03T04:02:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61706>

Gee, that would almost make the drive down there worth the effort!

### diane — 2012-11-03T05:24:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61717>

I bought almost 2 dozen today at the market - but they were way more than $3/dz. But Denver is a long drive!

### Glamazon — 2012-11-03T09:15:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61727>

> **ealachan;63431 wrote:** 6 oysters for $3 is a screaming deal! My local fish place gets $1.35 each for them, and that's not even shucked!

It's a genuine happy hour! It's 50 cents an oyster served shucked on a bed of ice with horseradish and cocktail sauce. I think I will be eating my dinner there more often. It's way cheap. And they are good.

The Rocky Mountain Oysters are good too.

[http://www.thefreshfishco.com/](http://http://www.thefreshfishco.com/)

### ealachan — 2012-11-03T11:20:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61735>

I will have to just take your word on the Rocky Mtn oysters...never had them. I'm still trying to work my way up to liver, so deep-fried testicles are still a long ways off for me...

### Glamazon — 2012-11-03T14:25:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61743>

> **ealachan;63500 wrote:** I will have to just take your word on the Rocky Mtn oysters...never had them. I'm still trying to work my way up to liver, so deep-fried testicles are still a long ways off for me...

You can also cook them in stew. They have a slight gamey taste. More like a dark meat versus a light. Definitely not a strong taste like liver. I have a hard time choking down liver unless it is in liver sausage.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-03T18:16:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61798>

> **Glamazon;63509 wrote:** You can also cook them in stew. They have a slight gamey taste. More like a dark meat versus a light. Definitely not a strong taste like liver. I have a hard time choking down liver unless it is in liver sausage.

Forget frying & stewing, raw is the way to go!

Said by someone who has never experienced them and likely never will

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-03T20:38:15+0200

dayum, that is a good price. Think they will let me buy them 100 at a time?

### Glamazon — 2012-11-03T20:42:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-in-denver.4643/post-61827>

you could always ask. i don't think there is take out. That's better than buying em retail. 6 dollah for a shucked dozen. I was wondering who there supplier was. Whenever we take our field trip we can head on over there. They are just a couple of miles apart.

### Glamazon — 2012-11-04T21:33:43+0200

[http://www.cnn.com/2012/10/19/health/fish-oil-brain-injuries/index.html?goback=.gde\_1190417\_member\_177038682](http://http://www.cnn.com/2012/10/19/health/fish-oil-brain-injuries/index.html?goback=.gde_1190417_member_177038682)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T01:45:57+0200

it's an Epi paleo win for sure.......biology always trumps bull\*\*\*\*y science. If I could show you all the cases I have used what I know to help people using this knowledge........and the saddest part.......docs are afraid of it. This kills me as a surgeon. watch this video.......I did her case after my own biohack to prove the validity in CT in me and one other patient. The general surgeon who sent her to me is still in shock. She is a starfish now. \[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;83LMKY5LorA\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=83LMKY5LorA\[/video\]\[/video\]

### hope2conceive — 2012-11-03T15:24:15+0200

I think you are getting us to where we all need to be....the word doctor comes from the Latin docere, which means to TEACH...doctors are meant to teach us...and you are the quintessential teacher. I am SO glad you are!!!!!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T17:07:05+0200

### nonchalant — 2012-11-04T03:36:11+0200

I bend at the knee and give you a giant thank you.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T03:27:31+0200

### fitness@home — 2012-11-03T03:34:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61692>

### teller — 2012-11-03T05:10:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61712>

I haven't posted much yet, but I've been lurking for a long time. I want to thank everyone for sharing their n=1. I have a doozy to share with ya'll if I can get out of my own way first...

### diane — 2012-11-03T05:17:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61713>

Teller - we look forward to hearing it!

Yes, thank you to everyone for sharing their stories, giving support, and sharing thoughts and opinions. I'm learning so much and hope I can pay it forward.

### Susan M. — 2012-11-03T05:19:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61715>

I also do not post much and I have also been observing ever since Dr. Kruse began posting. Thank all you lovely people who do post.

### caroline — 2012-11-03T09:36:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61730>

Thankyou Dr. Kruse and everyone for all the information and wonderful stories and caring that goes on here. This is an amazing forum and my life has been so greatly enriched beyond measure. In central Asia they have a wonderful greeting that just blows me away and I would like to offer it to all of you - I put my left hand on my heart and bow to you.

### SeaHorse — 2012-11-03T14:22:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61741>

Hi again all....I've gone back to non-posting mode recently, not because I don't have anything to say (!) But because I'm trying to spend more of my day away from the computer and that is because I'm feeling so much better!!! So, as I said to Dr. K in my last consult....I'm a success story. I've learned and taken to heart so much from this forum that I want to go out and get back to work with my clients and horses. This time last year I was in total dread of the coming winter....not so much this year. In fact I'm still jumping in our pond two or three times a week besides stepping up the cold showers.

Some starfish may swim away for awhile in grateful appreciation that they can! So thanks to all of you and Dr. K for being here. And I'm still reading up on new posts etc.....won't give that up for anything.

Cheers, SeaHorse

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-03T14:30:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61745>

Not that i think this is all about me, but I am often struck that as i got these diseases a forum and a site like this appears to allow me to heal myself and help others. Thanks to all and especially DR. K.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T15:58:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61761>

It's helped me immensely to read others' journeys here. When you first started writing about CT, I thought, well I'll never do that...too "out there". But everyone started writing about their experiences, so I thought, ok, electronic peer pressure here...I'll jump in the icy water!

It's the combination of the blogs + the comments + your responses to the comments + the forum = change.

And I love to hear the differing opinions and the open discussions. Each N=1 might be different, depending on our different conditions to which we came here, and hearing the discussions really helps me.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T16:13:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61765>

Every N-1 has a different context.......and that is a large point I make her daily.......just because one person has an ED or hashi's should not be the green light for you to apply their N-1 to your situation........this is the biggest error I see on the internet in Paleo sites and forums.......and no one says it aloud so people stop and think...........If I told you to jump off a bridge you would not but if you had Hashi's and I mentioned something to person X everyone seems to think if they have Hashi's they should try it........bad thinking.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T17:02:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61779>

I look at this forum as an extension of my own family in some ways.........

FAMILY teaches the habit of sharing. Running a successful FAMILY is an art and requires sophisticated skills. If you are happy in your personal life, your chances of success in your career or job are greatly increased in this life. Be thankful 19 days before family is celebrated in your house. If you're thinking about it now it means a lot more.

I have a vested interest in keep my family well.........so in this respect you are all in my family.

### Jonathin — 2012-11-03T18:09:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61792>

Your generosity and attitude continues to blow my mind!

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T18:15:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61797>

> **Jack Kruse;63546 wrote:** Be thankful 19 days before family is celebrated in your house. If you're thinking about it now it means a lot more.

Actually been thinking about family a lot, as I'll be seeing them all very soon. I've tried "starfishing" with them, but things barely get through. It kills me to watch diabetic mother eat "healthy Triscuits" because that's what the ADA says, or quad-bypass + multiple stent father eat lowfat cheese because that's what his cardiologist says. Or 5-mile-a-day running-addicted sister keep running like that because Runner's World Mag says so.

Every time I see them, I try to share more, but I do give so much thanks that I DO have them in my life to annoy them! LOL.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-03T18:43:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61801>

This is a family endeavor in my house the kids are taking to it like ducks take to water.

### SueC — 2012-11-03T22:25:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61852>

I'm the health "nut" in my family; no one else wants to change. But I work in a health food store, and slowly, so slowly, I am changing people's minds a tiny step at a time. I am deeply, deeply grateful for everyone on this site -- for the benefits in my own health and those I can pass on to anyone willing to listen.

### kathylu — 2012-11-03T22:42:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-like-to-post-an-fyi-for-all-my-forum-folks.4646/post-61856>

>
> I have a vested interest in keep my family well.........so in this respect you are all in my family.

So Dad, can I borrow the keys to the Ferrari?

Thanks Jack. I wouldn't spend my time here unless I believed that this is one of the most valuable communities I have ever seen!

# Thread: Supplements on hCG
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-on-hcg.4649/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: New Zealanders urged to take an at-home omega-3 blood test
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-zealanders-urged-to-take-an-at-home-omega-3-blood-test.4652/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T09:31:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-zealanders-urged-to-take-an-at-home-omega-3-blood-test.4652/post-61729>

> Omega-3 health expert and consultant to the World Health Organization, Stuart Tomc, is urging New Zealanders to take an at-home omega-3 blood test in a bid to improve public health and save the national health system millions of dollars per year.
>
>
>
> Tomc says the typical New Zealander consumes far too much omega-6s, leaving them chronically lacking omega-3s and more susceptible to diabetes, heart disease and obesity.
>
>
>
> Tomc arrived in New Zealand this morning ... He will explain how a simple at-home blood test will enable people to see what their omega-3 and omega-6 levels are and adjust their diet accordingly to achieve the proper balance. By doing this, Tomc says peopleâ€™s health will improve, alleviating pressure on the health care system.
>
>
>
> "The root of most health issues stem from inflammation," says Tomc. ...
>
>
>
> ... Tomc noted that todayâ€™s Western diet, which has more processed foods than at any other time in history, has completely thrown essential fats out of balance. "We over-consume the inflammatory omega-6s which has left us chronically lacking omega-3s. This makes us more susceptible to conditions like diabetes, heart disease, and metabolic syndrome (i.e. obesity). For example, the average American eats 10 times more omega-6 fatty acids than omega-3, and as a consequence, the United States has the highest rate of obesity in the world. New Zealand also has a high rate of obesity with one in three people overweight, and one in four adults obese." ...

[http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234](http://http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T17:03:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-zealanders-urged-to-take-an-at-home-omega-3-blood-test.4652/post-61780>

sounds like me.........LOL

# Thread: Is cold water splashing outside effective as CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### gkalmens — 2012-11-03T16:01:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61762>

I would like to try splashing cold water on my body while standing in a swimsuit outside when it is below 40F (5C). Is anybody doing it? Is it effective compared to ice baths? How long should such sessions last?

\- Gene

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T16:08:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61763>

is it better than nothing? yes. Is it optimal like getting in the pacific ocean? not close.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T16:15:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61767>

For me, believe it or not, sitting in the cold water (50-52 F) is actually easier than cold-water splashing or showering. Just submerging my entire body actually feels better.

When I first read about CT, I, too, splashed some cold water, and then I tried to take cold showers. Brrr, and not pleasant. At all. So one Saturday when I was bored, I dumped my entire freezer's icemaker full of ice into my tub, took a meat thermometer with me, and just jumped in. It was about 58F. I was so surprised that, while GETTING in was tough, once I was fully IN, it was actually really nice.

### ealachan — 2012-11-03T16:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61768>

Splashing is like torture for me! I'd way rather just get in and stay in.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T16:49:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61775>

i agree with the ladies too

### gkalmens — 2012-11-03T23:58:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61866>

I don't have a problem taking cod baths and I stand them pretty easily. I did it last spring; in summer I soaked in Lake Michigan for 30 min when it was cold enough. I take cold showers in the morning and before bed plus after gym. I also walk every weekday to the train station and back (25 min each way) in very light clothing (I've added gloves lately though). I thought to add something less time-consuming to my late fall-winter routine than cold baths. Given the answers, I will continue with cold baths. Thanks!

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-04T01:24:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61876>

I think if you're going to add to the cold baths, face dunks would be great....elliciting the mammalian dive reflex.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-11-04T03:50:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61907>

> **Jack Kruse;63542 wrote:** i agree with the ladies too

Me three!!

### gkalmens — 2012-11-04T05:14:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61919>

I hold ice packs fresh from the freezer to my face as long as I can stand it. At the same time, I can breathe freely; with face dunking I need to hold my breath. I wonder whether ice packs are an acceptable substitute for face dunking.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-04T15:41:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-61959>

> **Randychaps;63680 wrote:** Me three!!

me 4 full immersion

### philerup — 2012-11-05T09:26:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-62165>

full immersion is superior to any other method imo. i used all methods and only get a real good shiver after my cold baths. the shiver lasts quite a while.

### Adria — 2012-11-05T16:57:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-cold-water-splashing-outside-effective-as-ct.4658/post-62204>

Watching my animals.. they will not swim in the cold weather but will purposely run through the water, splashing! We have 20's here now and snow and (my lab especially) loves to run in the shallow water in the creek behind our house. Once frozen, he'll try to break the ice at the water's edge and plunge his legs into the cold. They're not interested in the big lake or in swimming til spring.

# Thread: Hello everybody!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-11-03T20:00:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61818>

We were hit by Sandy so we live very much Kruse like these days. It is very dark and cold every day ! Running water is our only luxury. Thanks God for that!

No cell phone, no internet service, so I missed a lot on this forum. I just got a chance to get on a computerto post this for a few minutes. Hopefully, our power should be restored by Nov. 11th., I hope.

Have to fetch some gas somewhere. Life is very simple right now. We have to concentrate on the basics.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-03T20:03:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61820>

Good luck, you are missed - hope recovery is helped by the back to nature forced on you

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T22:00:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61844>

Destiny you will be better for it......I promise you. I went through this with Katrina.

### caroline — 2012-11-04T02:03:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61884>

Big hugs to you and your family - thinking of you.

### kathylu — 2012-11-04T04:14:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-61912>

Absolutely thinking of you! So many people here (Washington/Baltimore) really sighed with relief that the storm didn't come 50 miles further south...then we would have been in a heap of hurt. As it is we still have thousands of folk without power in Maryland, though (fortunately) I'm not one of them.

### Destiny — 2012-11-14T01:59:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64147>

Thank you, all! We are back in the wired world. I am just happy to have it behind me. Unfortunately, there are still many people who cannot say that.

### caroline — 2012-11-14T07:23:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64179>

Being wired is great isn't it - missed you. xo

### Destiny — 2012-11-14T13:16:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64216>

> **caroline;66138 wrote:** Being wired is great isn't it - missed you. xo

Missed you too! Yes, I feel connected with the world again !

### CarolMorris — 2012-11-14T17:56:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everybody.4661/post-64274>

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-14T21:18:21+0200

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thanksgiving-menu.4663/post-61836>

Hello to all of you on the road to Optimal

I would love to hear what you are making for the big T-day meal....if you have given any thought to it. Or, if you have done this a while, what have you done in the past?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T21:57:24+0200

oyster lobster shrimp and a nice seafood soup........and lots of squash dishes.......and Meat!!!

### Hope — 2012-11-03T22:46:57+0200

### kathylu — 2012-11-04T00:10:37+0200

First course of seafood stew, then rolled fresh (uncured) ham stuffed with cranberries, apples, nuts, sausage, celery and onions. Sides, mashed cauliflower w/ butter, arugula, fennel and sweet onion salad followed by coffee and paleo pumpkin pie. Hey, it's a feast you know....

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-04T01:33:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thanksgiving-menu.4663/post-61878>

For my Thanksgiving (in Canada) I had DUCK!! It was superrrr yummy! I had a morrocon sweetpotato and carrot soup, home made cranberry sauce. Roasted pumpkin wedges, roasted veggies. Also had curry mussels. I had a pie that was a different type of squash from pumpkin with farm cream etc. It was surely a feast for SURE!!

# Thread: CT 4, CT6, and the Leptin RX are getting some experience in Antarctica:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-ct6-and-the-leptin-rx-are-getting-some-experience-in-antarctica.4666/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-03T22:14:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-ct6-and-the-leptin-rx-are-getting-some-experience-in-antarctica.4666/post-61848>

A 38-year old man named Aaron Linsdau is seeking to become the first American to hike 1400 miles from Antarctica to the South Pole and back.

In Antarctic Trek, Temecula Man Aims to Make American History: [http://temecula.patch.com/articles/in-antarctic-trek-temecula-man-aims-to-make-american-history](http://http://temecula.patch.com/articles/in-antarctic-trek-temecula-man-aims-to-make-american-history) ~ Take a gander at what his diet will be constructed of? OSunds very Sherpa like to me when they climb Everest. He will subsist on upwards of 6,000 calories a day, consuming mostly BUTTER including two sticks for breakfast every single morning! Despite this "hypercaloric" nutrition plan, he still expects to lose 15-20 pounds. Can anyone say Cold Thermogenesis Protocol test? Cold + Ketogenic diet = massive health win. Follow his 2 1/2 month journey here: [http://www.aaronlinsdau.com/](http://http://www.aaronlinsdau.com/)

### Glamazon — 2012-11-10T15:41:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-ct6-and-the-leptin-rx-are-getting-some-experience-in-antarctica.4666/post-63369>

I looked at his FB page. He is packing high carb foods like peanuts and cereal, cookies and chocolate bars and about 60 pounds of kerrygold butter. He has pics of his prep.

# Thread: Ancient tools made from seashells
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-04T10:08:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-61935>

If you think you know you need to look for stone tools, what might you miss? ....

Here is an article "Shell Tools Rewrite Australasian Prehistory" from 2009:

> Dr Katherine SzabÃ³, of the University of Wollongong, has challenged the orthodox view that the early inhabitants of Australasia lacked sophisticated tool-making skills by showing that shells were the basis of their technology, rather than bone or stone. ...
>
>
>
> ... The shell tools \[these are ones from eastern Indonesia\] had been carefully chosen and shaped and **were, in fact, much more complex to produce than the stone tools,’ she says. ...
>
>
>
>
>
> Australasia’s high diversity of marine molluscs makes shell the logical choice of raw material, along with organic materials such as bamboo, for tools and weapons in a region that lacks easily worked stone. Despite this, shell is not a material that anyone has looked at in detail nor understands very well**. ...

[http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/shell-tools-rewrite-australasian-prehistory/](http://http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/shell-tools-rewrite-australasian-prehistory/)

Unfortunately, the full article is behind a paywall.

The archaeologist seems to specialize in molluscs and sea-travel:

[https://researchers.anu.edu.au/researchers/szabo-ka](http://https://researchers.anu.edu.au/researchers/szabo-ka)

### Melinda — 2012-11-04T14:49:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-61947>

Good find, again. Thanks for posting all the information you find.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T15:38:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-61957>

I think Aussie is leading the way to the Epi paleo lifestyle with their study of HG and human history.......great find.

### Michael — 2012-11-04T16:56:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-61993>

> **Jack Kruse;63733 wrote:** I think Aussie is leading the way to the Epi paleo lifestyle with their study of HG and human history.......great find.
>
>
> Yeah, it's nice, isn't it?
>
>
>
> I think the writing's on the wall now. There's some good archaeological evidence out there. It's also clear that so many health parameters are affected by the nutrients that are most abundant in seafood, particularly DHA and iodine.
>
>
>
> Our past is tied up with the sea and rivers - as the source of the nutrients for encephalization (as Cunnane persuasively argues, as you showed us), as a continuing source of easily-caught food, as the route out of Africa and across the globe ...
>
>
>
> This alone:
>
>
>
> Fish was a vital food source for prehistoric peoples. Not only is fish highly nutritious, and often relatively easy to catch (not requiring stone tools for either the hunting or eating process), it should be remembered that although many fish were small, several species of prehistoric fish were much larger than modern day equivalents, some even growing as big as a large mammal, so that a single catch could provide a substantial food source for a number of people.
>
>
>
> There were over 28,000 species of fish available to prehistoric peoples.

[http://www.uwhg.org.uk/reports/uwhg\_meetings/01\_apr\_10/01\_apr\_10.html](http://http://www.uwhg.org.uk/reports/uwhg_meetings/01_apr_10/01_apr_10.html)

The writing's on the wall. I think anyone from now on who publishes a book pretending to talk about human nutrition and our past who doesn't have at least one chapter devoted to discussing water-resources doesn't know what they're talking about.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T17:54:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-62013>

This is why the Paleo 1.0 books all look fabilish to me......none of them looks at this data and yet the data is buried in Cordain's latest work. You would think a group of unconventional thinkers would look at the huge evidence and examine it with the human physiologic context to guide nutrition decisions. But instead they are building a new paradigm and fostering a new dogma built on incomplete science........not going to end well in my opinion.

I just keep following the path that evolutionary biology and medicine are leading me too. Every step I uncover, is another day I recover.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-04T20:20:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-62060>

Dr. K, I keep wondering why you chose the Paleo community. Why not the Pescatarians? Is it because of Paleo's psudo-evolutionary point of view? Or Paleo's willingness to eat a variety of foods?

### Birdy — 2012-11-05T07:55:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-62156>

makes sense to me. I use shells as tools, much superior to using bone, because shells don't splinter. Never, ever have I said to myself, "hey, I think I'll make that tool out of stone,". However, I did "make" some bone meal using stones, for the garden. The thing is, had I had a some real fish bones, much much easier.

ha ha paleo? If I hear one more time, "the men went out hunting for game while their women gathered and stayed in caves...." We should all know that you don't need big muscles to catch fish.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T12:00:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-tools-made-from-seashells.4668/post-62176>

> **Birdy;63955 wrote:** We should all know that you don't need big muscles to catch fish.

Sure.

I think the _only thing the anthropologist who was the inspiration behind Born to Run_ got right was that hunting with missile weapons might be more difficult and uncertain that people had assumed. After that, of course, he jumped to the wrong conclusions.
