### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T17:35:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62008>

A “Chizhevsky Super Event” is named after famed Russian scientist Alexander Chizhevsky (1897-1964) who founded “heliobiology” (study of the sun’s effect on biology) and “aero-ionization” (study of effect of ionization of air on biological entities). Chizhevsky was also was noted for his work in “cosmo-biology", biological circadian rhythms and hematology.

Now if you think this is bunk consider what just happened in NYC and NJ: The Chizhevsky Super Event theory predicted this storm all the way back in March because of the solar maximum that occurred on 3/23/2012. His work is where I found out about the viral alterations in the oceans by the sun and how humans are impacted by it.

Chizhevsky is most notable for his use of historical research (historiometry) techniques to link the 11 year solar cycle, Earth’s climate and the mass activity of peoples, research that was confirmed by Western scientists earlier this decade in two important studies titled Amplifying the Pacific Climate System Response to a Small 11-Year Solar Cycle Forcing and Synchronized Northern Hemisphere climate change and solar magnetic cycles during the Maunder Minimum.

Important to note there are the “four components” of Chizhevsky's eleven year cycle and their approximate lengths: 1) a three year period of minimum activity characterized by passivity and autocratic rule; 2) a two year period during which masses begin to organize under new leaders and one theme; 3) a three year period of maximum excitability, revolution and war; 4) a three year period of gradual decrease in excitability until the masses are apathetic.

Of these “four components” our world has now entered into the Third Period: A three year period of maximum excitability, revolution and war. Sounds like Iran and Isreal no?

According to Chizhevsky’s research, RAS scientists state in this report, the defining characteristics to be looked for when our world enters into a Third Period “Super Event” timeline is a rapid overturning of existing weather phenomena combined with an equally sudden major changing of the global social-political power structures all occurring during a period of Solar Maximum. Can you super storm Sandy?

Interesting to note about the current Solar Maximum we are in was the 23 March 2009 scientific article titled Space Storm Alert: 90 Seconds From Catastrophe published by New Scientist that eerily describes somewhat exactly what is now occurring in the United States following their East Coast region being struck by what is being called a “derecho” storm that, in part, says:

“It is midnight on 22 September 2012 and the skies above Manhattan are filled with a flickering curtain of colourful light. Few New Yorkers have seen the aurora this far south but their fascination is short-lived. Within a few seconds, electric bulbs dim and flicker, then become unusually bright for a fleeting moment. Then all the lights in the state go out. Within 90 seconds, the entire eastern half of the US is without power.” Remember the crazy Aurora in NY and then two months later Sandy comes.......

According to NOAA's Storm Prediction Center web site, a derecho (pronounced “deh-REY-cho”) is a widespread, long-lived wind storm that is associated with a band of rapidly moving showers or thunderstorms.

Chizhevsky’s research, however, states that derecho storms are but a precursor to the much more damaging events associated with a “Super Event”, a finding made even more terrifying with the 2008 news that NASA's five THEMIS spacecraft had discovered a breach in Earth's magnetic field ten times larger than anything previously thought to exist. Solar wind can flow in through the opening to “load up” the magnetosphere for powerful geomagnetic storms.

RAS scientists in this report blame this “loading up” of the magnetosphere caused by solar flares for causing not only the catastrophic Russian floods, but also our present global weather chaos, to include: 1.) The unrelenting heat wave hitting the United States that has caused at least 30 deaths, hundreds of forest fires and billions in crop damage. 2.) The torrential floods and rain continuing to batter the United Kingdom. 3.) The catastrophic floods that have hit India leaving nearly 100 dead and millions homeless. 4.) The historic and severe drought that has hit northern and central Mexico. 5.) The historic drought that has ravaged Brazil’s northeast.

Most important to note about a “Chizhevsky Super Event”, this report concludes, is that while no one is able to predict exactly what the “main event” may be, the historic record leaves no doubt whatsoever that it is coming with terrifying suddenness and will most certainly upend the entire world as we know it.

Of particular concern for this particular “Chizhevsky Super Event”, say RAS scientists in this report, would be the sudden outbreak of global war combined with an equally catastrophic global flu outbreak such as that which happened last century during the Solar Maximum decade of 1910-1918 that saw the outbreak of World War I and the Spanish Flu Pandemic that combined killed estimated 500 million people. There ya go Cavemam!

Not known to but a few researchers was that in 2001 Canadian researchers warned that influenza epidemics are more likely to sweep the globe when the sun develops spots and sends its excess energy barreling toward Earth such as it is doing now.

### chocolate — 2012-11-04T17:41:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62011>

My grandmother allegedly died of the Spanish Flu. She was issued a death cert and to be buried before sundown. Her brother sat by her side with a shot gun until she came to and deliriously spoke in the night of crossing the great divide. I guess you could call it a shotgun wake. She woke up hungry and ok the next morning. Pretty neat. Everyone thought the river brought it. We had the only well in town above the river level and people drew water from it and left cigarettes and smoked and joked terribly afterwards and when I was a kid, you had to skinny dip with certain kids because they would be beaten if their folks knew they had been in the river. The fear was huge.

And the solar flares are freaky.

> Good grief. It's like stumbling into an asylum.

>
>
>
>
> > "This is thinking that, in truth, has nothing to do with Mayan culture," said Alexander Voss, an anthropologist at the University Of Quintana Roo, a state on Mexico's Caribbean coast. "This thing about looking for end-times is not something that comes from Mayan culture."
>
>
>
> Quite. The roots of this way of thinking are obvious enough:
>
>
>
> [http://oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/apocalypse](http://http://oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/apocalypse)
>
>
>
> It's coming from a culture that has not always taken the Revelation of St. John the Divine with the grain of salt it deserves. IIRC, that book nearly wasn't in the canon - and perhaps it's a shame it was. Not that the genre isn't older - see the book of Daniel. But you have to know how to read the genre - it's not so much "prophecy" as oblique commentary. It's a way of grumbling about the present under a cloak.
>
>
>
> Other cultures tend to think in cycles not unfolding stories heading in straight lines to a destination:
>
>
>
> Braswell compared the Mayan calendar, with its system of cycles within cycles, to the series of synchronized wheels contained in old, analogue car odometers.
>
>
>
> "The Maya long count system is like a car odometer," Braswell wrote. "\[The odometer on\] my first car only had six wheels so it went up to 99,999.9 miles. That didn't mean the car would explode after reaching 100,000 miles."

[http://www.foxnews.com/science/2012/10/01/experts-meet-to-debunk-mayan-calendar-end-world-stories/](http://http://www.foxnews.com/science/2012/10/01/experts-meet-to-debunk-mayan-calendar-end-world-stories/)

Calm down. The car won't explode, fortunately.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-05T05:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62143>

the earth gets constantly whacked with 174 petawatts of solar energy, constantly, every second of every day, and varying less than 0.2% for thousands of years.

a variance of even 0.1% is 1.74 petawatts.

every, nuclear, hydro electrc, thermal powerplants, all cars, trucks, warships, toasters and birthday candles all together put out 0.016 petawats of power,

a hurricane, 0.1 petawatts

The only Woo Woo is believing the sun does't affect every facet of our existence.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-05T05:28:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/universe-activity-influencing-human-behavior.4677/post-62146>

just looked it up,

The Canadian Journal of Infectious Diseases. 2001, shows correlation between flu pandemics and the 11-year sunspot cycle with a 4fold higher incidence of pandemics at solar peaks. Some of the epidemics studied where in years 1729, 1830, 1918, 1957, 1968 and 1977, all years with statistically significant higher than normal solar activity.

due to the small sample size, the statistical confidence in correlation over random chance is only 98%

on this topic, in my business the "business" seems to come in groups too. The old adage of when it rains it pours. Just as this board heated up this weekend, people started communicating more. . .people out in the world probably started feeling motivated to DO stuff. (hive behavior?)

So, I really would love to spend the day responding to all of yesterdays stuff - but alas, it's pouring here - and just for fun I looked at my agencies "booked showings", it's a great benchmark for a hot day on the market. Sure enough, this weekend was a giant bump in showings. And the house that has no action for two weeks that I was going to steal for a client - now has multiple offers so I'm off to hunt and kill. I'm most assuredly warrior, I love this kind of battle.

Even in a really down market, that ugly little house with no action usually gets lots of interest on the same days. It's like something in the universe tells people. . .GO FORTH AND NEST!

# Thread: Constipation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/constipation.4682/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-11-05T01:59:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/constipation.4682/post-62107>

Can anyone help me out? I've resorted to using Miralax the past couple of days, without results (yet). If I include more veggies/fiber/fruit in my diet, the problem gnerally goes away. But I seem to not tolerate them very well in other ways. So now I'm stuck (literally!). I just found out I have an unbilical hernia, and it bothers me more when I'm backed up (or bloated - which is great immediate motivation to avoid the foods that would help with the constipation).

I'm eating mainly sockeye salmon, steelhead trout, scallops, some beef, ghee, a serving of cabbage/carrots, and dark chocolate. Keeping to these foods keeps the bloating at bay. I know my T3 is sub-optimal (low-midrange during the day, low overnight), but not totally horrendous. Doctor won't re-test until mid November.

I'm having surgery on the (umbilical hernia in a week). I would love to get a grip on this little problem before then!

### ealachan — 2012-11-05T02:11:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/constipation.4682/post-62113>

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-05T04:06:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/constipation.4682/post-62132>

Lots of fat....especially coconut oil. My son had massive trouble with constipation and that was a goto preventative for us.

Vitamin c can also help if it is a matter of the urge being present but the stool is too stiff. Load with a gram ever 1/2 hour until your guts feel rumbly.

Good luck!

# Thread: Importing webinars into iTunes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-11-05T02:08:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/post-62111>

Hello, has anyone out there been able to import a webinar from the computer onto iTunes for import into a phone etc? I can't seem to be able to....with any of the .mp3 files. Hm.....

I so would love to have them with me on the road.

### shilohman — 2012-11-05T04:44:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/post-62140>

> **hope2conceive;63889 wrote:** Hello, has anyone out there been able to import a webinar from the computer onto iTunes for import into a phone etc? I can't seem to be able to....with any of the .mp3 files. Hm.....
>
>
>
> I so would love to have them with me on the road.

Bring up iTunes

click File->click 'Add to Library'

Navigate to where your .mp3 resides

Open it

Now that you have the .mp3 in your Library do:

In iTunes on the left column you will see Library and under that you will see

Music

click Music

and look for the mp3 that you opened, since the webinar mp3s do not have the any tags

you will not see a lot of information.

click to select the mp3, then click file and then New Playlist from Selection

You should now see the name of the mp3 under Playlist in iTunes

You now can double click on the name and rename it if you want to make the name more meaningful.

Hope this helps.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-05T04:48:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/importing-webinars-into-itunes.4683/post-62141>

Al I sent you a link over skype......make sure Kathy watches it.......very important.

### diane — 2012-11-05T03:02:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-great-example-of-not-accepting-the-diagnosis-youre-given.4685/post-62122>

An inspiring story of how one woman didn't accept her diagnosis of a learning disorder, and now helps others learn.

Barbara Arrowsmith-Young is the Director of Arrowsmith School and Arrowsmith Program. She holds a B.A.Sc. in Child Studies from the University of Guelph, and a Master's degree in School Psychology from the University of Toronto (Ontario Institute for Studies in Education). Arrowsmith-Young is recognized as the creator of one of the first practical applications of the principles of neuroplasticity to the treatment of learning disorders. Her program is implemented in 40 schools internationally.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;WUWlQtcQZtE\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WUWlQtcQZtE\[/video\]\[/video\]

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-11T15:40:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-fatigue-rx-brain-gut-16.4686/post-63583>

Huck I almost felt the same. I some how got some infection so I'm treating that atm. I'm also heavy loading my digestion. My focus is stomach acid. This is not based on anyone else's info except for my Sx, overall life Sx and tests. So I work diligently to correct this imbalance. I know that even though I eat super clean doesn't mean I'm magically exempt from any disease or illness coming my way, just means way better resiliance against them and wayyyyy less negative effects. I have to keep in mind my own peculiar 'defects' that my body/genes came in from the start.

I will give my phone as an example. It kept shutting off by itself. I ran some low level tests to determine what could be wrong. One thing I noticed is it seemed to last longer when plugged in. So I determined it could be something with the battery. I went to order a battery (not pay until it arrives) and then test new battery to see if that is the cause. In the interim I still felt like this was not exactly the explanation. It was not really perfect with the plug either...just a little better. So another time when I pulled the battery out I decided to look around on phone itself. (I figured to myself it could be a defect on the hardware and in a place I can't access). I did notice however that four metal things were there and it looked like one was a little crooked. It didn't line up with the other three. I wanted to be careful but I decided to nudge it just slightly. I did. The phone works now. I found the true defect. The battery ends up working just fine. (I know there's a lifetime on it, but for now it is still working).

So how this works to this thread is that I think we can fix a lot of external problems like overall hormones and sometimes forget our own little defects which need to be addressed otherwise we see reversal of health again despite Epi-paleo and circadian cycles. (Albeit they really DO help a lot and make things way less damaging). Please be sure to always identify what is your personal defects as well as what your strengths are.

Here's a personal example. On the surface I was thinking that I only had hormone troubles (primarily the main issue last year that I was most concerned about). Well it turns out that I fixed any surface hormone problems in terms of excess estrogen and the like. I know that this is not my underlying defect though. I know that digestion (for one example) is more of an underlying defect for me since birth. I look at the kinds of things that affected me at birth and childhood versus now. Very similar findings. Even at the onset of puberty I did not have trouble with PMS, or hormones giving trouble. I had a regular period. I did not gain excessive weight, no cramps, or mood swings. I just gained period and breasts. It was not until 3 years later that I started to have more in common with other people about cramps etc. Even still, not until 19 did I for the first time have REALLY bad cramps. So anyways what this means is that hormone imbalance is/was not my primary defect. It only seemed to be 'affected' as a result. So now I sit with reversed hormonal issues and a new focus on my personal digestive issues etc.

Long post so thanks for reading.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T21:26:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fritdjof-nansen.4696/post-62243>

I like all these quotes:

> Never keep a line of retreat: it is a wretched invention.
>
>
>
> I demolish my bridges behind me - then there is no choice but forward.
>
>
>
> It is better to go skiing and think of God, than go to church and think of sport.
>
>
>
> War will cease when men refuse to fight.
>
>
>
> Never stop because you are afraid - you are never so likely to be wrong.
>
>
>
> The difficult is what takes a little time; the impossible is what takes a little longer.
>
>
> [http://galaxyofquotes.sumobaby.net/authors\_quotes.php?authorId=6](http://http://galaxyofquotes.sumobaby.net/authors_quotes.php?authorId=6)
>
>
>
> I was wandering through our county town a couple of weeks ago and a second-hand bookshop had in the window a copy of the first edition in English translation of _Farthest North. Wish I could have afforded it.
>
>
>
> An amazing man - in exploration, in diplomacy, in science.
>
>
>
> Wikpedia only mentions Nansen and science with reference to the expeditions in the Fram_:
>
>
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fridtjof\_Nansen#Scientist\_and\_polar\_oracle](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fridtjof_Nansen#Scientist_and_polar_oracle)
>
>
>
> But there's much more:
>
>
>
> Did you think that Fridtjof Nansen only led expeditions to the Arctic? If you did, you were mistaken. Welcome to the world of neuroscience at the Faculty of Medicine. Here, Nansen's legacy is everywhere.

[http://www.med.uio.no/klinmed/english/research/news-and-events/news/2011/nansen-brain-legacy.html](http://http://www.med.uio.no/klinmed/english/research/news-and-events/news/2011/nansen-brain-legacy.html)

[http://www.apollon.uio.no/english/articles/2008/hjernen-nansen.html](http://http://www.apollon.uio.no/english/articles/2008/hjernen-nansen.html)

# Thread: Staying optimal while traveling
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-11-05T21:46:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62249>

I used to have to travel fairly often for work. Thankfully the economy took a nose dive about the same time as my health (ok, no - I'm not thankful that the economy sucks, but the timing was kind of advantageous for me, just sayin') and due to budget cuts I really only had to make a few overnight trips a year. For the most part they've all been within driving distance (< 8 hours or so) and were only for 2 nights, which made things easier.

I'm now headed to Orlando for business the week after Thanksgiving and will be gone for a full week (4 nights business + 3 nights pleasure!). Any suggestions to help with staying on the right track?

I'm already planning tons of seafood, of course. I'm staying in a condo so I'll have a full kitchen. I'm going to try to hit Daytona Beach once or twice for a dip since it's only about an hour from Orlando.

I'm just a little nervous about the whole trip messing with my routine. In one regard I think it may actually be good for me - a chance to relax and unwind. DH is coming down for the last 4 nights and we'll make a mini-vaca out of it, which no doubt will be great for us. On the other hand, I feel like I've finally got this lifestyle down with a good routine - CT first thing in the AM before work, lights out at dark, etc, etc. I hate to stray from that.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-06T00:55:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62284>

Bring your sleep mask and orange goggles.

I try to get a room far from the automobiles. It seems that people like to beep their car alarms all night long.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-06T02:38:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62294>

I stay cold and I eat no differently......sleeping on the road is the roughest for me......so I turn the room into an icebox. When my wife is not around it is harder for me to sleep.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-06T02:57:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62298>

I bring enough food for the trip and rarely eat out. Seaweed snacks, canned salmon, bacon, grilled hamburgers, etc. I use my sleep mask and orange glasses like Nonchalant suggested. While every thing I do while traveling is not optimal (staying up too late and business meetings-no glasses there!) I try to keep up the good work but don't obsess about it.

### caroline — 2012-11-06T10:39:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62323>

> **Jack Kruse;64101 wrote:** I stay cold and I eat no differently......sleeping on the road is the roughest for me......so I turn the room into an icebox. When my wife is not around it is harder for me to sleep.

I think the biggest reason I think you are so cool \[apart from your great brain and what you share with all of us\] is the high regard you have for your wife. I love that.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-06T13:26:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62334>

I've taken 2 business trips since embracing optimal.

1st time around the hotel we stayed in provided a full breakfast of eggs bacon sausage w/the room rate. WIN for the rest of my meals I chose only foods that were meat/veggies -sine we were in Texas that was pretty easy

2nd time around I brought oysters & sardines bpc (brought my own grounds, ghee, coconut oil, camping coffee maker & stick blender). I ate fresh gulf shrimp for lunch every day oysters & sardines for BAB & dinner. And damn was I hot. I kept my room AC at 66 and it was still too hot

Slept like a baby but then again I was at an on airport hotel and I always sleep better when I'm in the pattern.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-06T21:07:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/staying-optimal-while-traveling.4698/post-62406>

Thanks for all the tips and advice. I think I'll make an effort to reap the benefits of both being away and relaxing while also (hopefully) sticking to my routine as much as possible. Will definitely pack the orange glasses and some candles . . . they'll come in handy when DH is down anyway

Truth is, we're usually in bed these days by 8:30 anyway! LOL! Sooo, it's not like we're party animals. I'm pretty sure we'll still crash early. I'm just hoping the first few days there on business don't mess with me too much. I'm sure I won't sleep well, so I'll really have to focus on diet and cold.

# Thread: Waterâ€™s Edge Ancestors
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-06T21:16:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62412>

This is a good round-up of work at Pinnacle point and elsewhere by _Science News.

> In a cave hugging South Africaâ€™s lush southern coastline, Curtis Marean suspects he has cornered a wily Stone Age crew that brought humans back from extinctionâ€™s brink. These plucky refugees of continent-wide desolation were able to pull off such a stunning evolutionary turnaround because they got lucky. A coastal oasis near the bottom of the world spread its sheltering arms in the nick of time.
>
>
>
> Marean proposes that it was there, where the Arizona State University archaeologist now conducts excavations, that **humankindâ€™s mental tide turned sometime between 164,000 and 120,000 years ago. Seaside survivors learned to read the moonâ€™s phases in order to harvest heaps of shellfish â€” **brain food extraordinaire â€” during a few precious days each month when ocean tides safely retreated. ...
>
>
> Lots more. The full article is well worth a read.
>
>
>
> Another good short quote:
>
>
>
>
> > ... **some scientists now argue that red meat has been oversold as a dietary staple.**
> >
> >
> >
> > At a meeting of the American Association of Physical Anthropologists, held in Minneapolis in April \[this was 2011\], researchers argued that ancient menus focused heavily on food from lakes, rivers and oceans. New work presented at the meeting pointed to lakeside fishing in East Africa nearly 2 million years ago, the shoreline shellfish harvesting among Homo sapiens at Pinnacle Point starting more than 160,000 years ago and sea voyages to Pacific Ocean islands by an unlikely group of New World settlers around 12,000 years ago.
>
> **
>
> > ...
>
>
>
> As well as the Arizona State University people ([and see website here](http://http://iho.asu.edu)), a researcher from the University of Oregon in Eugene is quoted. And so is South African anthropologist David Braun of the University of Cape Town:
>
>
>
> â€œA large rock is probably all that ancient hominids would have needed to catch fish,â€** Braun said at the anthropology meeting. Even simpler fishing techniques may extend deep into primate prehistory. Orangutans have been found to snatch catfish from shallow ponds bare-handed and chase the finned snacks out of deeper water with poking sticks

[http://www.sciencenews.org/view/feature/id/332828/title/Waterâ€™s\_Edge\_Ancestors](http://http://www.sciencenews.org/view/feature/id/332828/title/Waterâ€™s_Edge_Ancestors)

Isn't it interesting to know that ourang-outangs will do this?

It's kind of embarrassing that Cordain can be found saying from time to time that he works closely with archaeologists and anthropologists. Clearly he doesn't really_ pay that much attention to what's going on in that world or paleo would know what these people are doing and saying. He wouldn't still be pushing the ninety-year-old savannah hypothesis. When did you last see a mention of Pinnacle Point on a paleo site? If it ever has popped up on a major paleo site it's done so very quietly and briefly.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T04:17:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62479>

Very interesting article. NIce find, Michael.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T04:22:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62480>

Michael I have been saying that for a while.......Guys that protect the paleo franchise are the ones who want me to be silent.......but the science makes my words bolder.

### Michael — 2012-11-07T08:46:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-edge-ancestors.4709/post-62493>

I don't mean to grumble about people in the movement. There's a bit too much of that already on many paleo sites. Just to mention what they seem to be missing (and perhaps imply some people shouldn't make quite such large claims and take quite such a confident tone). It's difficult not to point at online resources like that and not see the incongruity of what they're saying.

It's not just Cordain. Robb's the same. Again, I don't want to make personal criticism. As a person, he seems a nice bloke. But how many times has he metaphorically speaking "dined out" on the story of hitting an elk with a spear-thrower dart on "I, Caveman"? I don't get U.S. TV but as I understand it, they took him and some others to a high plateau and ... well, lets's stop there. He obviously didn't say to them: "A more realistic scenario would be to set us down near a well-stocked river. Or on a seacoast with plenty of shellfish and regularly visited by marine mammals." He wouldn't have said that, because he wouldn't have known.

I don't think "cavemen" would set up camp somewhere _away from_ resources. Why would they do that? The picture of throwing missile weapons at a venture even seems problematic to me. Doubtless it was a way of procuring food at times, but I'm sure hunting must much of the time have been much more conceptually developed and far more organized. There's a classic tale of travel in 18th century Canada. Ray Mears has discussed it on TV before - Samuel Hearne's journey. At one point they stop to hunt and spend some days in the forest filling gaps with brushwood and felling trees in other places. They then drive the deer into this arrangement which funnels them into a killing zone. And that's just a few men on a journey.

Throwing a missile at a venture at a four-footed animal is probably what the average person pictures "cavemen" doing.

This gets even more questionable when we start thinking about, say, Homo erectus, let alone Australopithecines.

Strangely, J. R. R. Tolkien seems to have intuited the answer to how you feed yourself without much infrastructure.

Gollum, of course, is a solitary creature. (His real name, Smeagol, is cognate with an Anglo-Saxion word meaning selfishness, which is a linguistic game on Tolkien's part.) He's fallen out of society. He's got no shops to go to; he's not connected with a social network in which people are growing, trading, preparing, cooking, selling, and serving foodstuffs. He's got no tools. He hasn't even got a tinderbox. What can he do? He can catch fish with his bare hands and devour them raw.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;fYiCPmwOV4A\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fYiCPmwOV4A\[/video\]\[/video\]

A small hominid with, at this point, limited strength and intellectual abilities and little or no speech would be in much the same position.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Alzheimer's detected decades before symptoms
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-07T16:39:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-62516>

> Researchers have found some of the earliest signs of Alzheimer's disease, more than two decades before the first symptoms usually appear.
>
>
>
> Treating the disease early is thought to be vital to prevent damage to memory and thinking.
>
>
>
> A study, published in the Lancet Neurology, found differences in the brains of an extended Colombian family predisposed to develop an early form of Alzheimer's ...

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-20206267](http://http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/health-20206267)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T16:45:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-62520>

you eat Epi-paleo and guess what? Beta amyloid goes bye bye..........Just sayin'

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T16:55:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-62523>

> "Brain scans of 20 people with the mutation, aged between 18 and 26, already showed differences compared with those from 24 people who were not destined to develop early Alzheimer's.
>
>
>
> The fluid which bathes the brain and spinal cord also had higher levels of a protein called beta-amyloid."

So their brains are already compensating for poor nutrition that early in life? Can't it just steal resources from other parts of the body? Perhaps, if it were a deficiency. But when it is being assaulted with carbs or fructose or MSG or whatever, it has to defend itself somehow. Enter beta amyloid.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T16:59:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-62525>

No......neurons can adapt for a while......but ultimately they swell and get overcrowded in the cytosol and protein folding is altered and beta amyloid is the result. Go re read the blog now is your protein bent or how PD and AD are related......and then think about whay you read here and what I said........AD is 100% and Epigenetic disease of modern diets.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T17:18:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-62535>

Oh, yeah, it's altered protein folding. Lack of DHA, inflammation, uncoupled mitochondria... Deep Malnutrition indeed.

Sorry, I forgot. ;-)

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T17:31:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-62540>

When did "cytoplasm" become "cytosol"? I want all the terms I learned in 9th grade to stay put!

### chocolate — 2012-11-13T03:35:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alzheimers-detected-decades-before-symptoms.4718/post-63932>

> **Jack Kruse;64363 wrote:** No......neurons can adapt for a while......but ultimately they swell and get overcrowded in the cytosol and protein folding is altered and beta amyloid is the result. Go re read the blog now is your protein bent or how PD and AD are related......and then think about whay you read here and what I said........AD is 100% and Epigenetic disease of modern diets.
>
>
> Cornell Researchers Find That Starvation or Overeating Affects the Genome
>
> By Christie Rizk on Nov 07, 2012
>
>
>
> It’s well known that overeating or malnutrition affects the human body's ability to function. Now genetics researchers are going beyond physiology to delve into how nutrition impacts the genome. Some in the emerging field of nutrigenomics are investigating how diet alters the way DNA is transcribed into RNA. Othes are looking into how nutrients influence gene expression.
>
> Recently, the Qian lab found that the same sets of transcripts can produce completely different kinds of proteins when subjected to different nutrient or stress signals. The scientists were most surprised by their discovery that too few or too many nutrients can cause messenger RNA to start and stop synthesizing proteins at different locations, thereby changing the makeup of the final protein product.
>
>
>
> "Starvation totally resets the whole translation machinery,” Qian says, by selectively translating part of the mRNA to help the cell survive. But, he adds, "if you have too many nutrients, it can do the opposite and suppress parts of the gene translation and up-regulate the other parts," leading to obesity and related diseases.

[http://nygenome.org/blog/cornell-researchers-find-starvation-or-overeating-affects-genome](http://http://nygenome.org/blog/cornell-researchers-find-starvation-or-overeating-affects-genome)

# Thread: Kids as Starfish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-11-07T18:03:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62559>

Two stories I had to share with yâ€™all cause I know youâ€™ll appreciate them much more than any of my real-life friends/acquaintances!

My 8-year old son came home from school yesterday and said the teacher was handing out Jolly Ranchers for treats for doing well on their spelling test. Incidentally, DS got a 17/12 â€“ 141%. There were 5 challenge words that counted as bonus points and he got them all right. Anyway, he got the best score in the class and the teacher told him he had first pick and could choose 5 candies. He said â€œno thanks.â€

Of course the teacher was shocked and the kids all told him he was crazy. One asked him why he wouldnâ€™t want them. He told them all about the chemicals used in the food dyes and went on about how we donâ€™t eat crap that isnâ€™t real food â€“ he defined this for them as food thatâ€™s eaten without being pumped full of junk and made in a factory.

A little girl asked him later at lunch if her strawberry milk had chemicals in it to make it red like the Jolly Ranchers and he read the ingredients on the bottle and showed her where it listed Red 40. He said she put it down and went back in the line to get a water bottle.

Now, if an 8-year old can help to educate his peers about this why canâ€™t adults get it together and set the example for their kids?!?!

Next little story: 12-year old daughter woke this morning not at all happy about the outcome of the election. Weâ€™re generally fiscally conservative/socially liberal people, so we can swing either way in an election. This particular year weâ€™ve been talking quite a bit about Obamacare, so sheâ€™s up to speed on what that is and what it may mean for us and everyone. We talked about it a little this morning and she said â€œThis is why Dr Kruse was put in our lives. Weâ€™re lucky that we know enough to hopefully never need a doctor again if we just live like weâ€™re supposed to.â€ She often repeats Jackâ€™s mantra â€œwhen you know better, you do better.â€ I love that she is getting it

So keep plugging away with your kids. It will sink in! We're still not perfect and the kids certainly haven't drank any MHS's, but I have to say they are pretty darn cooperative and willing to try just about anything I put in front of them as long as they understand WHY.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T18:37:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62581>

That's great! Turning down 5 jolly ranchers, wow! Glad he is explaining it to his peers. I guess when you aren't filled with preconceived notions, it is easier to listen to something different.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-07T18:41:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62584>

Big win for you and the kids' friends!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T19:35:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62602>

Pure awesomness seeing two starfish created.........and the most remarkable thing is their little friend behavior was changed on dime over milk...........kids get it.........I cant get adults to get it........after 18 months of blogging. Amazing........made my yr really.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-07T19:59:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-as-starfish.4720/post-62611>

> **Jack Kruse;64441 wrote:** Pure awesomness seeing two starfish created.........and the most remarkable thing is their little friend behavior was changed on dime over milk...........kids get it.........I cant get adults to get it........after 18 months of blogging. Amazing........made my yr really.

Well, thanks so much to you Jack for sharing all your knowledge and wisdom. The time you spend on creating starfish and enabling us all to take charge of our own health is amazingly generous. My daughter was thrilled to read your lengthy response to her question yesterday about flexibility. It blew her mind that so much is related to hormones. I've been drilling that into her over and over, but I think reading your response yesterday just tied it all together perfectly for her.

She has now committed to taking cold baths every other day and, knowing her determination, I'm pretty confident she'll stick with it! Her new favorite meal is a shrimp/bacon/avocado salad that we all love. She'll eat this daily if I make it! Considering she wasn't a fan of seafood before, I'd call that success

### Inger — 2012-11-07T20:52:32+0200

BJK77.. wow.. I loved to read this...!!!! Thank you so much for sharing!!! Wow. Kudos to you. You just made me feel so warm in my heart right now, I had no easy day. I know we have so much power when it comes to kids. They look up on us.. they do. Kids are just so beautiful. I was visiting my brother and his wife and 2 kids in summer, and I took them with me a lot out in the nature.. and they loved it. They loved to eat the wild herbs I showed them, the wild berries, all. Kids still have much of their natural instincts. They loved to be in the cool water all day long.. and eating mud..lol.

We have so much power. Much more then we are aware of.

Thanks for sharing your story. You must be so proud of your family. It is so disappointing that adults you care so much about won't listen at all - but a child gets it. amazing stuff!

Go up in elevation so you have CT year-round. Not knowing what you really want - I'd say Wyoming or Montana but then I don't prefer crowded locales. But that's only thinking in US-terms - I have no idea where I'd choose if the world were my oyster.

### Inger — 2012-11-07T21:09:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62642>

I am thinking.. the Lofoten Islands in north Norway where my mom comes from.

The family have a cottage there up in the moutains. It is so beautiful. So much fish in the surrounding sea. So much seaweeds, bladderwrack every where. I am thinking this must be a really healthy place to live for sure... My mom is very healthy and slim even if 60 yo and beeing a mom of 15 kids, she was swimmimg a lot in the cold Atlantic as a kid, she told me they were totally blue as kids but still were swimmimg the whole summer..lol It does not get very warm there in summer eather. I guess they were quite cold adapted. I am thinking I got my love to cold water from my mom.

She told me they had to go skiing to school in deep snow, very far. Every day. No school busses or anything. They had fish too on the menu quite often. But no one was eating seaweeds tho. I would if I move there one day. I am thinking about moving to Norway anyways. They have more and better fish there. The Baltic sea is polluted for sure, not so the Atlantic. You can go fishing wherever you want for free, and you get a lot of fish!!! Big fishes! I need to move there. My brother has invited me to live by him he lives alone in a small own house he buyed. I am considering it for real. I have nothing that keeps me here really. In Norway you get Jobs easy, they have only around 3% of their population unemployed. You get crabs there too, they are easy to catch by yourself. And mussels. And fresh raw shrimps. Talk about heaven...

### prAna303 — 2012-11-08T14:07:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62864>

Right where i am now...

Many of my old friends move to the big cities, abroad and away. I have stayed here since birth. I love my hometown because i have adopted around it.

To the south i have the mountains with endless trails, boulders to climb and the sun to adore. Just 5 minutes from my door.

To the north, very much the same as south. About 10 minutes away.

To the east i have the sea with plenty of fishing and freediving. Give me 10 minutes and i hit that element.

To the west i have the deepest of forests. Here i can run for hours and never meet a single human.

I live in the elements for up to 10 hours a day and when i want to listen to great classical music i can go to the church for free. I can watch art, dance, music and other nice stuff in my hometown. ItÂ´s great.

On top of this i get four seasons with minus 25 degrees in the winters and plus 25 degrees in the summer.

But the most important things is: You see the world as you are.

### stacyj — 2012-11-08T15:26:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62874>

Anywhere not in a densely populated area, with room to grow my own vegies, have some livestock, have room for my family......kind of like the 380 acres I was raised on in Nev....

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-08T15:49:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62876>

I love the idea of completely re-thinking houses. They've evolved through the whims and necessity of the building industry. I think there is tons of room for improvement there.

I am on the hunt constantly for the perfect "forever" house to renovate and area. I have a great architect who's mom lives off in the mountains without electricity and Architect HATES it - but she did her mom's house and she can think out side the box.

So far the working design has a basement type sleeping chamber room. A landing area/entry to sleeping rooms with a small functional potty/brush your teeth kind of bathroom. No shower/bath etc there.

The "sleeping chambers" are sort of on basement level - depends on site (radon issues in mountains). Each chamber is much smaller than a normal bedroom, with a built in bed, low ceiling on one side and a half-walk-out large window on the other side. This is can be opened for fresh air flow, but should remain relatively quiet because of being mostly underground. It is also set back a few inches so black drapes/blinds on timers will be in front of it. Should be super energy efficient and easy to to the 50 degree sleeping temp. Blinds scheduled to come up with the sun. . large window is a mini-garage door, so it actually OPENS and lets in real air and light when desired.

No closets and none of the extras like cable/tv/whatnot. One outlet by the bed for light. . .and a vented "candle place" (smaller version of fireplace) for fire for lighting should that be what is desired.

Walk up the landing steps and to one side is the giant "dressing" area. Kind of a triangle. The dressing area has a washer and dryer IN it, with sitting room, dirty clothes storage, shoes, large mirrors. One side has a door coming from the outside and a mudroom function (snowy, sandy wet clothes and boots can come off here), One side has a door going through to the house bathing facility and the third side transitions out to the public space.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-08T16:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-place-to-live.4721/post-62877>

The new thing in houses is for everybody to have a complete stand alone life and every bedroom is large and a master. The newer nicer bedrooms are like little hotels so nobody has to interact or share. There is lip service to a "great room" kitchen type thing - but the expensive houses mean all the grownups work and all the kids are booked with activities. It's barely more than a fast food joint.

It drives me nuts!!!

My more modest clients are much happier people/families than my 1% ers. The de riguer 1% life is lonely and it seems to be a parasitic organism.

Modern healthcare needs an epistemologic foundation.

Chemistry has it

Biology does not............

### ealachan — 2012-11-07T20:20:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62630>

I buy it, 100%. I know too many hardcore fundamentalists that are super nice people but that truly believe evolution is totally made up B.S. I respect their right to believe that if they want to, of course...but, yeah. I don't find those figures hard to swallow AT ALL.

### Michael — 2012-11-07T20:58:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62640>

> **Cavemam;64464 wrote:** DO NOT BELIEVE. This has got to be one of those "bad study" type things. I want to know details on methodology of survey.

That aside, it's a skewed sample. It's The West plus Turkey. And Turkey comes bottom. It would.

"The West" essentially = Europe + Europe's diaspora ... in this case minus Australasia. And modern Japan really counts as the West

But what if you included non-Western countries other than Turkey? Would they agree? Would they even understand the question?

In some ways the U.S. isn't like the rest of the West, because it was neither overrun in the political/military upheavals of the twentieth century (like much of continental Europe) nor pretty much bankrupted by those upheavals like Great Britain. The cultural landscape of Europe would look different if it were not for that. Then you have to ask larger questions - like what's the _downside of having the kind of culture Europe has nowadays? There are interesting demographics here, too. People on the right - such as Mark Steyn - would point out that religious societies seem to maintain population numbers - as the U.S. does up to a point. Demographically, Europe is dying and what replaces it will likely be a _different_ religious culture that has rather less_ respect for science.

### Inger — 2012-11-07T21:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62646>

Yeah. When talking about religiousity my heart bleeds. My almost whole family is very religious.They are polite and always want to listen to my health advice, after all they feel my energy and can see I do well. But. There is the big BUT. GOD is the big guy after all. He will save us. Food does not really matter after all. So they have no consistency, they try a little, but then fall into the old patterns, because God will resolve our problems we just need to pray. Or believe more. While we have the holy spirit we are saved anyways. It is not all about food. But they keep suffering. They need their coffee and cakes after their meetings. I need to not start to talk about this because it hits a point that hurts big time in me. I have seen people suffer bad because of their religious fanatic believes. My sisters husband that are very religious is at the edge of prostate cancer in young age and with 7 kids. But God will help, no need to change their very bad eating habits. My heart bleeds. You can never win an argumantation with someone that believes they have the holy spirit and you have not. It is doomed to fail. So I have learned to not even start it.

But my heart still bleed. Especially for the children. They are not even allowed to go out and play before having a prayer hour with their parents. They so much want to go out, and how healthy would it be! But no.. OMG I have cried alone in my room after facing this. But I can do nothing. Yes I can. I can take them with me after their prayer hour and show them all the beauty in the nature. OMG I can tell you the kids loved it. Me too. I enjoyed every second. Very beautiful memories. I so hope I have planted some good seeds in the kids for the future..

Mmmm.. I wonder if I talk totally out of concept here. If, I beg you all my pardon. I just enjoyed some nice Malbec.. and that might have an impact..

My cat wants my attention.. she is laying on the floor talking to me with her feets up in the air..lol

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-08T17:21:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-62893>

What a sad survey. I live in Texas, and I grew up in the heart of the Bible Belt, so I do not believe these figures are skewed.

I happen to be single these days, and there's an online dating site I was on called OKCupid. They have all these "matching" - type questions; once can answer zero or up to about 1000 questions, and the site supposedly "matches" you. Everything from eating habits to sex.

Anyway, the one set of questions I always looked for first to see how a guy answered were the evolution-type questions:

\--Do you believe in evolution?

\--Should creationism be taught in schools?

Etc.

Any "incorrect" answers to these questions caused me to automatically delete these guys from my search. By "incorrect", I mean the "No" to the belief in evolution, or the "yes" to teaching creationism, etc.

Trouble was, I had only about 5% of the men left. Literally. So I got off the site, and I'm still single.

I'd say about 75% of the people said the earth. And they were not joking. So sad.

> DarleenMB--That's just so...sad. I live in Texas, so in light of the election, I am now hearing on Facebook the resurrections of the "Texas should secede" mantra. Texas, my friends, is not the center of the U.S. But there are enough "Secede" bumper stickers around here to make one believe otherwise. Sigh again.

That might not be all bad. I'm staying up nights worrying how to accomodate a 51st star on the flag if Puerto Rico joins up (they just voted for statehood). If Texas leaves and PR joins, then we still have 50 stars and no worries about the flag!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-09T21:49:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-63200>

> **Inger;64510 wrote:** Yeah.. I see you Cavemam. But the strange thing is, it does not work...?! They still are sick and suffering..!!!!? So it means their beliefe does not work at all, even if praying and believing so much!? I could tell you more stories about similar things OMG on this I am a experienced woman... huh. Sadly. Growing up in a environment of believers... I have seen a lot. I have seen a lot what hurts.. hurts.. hurts me!!!
>
> Now.. there was also some good for sure.
>
>
>
> Tell me more about this Cavemam. Please tell me more. You seems to know something I do not, or your experiencens differs from mine!

Inger, this reminds me of a joke that a priest once told me. It goes like this (bear with me):

"A man is in his house when the town he's in begins to flood. As the streets fill with water the police come by and tell him to get out of his house. He says, No, God will save me. Then the water gets higher and people come by in a boat. He is now trapped on the second floor of his house. Get in, they tell him. No, he says, God will save me. The water continues to rise and he is now on the roof. A rescue helicopter appears and tries to winch him up to safety. He again refuses, saying God will save him. He then drowns. When he gets to heaven he is angry and says to God, "I believed in you. Why didn't you save me?!?!" God tells him: "I sent the police, a boat and a helicopter. What more did you want?"

From my understanding and my reading of religious texts, working with the notion of God in your life is an act of co-creation. In other words, there are always forces beyond your control; however, there are always choices within your control as well.

When people abdicate their personal choice I do not think this is because they trust that God will take care of them. It is often that they fear taking personal responsibility. It is hard to do. Yet we all must carve out our own way in life, regardless of whether we hold strict religious beliefs or not. And religion is just one of many walls we can hide behind. In the end, we cannot hide from life no matter how hard we try.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-09T23:37:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-63220>

I have had no personal relationship with God or Devil, but lately I am mighty impressed with the Sun.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-adoptation-of-optimal-cause-doctors-and-patients-trouble.4722/post-63257>

Yet we all must carve out our own way in life, regardless of whether we hold strict religious beliefs or not. And religion is just one of many walls we can hide behind. In the end, we cannot hide from life no matter how hard we try.

Lauren,

thank you! That was eye opening what you wrote, indeed. That is what I think too, that they have a mismatch in their thinking. It is not their religion per se. But rather that they have not understood..

Like Jesus says somewhere in the Bible (I know lots of stuff from the bible because we had to read and hear from it all the time as kids), if your light is darkness, how big is then your darkness! Or he says; If I could only open your eyes and make you see..

I am so far from religious.. because I got that all in my throat because of how I was raised and because of what I see today how it makes change impossible and suffering continues.. But I still love a lot what Jesus said. He was a very wise man in my eyes.

# Thread: Report on Vit-D biohack,   My N-1.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/report-on-vit-d-biohack-my-n-1.4723/>

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-07T20:10:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/report-on-vit-d-biohack-my-n-1.4723/post-62618>

I have absorption issues due to a bypass.

I tested Vit D on 4/26, It was 21.

I spent the summer in the sun, well over 1 hr per day on average.

I also supplemented with 20-30iu a day with a variety of oral methods and brands.

I tested Vit D on 9/7, It was 21.

I bitched and whined and complained and had an idea. I did a homemade vaginal insert of 20 iu a day from about the end of September through last week.

I tested Vit D on 11/1, it was 77 in a range of 30-100. Topquartilebaby!

\*\*\*The two medical professionals I told about it said no to this. Not sure if because they didn't think it would work, or because they thought there might be some vaginal harm. Vajayjay seems the same to me.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-07T23:53:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/report-on-vit-d-biohack-my-n-1.4723/post-62699>

TMI ALERT. I shall use ephemisms to trick the googles.

When I went sunning down south, I utilized the gelatin capsules that had olive oil in them. I used a pin to poke some holes in it so the oil/d mix would leak out.

These holy capsules would check into the hotel in groups of 4-6 with 5K iu each when ever I remembered during each day. Time was variable.

Some words of advice: This is not a mess free endeavor. Many capsules would try to escape and they did like to run for their life.

I also tried the pure liquid I found that had Vit-d in olive oil, but they were like sprinters and ran out faster than I thought would do any good for the cause.

# Thread: Oh! ho! We strike gold again
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-07T20:16:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62624>

I always thought the bow and arrow was late in coming. Mesolithic - that's what I was taught.

Maybe not. New post on the Smithsonian blog today. Pinnacle Point again:

> The bow and arrow is an ancient weaponâ€”going back at least 71,000 years, a study published in Nature suggests. Archaeologists working at South Africaâ€™s Pinnacle Point cave site uncovered a collection of tiny blades, about an inch big, that resemble arrow points, likely belonging to prehistoric bow and arrows or spear-throwers. The researchers say the discovery is further evidence that humans (Homo sapiens) started to act and think like modern people early in their evolution. ...
>
>
> [http://blogs.smithsonianmag.com/hominids/2012/11/early-bow-and-arrows-offer-insight-into-origins-of-human-intellect/#comments](http://http://blogs.smithsonianmag.com/hominids/2012/11/early-bow-and-arrows-offer-insight-into-origins-of-human-intellect/#comments)
>
>
>
> Golly, I wonder what might have caused that jump to where people started to "started to act and think like modern people". ... could it be down to consuming fish, shellfish, and marine mammals?
>
>
>
> Pinnacle Point, where they could eat marine creatures like shellfish, whale, and seal.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle\_Point](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle_Point)

This might possibly be an "I told you so" moment for Jack. LOL

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T23:33:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62692>

Michael........you are becoming my favorite starfish........This stuff can help change many of the thinkers out there who espouse bacon and elk first. When eat better we learn better. #Epipaleo it forward!

### Michael — 2012-11-08T08:20:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62847>

More here, including a quote from the project director:

> "Every time we excavate a new site in coastal South Africa with advanced field techniques, we discover new and surprising results that push back in time the evidence for uniquely human behaviors," said co-author Curtis Marean, project director and Arizona State University professor in the Institute of Human Origins
>
>
> [http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/11/07/small.lethal.tools.have.big.implications.early.modern.human.complexity](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/11/07/small.lethal.tools.have.big.implications.early.modern.human.complexity)
>
>
>
> In this version it says the microliths may be for arrows to be shot from a bow or for darts to be thrown from a spear-thrower.
>
>
>
> Another comment on the importance of these coastal sites in Africa:
>
>
>
>
>
> "This is why continued and well-funded fieldwork in Africa is of the highest scientific priority if we want to learn about what it means to be human, and where and when it happened," said Marean.

Science News has reproductions, showing how the microliths may have been hafted:

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### ruby — 2012-11-08T08:46:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oh-ho-we-strike-gold-again.4724/post-62851>

When the Sea Saved Humanity:

Key Concepts

At some point between 195,000 and 123,000 years ago, the population size of Homo sapiens plummeted, thanks to cold, dry climate conditions that left much of our ancestors' African homeland uninhabitable. Everyone alive today is descended from a group of people from a single region who survived this catastrophe.

The southern coast of Africa would have been one of the few spots where humans could survive during this climate crisis, because it harbors an abundance of shellfish and edible plants.

Excavations of a series of sites in this region have recovered items left behind by what may have been that progenitor population.

The discoveries confirm the idea that advanced cognitive abilities evolved earlier than previously thought—and may have played a key role in the survival of the species during tough times.

[http://www.nature.com/scientificamerican/journal/v303/n2/full/scientificamerican0810-54.html](http://http://www.nature.com/scientificamerican/journal/v303/n2/full/scientificamerican0810-54.html)

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1297765/Last-humans-Earth-survived-Ice-Age-sheltering-Garden-Eden-claim-scientists.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1297765/Last-humans-Earth-survived-Ice-Age-sheltering-Garden-Eden-claim-scientists.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-08T14:57:38+0200

# Thread: parallel analogy to what Jack is driving for in the future of health.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-11-07T20:47:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62637>

community based health, driven by collective passion and love.

This forum is like the Shinto shrine, and Perl support.

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xe1TZaElTAs&feature=related\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;Xe1TZaElTAs\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xe1TZaElTAs&feature=related\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;Xe1TZaElTAs)

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-07T22:39:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62667>

beautiful... absolutely beautiful

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-07T23:15:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62679>

The website is not work to me.......it is my passion. I never thought about in the terms in the You tube video.......but one of our members (Jason) sent me an email a few months ago just shooting the \*\*\*\*.......and he asked me what this site was to me.......my comment to him seemed to affect him and he sent me back a reply that I pondered deeply.......because I had not quite thought about what he was asking or how he was feeling about the site........and when I read his reply I got what he meant........What he meant was in that You tube clip..........

### Inger — 2012-11-07T23:17:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62680>

Yes... nice Video. I like this community.

It feels like home.

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-08T00:41:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62718>

It is truly astonishing to contemplate the depth and possibility within the truth in this clip.

"Ask yourself not 'what's the business model?' but 'do the people who like it take care of each other?' That turns out to be the better predictor of longevity."

Based on that, this community, and Jack's work, will be around for a very long time.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-08T00:50:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62725>

Coriander........you get it but some others here dont........you should see some of the complaints. This site offers tons of free stuff.......and they still want more for free. Our gov't creates this entitlement.......but peoples lives and behaviors create the demand for the service. The goal here is teach people just how much control they really have over their own health........when they are clueless about it.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-08T01:50:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62767>

I am so grateful for the knowledge that is passed along to us. I love knowing that this community is full of individuals all on similar, yet different journeys to improving ourselves. I love that it's a "family" where we are given so much for so little - wisdom, encouragement, support, etc. I often wish I could post more and be more active on the forum, but I just don't feel I have the time right now. I do try to contribute as much as I can just as a way of paying it forward for all the wonderful benefits I've gleaned from being here.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-08T02:19:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62778>

> **Jack Kruse;64564 wrote:** Coriander........you get it but some others here dont........you should see some of the complaints. This site offers tons of free stuff.......and they still want more for free. Our gov't creates this entitlement.......but peoples lives and behaviors create the demand for the service. The goal here is teach people just how much control they really have over their own health........when they are clueless about it.

I've seen a few of them myself, though I'm sure lots of them aren't posted on the forum.

### caroline — 2012-11-08T08:15:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parallel-analogy-to-what-jack-is-driving-for-in-the-future-of-health.4726/post-62846>

> **Coriander;64557 wrote:** It is truly astonishing to contemplate the depth and possibility within the truth in this clip.
>
>
>
> "Ask yourself not 'what's the business model?' but 'do the people who like it take care of each other?' That turns out to be the better predictor of longevity."

# Thread: Normal HS CRP

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-11-08T21:49:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-hs-crp.4742/post-62978>

Hi all, can anyone share any reasons why I would have a fairly normal HS CRP (0.2) despite having all the classical LR symptoms and issues...and not great other labs?

Also, it appears my RT3 just went from 17 to 20.4.

I am totally confused....

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-09T00:52:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-hs-crp.4742/post-63025>

Yes.......cellular energy is nto working optimally yet.......if your TG spiked it is a sign of Mg depletion, L-carnitine issues, lack or recycling of D Ribose, and very low CoEnzy Q10. This is also know as paleo flu or low carb flu.

### hope2conceive — 2012-11-09T01:13:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-hs-crp.4742/post-63028>

Thanks so much, Dr K! I will research that right away....

May I ask...what is TG? I can't find what it stands for.

Whatever it is, it sure explains my symptoms....thanks again!

### kathylu — 2012-11-09T04:21:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-hs-crp.4742/post-63066>

> **hope2conceive;64870 wrote:** Thanks so much, Dr K! I will research that right away....
>
>
>
> May I ask...what is TG? I can't find what it stands for.
>
>
>
> Whatever it is, it sure explains my symptoms....thanks again!

TG = triglycerides

### Kristen Harvey — 2023-06-13T14:39:01+0300

# Thread: Let's talk about sex baby, let's talk about you and me, let's talk about sex . . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 348
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2012-11-08T22:39:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-62988>

Iâ€™ve been wanting to start a thread about sex and sexual energy for a while so here goes. Iâ€™m particularly interested in hearing from peri and/or post-menopausal women who have not taken the BIHT route but are using the epi-paleo RX, circadian biology and/or other Dr. K-inspired ideas to reestablish their sexual energy. Mine took a dive about 4 years ago following a series of super stressful events that happened within a short period of time. I was probably already in adrenal fatigue but those events pushed me over the edge. Although my overall energy and cognitive function are definitely coming back online and it looked like it would for a while, my sexual energy has not yet returned to itsâ€™ former glory (I like to say I was a 12 on a scale of 1-10 but am now a 5 or 6). I'm not sure. Maybe I just need to give it more time. If anyone has any words of wisdom or personal experience to share, Iâ€™d love to hear all about it.

### curves64 — 2012-11-08T23:41:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63002>

> **Thor;64836 wrote:** .
>
> I always say use it or lose it and practice makes perfect... more so in men - without daily ejaculation the testicles shrink to the size of grapes and the prostate expands to the size of a small lemon. Without daily erections, Penile tissue begins to die and suffocate for fresh O2 Not to mention the release of Oxytocin from orgasim
>
>
>
> Maybe somebody should open a Paleo Swingers Club - call it the "cave" lol

I know this experience is different for men and women. I'm all for "using it," and "use it" daily, if not twice a day lol. I just miss the intensity and frequency of my orgasms. They were such a vital part of who I was/am that I hate to think of living for another 50 years with that part of me being mediocre.

### ealachan — 2012-11-08T23:45:47+0200

### ealachan — 2012-11-08T23:50:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63007>

curves64, I'm not peri-menopausal and I'm not on any BHRT, so I can't speak to that...but I will say that after a few months of epi-paleo, I don't need nearly as much "convincing" from the hubs to get it on as I used to. There was a time when even him leaning in to kiss me just made me cringe because I knew where he wanted it to lead and I just DIDN'T want anything to do with it. It hurt his feelings, it hurt my feelings, it was a bad scene. Things are MUCH better now. He's happier, I'm happier, the only one that's kind of bummed about everything is the dog because he gets kicked off the bed a lot more nowadays...

### curves64 — 2012-11-09T00:58:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63026>

> **Jack Kruse;64866 wrote:** I am going to do a series on this one day........

I'm beginning to see that as you further align with the natural rhythm of your body by paying attention to circadian biology and feeding it the food it was designed to eat, you naturally align with your confidence, your sexual energy and your intuition. One flows from the other effortlessly.

Can't wait to hear more of your thoughts on the topic.

### ealachan — 2012-11-09T01:42:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63035>

> **curves64;64860 wrote:** Love it!!!!
>
>
>
> Ealachan, would you say that you feel more confident and connected to your body, or is it that your sexual energy has increased or both? I love hearing your story. Thank you.

Both I guess, now that you've made me think about it. My confidence has certainly been helped somewhat by losing a bit of weight, but I also certainly feel more desire to actually do the deed as well. Also, I've never been unable to orgasm, but I certainly have noticed that it doesn't seem to take quite as much time / effort now as it used to.

While I'm at it (no pun intended), I'd like to point out something else interesting I've noticed lately: my husband seems to be able to tell when I'm in the mood without me saying anything. I don't know if I'm emitting pheromones or what - it's the damnedest thing. I'll sit there minding my own business and happen to think of something that makes me think of sex, even briefly, and like 5 minutes later there he is, suggesting we get busy!

### diane — 2012-11-09T01:48:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63036>

I'm perimenopausal, not on BHRT (yet - although trying). I can't say I've noticed much of a dip in sexual energy, even before doing the things here. But, I can say that it's been increased even more by epi-paleo, getting my proper sleep, circadian rhythms. And after a cold bath? Get the fire extinguisher out!

hmmm. Testing the waters here. So to speak.

The BHRT did NOT fix my missing libido which has disappeared along with a few other things. sigh. darn it. It's almost impossible for me to talk about sex without wanting to go hide someplace. bear with me.

### curves64 — 2012-11-09T03:53:18+0200

### kathylu — 2012-11-09T04:08:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-about-sex-baby-lets-talk-about-you-and-me-lets-talk-about-sex.4744/post-63063>

Darleen, there is nothing to be embarrassed about! It's not like you have to stare at us over morning coffee. Funny story...hubby and I spent 2 weeks on St. Martin a few years ago, really nice btw. Lots of nude beaches on the French side...and you know how we Americans are about going "au naturel", but the hotel we stayed at was on Cupecoy Beach, clothes not required. Early in the vaykay, my husband stands up, declaring "I will never see even one of these people again in my life!" and that was the end of clothes on the beach for him for the rest of the trip! I wasn't quite so relaxed...but I was really amused and happy that he was able to leave social conditioning behind.

### diane — 2012-11-09T04:33:32+0200
