# Thread: paleo-era homosexuality?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 38
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-11-10T00:03:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63231>

Let me preface this by saying I am not interested in debating the **morality**of homosexuality here.

The "let's talk about sex" thread got me thinking about this, and I didn't want to hijack that thread, so I started a new one. What I'm interested in talking about is whether or not anyone thinks that homosexuality existed in paleo-era humans. There are certainly plenty of animals that display homosexual tendancies in the modern era, even though homosexuality from a purely procreational standpoint appears to be a disadvantage (ie: no passing on of genes).

Part of me thinks that without the constraints of a society trying to make paleo-era folks feel like they needed to "fit in", that they were likely free to have sex with (and love) whoever they wanted. Another part of me says sure, that's all well and good, but back when it was all about whether or not man survived at all, wouldn't the biological emphasis be on making every copulation "count", in the procreational sense?

I think it's an interesting contrast. Discuss, if you'd like.

### shah78 — 2012-11-10T00:15:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63232>

Let me answer your question with a question. Do you think the heterosexual sex was confined to only genital to genital contact. Take away four thousand years of Western and (even Eastern ) organized religions and I imagine you would have found lots of Paleo (and even early neolithic) people really getting" their non-procreative freak on". Why would homosexual contact be any more "taboo". Taboos are soooooo neolithic!

### Huck — 2012-11-10T02:11:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63250>

Humans and culture go together.

Culture is developed over hundreds and thousands of years based on what works. It's not always right, but it is based on what people believed to be truth. Over time, we learn that some of these truths are either not true or are no longer pertinent, but parts of culture stand the test of time.

A pet peeve of mine is that our current culture in the US and the western world, is to quick to throw away cultural values and norms without a serious analysis of the consequences of such action. For example, I object to gay marriage because something in our past culture determined that it was wrong. Before I am willing to accept gay marriage, I want to know why our culture said it was wrong and why that is a wrong conclusion. I believe that culture is actually the passing of wisdom from one generation to the next.

Although I am not a religious person, I feel the same way about religion. Religious morality has served mankind for thousands of years, so some of it must be working for us. I hate to see it totally rejected by so many in our society when it has served a great purpose.

Most people around the world just try to get by. Customs and religion give them a framework to live by. So my question is, why was homosexuality labeled as taboo by all major religions and all or most modern cultures?

### shah78 — 2012-11-10T02:28:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63253>

With all due respect, a lot of hand waving to justify bigotry. They did similar hand waving regarding slavery down in Dixie back in 1820 and around Munich in 1938. If you don't like homosexuals, blacks or Jews, just say it. Don't hide behind "deep" pondering over moral and/or cultural issues. All obfuscations leading to the same result:declarations and acts of hatred against insular minorities.

### LinD — 2012-11-10T02:35:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63254>

> **Huck;65112 wrote:**
>
>
>
> So my question is, why was homosexuality labeled as taboo by all major religions and all or most modern cultures?

Because major religions' are based on the Holy Bible, and God's Word says it (homosexuality) is an abomination.

Also, the parts 'fit' between man and woman. Yay!

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-10T02:49:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63255>

There can be no doubt that homosexuality existed in the paleo era. It has survived hatred, vilification, and legislation. It will continue as long a there are human beings to love and be loved.

I can't imagine that it would ever have been a threat to survival of our species. Homosexuality is just one of a variety of sexual expression. I think that bisexuality would have been far more common; I mean, if you want to get it on, who's the closest attractive unguarded/willing partner might be the only consideration. Fun is fun after all; and it's what we humans gravitate to.

I'd be interested to know how the Kitavans viewed same sex relationships, given the sexual freedom with which they were raised. Because if you don't know where babies come from, it surely can't matter who you have sex with.

### Huck — 2012-11-10T02:57:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63256>

> **shah78;65115 wrote:** With all due respect, a lot of hand waving to justify bigotry. They did the similar hand waving regarding slavery down in Dixie back in 1820 and around Munich in 1938. If you don't like homosexuals, blacks or Jews, just say it. Don't hide behind "deep" moral and/or cultural obfuscation.

No need to be insulting. I have nothing against gays. As someone trained in Sociology, I have a respect for culture, custom, and religion and value them as a stabilizing influence on society.

That is how I look at cultural events and actions. What is the benefit to society of gay marriage? What is the downside? Are civil unions a better alternative?

These type of issues should not be decided based on emotion. but on the benefit to society. Slavery was obviously not in society's best interest and was corrected in the western world, but still practiced in other parts of the world. Our culture isn't perfect, but it is self correcting over time. I see no reason to be in a hurry to change cultural mores that have been accepted for thousands of years.

In the US we are all equal under the law. That overrides any cultural or religious beliefs we may have. But whether gay marriage is purely a cultural and religious question or a legal question is yet to be settled.

On the other hand, I believe in empirical data and when culture is proven wrong, it should be changed.

### ealachan — 2012-11-10T03:07:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63259>

> **Coriander;65118 wrote:** There can be no doubt that homosexuality existed in the paleo era. It has survived hatred, vilification, and legislation. It will continue as long a there are human beings to love and be loved.
>
>
>
> I can't imagine that it would ever have been a threat to survival of our species. Homosexuality is just one of a variety of sexual expression. I think that bisexuality would have been far more common; I mean, if you want to get it on, who's the closest attractive unguarded/willing partner might be the only consideration. Fun is fun after all; and it's what we humans gravitate to.
>
>
>
> I'd be interested to know how the Kitavans viewed same sex relationships, given the sexual freedom with which they were raised. Because if you don't know where babies come from, it surely can't matter who you have sex with.

That's what I'm curious about. Like you said, on the one hand, fun is fun and we gravitate to it, but on the other hand I don't think it's a question that many of us are truly hard-wired to want to further the species. I'm just genuinely curious if we naturally evolved with the ability to understand that sometimes sex is just sex for the sake of fun, or if that's a more "modern" way of thinking. Switching between genders of sexual partners was certainly a pretty common practice in Roman and Greek times, and surely before. I wonder if it's just always been a part of us, like it seems to be with other animals, or if we basically had to evolve enough of a brain to get past the biological imperative of furthering the species.

### shah78 — 2012-11-10T03:12:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63262>

Dear Huck,The beauty of the gay rights debate is that people of our generation (and older) will sooner than later die off and the empirical data debate will simply go "puff". Young people have almost no hangups on this subject.

### kathylu — 2012-11-10T03:13:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63263>

> **Huck;65119 wrote:** N
>
> In the US we are all equal under the law. That overrides any cultural or religious beliefs we may have. But whether gay marriage is purely a cultural and religious question or a legal question is yet to be settled.

Huck, that's not really true concerning gays, is it? Aside from the emotional and social aspects of marriage, gays in a committed relationship have no legal status or protection in most states. The obvious example is that they have no right to make medical decisions for one another. There are many other examples. Civil unions do not equal marriage, they only approximate it...and they don't eliminate the aura of "2nd class citizen". Smacks of the "separate but equal" racial claptrap of the 50's and 60's. Gays are people too...I don't see any good reason to restrict their legal rights.

### kathylu — 2012-11-10T03:15:59+0200

### Huck — 2012-11-10T03:27:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63268>

> **kathylu;65126 wrote:** Huck, that's not really true concerning gays, is it? Aside from the emotional and social aspects of marriage, gays in a committed relationship have no legal status or protection in most states. The obvious example is that they have no right to make medical decisions for one another. There are many other examples. Civil unions do not equal marriage, they only approximate it...and they don't eliminate the aura of "2nd class citizen". Smacks of the "separate but equal" racial claptrap of the 50's and 60's. Gays are people too...I don't see any good reason to restrict their legal rights.

The purpose of marriage was to sanctify the family unit, man and woman, for the purpose of raising children. Many, me included, dislike stealing the term marriage to mean something different than what it has meant in culture and religion.

Civil unions give gays and anyone else, all the legal protections of marriage, but preserves the term marriage in its historical usage. So civil unions resolve any legal issues. The fight over what it is called is a cultural debate, not a legal one.

### Huck — 2012-11-10T03:29:43+0200

### ealachan — 2012-11-10T03:53:49+0200

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-10T03:57:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63281>

> **ealachan;65144 wrote:** You didn't answer the question I posed, though. I know we're capable of having children, but does the fact that we got married with an expressed agreement to NOT reproduce devalue our marriage, by your logic?
>
>
>

Thats assuming there is an intrinsic value to marriage as if its a product. It is symbolism where the only value is to the individuals interpretation. Nobody can make your experience more or less than than it is by naming it.

### Michael — 2012-11-10T09:45:19+0200

> **LinD;65117 wrote:** Because major religions' are based on the Holy Bible,

Buddhism isn't. If you take a wider geographical and historical perspective you find that Buddhism has actually been the world's most prolific religion, holding sway over pretty densely populated areas of S. E. Asia for a very long stretch of time.

Don't fall into perspectival errors about what is and is not of historical and cultural relevance just because of where you happen to live.

Buddhism, of course, expects similar standards of sexual behaviour to Christianity (and in addition frowns on alcohol). It hasn't always got them: Japanese Buddhist monasteries were noted for homosexual activity at one time.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-10T14:25:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-era-homosexuality.4759/post-63343>

Getting back to evolutionary or survival function there are species of lizards where the males become homosexual when food in the environment becomes scarce. I think this is genius. It allows the males to remain reproductively viable while not actually reproducing. When conditions become right they become hetero again. I think there may be is a similar mechanism at play in humans related to population.

> > This quote from wikipedia is a good summary.
> >
> >
> >
> > Marriage (also called matrimony or wedlock) is a social union or legal contract between people called spouses that creates kinship.\[citation needed\] The definition of marriage varies according to different cultures, but is usually an institution in which interpersonal relationships, usually intimate and sexual, are acknowledged. Such a union is often formalized via a wedding ceremony. In terms of legal recognition, most sovereign states and other jurisdictions limit marriage to two persons of opposite sex or gender in the gender binary, and some of these allow polygynous marriage. Since 2000, several countries and some other jurisdictions have legalized same-sex marriage. In some cultures, marriage is recommended or compulsory before pursuing any sexual activity.
> >
> > [wikipedia](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marriage)
>
>
>

> **Cavemam;65330 wrote:** Ok peanut gallery! As much as I'm dying to know all about your particular kinks. . .I was sort of hoping for a little talk about the BRAIN WIRING for these things.

Ok, so I recently read a theory speculating that foot fetishes may be caused by the way our sensory maps are located on the brain. The area for feet sensory processing is usually right next to the gonad sensory processing area (as in, physically right next door on our brain). This might be because in the womb the feet are usually pulled up near the nads as the brain is developing the ability to sense those areas. A little cross firing/wiring and you have some uber nice sensations when you see/think about/... feet. i think the book I was reading was The Brain that Changes Itself by Norman Doidge

[http://www.amazon.com/Brain-That-Changes-Itself-Frontiers/dp/0143113100/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1352596355&sr=1-1&keywords=the+brain+that+changes+itself+by+norman+doidge](http://http://www.amazon.com/Brain-That-Changes-Itself-Frontiers/dp/0143113100/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1352596355&sr=1-1&keywords=the+brain+that+changes+itself+by+norman+doidge)

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-11T19:48:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sex-sub-forum-topic-three-fetish.4760/post-63640>

Fetishes are ideal for our "monogamy" that current life dictates. It adds a third leg to the relationship that allows for some seperation of church and state. So it allows a fulfilling sex life that isn't dependent on the relationship and also not a threat to it.

Fetishes are more a problem in the beginning as the prince/princess ideal doesn't allow for someone to be aroused outside of the romance. Once someone can get past that notion, OR join in the fun. . .a much better construct for successful companions and mates.

Just like with Pam though, if the person with said fetish is out there to fill that instead of filling a companion/romantic relationship then it is just creepy.

People with fetish: This should unfold at the exact same rate as all sexual conversations. You don't start drawing maps to your orgasm until well after a relationship and sex start to happen. I think fetish is like that. TOO MUCH TOO SOON!

note for single men: If you let the woman "discover" your fetish, you will have a convert.

### ruby — 2012-11-10T04:29:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63287>

Where does this "carbs are the body's preferred fuel source", "brain and heart prefer carbs as fuel" stuff come from? Anybody knows of any research that shows how we know what the body's 'preferred' fuel source is? Any paper that documents it? This is some kind of official mantra that I keep hearing all around but where are the supporting facts?

### Michael — 2012-11-10T09:30:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63328>

I think because practically speaking it's been found that's what it will use if they're around. This is why people who aim to be in ketosis consume such low levels of carbohydrate (

### ruby — 2012-11-10T15:28:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63364>

Thanks, Michael. I will check out the book. The "preferred" fuel source implies to me that glucose is intrinsically superior as an energy source to ketones (better quality, better/less damaging end products etc) rather than just "preferred" as in terms of a simple economic cost/benefit principle (less energy required to utilize it). What is your take?

### Michael — 2012-11-10T16:19:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63375>

> **ruby;65234 wrote:** What is your take?

I don't think I know enough to have a useful opinion.

I think you're right in suggesting "preferred" could have several senses. Maybe people slip between these sometimes without noticing?

I do wonder if the body seeks to do SOMETHING with glucose in a hurry - burn it for energy, store it as fat, whatever, because the imperative is to get it out the bloodstream before it does too much damage. AFAICT, some of the people loosely connected with the WAPF (Chris Masterjohn, Stephan Guyenet, Chris Kresser) want to play down the role of glucose in damage to bloodvessels etc. and say that it's all more complex than that. I dunno. Maybe that's wishful thinking - and not necessarily for the same reasons in each case.

To be frank, I sometimes think people develop loyalty to someone who points a way out of the mire to them. Chris Masterjohn, who seems a very nice and an exceedingly smart man, obviously reverences Weston Price. Robb Wolf seems to feel the same way about Professor Cordain. Both men had been vegetarians and got very ill from that. Suppose the one had read Cordain at that time and the other Price. Would their opinions have been reversed?

You seem to be very adept at trawling through info on the net, so if you ever come across a more precise definition of "preferred" in terms of body fuel, please let me know

My simplistic and possibly ill-informed take on carbs seems similar to yours in that the body for all those thousands of years made its own supplies and this is the preferred state of affairs. Now when we consume carbs (pure and loads of them) and flood it with them, it kind of goes into panic to put out inflammation "fires". And after a while it stops succeeding.

I agree with your loyalty theory, like baby duck-style imprinting. Same happened to me

The diet was pretty good: high in fish, fresh produce, olive oil, and local wine (which would be weak but high in antioxidants) - but hardly low-carb, since it also contained a fair amount of bread and pulses.

Perhaps you can get away with that if_ you get the sleep and don't stare at TVs into the night and have a realxed way of life and no central heating.

### ruby — 2012-11-11T11:25:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63561>

Frank Marlowe's "The Hadza hunter-gatherers of Tanzania" gives a good overview of the lifestyle of current tropical hunter-gatherers.

Seasonality rules. Tubers, which are around throughout the year, are fallback foods when preferred things like meat, honey, berries etc are not available. Lifestyle is wake up between 6:30-7:00am and become active gradually. Eat a bit of leftover food and go foraging around 9:00am. Nap at midday. Start cooking around 5:00pm and eat around 7:30pm. Bedtime is 9:00pm. Ocassionally they stay dancing and singing until midnight. They eat:

17.2% berries, 23.5% tubers, 19.2% baobab, 14.2% honey, 19.3% meat (mammals 6.4%, birds 84.4%, reptiles 0.2%, invertebrates 0.1%) and 6.6% other. Average body fat for adults is 20% women, 11% men.

Looks like significant carbs to me ....

### Michael — 2012-11-11T11:59:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-63563>

Yeah, they seem to eat a lot of honey at times.

I suppose taking what they currently do as representative would be problematic inasmuch as the land that's left to them is probably marginal. Also, they're subject to game laws. The government aims to preserve the game for tourists on safari to view. I guess that wouldn't cover the birds (84%) but they'd probably have to think twice before killing, say, giraffe.

But they seem to be doing OK on what they are getting, whatever.

### ruby — 2012-11-16T07:31:05+0200

seems brain does prefer glucose.

Robert S. Horn of [http://www.medbio.info/](http://http://www.medbio.info/) says:

A stable blood glucose level is absolutely essential for normal brain function. The brain can only use glucose or ketone bodies as its fuel. Ketone bodies (acetyl acetate or ÃŸ-hydroxybutyrate) cannot replace glucose as the brain's energy source on short notice. About 10-14 days are required to increase plasma ketone body levels such that they can provide energy for neural tissues. At most they can provide about 50% of the brain's energy, the rest must come from glucose.

### Michael — 2012-11-16T20:55:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64780>

Nora seems basically to follow Dr. Rosedale in her macronutrient ratios - and, IIRC, credits him explicitly in the back of her book where she gives protein recommenations.

Jack doesn't see quite eye-to-eye with Dr. Rosedale, although he agrees wirh him in some areas. I asked him about Dr. Rosedale's views on the Ask Jack board, and he said he was forgetting how animals stay warm in winter.

AFAIK, protein >>> glucose isn't the only route for gluconeogenesis, although it's the most important one. In any event, Nora's/Dr. Rosedale's prescription provides enough to make as much glucose as is needed as well as to provide for body-building needs.

I think - and how would I know? - that Dr. Rosedale may be over-rating the longevity value of calorie and/or protein restriction. I don't think there's any firm evidence for it in humans.

I think Dr. K would be at ease with a little more protein, and would tell us we don't need to count.

He certainly doesn't believe in year-round ketosis. Carbs are OK when in-season in his book.

Also, I think some of the parameters around what happens on a very low-carb diet CHANGE when that VLC diet is also high in iodine. (Cf the "staying warm in winter" comment.) Dr. Rosedale's diet is not a high-seafood diet.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T21:06:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64782>

Rosedale's diet makes the same mistake as Cordain, Wolf, and Sisson......Seafood is the missing part. Add that and you got the perfect template. None of them get the complexity that neural chemistry brings to the diet and to metabolism and physiology. Brain gut 5 gets into this huge.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T21:09:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64783>

This notion that the brain needs exogenous glucose is pure folly.......our liver makes all we need. We do not need any exogenous carbs period. But this does not mean we cant use carbs when they grow. This is where the paleo crew tries to speak for me. #Epicfaileo

### Michael — 2012-11-16T21:38:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64787>

The other thing I remember from Dr. Cunnane's superb book is that iodine is also involved in several steps in ketosis. That I didn't know. I haven't tried to check, although I have the chemistry to do so, partly because I rather dislike chemistry, having been pushed that way against my natural direction at a young age.

I also suspect no account of ketosis would really be complete without an account of energy metabolism in babies. Newborns seem to go into it relatively easily - and that they should be able to seems to be a must. The large human brain is - and this is something people miss - a huge survival problem (although in the long run it may confer some survival advantages on us). If your brain went short of fuel for any length of time it would mean damage and death - hence the baby has to be able to switch between energy substrates easily. I'm indebted again to Dr. Cunnane's book here. I'm not sure quite why it's easy for the baby to get into ketosis: it may be partly the composition of the milk (although that isn't particularly low in sugar), but it seems partly to be linked to the all-over fat layer babies have. Even their wrists are dimpled. The fatty-acid composition of that fat layer is quite unusual. (And there's presumably a connection to post-partum depression here - the baby's robbed out the fatty acids it needs from the mother.

These seem like deep, complex and interesting matters to me. I think trying to approach it all merely on the level of macronutrient ratios, as many seem to be doing, is no good.

I feel very grateful to Jack for opening up a deeper view on these things.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-16T21:43:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64788>

Thanks to you, again, Michael and to Ruby and Dr Kruse. The more times I circle around this knowledge, the better I understand it. I appreciate all the guidance and help!

Interesting re: babies, brains and ketosis. The literature is clear that breastfeeding mothers are partially protected from post natal depression (and that even when babies have mothers who are depressed, if they are fully breastfed they show normal neurological patterns in testing whereas formula fed infants do not). So not only does nature set us up with ketosis, it has a natural mechanism for optimal for the adults supporting these infants, too. The composition of breastmilk also contains leptin, so there is another factor in how this optimal metabolic circuitry is established.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T21:51:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-64790>

Babies are in ketosis for the first 6 yrs of life.......want to know why? Ketosis is how we myelinate. Think about what really causes MS now?

The nonsense I read from so many paleo book authors is infuriating because they have zero clue about how a human brain is made, born and grows. It is made immature on purpost to fit out the vag and then it myelinates postnatally.......by ketosis......mothers milk is the fuel to do it upon. Once myelinated it can function on a wider range or macro's.......but it is not optimal. Over a life time we marginally lose function when we eat outside the brain's biology. Sustained carbohydrate eating will hurt the physiologic parameters of brain function......it is not arguable unless you are a paleo person who bought the dogma found in their books hook line and sinker. They pride themselves on understanding evolution but few of them understand human brain evolution and this is what sets the standard for what we should eat for our species.

Once myelinated it can function on a wider range or macro's.......but it is not optimal. Over a life time we marginally lose function when we eat outside the brain's biology. Sustained carbohydrate eating will hurt the physiologic parameters of brain function......it is not arguable unless you are a paleo person who bought the dogma found in their books hook line and sinker. They pride themselves on understanding evolution but few of them understand human brain evolution and this is what sets the standard for what we should eat for our species.

WOW! That is just so cool to know!

### Michael — 2012-11-17T10:27:47+0200

There she was recommending something like 3 to 4 full platefuls of vegetables a day, on the basis, I suppose, that you'd get boatloads of vitamins and phytonutrients that way - anthocyanins, things like that. It seems to be working, although people had always said MS was incurable.

But here's the thing. Someone said in the comments something along the lines of "imagine chewing your way through that lot," and Dr. Eades said something like: Is it what she's eating or what she's _not_ eating?

Later when I read one of the few books by Dr. Kwasniewski that's in English I found he too claims big improvements in patients with MS. Now his diet looks nothing like hers. I suppose most of the material on that will be in Polish; but I suppose if he says that, it's what he's found.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-12-10T19:34:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-67056>

Ron Rosedale's talk at AHS (which I recently watched online for free) discussed this too. Â He concurs with Dr Kruse. Â He also points out that all fibrous veggies break down into fatty acids, so that's eating fat too. Â He totally agrees that we could never have evolved into humans with carbs fuelling the brain and further agrees that our liver makes all the glucose we need. Â He points out that you 'could' argue that instead of the liver making glucose you can feed it to the liver through exogenous carbs. However, he suggests (and this seems reasonable to me) that your body does a much better job of knowing when it needs glucose than you do; so if you eat it, you are dictating when your body has to deal with glucose; if you let your body make it, it will make it at times when it's needed. I think this makes sense. Â And given what he says about fibrous veggies, it stands to reason that one could maintain ketosis throughout the seasons, even while eating seasonal veggies when they are growing. Â I suppose the main difference would be starchy tubers and higher glucose veggies like carrots and beets that I think Wahls promotes.

> In fact, the late George Cahill did an experiment many years ago (probably would never get IRB approval to do such an experiment today) to demonstrate how ketones can offset glucose in the brain. Subjects with very high levels of B-OHB (about 5-7 mM) were injected with insulin until glucose levels reached 1 mM (about 19 mg/dL)!Â  A normal person would fall into a coma at glucose levels below about 40 mg/dL and die by the time blood glucose reached 1 mM.Â  These subjects were completely asymptomatic and 100% neurologically functional.

Â 

Not that the brain is the only one requiring some glucose, though.

### ruby — 2012-12-13T06:48:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-know.4761/post-67326>

The info is all out there, it's just overlooked or ... You don't need a PhD.

In the most basic, high-school level nutrition course they teach you about essential stuff that your body needs. These are amino and fatty acids (protein and fats). No mention of carbs (cos there's no such thing as essential carbs).

Essential is defined as: body needs to have it and can't make it. So by definition, essential nutrients are exogenous.

Yet, nobody really highlights or registers the significance of the fact that 'essential' and 'carbs' don't go together.

Rather they go on insisting that if you don't get at least 50% of your diet in the carb form you're setting yourself up for deficiencies and worse.Â

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-10T06:32:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-pressure-then-and-now.4762/post-63306>

I have not had any labs done since starting VLC paleo near the first of Spring transitioning to VLC epipaleo however I took my BP at one of the sit-down BP machines at the local K-mart pharmacy. Early in the year my BP was 130 something over 80 something. Two weeks ago I used the same machine and my BP was 114/69. This is after losing a total of 52 pounds since early Spring. I AM considering getting a glucose meter to satisfy my curiosity about the effect of ketones, certain foods, and at different times of the day.

In case someone could use the info, I read today that IV vitamin C can cause false high blood glucose readings. Anecdotally, someone on the Vitamin C Foundation wrote that oral liposomal vitamin C gave him higher glucose readings. Could be another case of a false reading? It made me wonder how the brain utilizes vitamin C when the body is in a ketogenic state since glucose is just one enzyme away from being vitamin C.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-10T09:19:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-pressure-then-and-now.4762/post-63321>

BP is a function of the bio energenic metabolites for energy replenishment.......IV Mg has massive effects on BP. Just look at pre-eclampsia patients......and when you back it up with support for the mitochondria your BP will further go down. To get it to work longer term you need to slowly replenish those factors........niacin, B6, Mg, L-carnitine, D ribose, CoenQ10

It simultaneously improve cardiac energy generations all both lower your BP big time.

> Sodium, Potassium, and Heart Health
>
>
>
> The 2010 DGA recommends consuming less than 2,300 mg of sodium per day. Certain populations are recommended to further reduce daily sodium intake to 1,500 mg. Yet the overwhelming majority of Americans arenâ€™t in step with official dietary guidelines when it comes to sodium.
>
> > Specifically, 95.2 percent of those recommended to consume less than 2,300 mg donâ€™t meet this goal. Adhering to a 1,500 mg sodium diet appears even more difficult with 98.8 percent exceeding this recommendation. The average American eats more than 3,300 mg of sodium daily. Conversely, more than 98 percent of US adults fail to get the recommended 4,700 mg of potassium per day.
> >
> >
> > Clearly, Americans consume more sodium and less potassium than is recommended. However, recent data suggest that our sodium intake from all sources has not changed significantly since 1988-1994, but our potassium intake is in decline. As a result, the 2010 DGA identified potassium as a â€œnutrient of concern in American diets.â€￾ Simultaneously meeting sodium and potassium guidelines continues to be a challenge, both for consumers and public health officials. A recent publication from Dr. Drewnowski and colleagues examines this important dilemma.
>
>
>

MTHFR !Â  Treat the methylation issues and no more headaches.Â  I'll lay a million dollars on the table.Â  I could cite more than 5,000 studies and research papers showing clear evidence that it's proof MTHFR is the root of bad headaches/migraines!Â  Nothing else....Â 

Â 

Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase Gene Variant (MTHFR C677T) and Migraine: A Case Control Study and Meta-analysis**

MTHFR C677T polymorphism was associated with migraine with aura (MA) (OR 1.31, 95% CI 1.01-1.70, p = 0.039) that remained significant after adjusting for age, sex and depression status. A meta-analysis of 15 case-control studies showed that T allele homozygosity is significantly associated with MA (OR = 1.42; 95% CI, 1.10-1.82) and total migraine (OR = 1.37; 95% CI, 1.07-1.76), but not migraine without aura (OR = 1.16; 95% CI, 0.36-3.76). In studies of non-Caucasian population, the TT genotype was associated with total migraine (OR= 3.46; 95% CI, 1.22-9.82), whereas in studies of Caucasians this variant was associated with MA only (OR = 1.28; 95% CI, 1.002-1.63).

Â 

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3120667/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3120667/)

MTHFR !Â  Treat the methylation issues and no more headaches.Â  I'll lay a million dollars on the table.Â  I could cite more than 5,000 studies and research papers showing clear evidence that it's proof MTHFR is the root of bad headaches/migraines!Â  Nothing else....Â 
>
> Â 
>
> Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase Gene Variant (MTHFR C677T) and Migraine: A Case Control Study and Meta-analysis**
>
> MTHFR C677T polymorphism was associated with migraine with aura (MA) (OR 1.31, 95% CI 1.01-1.70, p = 0.039) that remained significant after adjusting for age, sex and depression status. A meta-analysis of 15 case-control studies showed that T allele homozygosity is significantly associated with MA (OR = 1.42; 95% CI, 1.10-1.82) and total migraine (OR = 1.37; 95% CI, 1.07-1.76), but not migraine without aura (OR = 1.16; 95% CI, 0.36-3.76). In studies of non-Caucasian population, the TT genotype was associated with total migraine (OR= 3.46; 95% CI, 1.22-9.82), whereas in studies of Caucasians this variant was associated with MA only (OR = 1.28; 95% CI, 1.002-1.63).
>
> Â 
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3120667/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3120667/)

Great information - but this isn't true for everyone... Â I've had migraines since I was 10... 23&Me shows now MTHFR defects. Â  Not everything is attributed to this. Â  everyone has their own n=1. Â good info however.Â

# Thread: Ribosylation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-11T12:35:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63566>

Is there a concern over possible increased ribosylation end products (AGE) from taking too much D-ribose? There was an in vitro and in vivo mouse study showing even more ribose induced advanced glycation end products in mouse brains than were glucose AGE products in mouse brains. Is it a matter of having all the necessary mitochondrial cofactors and proper dosing to properly utilize D-ribose so that it doesn't float around producing excess AGE?

I understand that fat burning by mitochondria produces less free radicals/less inflamation than mitochondria burning glucose. Is it the same with mitochondria utilizing ribose?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-11T14:07:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63571>

D-Ribose is not physiologically used at all in glycolytic pathways. It is used only in regeneration of ATP and it salvages xanthine and hypoxanthine from AMP in ischemia...........It is vital in RNA and DNA construction too but it comes direct from pentose phosphate pathway exclusively under the control of two allosteric enzymes. It is not uses in the Kreb cycle to burn for energy so the answer is not in humans.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-13T07:38:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63966>

Geez Louise! Why didn't anyone warn me about Ribose Breath?

Does this stink only appear when mixing the powder with water or will it happen if I swallow capsules, too?

The ingredient label says the only ingredient is 100% D-ribose powder. Would ribose smell/taste any different than D-ribose?

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-13T08:05:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ribosylation.4785/post-63968>

Nevermind. I read that D-ribose is more bioavailable than ribose.

# Thread: Mark, I never thought it of you
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-11T19:18:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63624>

I always liked Mark Sisson, but how could he even think of linking this?

> Despite a general trend for larger mammals to have larger brains, humans are the primates with the largest brain and number of neurons, but not the largest body mass. Why are great apes, the largest primates, not also those endowed with the largest brains? Recently, we showed that the energetic cost of the brain is a linear function of its numbers of neurons. Here we show that metabolic limitations that result from the number of hours available for feeding and the low caloric yield of raw foods impose a tradeoff between body size and number of brain neurons ...

[http://www.pnas.org/content/109/45/18571.abstract](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/109/45/18571.abstract)

Put my leaves and twigs on the fire and everything's solved ... or is it?

How about asking a brain chemist?

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;TUpuhXbUX64\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TUpuhXbUX64\[/video\]\[/video\]

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-11T20:26:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63655>

How did we overcome the metabolic constraint? Re read Brain Gut 13.......optimal performance is a function of the DHA quantum effect on increasing energy production 60% at the Mg dependent ATPase.

Simple. You do not want to know this when you pushing eating meat and meat based supplements to your audience.

This is called going perpendicular.......something few in paleo are capable of.

### Michael — 2012-11-11T21:11:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63674>

> **Jack Kruse;65559 wrote:**
>
> Simple. You do not want to know this when you pushing eating meat and meat based supplements to your audience.

I dunno, Jack. I don't think it's calculated; I don't even think he's engaging in self-deception. It just seems to me he's got a pretty loose grip. He's an energetic man and regularly puts out lively and well-written stuff that's he's put thought and research into ... and yet, like the newspapers, he's got space to fill and if someone says something about the Palaeolithic and it makes it into print he'll throw it out there come what may.

But maybe I shouldn't have posted this thread. And now you comment on my post, and there are probably people in Paleo who'll hate you for doing so.

The other day I accidentally stumbled on what seemed to be a paleo hate-site (really) where anyone can post stuff meant to attack other people in the movement. Internal hatred seems to be becoming a disease in the movement. I didn't stay long, but I caught a post from one lady who was complaining about _you on account she said she'd posted some pieces in 2010 saying the movement was overlooking the evolutionary and nutritional importance of seafood, pre-dating your championing of the evidence. Well, good for her. But I guess the upshot is that now we take against people because we agree with them_. Yikes!

I think the movement will have to come round on this one. There's too much evidence. That encephelization is down to cooking just seems barmy to me. I can't see people as clever as Cordain falling for that one.

I stumbled on some interesting material discussing shellfish gathering in Turkey into the Neolithic recently:

[http://www.yesilova.ege.edu.tr/zaferderin-%20makale.pdf](http://http://www.yesilova.ege.edu.tr/zaferderin-%20makale.pdf)

This stuff is cropping up all over the place.

I think if you add the increasing archaeological evidence for the exploitation of water-resources to the increasing evidence for the importance of the nutrients found most abundantly in them - are omega 3s ever out of the news now? - then eventually people have to take notice.

My guess: it may be all liver and bacon now, but within the next two to three years there will be some interesting and useful presentations on water-resouces at AHS.

And if people like Jimmy Moore are half-awake they'll be on the phone to people like Crawford, Cunnane, Kuipers, Broadhurst, Hibbeln, etc., too.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-12T04:39:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-i-never-thought-it-of-you.4789/post-63776>

Mike......I could care less what they think. I only care about people who seek their answers. Mr. Moore is not really interested in truth.......he is interested in market share and following the pack. He is a real nice man but not a man of science. Me I like going perpendicular with my thoughts. I do not need to straddle the land mass.......I like going straight out to the open ocean and finding a new world........Where Homo's solution really is. The paleo solution is a step int eh right direction from a SAD........but that is all it is.........and we have a cadre of "nice guys" protecting that franchise.......but none of their info will save our species long term. They are like a band aid on an arterial wound a nice thought that is grossly ineffective.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-13T03:47:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-63938>

My sister has decided to give up wheat!!!!!!!!!!

I am so, so happy!

She is tired of being sick, year after year after year.

### caroline — 2012-11-13T09:29:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-63976>

such good news for you and her and others in your family - you are shining the flashlight as J.K. says

### nonchalant — 2012-11-13T18:13:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-64045>

Thanks!

I actually stopped pushing; stopped talking about my diet totally.

It's hard for the baby of the family to take the lead, sometimes.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-13T19:36:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-64074>

> **Nonchalant;65998 wrote:** It's hard for the baby of the family to take the lead, sometimes.

My older brother still knows everything. So much that there's no point discussing anything since all he can say are conversation ending remarks... My little sister on the other hand, calls all the time for advice! Crazy how sibling order continues to play a role in how we see each other, even into ages when it no longer matters. Good for your sister for giving up wheat! I always tell people it is a great time to go gluten free, because it's such an exploding niche diet. So many things are being labeled gluten-free, and products are even being reformulated to be gluten free (like Rice Crispies - how hard was it to remove to malt and replace with a different sweetener?). It makes the transition easier anyway.

### fitness@home — 2014-10-22T22:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144747>

I just had a wonderful realization...let me sit back and enjoy it just a while longer

Not everything I talk about (regarding health-related subjects) goes in one ear and out the other! Every once in a while there are glimmers of hope that more will make it through.

DH just called me and asked, if I were him, would I get a Flu vaccine and the Hepatitis B Series? I decline the flu shot every year. Both are offered at his work. I told him in the end it is his decision, but he should look into ingredients before deciding. Sent him a couple of websites, one being:

[http://vaccines.procon.org/view.resource.php?resourceID=005206#header\_hepb](http://vaccines.procon.org/view.resource.php?resourceID=005206#header_hepb)
I realize vaccines are a volatile subject with many different opinions. The number of vaccines that I received as a child is a small amount compared to current standard practices. It has increased substantially.

**The real point of this is that I _actually had an impact._** It made him stop, think and look for more information and opinions before making a decision.

### nicld — 2014-10-22T22:42:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144750>

^^^ I skipped the flu shot this year too. We will see.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-10-23T02:55:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144777>

I went to a funeral today for a partner who died in a wreck............had people sitting around me during the service listening..........as I was walking out several asked me about health.......it turns out my partner who died read Tensegrity 7 on my laptop before I posted it and was talking to the nurse about it........apparently it made an impact. Now people who were deaf to my science are interested. It took sitting in a funeral to move them to action. Sometimes you can never predict what will do it.........they key is you must not stop producing the content of your knowledge like you're on a conference call...........you never know who is hearing vs listening.

### nicld — 2014-10-23T05:04:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/family-members.4813/post-144797>

^^^^^ My condolences.

Isn't this bizarre? Who'd a thunk it?

from the link above:

Men in committed relationships choose to keep a greater distance between themselves and an unknown woman they find attractive when given the hormone oxytocin, according to new research in the November 14 issue of The Journal of Neuroscience. The findings suggest oxytocin may help promote fidelity within monogamous relationships.

90% of men have a penis under 7 inches,huh? LOL....for some reason I am always choosing those in the 10% range or else that stat is not correct. I thought 6-7 inches was a norm in my personal experience.

These were mouse studies in the article. According to the article: It's been established that vasopressin is released into the bloodstream during sexual arousal in men—but whether that corresponds with higher uptake in the brain is unclear. Although receptors for both vasopressin and oxytocin are found in the human brain, the distribution does not resemble that seen in either the monogamous prairie vole or the promiscuous montane vole.

Penis size is not related to cheating behavior. Like Dr. K, I know someone who had a teeny penis and he was a huge horn dog and had an open marriage. He slept around constantly. He was married, loved his wife, but they had an agreement they could both have sex with others outside the marriage and they did. So this isn't really cheating behavior but it is not typical pair bonding.

vasopressin is a powerful drug with intense and dangerous side effects. I am used to administering it in an ICU setting by IV, but it can be given also subcutaneously, intramuscularly, and I think by inhaling. I don't think it will ever be ok'd for at home use. [http://www.drugs.com/mtm/vasopressin.html](http://http://www.drugs.com/mtm/vasopressin.html)

I enjoyed this book.

[Sex at Dawn: How we Mate, Why we stray and what it means.](http://http://www.amazon.com/Sex-Dawn-Stray-Modern-Relationships/dp/0061707813) Sex paleo style. "For the husband and wife team Christopher Ryan and Cacilda JethÃ¡ in their new book Sex At Dawn, this example is one of many that suggests the human species did not evolve in monogamous, nuclear families but rather in small, intimate groups where "most mature individuals would have had several ongoing sexual relationships at any given time." We are the descendants of these multimale-multifemale mating groups and, even though we've constructed a radically different society from our hunter-gatherer forebears, the behavioral and psychological traits our species evolved in the distant past still manifest themselves today. Ryan, a psychologist, and JethÃ¡, a psychiatrist, argue that understanding human sexual evolution this way helps to explain our species' unique creativity inside (as well as outside) the marriage bed. It may also shed light on why fidelity has been such a persistent problem for both men and women throughout recorded history."

>
>
>
> In fact, researchers claim that monogamy may not be genetically inherent to humans as a species. Even man's closest primate relative, the bonobo chimp, is sexually active without distinction for partners or pair bonding. The female bonobo monkey chooses a mate, drags the male out into the jungle and has hot monkey sex with him for about two weeks before bringing him back into the pack, spent and exhausted. Then, she chooses another male and repeats her process. And she is our closest primate relative. (Females of our species sometimes do the same!)

### John Dalton — 2015-04-16T11:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oxytocin-keeps-men-in-line.4835/post-161393>

> [Thor said:](/goto/post?id=64154)
>
> ...
>
> It's Vasopressin in men not oxytocin.... Somebody isn't aware of how the body works. oppps
>
> Vasopressin is not the same thing as oxytocin although they may have effects in common.
>
> Vasopressin is also known as "anti-diuretic hormone". It doesn't make you pee, it stops you peeing. It is not affected by caffeine but it's production is suppressed by alcohol.

I dreamed about Jack. It was a very real dream, like thouse crazy real dreams one has once in a while.

"He was the teacher and I was sitting in the classroom. He gave everyone a 500 â‚¬ bank note. I was thinking.. wow, what a generous man.. I was filled with awe. Then.. everyone went to the drugstore together, to change this 500 â‚¬ note to a huge brick of butter. It was really huge, it must have weighed about 5 kg or something. It was like a gold bar but it was butter. I needed to wait on the counter for my butter because the pharmacist needed to ghange my note to the butter and then she got so impressed about the huge butter brick that she wanted her colleague to picture her with a camera holding the butter... I got so impatient waiting because I thought it took forever, so that I woke up..."

Another dream from lately.

"I dreamed about kissing someone. It was nice.. then we stopped moving our lips and tongue like you do when kissig and just hold our lips together.. for long.. and then it gradually increased such a strong electric feeling it was amazing.. it got so strong that I woke up! Huh!"

I like it..

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-14T14:43:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dreamed-about-jack.4843/post-64229>

When you dream it tells us your brain is actively making and pruning new neural connections so once again Inger intuition is correct. When you do this consistently change is right around your corner. When we dream we don't talk change, we begin to live it........it helps mandate change exists in your current life. I have learned to embrace change because nothing diminishes anxiety faster than action. I came to realize each person’s task in life is to improve our former selves. Not all of us get this text message in time. All of our dreams can come true if we have the courage to just pursue them at some level. Our life does not get better by chance; it gets better by accepting change.

### Michael — 2012-11-15T16:20:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dreamed-about-jack.4843/post-64473>

> **Inger;66467 wrote:** @ Michael... I am now thinking I got it all wrong with the trolls.. lol. You see, in my imagination they are not evil at all. But I like to see the beauty in everything so I got to wonder if I might have made my own trolls in my mind that has nothing to do with the real ones.. at least in that way that they are evil? hmm..

I don't know. I'm not a folklorist (and not a lot of other things). I don't think they were supposed to be seen as harmless, but they may have morphed over time. Look what happend to fairies! If you're at all interested in literary history and you ever see a copy of C. S. Lewis's _The Discarded Image have a look at the range of beings medieval people believed in and how they saw them. Fairies could definitely be dodgy and tended to be morally "neutral" at best. They could bewitch you and carry you off, make you forget who you were. I guess Keats's Belle dame sans merci_ is the last hint of that - the main character in the poem is sexually captivated by the dame, who is obviously a fairy. Not like the tiny sentimental gossamer-winged Victorian fairies - like effete butterflies sitting on flowers - at all. Powerful and ambiguous beings.

There's still an expression in Ireland "he's away with the fairies" giving some hint of the former conception.

EDIT

Elves were believed to wound people. The Anglo-Saxons, finding microliths - tiny flint bladelets - from the Mesolithic in the fields, and not knowing what they were, had it that they were elvish weapons. If someone, or some farm animal, got ill and they didn't know why, they said maybe he had been hit with "elf shot".

> **Inger;66171 wrote:**
>
> "I dreamed about kissing someone. ...
>
>
> Found this. Setting by Henry Purcell of this text:
>
>
>
> Sweeter than roses, or cool evening breeze
>
> On a warm flowery shore, was the dear kiss,
>
> First trembling made me freeze,
>
> Then shot like fire all o'er.

> For all I touch or see since that dear kiss,
>
> I hourly prove, all is love to me.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-16T01:04:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dreamed-about-jack.4843/post-64595>

> **Cavemam;66559 wrote:** She sings technically. Vibrato too contrived for my taste. Her runs seem lifeless and forte is breathy.

Ah Cavemam, you're a singer? Yes I can see that this singer is more technical and there could certainly be more fluidity overall. Perhaps a little more support from the diaphragm and more relaxed throat would produce that extra umph. I think she's pretty decent though and for sure I've heard WAY worse. It was still pleasant to listen to.

Btw, this thread was about dreams. If I have a 'bad dream' I now am afraid for my immediate health. I have noticed in the last year that any time I had a bad dream it was only followed by an acute illness or just general badness all around. I include that as one of my health markers in addition to any other more typical parameters for good health.

So, whenever I have a good dream or even just a neutral dream and I can recall it well, then I can expect only positive things. If I don't dream and/or don't recall them, then it's more 'neutral' but of course I stay vigilant just to make sure things are going in an upward trend.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-16T01:08:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dreamed-about-jack.4843/post-64597>

Haha...so if I look to last November (before I knew of this website lol)...the rate of actual nightmares was pretty much every night or every few nights. The following winter was ....not that good to say the least. The actual month of November was horrendous and I do not want to experience that EVER again!

Anyways, at least in January I found this site and from then only positive things have been happening. (Well I mean overall not to say there weren't any bumps or roadblocks). I'm very glad for this site for more than just the physical health aspects I've gained, but for my mental and emotional well being- that is priceless.

If you feel a blog in your fingers do not think just let the fingers flow. Get the mind out of the way and let everything be zen. When you paint let the fingers feel the brush to get the picture out. The ink will touch the brush and know what the fingers want to accomplish. When your in your natural state you just automatically get outof your own way. Meditation brings you to places you can get out of your own way easily. Some of my best artwork and blogs are written when i feel the words in my fingers.

# Thread: New Nordic Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-14T21:36:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64314>

This movement looks quite interesting - seems to have been around for a while but I only just heard of it today.

> Move over cavemen and Mediterraneans, Vikings are the new diet gurus.
>
>
>
> Claus Meyer, co-founder of the Danish restaurant Noma, twice named the best in the world, has been collaborating with nutrition experts to combat the obesity epidemic. ...
>
>
> [http://shine.yahoo.com/healthy-living/the-new-nordic-diet-the-next-big-weight-loss-trend-2607925.html](http://http://shine.yahoo.com/healthy-living/the-new-nordic-diet-the-next-big-weight-loss-trend-2607925.html)
>
>
>
> The cuisine seems to be based on plenty of fish, shellfish, free-range meat, nuts, vegetables and fruit, and seaweed. Sounds good to me.
>
>
>
> There's a hint of "eat up your bread" and of low fat about it, but this is light years ahead of the dreadful diet the BBC was pushing at its audience that I linked this morning.
>
>
>
> Unlike the BBC, Claus Meyer does take a swipe at:
>
>
>
>
> > processed starches in our pantry and produce shipped from across the globe
> >
> >
> > says that everything should be home-cooked and says to eat the food grown in your region.
> >
> >
> >
> > The BBC, readers of this board will recall was pushing non-home-cooked made from processed ingredients to people silly enough to take its advice:
> >
> >
> >
> > **Soya protein is also an excellent substitute for meat and is available in a convenient and tasty form **in** many ready-made meals**.
>
>
>
> And people do seem to be getting weight-loss results with the Nordic Diet - not surprising I guess as it's real food.
>
>
>
> Add in some more fat and take the cereal grains out and it would pretty much be Epipaleo.
>
>
>
> Looking at the guy's site, the bread he's making himself is from [heirloom varieties of grain, mostly rye](http://http://www.clausmeyer.dk/en/grupe___meyer_flour.html), unbleached and unadulterated four, and baked properly. I think this stuff is probably better out the diet, but if you're going to have it in at all, _this_ is the way to do it.
>
>
>
> While on the surface this diet is closer to the mainstream that what we do, look closer and it's a long, long way from the toxic industrlalized diet people like the BBC are pushing.
>
>
>
> I love the chef's manifesto, too:
>
>
>
> 1\. To express the purity, freshness, simplicity and ethics we wish to associate with our region.
>
>
>
> 2\. To reflect the changing of the seasons in the meals we make.
>
>
>
> 3\. To base our cooking on ingredients and produce whose characteristics are particularly excellent in our climates, landscapes and waters.
>
>
>
> 4\. To combine the demand for good taste with modern knowledge of health and well-being.
>
>
>
> 5\. To promote Nordic products and the variety of Nordic producers – and to spread the word about their underlying cultures.
>
>
>
> 6\. To promote animal welfare and a sound production process in our seas, on our farmland and in the wild.
>
>
>
> 7\. To develop potentially new applications of traditional Nordic food products.
>
>
>
> 8\. To combine the best in Nordic cookery and culinary traditions with impulses from abroad.
>
>
>
> 9\. To combine local self-sufficiency with regional sharing of high-quality products.
>
>
>
> 10\. To join forces with consumer representatives, other cooking craftsmen, agriculture, the fishing, food , retail and wholesale industries, researchers, teachers, politicians and authorities on this project for the benefit and advantage of everyone in the Nordic countries.

[http://www.clausmeyer.dk/en/the\_new\_nordic\_cuisine\_/manifesto\_.html](http://http://www.clausmeyer.dk/en/the_new_nordic_cuisine_/manifesto_.html)

Seasonality, good taste, tradition, ethics, animal welfare, localism, social responsibility. This is what food should be about: not just fuelling up.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T21:48:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64318>

If bread were made like that, and eaten seasonally (autumn), and you were healthy to begin with, and not too much bread was eaten per day.... it would probably be just fine.

### caroline — 2012-11-14T22:38:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64327>

About 2 years ago I saw Rene Redzeppi at the Opera House here in Sydney. He was involved with Noma. He is an amazing young man and very humble - even tho his restaurant had just been named #1 in the world. It was the most fascinating and electric evening. He talked about all the stuff you mention. Later there was about a half dozen other people - food critics, chefs etc talking about all this. One Chef from Melbourne had brought various and assorted sea weeds/vegetables that he had picked at the beach that morning before coming to Sydney. I wish I could remember more of that evening - I just loved every second of it. They even placed some sea vegetables on everyone's seat! I often think of that evening when I am down at the beach and wish I could remember and identify the stuff they showed us. He is a magic young man. A friend of mine has been to Noma a couple of times. It really pushes the boundaries. For some reason I think Noma has closed - I could be wrong. I have his cook book - I am going to go and get it out. Thanks for reminding me of that wonderful evening.

### Michael — 2012-11-15T09:24:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64427>

That sounds like an amazing evening, Caroline.

I guess this is the book?

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Noma-Time-Place-Nordic-Cuisine/dp/0714859036/](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/Noma-Time-Place-Nordic-Cuisine/dp/0714859036/)

So he's the co-founder of Noma with the other bloke. I see as well as an emphasis on seasonality he's interested in foraging food from the wild.

Over here Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall (River Cottage) used to be big on that. Not just nettles and such with Hugh either. He once got death threats against his baby son after he was filmed climbing a tree to gather rooks from their nests.

I found Pubmed has some articles on the diet:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=new%20nordic%20diet](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=new%20nordic%20diet)

It's interesting to see from pubmed that they're trialling offering it in schools - shades of Jamie Oliver there. Pubmed was how I found out about the New Nordic Diet, actually. I happened to be looking for something to do with shellfish and up it popped.

I had found this on Amazon last night and ordered a copy:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Nordic-Diet-Organic-Promote-Lifestyle/dp/1616081899/](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/Nordic-Diet-Organic-Promote-Lifestyle/dp/1616081899/)

I'm not much of a cook, but I do enjoy trying sometimes. I like looking through cookbooks, too, specially if they've got good photographs.

### caroline — 2012-11-15T12:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64435>

It was an amazing evening Michael. That is the cookbook that I have. I have 45 boxes of cookbooks in the garage from when I moved. It doesn't seem to be in my apartment so I will look for it tomorrow. I had forgotten about it. It has so much information on scavenging for stuff. I just love seaweed now so I would like to be able to identify some of these things. Do you know the website ......Nom Nom paleo. ?

She has lots of good recipes and lists other paleo cookbooks. What do you like to eat/cook? Do you do MHS? That actually sounds kind of erotic - will blame Inger for that! Have your dreams improved/moved towards butter and banknotes and kisses?

### Michael — 2012-11-15T13:35:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64446>

45 boxes! That is impressive.

I heard the name of that site, but haven't seen it.

I used to like the Elizabeth David books, because they weren't just recipe books but brought a whole world to life as well - what it was like to be in that part of France (or wherever), how you shopped there, all the history and culture around it.

I don't recall dreaming anything particularly interesting last night. I'd have been hugely amused to have had the dream OptimalBound had - Jack sitting in a floodlit cave at midnight, eating a banana and laughing. That's hilarious!

### caroline — 2012-11-15T13:49:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-nordic-diet.4849/post-64451>

Have you seen any books by Patricia Wells? She was a food writer in Paris. Great insight into Paris and a lot of fun stuff about markets. You are lucky to live so close - do you travel often? I always brought local cook books home when we travelled (hence why I have so many) you get so much local color/history/insight into people's lives from their cooking. I have spent a great deal of time over the years reading cookbooks. Great memories actually.

# Thread: Bright light at night may trigger depression
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bright-light-at-night-may-trigger-depression.4856/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-15T01:44:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bright-light-at-night-may-trigger-depression.4856/post-64365>

Chronic exposure to bright light at night elevates levels of a stress hormone which results in depression and lowers cognitive function.

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2232957/Is-laptop-making-depressed-Bright-screens-night-trigger-condition.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2232957/Is-laptop-making-depressed-Bright-screens-night-trigger-condition.html)

# Thread: Research Shows Carbs Fuel Brain Diseases  Fat Helps Heal Brain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-15T02:13:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/post-64370>

.

Yet another reason not to eat carbs - research shows carbs cause brain disease:

**Carbs & Sugars Raise Risk of Alzheimerâ€™s Disease**

Nov. 14, 2012

[https://www.worldhealth.net/news/carbs-sugars-raise-risk-alzheimers-disease/](http://https://www.worldhealth.net/news/carbs-sugars-raise-risk-alzheimers-disease/)

Previously, studies suggest that high caloric intake associates with an increased risk of cognitive impairment. Rosebud O. Roberts, from Mayo Clinic (Minnesota, USA), and colleagues analyzed data collected on 1,230 men and women, ages 70 to 89 years, who were surveyed on their dietary patterns of the previous year. At that time, their cognitive function was evaluated: only the roughly 940 who showed no signs of cognitive impairment were asked to return for follow-up evaluations of their cognitive function. About four years into the study, 200 of those 940 were beginning to show mild cognitive impairment, problems with memory, language, thinking and judgment that are greater than normal age-related changes. The subjects who reported the highest carbohydrate intake at the beginning of the study were 1.9 times likelier to develop mild cognitive impairment, as compared to those with the lowest intake of carbohydrates. Participants with the highest sugar intake were 1.5 times likelier to experience mild cognitive impairment , as compared those with the lowest levels.

The team also determined that the subjects whose diets were highest in fat -- compared to the lowest -- were 42% less likely to face cognitive impairment, and those who had the highest intake of protein had a reduced risk of 21%. When total fat and protein intake were taken into account, people with the highest carbohydrate intake were 3.6 times likelier to develop mild cognitive impairment. The study authors urge that: â€œA dietary pattern with relatively high caloric intake from carbohydrates and low caloric intake from fat and proteins may increase the risk of \[mild cognitive impairment\] or dementia in elderly persons.â€￾

Go Fat  woooo another win.....

### nonchalant — 2012-11-15T03:20:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/post-64393>

> **Thor;66366 wrote:** The subjects who reported the highest carbohydrate intake at the beginning of the study were 1.9 times likelier to develop mild cognitive impairment, as compared to those with the lowest intake of carbohydrates. Participants with the highest sugar intake were 1.5 times likelier to experience mild cognitive impairment , as compared those with the lowest levels.

Worse than sugar?!

It would be good to remember that there are no essential carbs.

### chocolate — 2012-11-23T15:35:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/post-65454>

http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/research-shows-diabetes-drug-improves-memory-15120
Quote:The molecule, called extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK), becomes hyperactive both in the brains of Alzheimerâ€™s patients and in the mice at a disease stage corresponding to mild cognitive impairment in human Alzheimerâ€™s. This excessive activity leads to improper synaptic transmission between neurons, interfering with learning and memory.

Rosiglitazone brings ERK back into line by activating whatâ€™s known as the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (PPARÎ³) pathway, which interacts with genes that respond to both PPARÎ³ and ERK.

â€œUsing this drug appears to restore the neuronal signaling required for proper cognitive function,â€ said UTMB professor Larry Denner, the lead author of a paper describing this work now online in the Journal of Neuroscience. â€œIt gives us an opportunity to test several FDA-approved drugs to normalize insulin resistance in Alzheimerâ€™s patients and possibly also enhance memory, and it also gives us a remarkable tool to use in animal models to understand the molecular mechanisms that underlie cognitive issues in Alzheimerâ€™s.â€

ERK dysfunction in the Alzheimerâ€™s mouse model was discovered several years ago by UTMB associate professor Kelly Dineley, senior author of the Journal of Neuroscience paper. But putting together the protein, gene and memory pieces of the puzzle required a multidisciplinary translational research team including animal cognitive neuroscientists, biochemists, molecular biologists, mass spectrometrists, statisticians and bioinformaticists.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rosiglitazone
PPARs are expressed in fat cells, cells of the liver, muscle, heart, and inner wall (endothelium) and smooth muscle of blood vessels. PPARÎ³ is expressed mainly in fat tissue, where it regulates genes involved in fat cell (adipocyte) differentiation, fatty acid uptake and storage, and glucose uptake. It is also found in pancreatic beta cells, vascular endothelium, and macrophages.\[9\] Rosiglitazone is a selective ligand of PPARÎ³ and has no PPARÎ±-binding action. Other drugs bind to PPARÎ±.

Rosiglitazone also appears to have an anti-inflammatory effect in addition to its effect on insulin resistance. Nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-ÎºB), a signaling molecule, stimulates the inflammatory pathways. NF-ÎºB inhibitor (IÎºB) downregulates the inflammatory pathways. When patients take rosiglitazone, NF-ÎºB levels fall and IÎºB levels increase.\[10\]

Rosiglitazone may also benefit patients with Alzheimer's disease who do not express the ApoE4 allele.\[11\] This is the subject of a clinical trial currently underway.\[12\]

Rosiglitazone may also treat mild to moderate ulcerative colitis, due to its anti-inflammatory properties as a PPAR ligand.\[13\]

A clinical trial has suggested these agents may be of use in treating malaria.\[14\]

â€œWe were extraordinarily lucky to have this diverse group of experts right here on our campus at UTMB that could coalesce to bring such different ways of thinking to bear on a common problem,â€ Denner said. â€œIt was quite a challenge to get all of these experts communicating in a common scientific language. But now that we have this team working, we can move on to even more detailed and difficult questions.â€

this article seems to fit here. the drug uses fat cell stuff

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-23T15:57:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/research-shows-carbs-fuel-brain-diseases-fat-helps-heal-brain.4858/post-65457>

Chocolate re read the opening paragraph from Brain Gut 5:

GEEKS: The implications of Brain Gut 4 mean that to develop a brain seafood is required part of the program to maintain it. Nutrients and many other environmental factors have also been found to influence epigenetic programing of our DNA either directly or indirectly via metabolic sensors. Peroxisomal proliferator-activated receptors (PPARâ€™s), the vitamin D receptors, and the retinoid X receptors (RXR), and the retinoic acid receptors (RAR) are all examples of the nuclear receptors that interact with the brain cell membranes to control inflammation and metabolism all over our bodies. It turns out that PPARâ€™s are the receptors that are at the crossroads of where inflammation and metabolism actually cross. These are specialized lipid sensors that pay attention to our balance of omega 6 and 3 levels. 3% of the human genome is directly or indirectly controlled by the endocrine functions of the Vitamin D system. This shows you how environmental signals are intertwined into the expression of DNA in humans. The Epi-paleo Rx controls inflammation at a hormonal level in the brain better than the more famous paleo diet.

NON GEEKS: What we eat is really damn important when you have 3 lbs of lipid in your head called a brain. If you eat outside our design you get sick.
