# Thread: The Love of your life........say something about it/them
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 81
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T05:00:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64404>

You know you're found amazing love when you can't fall asleep next to her, because your own reality is finally better than anything ever found in your dreams before. And in the memories of those dreams, you recollect desires just to be the air that enters her quietly, while it sustains her for just that moment, but it becomes the air that fuels you. In that wonder, your goal is to be unnoticed by her, but simultaneously necessary to sustain her. In turn, she exceeds your imagination, your forgetting, your opiate, your complete oblivion..........

Make love your remedy.

### caroline — 2012-11-15T05:47:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64412>

Dr. K - you are a very romantic man - and gentleman... ...so beautifully said

Love to me ......the song -" You Lift me up" I cried the first time I heard it and I have cried every time since. For me it says it all. I cry just thinking about the words.

Is Sandy going to participate in this thread? She could be annonymous

### Inger — 2012-11-15T12:58:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64436>

Jack, that was so beautiful what you wrote. Very beautiful. This is love.. amazing love..

He was the sun and moon and the stars.. all in one...

We were intimate.. and I closed my eyes to fully enjoy the amazingness feeling him inside me.. as I opened my eyes again he looked straight into my eyes.. He's eyes and how he looked at me got forever burned into my mind. It is almost 5 years ago now.. but I can recall it anytime as alive as yesterday. These eyes and what they express are what I often see in front of me releasing oxytocin alone. It needs nothing else... the most beautiful love from a spirit so tender and full of beauty.. but at the same time so strong, the most diciplined human beeing I ever met. He had and was everything I was not.. and he still looked at me in a way that goes so much deeper then adoring...

The beautiful memories he gave me the whole year I got to see him almost every week, have carried me through the hardest times since, they keep me sane and raise me up when I think I have lost it

### ealachan — 2012-11-15T16:34:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64479>

My husband gave up everything he'd known for 35 years, left all his family and friends, packed up all his stuff and moved 3500 miles to a completely different country to be with me. It might not sound all flowery and poetic and metaphysical and lah-de-dah, but that's some real-deal love right there. It still amazes me that he was willing to do that. It completely wrecks my head that he thought I, of all people on this Earth, was worth that.

### Inger — 2012-11-15T16:44:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64482>

@ Caroline,

I love this song too...

@ Elachan, that is amazing... wow. You are one blessed and happy woman for sure.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T17:18:40+0200

### nonchalant — 2012-11-15T17:37:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64497>

My husband and I are empty-nesters. Once the hustle and bustle and conflict over how to raise the kids was all over between us, we were left in a sort of no-man's land. We were going on inertia. But then we created a goal for our lives, and we have both thrown quite a bit of effort and thought and creativity into that dream. We have enjoyed making plans and decisions, and working to realize those plans. Our love and trust has bloomed anew in the process, and we both feel like teenagers again, in our relationship. Actually, it's better than when we were teenagers. We have decades of experience with each other now, and we appreciate each other more, know each other better, and we have confidence in each other.

The only concern that I had, in our dream, was the possibility (probability) that illness would bring an early end to it. We would have to move close to a hospital somewhere, and we would be apart from each other for long periods of time. We would still have each other, but we wouldn't be living our dream. That concern has vanished, thanks to the good doctor. I feel like I know, now, how to live in a manner that is healthy for our bodies. And yes, it meshes beautifully with our goal.

Dr. K, you have made our dream a long-term possibility.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T17:41:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64499>

I am no dream maker for anyone else........I am only a dream innovator for others........I show you bits and pieces of what makes us......us and allows you to see you differently in a new reality.......then you dream and create it.

### Michael — 2012-11-15T17:46:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64501>

We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep.

I grew up under the impression that I wasn't worth anybody's time. It's a hard habit to break, but little by little, I'm getting there. The older I get, the less of a crap I seem to give about what people think of me, which is really helping. By the time I'm 60, I should have the world by the ass.

### ColdBren — 2012-11-15T18:55:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64514>

### Meg — 2012-11-15T20:26:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64543>

There are so many threads that I feel inept to respond to but not this one. Been married to the same guy for twenty five years and have four kids.

My husband has and does give me many things but one of his greatest gifts is the gift of words. I was raised in a home were there was not much communication. You had to guess whether you were "pleasing" mom & dad and constantly take the temperature in the air to know how they were feeling. Kind of stressful, cause I was a pleaser. With my husband there are NO guessing games. What you see is what you get and you always know what he's thinking and where things stand. Can you say FREEDOM! Now, with all that talking, (because being inside a man's head all the time can be a bit overwhleming) comes constant affirmation that he is devoted to me and the kids and is not going anywhere, ever.

The combination of these two things gave me life! And the freedom to explore places inside of me that I never would have been able to otherwise. I do give him alot as well. I put up with his insanity because he is one of a kind. We both have sacrificed alot for each other and for the family but it has been worth it. Like good health, good relationships take alot of work and usually require making decisions that go against the grain to many. But, as someone we all know says (well kinda) when you know better, you choose better!

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-15T21:21:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64549>

I am so envious of all of you! I wish I had someone in my life and had such mutual affection!!

there was one guy that I dated after my divorce.....he was the love of my life, but he decided after a year and a half that marriage was not in the immediate future, and he wanted to pursue some personal goals. We broke up six years ago and I have never felt that way about anyone else, and frankly, I'm afraid that I never willl.......I guess I'm gonna be one of those old ladies with a bunch of cats!!! hahahah!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T21:52:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64558>

I do not want this to be taken wrong.....my intent is to help: Maybe it is not them.......maybe it is the person in the mirror. Why do I point this out? I've been diviorced and I can say that I first had to begin to like then love me.......before I could ever find the love of my life. I firmly believe if I met my wife before I did I would have ruined it because I was a shadow of what I later became. I began sculpting me.........and when I chipped off all the crap........then I had a chance to love. My wife became the exercise to further fix that neural network........and yes I believe my inability to love was caused by the things in my environment both that I could not and could control........when I realized it I changed them and in turn changed me........

my own experience is the same. It wasn't until I decided I was worth my own effort did I meet my husband. He is my heart, my soul, my bestfriend and lover. Every day he makes me a better person. Even when we're have a rough time of things - he's focused on making us better as a family. there are times he disappoints me and it does break my heart. but since beginning the journey to optimal - I now can let him know this w/o anger or resentment - just honest open feelings from which we can continue to build and shape our journey in life together. He has told me I do the same for me, a he does for me. I couldn't ask for a better life partner.

### BJK77 — 2012-11-15T22:06:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64563>

Love . . . one of my favorite topics. I’m blessed to be married to my high school sweetheart (even though I broke his heart a few times in high school). We had so many people tell us it would never last, that it was just puppy love. We’ve now been married for 14 years and it’s truly been amazing. Y’know how when you first get married everyone kind of warns you that there will be trials and tribulations and you’ll fight over money or whatever?!?! We’ve never had that.

From the moment we said “I do,” it has just felt absolutely right. There was never a doubt in my mind that this was the man for me. I’ve never taken compliments well, always been harder on myself than anyone else would ever be and really can just be a PITA! That has never once stopped my husband from telling me every day that I’m beautiful and he loves me and he feels so lucky to have me in his life. To this day, I still make a joke out of his compliments. I don’t know why, but I struggle to accept the gifts he gives so generously. No matter, he still gives and gives . . . .

I’m here on this forum because of him and my amazing kids. Of course I want to be better for me, but more importantly I want it for them. They’ve all given me so much and I want to return it all tenfold. When I was at my sickest and could barely walk up and down the stairs due to exhaustion and pain, my husband would have carried me without a second thought. My pride kept this from happening, but he was an unbelievably solid rock that I always knew I could count on. He never questioned me, never thought I was just being lazy or was a hypochondriac. He always believed in me, always supported and encouraged me and always, always loved me. When I got upset over spending so much $$ on myself for labs and doctors, he’d tell me over and over again the money meant nothing if he didn’t have me. When I couldn’t go outside and play with the kids because I literally felt like I was dying, he’d come up with activities we could do inside – even lying in bed if that’s all I could do.

I could go on and on. I am blessed beyond belief.

I began sculpting me.........and when I chipped off all the crap........then I had a chance to love. My wife became the exercise to further fix that neural network........and yes I believe my inability to love was caused by the things in my environment both that I could not and could control........when I realized it I changed them and in turn changed me........

I had a similar experience. I actually "met" my husband online in 2001. We talked via email and phone for 5 years before we ever met in person. I changed a LOT as a person in those five years, as did he. I honestly believe that if we had met each other in person in 2001 instead of getting to know each other and learning from each other for those five years, we wouldn't be together now. He said something to me that I specifically remember stopping me in my tracks and really making me look around at how I was living my life at the time. He said "You make me want to be a better person". At the time, I remember my self-absorbed drunk-ass 22 year old self thinking "well what the hell does THAT mean?" but the more I looked around at my life and the people in it, the more I thought about it...the more I realized that HE made ME want to be a better person, too. I started to make those changes slowly, one foot in front of the other, and I have him to thank for that.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T23:31:25+0200

Oxytocin connects us to other people; oxytocin makes us feel what other people feel. Oxytocin connects our brain back to the giant pharmacy in our own brain. Moreover, it’s easy to cause people’s brains to release oxytocin. Let me show you. Go hug somebody.......

### caroline — 2012-11-16T04:14:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64633>

> **Inger;66479 wrote:** @ Caroline,
>
> I love this song too...
>
>
>
> @ Elachan, that is amazing... wow. You are one blessed and happy woman for sure.

That song is so important to me and I can't believe I wrote the title down wrong - the song is - You raise me up. The first time I heard it - Josh Groban sang it and I still remember it so vividly.

The words - you raise me up to more than I can be. And - I am strong when I am on your shoulders - in fact all the words in that song are how I feel about my husband. I loved that man with all my heart and soul and it will never change. He will be in my heart forever.

### caroline — 2012-11-16T04:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64637>

> **ealachan;66503 wrote:** I grew up under the impression that I wasn't worth anybody's time. It's a hard habit to break, but little by little, I'm getting there. The older I get, the less of a crap I seem to give about what people think of me, which is really helping. By the time I'm 60, I should have the world by the ass.

I grew up that way too and yes, because of my husbands overwhelming love and devotion to me - I take less crap these days too etc. but it is pretty hard to ignore those growing up years when we should have had love and didn't. But a husband's/wife's endless love gives us such confidence to be all we can be.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-16T05:48:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-64656>

This afternoon at the grocery I bought 2 Kevita's. One for me, one for my husband. I opened mine to have before my dinner. Hubby thought we were sharing..... he started reaching for it.... and I told him that his was in the fridge. He looked hurt. I wanted to take it back. Even if I drank all mine and didn't share I know that when he opened his he would let me drink as much as I want. I told him that, and that I knew that. I could tell he was mustering all his manliness not to shed a tear....

It sounds like just a thing about a drink but it was a bigger deal than that.

I love hearing everyone's love stories.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T02:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65059>

...mirror neurons allow us to learn by mimicry, experiencing the state of another person, to feel what they are feeling.

when you are bonded with someone,,, when they experience pleasure, you feel their pleasure as your own.

They in turn, feel what you are feeling, creating a feed back loop of ever increasing intensity.

like an optical cavity in a laser gain medium being pumped to produce a population inversion, until...

spontaneous emission, superluminescent light, a crescendo of coherent photons spring forth, released forever from the bonds of their metastable orbits...

_erotic poetry for geeks._

### diane — 2012-11-18T04:04:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65081>

After our first date, I described it as the Seinfeld episode where Jerry meets Janine Garafalo and they were so much alike - "it was like I was dating myself!". There was something about him, and I definitely wanted to know more. When I was leaving, we found out we had the same birthday, which explained some of the similarities. When we were talking, it was like I was looking in the mirror - same sense of humour, same hand movements.

That was 2004, and since then, there are still a lot of ways we're similar, and a lot of ways we're different. I appreciate the differences as much as the similarities. He often balances me out. And drives me crazy - which tells me that the spark is still there, because when they don't drive you crazy, it's usually gone.

The last few months he has had a job where he's gone during the week and home on the weekend. It's been an adjustment, and I really miss him. But then sometimes am worried that I'm getting used to being on my own - and like that I can keep the house cold and dark!  But when he's home it feels right that we're together.

I've been working a lot on myself lately - not just physically but mentally. I know I've changed a lot in the last 6 months, and my perspective on things is much healthier. And I know that he's going through some things lately, so I'm trying to figure out the best way for me to be supportive so he can get to where he needs to.

He has introduced me to so many things that I didn't know before him. He lets me be who I need to be, and do the things I need to do. I can be an individual, and be a couple at the same time. But a little too much individual lately, I think. We're definitely not the sappy type - he makes me laugh, he makes me hot. And that's what's important to me.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-18T04:22:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65085>

I didn't even know I was looking for the love of my life until I found him. I'd been in a bad, dead-end relationship that I had recently ended and started therapy to deal with it (but I managed to dump the bad guy before therapy - good on me!). My DH and I met when we were both 47, never married, no kids. He had left his job and life in California to move here to find somebody. A mutual friend suggested we meet and within a few weeks, I knew I had met my match. We are compatible in so many ways and share the same values. I had basically given up on meeting someone and the idea of dating was just plain frightening. It's just as well we met when we did; neither of us was really mature enough or ready when we were younger. I like to think it was fate. There were so many little things that had to happen in my life just at the right time and place to put me where I was to meet him. Good things do come to those who wait.

### caroline — 2012-11-18T05:09:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-65099>

I don't think we have talked about this.....Does anyone believe in love at first sight?

I can start......I emphatically do - well at second site

The first time - I wasn't paying any attention at all - other things going on \[hockey game/Canada - what can I say\] .......BUT

The second time - I saw him across the street \[He came to find me} and I will never forget that feeling - it was overwhelming. I have forgotten a lot things - but not that!

It's the little things he does. Â He made me cry today because of this:

It is engraved with both our names and our anniversary date. Â He knows I have a soft spot for Disney's Cinderella. Â Short story ... when I was little I had a Cinderella watch with a pink grosgrain band that came in a plastic "glass slipper." Â I also had a windup toy of Cinderella and the Prince in dance pose ... you wound it up and it played the music from the movie as they spun around the floor. Â Sure wish I still had them.

Even though I ceased believing in "happily ever after" I still get silly when I see "Cinderella" at DisneyWorld. Â so over the years he has given me 4 (now) Cinderella snow globes and a mug. Â He spoils me silly. Â And I never feel like I really deserve it but I'm working on that.

Inger

I totally agree and love what you sayâ€“- a healthier brain allows us to listen better, relate more, open up wider without the need for drama or conflict.Â  Itâ€™s only recently that I understand the wisdom of your words.Â  Â Â 

But please tell me what you make of this.Â  For the last five years I did a lot of internal spiritual and personal growth work; I mean a lot. After my last drama-filled relationship in 2006, I made a commitment that I would never do things the â€œold wayâ€ again.Â  If that meant I had to be â€œaloneâ€ so be it.Â  I decided I would be alone for however long it took to learn a â€œnew wayâ€ of being in relationship. Â For part of this time, I dated.Â  I met scores of men.Â  Many were interested in knowing me better, yet no one seemed quite right, and I would move on quickly.Â  Looking back, I realize I had some pretty severe un-diagnosed adrenal fatigue that I didn'tÂ identify until I discovered Dr. Kruse.Â 

So the connection Iâ€™m making is that no matter how well or how much work I was doing psychologically or spiritually, I needed to heal my brain first and let love flow from there.Â  Iâ€™m not saying I was standing still waiting for Dr. K., just that all the internal work I had done didnâ€™t really begin to sprout until I began to heal my brain.

After my last drama-filled relationship in 2006, I made a commitment that I would never do things the â€œold wayâ€ again.Â  If that meant I had to be â€œaloneâ€ so be it.Â  I decided I would be alone for however long it took to learn a â€œnew wayâ€ of being in relationship. Â For part of this time, I dated.Â  I met scores of men.Â  Many were interested in knowing me better, yet no one seemed quite right, and I would move on quickly.Â  Looking back, I realize I had some pretty severe un-diagnosed adrenal fatigue that I didn'tÂ identify until I discovered Dr. Kruse.Â 
>
>
>
> So the connection Iâ€™m making is that no matter how well or how much work I was doing psychologically or spiritually, I needed to heal my brain first and let love flow from there.Â  Iâ€™m not saying I was standing still waiting for Dr. K., just that all the internal work I had done didnâ€™t really begin to sprout until I began to heal my brain.

Â Curves64, what I make of that? It fits with my observation... I can share a experience I had this Christmas that tells something about how weird things get when the brain is far from optimal. I got a Christmas message from one of my sisters. She told me she want to forgive me all bad stuff I had done to her (she listed it up - stuff I did not even remember about to be honest and happened about 13 years ago) and that had made her suffer for all these years and she wanted to go into the new year with forgiveness and freedom.Â 

Now, in my view forgiveness does not work that way. If I forgive someone something I just forget about it and does not let it rule me anymore, no bitterness, no suffering. To go and tell people what bad stuff they have done to me feels totally wrong. If I had done something bad, I go to the person and tell what it was and apologise. The bad things I have done, not what the other has done.. it is just a way to add guilt to other and has nothing to do with forgiveness IMHO. Often it might be just misunderstandings. And in my view, if a person is not aware of what it have done and ment it good but it turned out wrong because of unpredictable happenings it is not even guilty.. IDK I prefer to forget bad stuff others do to me because I just go forward it makes me feel much happier.

The message from my sister was a weird Christmas gift. She has a lot of bitterness inside her and I can see on her face and body comp that her hormones are so messed up. She tries to do healthy stuff but she does it a bit wrong... so the results are not too good.. and it is very easy to see in her actions and how she tackles conflicts. It is quite sad actually. I need to add, my sister is very spiritual and does lots of spiritual work.. reads spiritual books and stuff.. I sometimes wonder, heck- how can it turn into something opposite? But I am sure it is the brain...

Another example. My menatlly ill brother is home for Christmas. My parents whitnessed how he got so much better last summer as he lived here and ate a epipaleo diet. They thought it was amazing. He is bad off now after quitting the epi paleo, now eating a vegetarian diet and living in a menatl hospital. Now.. they put factory made Christmas cakes in front of him.. bread.. sweets.. even if the know it does him bad. They want him to eat healthy, yes, but hey see absolutely no responsibility in their own deeds.. they cannot get the connection! That every action I do affects my surroundings too... it is so weird. I need to exersice hard mentally to not let the crazyness in on me. I still have issues with staying calm. It is hard to look at. But I am thinking it is something I need to learn too and that I can gain something valuable from the whole thing. If it is only to do not care what they think if I just stay up in my own appartement and do not participate in their feast. Because I need to be with me a lot to manage that all.

I am still very much at the beginning of my own healing and I need to take care of me first, or I will soon loose power to do the good stuff myself!

So what I wanted to say is, if the brain is not working well stuff turn up down somehow. Yeah.. and in my personal life it is stuff like guilt. Instead of facing issues and changing things, one gets possessed with guilt... freezing guilt that makes one unable to change anything! Because one is not able to see it as it is. The issue. To face life with courage and solve the issue. Because there always are a solution..!

Â I would say.. plenty!

### Charlotte — 2012-12-26T07:13:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-68418>

Hi Inger, what you wrote resonated in me. So I want to give you my thoughts on this.

Obviously your sister has been holding on to some old stuff. But, what I think and what I find quite useful is, that she reached out to you by writing you this letter. Obviously, she did not know how to do it better, but to me it seems that she wants to enter into dialogue with you to settle these old matters, not really with you, but with herself really. So you can actually help her by entering into this dialogue and if it is only to help her to gain a little more insight into what is actually happening here. No need to apologize or feel guilty. We are who we are and we as humans make mistakes. All of us including your sister.

There is a beautiful story about a monk and his apprentice who one day were out on a journey. They came to a river which was full of water from recent rain and had a huge current. A woman was standing at the river bank, not daring to cross. The monk asked the woman if he could help and as she agreed he carried her across the river. The apprentice was shocked, as one of the rules of the monastary were to not touch any woman. On the other side, the monk dropped her and went his way with the apprentice. The apprentice could not let that go and chided the monk for touching the woman and so finally the monk said: "I left her there at the river bank, why are you still carrying her with you"?

So I think that you can actually help your sister by taking her up on her attempt to enter into dialogue with you and have just that. It is quite clear to me that you never wanted to hurt her and that if you did anyhow, let her know that that was never your intention and then bring the attention to the real issue which is something else and lies much deeper. Since I can only speculate about that, it is for you and your sister to find that out together. It could very well be that this dialogue brings on a new sister relationship beneficial to both of you. And we cannot ever have enough sisters and brothers.

And if you come from a place without guilt you will never feel the need to defend yourself, but concentrate on the real issues. In the end, all she wants from you is your unconditional love. Obviously she loves you very much or she would not have done that. So keep your focus on that, open your heart to her and BIG things will happen.

And most important to me in my life is always now to observe what any situation is doing to ME. Why am I reacting the way I do, what makes me resonate to somebody else's action and what underlying issues do I have to feel hurt, angry, defensive or whatever? That way I find out a lot about myself.

From all I know about you (which unfortunately is not as much as I would like to yet) I know that you come from a really good place and it is there for anybody to notice. In my book you can never do wrong intentionally. You have great instincts, a big heart and an intelligent brain. Use it for your sisters good and you do yourself the biggest favour.

As for your brother, I am not sure what to say. Aside from feeling really sorry for him and your family. But what I know beyond doubt is, that everybody is his own forger of destiny (GlÃ¼ckes Schmied). I do not know how to say it right in English. He is responsible for his actions and so is your family. Sick or not. It seems he is by himself enough to still make his own decisions.

So concentrating on your own well-being, is definitely the right choice. Maybe one day soon, when your family really sees the changes in you they discover that changing their own ways might not be a bad idea.

I hope you have a just as beautiful sunny Christmas day as we have here.

### Inger — 2012-12-26T07:54:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-68419>

Charlotte.. thank you for your insight! What I have come to recently is that the fact that someone is my sister or brother physically means nothing. It is weird but I do not feel tied to some of my siblings anymore. It is not sad, it is just..nothing. I need to move on.

I have been far too tied to family bounds, that have been my weakness and have caused me pain that was not productive at all. I know this sounds cruel somehow.. but there are individuals that have a spirit that destroys instead of building up. I have realized it is wasted time to focus on them. Wasted time. I need to use every minute of my life for creating.. creating something beautiful! It is so valuable to me. I am not letting people close in my life that tear me down (they are not coming close either when I speak the truth..hmm I have realized and that is when I get even worse in their eyes but I have learned to not care one bit about living up to others expectations.. ### Charlotte — 2012-12-26T10:35:05+0200

I totally understand, Inger. After all, you are entitled to choose your family. That is what I do too. ### Charlotte — 2013-01-03T15:07:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-69179>

I also want to share a story about love. A little bit different though.
One of my oldest friends whom I met 35 years ago met her husband through me. It was love at first sight and they got married and have 3 beautiful children, of which the oldest daughter already has 2 children of her own. After 30 years of marriage, her husband started a relationship with another woman. At first secretly, then he could no longer hide it. My friend was devastated, scared, hurt beyond belief. At the same time, she got a real bad infection in her leg, broke her hand and developed a real painful osteoarthritis and then her Mom died who she had been taking care of. A big burden all at once. She went through all the stages of anger, frustration, helplessness, denial, you name it. I stood by her as much I could trying always to keep her focus on herself, not on her husband.
To make a long and painful story short. This Christmas, three years after the incident, she invited her still husbands girlfriend into her house to spend Christmas with her and her husbands children and grandchildren. Why did she do it? Her goal was, to not let resentment, hurt, pride or anything else be in her way as she knew that this was her way of healing and accepting things as they are and loving herself and him for what they have become. She now has a better relationship with her husband than in many years. They still live in the same house, work together, spend free time with each other, but do not live together anymore as husband and wife.
I admire her for not trying to destroy the family, the work they have done together, the friendships they have created but bring it to a new level which is carried by mutual respect and love for each other and their family.
Love has many faces and this is one that touches me at my core as one must reach deep inside into the darkest place and fill the void with abundance of love. She has always been a very beautyful woman, but now she is absolutely radiant.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-03T22:35:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-69251>

I think this is a lot more common than people want to know.

### fitness@home — 2013-01-04T10:02:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-69282>

Charlotte, kudos for being such a good supportive friend!

In a "past life" (that's how I think of it now) I experienced a similar betrayal from a loved one. It really made me ill...my body expressed it as horrible IBS, anxiety, insomnia, etc. Unlike your friend, I could not accept the circumstances.

My healing process included learning to let go of the anger and negative thoughts. It took well over six months to heal myself. Part of that healing was with a Naturopath that also practiced EFT - - it really helped. I would visualize those negative emotions as a huge dirty ball of string sitting in my stomach and start tapping. By the end of each session the negative energy was greatly reduced.

I wish your friend well in the path she has chosen.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-04T10:47:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-69288>

okay, I try to explain. My friends life is in transition ( like everybody elses too). Where she will go eventually neither she nor anyone else knows and it is not important,
Anna, why the husband does not live with his girlfriend, we do not know, neither my friend nor me (maybe not even he himself) and that is not the point. And these are questions, that only he can ask himself and know the answer. The point of this story to me and her is, that she could work through all that despair and come out as a free and more loving person than before. She lives in that house still because it is her home, that she has created for herself, him and their family. The children have fond memories of their upbringing there and she does not want to take it away from them, but not even that is the point. SHE IS FREE! And that is the point. There are no questions like: where are you going, when are you coming. He is now a dear friend who has his own life and so has she. As bad as it all was at the time, now she is greatful, because it was a great opportunity and she seized it, to work through all of HER issues and come out of it stronger than ever, And in the process she learned so much about herself. She also realized that there is much more to love than just the personal. Envy, hatred, jealousy, greed and all those emotions, with which we have to deal all have the same basic emotion which is fear. This is why it is so important to face our fears and go there the minute we detect it and examine it. As NOBODY can really love as long as there is fear. Pure love (at least in my book) is undestroyable as the element of fear is not part of the equation. If we talk about love as we know it from our upbringing, it is always connected to conditions. Love me, but me alone, do that and I love you, and soon love turns into hate when the conditions are not met. To me, that is not love. Love is unconditional, makes no demands, doesn't collect debts, and is not even selective. I know that this is a tall order and we are mostly not able to live up to it, but deep in my heart I know that this is true, because I have felt it, and on the rare occasions that happened this love was just expanding from inside in all directions including and embracing ALL, the so called good and the bad, equally. In these moments the world was a magical place and I cherish these moments and they have burned into my memory as that which I strive for. So this is the place my friend came to and the love for her husband does not have that personal aspect anymore. And so to me that is a story of true love.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-04T10:49:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-69289>

When faced with extreme anxiety or stress from life my best advice to help you through it? Stand in the ocean when it is 50-55 degrees with your feet firmly planted in the sand.........you will ground yourself to improve your autonomic nervous system faster than anything that I know of. It is amazing. Once done, then walk on the beaches edge with feet in water. I have told many an educational consultant this......I do it every time I am near a body of salt water. It combines, CT, earthing, and Mg floatation to lower HS crp and improve LR and mitochondrial function in nanoseconds because of spin.

### DarleenMB — 2013-01-04T12:39:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-69307>

Charlotte, that was beautifully said. In my opinion too many people think love means ownership of the loved one. The implied notion that "you belong to me and me only" just sets people up for heartache. You cannot hold love in a closed fist -- it will kill it quicker than anything. an open hand and an open heart makes love blossom.

It sounds to me as if your friend's husband still loves her. It IS possible to love more than one person at a time.

### curves64 — 2013-01-04T15:11:29+0200

In the process, she found freedom -- freedom to be happy no matter what the external events. Happiness is an inside job. Surely, this change in perspective will bring more love into her life. Bravo!

### Inna — 2017-12-10T21:24:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-229962>

I found this thread - and yes Love is one of life’s most important healers.

David was and remains the love of my life, this was my third marriage, we very together for just 10 years, and I consider this time the best time of our lives. Unfortunately David is no longer alive, several years ago he fall victim of a senseless murder while visiting Belize. He wanted us to move there to live off the grid and to be close To nature. I was not there at the time, one of our numerous adventures, that I did not get to share, which saved my life, but not the devastation I felt having to continue life without David’s physical presence.

I came across the picture book that David’s daughter made for his birthday before his death, where David’s family wrote something for him, it was a beautiful book weaving a story of my husband and life well lived, love was palpable on those pages.

Here is my note written for David’s anniversary.

“My dear David,

I want to wish you a Very Happy Birthday.

It is hard to believe that you are approaching the age of maturity and yet also growing younger and younger in your heart.

Words can hardly described how much I love you and how blessed I feel to share life together. It is as though we met just – and yet if feels like I known you all my life.

As I backtrack in my memory – I remember the day years ago - I put personal ad in match.com saying: “I wish there would be a mathematical formula for the relationship”. Your response was very enthusiastic: “I love math and will be happy to help you work on that formula.”

We promptly arranged to meet in a Thai restaurant. You were 10 minutes late and just when I was about to leave you rushed in and the first thing you said after you caught your breath was: “I am glad you have waited!”

Indeed I am very glad that I have waited. Little did I know that from that time on I would be on a fast track for having life adventures. Life with you has been exhilarating, exciting, interesting, and full of surprises. When I feel it is as good as it gets – things get even better!

I recall how you asked me to marry you – and of course I said “yes, though we’ll have to wait.” You were very patient with me. You asked my father “for my hand” and my dad said looking at you with incredulity: “Why are you asking me, ask her!”

You blended so well with my Russian - Jewish family – as though you grew up in our culture. After being together for several years – and starting to look very alike – we decided it was time to get married. Within several days of that decision you arranged for us to have a Jewish wedding – and it was so sweet and meaningful. Later my family declared you “the most Jewish man in the family” since you were the only man in my family who had a traditional Jewish wedding. Needless to say I was very proud.

Life gets better and better. Our relationship has been a great teacher. And this is what I love about being with you – so many opportunities to learn and grow.

Thank you for sharing your life with me. I so appreciate your wonderful humor, your love and resilience through all this years, your integrity. I appreciate you helping me to grow and to be a better person.

I recall how I thanked your parents Don and Ardis for wonderful job they did with you. Don humbly remarked that you are still work in progress.

I wish you many adventures and selfishly hope we will have many years together.

We even talked about sharing a room in the nursing home – that would not stop us from having fun together. Being with you fills my heart with joy and light – and any place we are together feels like home.

Love, Inna”

### caroline — 2017-12-11T00:37:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-229967>

Thankyou Inna for sharing such a beautiful and personal story with us.

This is a pretty remarkable thread I think - I love it.

I think love is the centre of the universe - in whatever form it takes. Love lites us up and gives us courage and wisdom and a softness and boundless energy and the desire to be a better person.... and moves us forward in countless ways.

And so much more fulfilling when we are mitochondriacs!

I just read back thru this thread .....so, so many gorgeous stories.

My life has changed dramatically since my post ......we do indeed turn a corner and everything changes. I was lucky enuf to meet a remarkable man and we are on this journey together.

Funny thing - I didn't know I was looking for him .....but he was looking for me! I am lucky beyond words.

### Kari Dunlop — 2022-10-03T03:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-316259>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=69289)
>
> When faced with extreme anxiety or stress from life my best advice to help you through it? Stand in the ocean when it is 50-55 degrees with your feet firmly planted in the sand.........you will ground yourself to improve your autonomic nervous system faster than anything that I know of. It is amazing. Once done, then walk on the beaches edge with feet in water. I have told many an educational consultant this......I do it every time I am near a body of salt water. It combines, CT, earthing, and Mg floatation to lower HS crp and improve LR and mitochondrial function in nanoseconds because of spin.

I so love this Rx!!! Can't wait to get my toes in that chilly, salty water

And I love all of the love in this thread. Thank you [@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) for recently sharing the link with me. While my 'hell-th' journey has taken so much from me in many ways over the last 30 years, perhaps the biggest has been in the realm of relationships. I know there were so many parts of me that weren't ready. I had lots to figure out. I've never married, no kids but do have a wonderful kitty. All that to say, I was kind of crazy in relationships. I never felt at peace. Yet, ironically, it was through my health journey that I was led in directions I never new existed and now understand myself in ways that I never thought I could. And because of that, I've healed so many of those neurotic parts of myself

I also I never really had a self. My core was kind of like Swiss cheese and thankfully I now understand where that all came from. And now I'm happy to say I'm gouda...or 'good-ya'! I am far more kinder to myself and I am also not as triggered by things and people like I used to be. As I say in the work I do...I''ve come home to myself.

I haven't had a relationship in 20+ years. I recently crossed paths with someone who I would say was my first love and haven't seen in quite some time but I had the unimaginable gift of hearing things that I never would have anticipated hearing in 1000 lifetimes. I'm not sure what will come of our story. We don't live in the same city.

And [@Charlotte](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/3171/)'s words reminded me of something I learned years ago from A Course In Miracles and Marianne Williamson's book "A Return to Love"...the difference between holy and special relationships. Special being a riff on Tom Cruise's infamous line from Jerry McGuire 'you complete me' (ugh) and a holy relationship is where we are free to experience unconditional love regardless of what form it takes. Love is the only Truth. It is the remedy. It is our medicine. I'm just getting the opportunity to put it into practice again.

I used to get terribly anxious when good things happened to me. Like they couldn't be trusted or they wouldn't last. I was always surprised when going on a holiday and the plane landed. Every time. Now, I'm working on all of the things... all things circadian health and the leptin Rx.... from sunrises to sunsets and everything in between. I am open to more of the good things.... whatever they may look like.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-love-of-your-life-say-something-about-it-them.4860/post-316260>

so so proud of you Kari x

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11

### Michael — 2012-11-15T09:26:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64428>

A video for an "East Seasonably" campaign from 2009.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;AOin-1SyLtE\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AOin-1SyLtE\[/video\]\[/video\]

### caroline — 2012-11-15T11:25:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64432>

nice - do you have a garden?

### Michael — 2012-11-15T12:04:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64433>

No, unfortunately not. We had them growing up. My father would grow vegetables and soft fruit, and we had apple trees. We used to collect the elderflowers off the elder tree and make wine - elderberries with grape juice were better, though - made a deep red, strongly flavoured wine.

It's interesting from the point of view of social change. Gardens are dying a little, despite the popularity of TV gardening shows. (A parallel with cooking? The less people do it the more they view it on TV.)

At one time every farm labourer had an allotment he raised vegetables in AFTER a day in the fields. Now people don't want to spend a few minutes after a day sitting in the office. Rich people sell off part of their large gardens for builders to erect new houses on; the less well-off concrete down their smaller plots to park cars on.

I think there are some huge issues around how we get our food (many of them covered in that manifesto I linked). But what's the "paleo" movement doing? Discussing only eating in a 5 hour window, "carb back-loading", only eating this or that single food, "six packs" (silly term), or a host of other trivia.

### caroline — 2012-11-15T12:32:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64434>

You give everything so much thought".......impressive

### Michael — 2012-11-15T13:11:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64440>

It's interesting that people used to grow quite a lot, if they had the room at all. Not that everyone ate well in the past - they didn't - but they grew what they could themselves:

> Trees fill up every available space and corner—apple trees, pear trees, damsons, plums, bullaces—all varieties. The cottagers seem to like to have at least one tree of every sort. These trees look very nice in the spring when the apple blossom is out, and again in the autumn when the fruit is ripe. Under the trees are gooseberry bushes, raspberries, and numbers of currants. The patches are divided into strips producing potatoes, cabbage, lettuce, onions, radishes, parsnips; in this kitchen produce, as with the fruit, they like to possess a few of all kinds. There is generally a great bunch of rhubarb. In odd corners there are sure to be a few specimens of southernwood, mugwort, and other herbs; not for use, but from adherence to the old customs. The "old people" thought much of these "yherbs," so they must have some too, as well as a little mint and similar potherbs.

[http://www.gutenberg.org/files/27516/27516-h/27516-h.htm](http://http://www.gutenberg.org/files/27516/27516-h/27516-h.htm)

It's interesting that even in towns and cities people used to have more of a DIY attitude to food, too. Do recall the Sherlock Holmes story The _Blue Carbunckle_? If you do, you'll rememeber that they were raising geese in London backyards! I think there used to a lot of that kind of thing in British cities (veg, chickens), but "slum clearance" - which often seems to have meant replacing terraced housing stock that wasn't all that bad with tower blocks that rapidly became unlivable - in the 1960s finished that.

### caroline — 2012-11-15T13:35:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64447>

Now that you say that - a lot of people in Australia have gardens, some fruit trees and chickens in their back yard. Not unusual at all. It used to freak me out when we first moved here to see chickens every where. A lot of people fish as well. I think we kind of eat seasonally too - at least a little. Fruit and veg can be very expensive out of season. A very, very tiny basket of blueberries will often be $8.95. We can easily get grass fed beef and good pastured butter from New Zealand.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T13:41:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64448>

Michael where do you live now?

### Michael — 2012-11-15T13:49:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64452>

> **Jack Kruse;66445 wrote:** Michael where do you live now?

Still in England, Jack. South Coast.

### Michael — 2012-11-15T14:02:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64455>

> **caroline;66444 wrote:** Now that you say that - a lot of people in Australia have gardens, some fruit trees and chickens in their back yard. Not unusual at all. It used to freak me out when we first moved here to see chickens every where. A lot of people fish as well. I think we kind of eat seasonally too - at least a little. Fruit and veg can be very expensive out of season. A very, very tiny basket of blueberries will often be $8.95. We can easily get grass fed beef and good pastured butter from New Zealand.

I guess space is not at such a premium in Australia. And I suppose the habit hasn't been broken, so people just kept doing it.

Once something's lost it's hard to get it back, isn't it? If you see your parents looking after the chickens as you grow up, it's no leap in dark to do the same: you must know dozens of things about it that you don't even know you know and don't have to ask anyone about.

### caroline — 2012-11-15T14:20:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64459>

> **Michael;66452 wrote:** I guess space is not at such a premium in Australia. And I suppose the habit hasn't been broken, so people just kept doing it.
>
>
>
> Once something's lost it's hard to get it back, isn't it? If you see your parents looking after the chickens as you grow up, it's no leap in dark to do the same: you must know dozens of things about it that you don't even know you know and don't have to ask anyone about.

Absolutely true. My grandsons are growing uo with a garden and they are only little - 7 & 9 and they have been fishing with their Dad for years. They can identify all kinds of fish and I suspect they could clean one if they had to. It is second nature for them. Wonderful isn't it?

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T18:02:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-seasonably.4862/post-64507>

> **Michael;66449 wrote:** Still in England, Jack. South Coast.

You know what grows great there, with little need for space and maintenance? Kale...and it lasts all winter. You just strip the bottom leaves as it grows taller. It's a biennial plant, meaning it doesn't go to seed until second summer.

# Thread: Architecture and Neuroscience
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/architecture-and-neuroscience.4875/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### maggie — 2012-11-15T20:45:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/architecture-and-neuroscience.4875/post-64544>

I was really interested in the anecdote about Jonas Salk visiting an Italian monastery at a time when his research was at an impasse. He always claimed later that there was something about the architecture of the place that cleared his mind.

Corridors of mind: Could neuro scientists be the next great architects?

[http://www.psmag.com/culture/corridors-of-the-mind-49051/](http://http://www.psmag.com/culture/corridors-of-the-mind-49051/)

Today, the near 10-year-old Academy of Neuroscience for Architecture believes that neuroscience could make scienceâ€™s greatest contribution to the field of architecture since physics informed fundamental structural methods, acoustic designs, and lighting calculations in the late 19th century. In September, the academy held its first national conference at, fittingly, the Salk Institute, in La Jolla, California.

... research in the late 1990s by neurobiologist Fred Gage and other scientists confirmed the process of adult â€œneurogenesis.â€￾ New neurons continue to be born throughout life, particularly in the hippocampus, the part of your brain that processes new information on its way to being stored as long-term memories. This means that your capacity to add new memories and learn new skills can continue to expand. And how fast these cells are added seems directly influenced by the richness of our interactions with our environment. When Gage introduced these findings to architects at the American Institute of Architectsâ€™ 2003 convention, he pronounced an idea that is still sinking in: â€œChanges in the environment change the brain, and therefore they change our behavior.â€￾

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-15T21:12:48+0200

# Thread: true or false?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/true-or-false.4880/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-16T02:43:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/true-or-false.4880/post-64607>

The total amount of sugar present in the blood can support resting activity not more than about 40 minutes. Just walking increases glucose use to a point where the entire blood content is used up in about 15 minutes. Since mental activity is completely dependent upon stable blood glucose levels, there must be a way of smoothing out blood glucose levels. This is one of the major duties of the liver, which normally can produce around 200 mg of glucose per hour.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T02:59:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/true-or-false.4880/post-64611>

[http://www.medbio.info/horn/time%201-2/carbohydrate\_metabolism.htm](http://http://www.medbio.info/horn/time%201-2/carbohydrate_metabolism.htm)

### ruby — 2012-11-16T05:31:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/true-or-false.4880/post-64652>

yep, but that's the only mention I could find.

0.2mg glucose/hour - amazing how little can keep you going. How can then USDA recommend gorging on 130g of carbs per day?

# Thread: WIN For Seafood Diet - Possible Cure For Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/win-for-seafood-diet-possible-cure-for-cancer.4885/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-16T07:51:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/win-for-seafood-diet-possible-cure-for-cancer.4885/post-64673>

.

Wow- pass me the seafood... anothe win for eatting lots of food from the sea. Great research showing Seaweed maybe a cure for cancer. Here's the article:

Seaweed - New Research Points To Possible Cancer Cure

The answer for cancers of the immune system and other forms of cancer may have been found in an extract of common brown seaweed according to research presented at the AACR Dead Sea International Conference on Advances in Cancer Research. At the conference, researchers from the Hashemite University in Jordan reported that brown seaweed contains the **compound fucoidan which kills cancer tumors**.

The researchers used an extract of the seaweed on lymphoma cell lines cultivated in the laboratory and found that the extract suppressed lymphoma growth while leaving healthy cells intact. The researchers also noted a significant increase in apoptosis, or cellular death, in lymphoma cancer cells.

Lymphoma is an immune system cancer and it is divided into two classes, Hodgkinâ€™s and non-Hodgkinâ€™s, which are in turn further classified into B-cell and T-cell groups.

â€œSome forms of B-cell lymphoma are especially resistant to standard treatment and thus new therapies are needed,â€ said Mohammad Irhimeh, Ph.D., assistant professor of hematoncology and stem cells at the Hashemite University in Jordan. â€œIn this study, we looked at a new treatment strategy using novel active compounds derived from a natural source â€” seaweed.â€

Irhimeh said that research would continue to study the mechanism of fucoidan with the goal of conducting phase II or III clinical trials with human volunteers.

Previously, seaweeds containing fucoidan have been found to have anti-tumor activity in mice and some cell lines and Japanese researchers at the Biomedical Research Laboratories and the Research Institute for Glycotechnology Advancement found that seaweeds containing Fucoidan caused various types of established cancer cell lines to self-destruct. Examples of cancer cells that self-destructed due to fucoidan included certain kinds of leukemia cells, stomach cancer cells, and cancer cells of the descending colon.

About 4 percent of the total dry weight of many types of brown seaweed is made up of the polysaccharide known as Fucoidan. Fucoidan is a sulfated polysaccharide that has a complex structure. Its main components include a sulfuric esterified L-fucose, a healing sugar, and the trace elements of galactose, xylose, and glucuronic acid.

Fucoidan has been touted as one of the oceanâ€™s greatest treasures. Among the many benefits reported for fucoidan in addition to being an anti-tumor agent are: helps modulate the immune system, supports normal cellular health, supports blood circulation to native body cells, act as an anti-contraceptive, helps reduces cholesterol levels and supports healthy joint mobility at all ages. In addition, it is believed that fucoidan may help stimulate immune response when the body is attacked and may help regenerate healthy skin tissue.

The people of Okinawa, Japan enjoy some of the highest life expectancies in Japan and also consume one of the highest per capita amount of the seaweed kombu. The cancer death rate in Okinawa is the lowest of all the prefectures in Japan.

Fucoidanâ€™s anti-cancer properties may also be good news for dogs and cats. Lymphoma is one of the most prevalent cancers in dogs and cats, and as noted above, lymphoma is one of the types of cancer which fucoidan has been found to be the most effective.

# Thread: Surprising genetic link between kidney defects and neurodevelopmental disorders in ki
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surprising-genetic-link-between-kidney-defects-and-neurodevelopmental-disorders-in-ki.4889/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-11-16T12:33:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surprising-genetic-link-between-kidney-defects-and-neurodevelopmental-disorders-in-ki.4889/post-64692>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/surprising-genetic-link-between-kidney-defects-and-neurodevelopmental-disorders-in-kids-14986](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/11/surprising-genetic-link-between-kidney-defects-and-neurodevelopmental-disorders-in-kids-14986)

> Congenital defects of the kidney and urinary tract account for nearly 25 percent of all birth defects in the US and are present in about 1 in every 200 births. Eventually, an evaluation for genomic alterations will be part of the standard clinical workup. Patients with congenital kidney disease—who are currently lumped into one category—will be placed in subgroups based on their genetic mutations and receive a more precise diagnosis.
>
>
>
> “This changes the way we should handle these kids,” said kidney specialist Ali Gharavi, MD, associate professor of medicine at CUMC, associate director of the Division of Nephrology, and an internist and nephrologist at NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital.
>
>
>
> “If a physician sees a child with a kidney malformation, that is a warning sign that the child has a genomic disorder that should be looked at immediately because of the risk of neurodevelopmental delay or mental illness later in life,” he said. “This is a major opportunity for personalizing medical care. As we learn which therapies work best for each subgroup, the underlying genetic defect of the patient will dictate what approach to take.”

There is no mention of kidney function and keto, but it should be obvious.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-16T15:22:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surprising-genetic-link-between-kidney-defects-and-neurodevelopmental-disorders-in-ki.4889/post-64722>

This goes back to the heart palpitations thread........if the kidney is impacted the heart will be because the kidney controls the electrolyte balance of the body which in turn the heart relies upon.......if one is broken it affects the development and the physiologic function of the organ that relies upon it.

> It's not about the details of how to change, it's about realizing you could possibly have a different life than the one you have now. Can we all? I doubt it. For him, what's he going to do/have in his life that gives him more satisfaction?

For some reason, this has hit me and has really resonated with me. Just wanted to thank you for putting it in such a clear way.

Isn't it strange that many Prevention magazine for Diabetes have some kind of desert on the cover page?

I was just commenting on that to my husband yesterday when we saw those magazines by the cash register.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-19T16:09:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giggle.4896/post-65324>

> **Destiny;67316 wrote:** Isn't it strange that many Prevention magazine for Diabetes have some kind of desert on the cover page?
>
> I was just commenting on that to my husband yesterday when we saw those magazines by the cash register.
>
>
>
> No wonder many people with diabetes think that it is OK to eat deserts.

They all have the same parent company, and it's a business. I think the old adage of "Follow The Money" certainly is in play.

I like seeing all the newer companys that have a "do the right thing" vibe. . and part of their reason for being is a real service - could just be the boulder effect, we have TONS of local companies that produce the highest quality products and have a different manufacturing model - and don't seem to suffer from the same disease that large corporations get.

Of course, in Boulder, the "royal we" don't blink at paying $8 for a jar of fermented veggies or $10 for a small container of salsa and $15 for a pack of bacon.

It looks like there are cities cropping up everywhere that this new vibe is taking hold. . .Such a catch-22 though, you can't have equality - it doesn't exist in these things, and the "have not's" want to eat too. Short of Robin Hood sneaking in to re-distribute pastured bacon the riff raff NEEDS those cheap and subsidized products.

My hope is that more education will create more demand, and eventually instead of corn subsidies and god only knows what else government hidden support will actually go to creating food that won't kill.

The food we provide for the masses is criminal. There are no options for them to use their freedom and go out forge a living off the land and have a happy and satisfied life. It's slavery hidden as welfare handouts.

\*\*\*\*apologize if unintelligible today, major head infection and I can't be bothered to go back and proof/edit!

### ealachan — 2012-11-19T17:04:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giggle.4896/post-65339>

> **Destiny;67316 wrote:** Isn't it strange that many Prevention magazine for Diabetes have some kind of desert on the cover page?
>
> I was just commenting on that to my husband yesterday when we saw those magazines by the cash register.
>
>
>
> No wonder many people with diabetes think that it is OK to eat deserts.

It actually makes perfect sense. They're preying on the feeling of deprivation that comes along with a diagnosis of T2D and being told "no more sweets for you". When someone who feels like that sees a magazine advertising some kind of miracle sneaky way to get around the "no more sweets for you" rule, of course they're going to buy it.

Also, FWIW, my husband is T2D and he does fine with dark chocolate for dessert. Am I going to go and bake him a pan of brownies? Nope. But just because you're T2D doesn't mean you can't ever have dessert again.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-19T18:17:48+0200

### DavidL — 2015-11-27T20:33:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-dna-repair.4912/post-182746>

[http://thedailyorbit.com/how-plants-protect-themselves-from-too-much-sunlight/](http://thedailyorbit.com/how-plants-protect-themselves-from-too-much-sunlight/)

### DavidL — 2015-11-27T20:34:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-dna-repair.4912/post-182747>

Many [parts](http://thedailyorbit.com/how-plants-protect-themselves-from-too-much-sunlight/#) of this stress-response system have remained a mystery to plant biologists. Scientists have long tried to figure out how the alarm signal gets passed on from the chloroplast (the pigment that picks up the sunlight first) to the genes inside the cell’s nucleus.

Earlier studies have shown that the signal starts inside the chloroplast from the photosynthesis chain (the series of reactions that uses sunlight to make food) specifically from a protein called plastoquinone (PQ). PQ passes electric signals from one part of the plant’s food factory to another.

When the amount of sunlight is just right, [photosynthesis](http://www.redorbit.com/topics/photosynthesis/) proceeds smoothly.

When there is too much or too little light, however, the signal stops with PQ, keeping it in a reduced state – where it is devoid of oxygen. The piling up of reduced PQ molecules is the first sign that something is wrong.

Now, researchers have uncovered the missing link between this pool of reduced PQ and the genes: a set of proteins called heat-shock proteins.

Plants normally produce heat-shock proteins under stress, when the outside temperature shoots up – as their name suggests. These proteins latch on to specific parts of the plant’s DNA and start or stop production of proteins that protect or damage the cell, respectively.

Under excess light, the presence of the reduced PQ pool also triggers the release of heat-shock proteins, the researchers found. These proteins move from the outer area of the cell into the nucleus. Once inside, they bind to a specific gene and boost production of a key enzyme that breaks down toxic compounds.

The plant’s stress response is so quick that excess light alone is enough to trigger the release of the heat-shock proteins, even if there is no change in heat, the researchers observed from plants growing in a temperature-controlled room.

Plants reacted in a similar manner when they were also treated with low light and herbicide, albeit not as quickly as when they were under excess light, the researchers found.

# Thread: The Change Rx
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-change-rx.4919/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-11-18T14:01:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-change-rx.4919/post-65114>

For some reason, the blog won't let me post. First says my message is too long. Then when I break it up, it times out. So I thought I would post it here instead:

Good food for thought, Jack. One thing that stood out to me in this article was the following: "Good 'personal health estate planning' documents make them explicit so that you can track them over time and make adjustments based upon your outcomes and how the data may evolve our beliefs."

As Peter Drucker said, "What gets measured, gets managed."

If you want to be in business, you need a business plan. I will say that again: if you want to be in business, you need a business plan. All of us here are in business, whether we like to recognize it or not. It's just that some of us have profitable businesses, while others, not so much.

I belong to other health forums. One in particular has a very mainstream bent. As such, the function of that forum is to be one big shoulder to cry on for members who feel badly for one reason or the other. We all need that emotional outlet, but I don't see much improvement in member health as a result of just venting. What we do here, and on other Paleo blogs for that matter, is real business; and as such, it deserves the attention that will make that business successful.

I work daily in an environment that is constantly planning, evaluating and revising. From vision, mission and values, to balanced score card, 6-sigma and performance management, we are constantly referring back to our plan. If the plan does not exist, the business does not exist.

I am a big fan of the quantified self movement. ([www.quantifiedself.com](http://http://www.quantifiedself.com)) What gets measured gets managed.

I also am a fan of Tim Ferris, who, in the Four Hour Work Week, notes that really successful people are those who are always "tinkering" and refining.

Furthermore, I absolutely love the Khan Academy ([www.khanacademy.org](http://http://www.khanacademy.org)) where you too can become a polymath.

We are doing something important here. So I really second all of what Jack has said in this post. Make it real. Make it stick. Move it forward.

Best of luck and health to all.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-18T16:03:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-change-rx.4919/post-65121>

i posted it for you at the blog.

# Thread: Happy Anniversary to Dr. Kruse and all Starfish!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/happy-anniversary-to-dr-kruse-and-all-starfish.4934/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-11-19T02:43:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/happy-anniversary-to-dr-kruse-and-all-starfish.4934/post-65244>

Although this is my very first post, I've been visiting this site daily for one year today. It's been the most wonderful, healthy, hopeful year! I read every post and comment, and implemented each protocol as it was introduced--Leptin Reset, supplements, CT, EpiPaleo. I have lost 100 pounds, resolved pain in my hips and knees that had almost immobilized me, improved my memory and concentration, and regained my joie de vivre. I could never have accomplished this without the community you all have created here. It's something quite unique and powerful in the world of healthcare, I think. As a nurse, I had been feeling very pessimistic about how we care for people in this country. But your musings about personal responsibility for health have restored my optimism--or maybe it's just all the oysters! Anyway, I'm deeply grateful to you all. All best wishes!

# Thread: Low fruit, and bones to gnaw on
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2012-11-19T17:23:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65352>

I've been reading (upon my wife's request) Joe Dispenza's book, "Breaking the Habits of Being Yourself" and it has some good stuff. I have really seen myself and some long-held habits that I engage in with my thinking.

This book, along with Bruce Lipton's, "Biology of Belief", left me with the impression (and especially my wife and her friends) that my thoughts and beliefs control ALL my bodily processes - hormones, peptides, etc.) and say next to nothing about all the dozens of life forms that take up residence inside us and wreak havoc, or the poor dietary decisions we humans make.

But those authors kinda leave a lot of meat on the bone when it comes to esoteric hormones, neuropeptides, etc. It addresses low-hanging fruit like cortisol and oxytocin (stress and sex, both big book sellers) and does not talk much about all the other body processes that are affected by the environment and not so directly by your thoughts.

Having thoughts of a lion chasing you is easy to overcome (relatively) and thus one can lower their cortisol. TGF-beta? Not so much.

She and her friends are all about controlling their bodies with their minds and think they can do things that are perhaps outside the biological parameters of humans. I see my woman in her seventh year of chronic nasal drippage and the constant coughing to clear it. And her Pe/E ratio? Terrible. Thyroid/Cortisol? Trashed. The few tests I've seen of her's are all low, and in the bad way. I want to help so bad it hurts. I've offered but she announced that she is going to "walk her talk" and I totally respect that. I hope she makes it.

But what about a spider or tick bite, or a toxin from a reef fish? Or, what about leptin, gherlin, et al? And what are affects when we eat a lousy Western diet? Or, if we stay up and stare into a computer screen, can one overcome the effects light has on a eutherian mammal with their mind and it won't bother them? Do some people have that power?

Did my thoughts cause the reactions I've been experiencing on and off my whole life, or was is a neurotoxin or a lousy genetic download from my sick mother? A mold or bacteria has just as much right to live as my a$$ does, so I have no resentment building up cortisol wackiness and the resulting hormonal imbalances.

And what about genetic abnormalities passed down to us? Sometimes I think that people think we are born perfect and vibrant and it's our "decision" to get bodily wonky. I have had so many people point out my "responsibility" by the way I think but nothing about what other influences the world out there offers up in the way of incompatibility.

So where am I going with is?

I guess I'd like some thoughts on how the world works and how I can best optimize myself. Over and over this group that my wife hangs with offers up the phrase brought on by the quantum physics experiment where the,"observer influences the experiment" referring to the particle/wave experiments where whatever the scientists were looking for is what the experiment resulted in. I hear it often from Chopra, Braden, and others who are writing about this.

Any thoughts from you quantum eggheads?

Martin

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-19T17:46:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65360>

Martin, obviously your wife's thoughts are keeping her from actions that can help her.

Blue light from a computer, viruses and bacteria spread to us from insects, mold genetic code are all part of the world that is deterministic. Our freedom from determinism lies in our thoughts.... Because thoughts lead to solutions and decisions. Positive thinking no doubt helps our biology, but saying that a thought changes the laws of nature is just .... Wishing.

We have to use our thoughts to navigate through a world of laws (physics, chemistry, biology,etc.) that we can't change.
