**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65364>

Well said. Thanks J!

I have tons more thoughts on this so stay tuned.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-19T18:53:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65376>

> **Martin;67369 wrote:**
>
> I guess I'd like some thoughts on how the world works and how I can best optimize myself. Over and over this group that my wife hangs with offers up the phrase brought on by the quantum physics experiment where the,"observer influences the experiment" referring to the particle/wave experiments where whatever the scientists were looking for is what the experiment resulted in. I hear it often from Chopra, Braden, and others who are writing about this.
>
>
>
> Any thoughts from you quantum eggheads?
>
>
>
> Martin

some excellent thoughts here Martin.

I agree with Riverneighbours sentiments about thoughts affecting internal vs consensual realities.

the 'observer effect' is one of the most badly misrepresentated discoveries of quantum mechanic theory. It was one of the first discoveries, and looking back one can find it's roots in Maxwells equations. (google Maxwells demon)

in it's simplest form, the concept is not hard to grasp.

If you are are measuring the location of a particle, it must interact with another particle to do so. If you measure the temperature of a cup of coffee, the temperature of the thermometer is going to affect the temperature of the coffee. Using a smaller thermometer with less thermal mass is going to change it less. Using an optical thermometer seems like the perfect solution, yet..We see the coffee by bouncing photons off of it and receptors detect the photons. The act of intercepting the photons changes the actual energy balance and hence the "temperature" of the coffee. Nowhere in physics does it say the outcome will be what the experiments were looking for. Although this is a common problem in science, and the origin of the double blind.

QM does get deeper and 'weirder' than this, with particles theoretically entangled and existing in two places at the same time, but this is the start of the woo woo Chopra et al start talking.. They tell us that quantum physics proves that we change particles just by observing them. While it is based on a true sentence, it misapplies the physics definition of observe, the intention of the statement, and is disingenuous application of the field of quantum physics. Can internal reality be changed by a thought? sure. of course. Can that change induce one to physically affect and change the external/consensual reality of course. No "woo woo Volcano God, vibrational crystal energy" required. All these pundits do is say "See? it's too complicated to understand! therefore, it's majic, praise (whatever spiritual universal force/secret/ancient wisdom/Midi-Chlorian particles) and follow me, for I know the way! Notice none of these people have any background in physics, let alone QM? They posture, and puff, but have no content behind them, just feigning expertise in the subject.

thems my thoughts.

Cheers

### Michael — 2012-11-19T19:39:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65386>

> **Martin;67369 wrote:** ... how I can best optimize myself.

I think probably like this: eat how Jack tells you; get enough sleep; allow yourself to get cold and dark (as Jack also says); don't hold cellphones to your head; do some exercise (leaning towards the sort of thing that Erwan le Corre and the Exuberant Animal people do); meditate; and perhaps do some bodywork and/or neurofeedback.

I think our great problem is that we have transformed our environment so comprehensively and so fast, mostly on the basis of short-term thinking, and certainly without thinking about how what we're doing impacts on ourselves.

MT,Â  I wasn't trying to set up a Dawkins/Lipton opposition....Â Â  as I said I haven't even read Liption and I only pulled one idea of Dawkins.Â  And,Â  I've only followed the idea of memes in the philosophical writings so I think we're probably talking about completely different ideas that happen to use the same word.Â 

Selfish Gene as a whole may be obsolete but I still like the quote.Â  Even now that we know about epi genetics and don't have to feel doomed to genetic determinism we still cannot escape the fact that those switches behave based on a wide variety of inputs.Â  Affecting change for one's self means knowing more and more about the inputs that throw the switches in the gene machine.Â  Though we can change our own genes expression by acting on thoughts the genes are still a rules based system even though the inputs may be varied and complex.Â  No matter how hard I've thought about it, I still have not been able to think myself into becoming a mermaid ....Â Â Â Â there are still constraints that are determined by biologic laws that we cannot escape.Â  This part of the world still is determined for us, even if there are a lot of choices that we can make within it's constraints.

### Martin — 2012-11-25T12:20:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-fruit-and-bones-to-gnaw-on.4948/post-65560>

All interesting comments.Â 

Â 

I think people feed and follow what they have egoically developed in their lives. If they are all about "magic" and the supernatural, that feeds their imagination along those lines in which they are interested. Same with anything, I suppose.Â 

Â 

But when I have followed a religion/philosophy/movement, I tended to ignore the challenges from another viewpoint because it came up against my ego. I want to be right and have the "truth". But when something else showed up and shot holes in that viewpoint, I had to take a hard look at my considerations and usually made some changes. After years of being "fooled" by the latest and greatest info on Life to come down the pike, I guess I got a little jaded. Numerous discussions on the nature of the universe among my family and friends always left me with more questions than answers.Â 

Â 

The biggest thing in my life has been a quest for health. I have struggled, had few wins, but the chronic pain and lethargy always put me back in the game of finding a way out. I had an epiphany one day when I realized that if I compare my measurements - labs, body comp, etc. - with a healthy human individual, I could perhaps see where I was not doing well. Where to find those statistics?

Â 

That question turned out to be one of the most pivotal moments in my life. My "statue of David" moment might be overstating it a bit, but it's late and I'm going' with it. Asking that question lead me to this site and Doc's 30k foot view. And, most importantly, I realized that I can look to evolutionary biology as my guide and inspiration.

Â 

Nature, not some man-made corp/gov diet or a spiritual guru selling books and lecture packages, has become my compass.

If you have specific links please share them in a PM so I can review. For the most part if it says ACCESS DENIED then it has extra characters from the vBulletin to Simple Press migration. You should be able to remove anything after the post name to see it. The main problem is the old forum used a different database structure and some of that gets lost in translation.

Example:
http://www.jackkruse.com/forum-2/meet-greet/happy-anniversary-dr-kruse-and-all-starfish/page-3263/#p65257 will not work. It will say access denied because page-3263 doesn't really exist.

But these will. The first will go straight to that specific post and the last will show the entire thread usually starting at the beginning post.
http://www.jackkruse.com/forum-2/meet-greet/happy-anniversary-dr-kruse-and-all-starfish/#p65257
\-or-
http://www.jackkruse.com/forum-2/meet-greet/happy-anniversary-dr-kruse-and-all-starfish/

Hope that helps!

# Thread: Have some humans retained vit. C enzyme
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RubyKittany — 2012-11-22T14:35:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-65436>

In the body of a Vitamin C making mammal, the Vitamin C molecule is made from a few small modifications to the glucose molecule. While glucose is in abundant supply in humans and animals, there are four enzymes that are required to convert glucose into Vitamin C. Humans, have only three of these enzymes having lost the ability to make the fourth L-gulonolactone Oxidase somewhere in evolution. Some serious research should be undertaken to find out if indeed all humans are lacking in this critical liver enzyme. To answer the question as to why humans lost the ability to make their own Vitamin C one can only assume that because their diets at the time were abundant in Vitamin C. Humans are the only species to eat meat and be unable to produce their own Vitamin C.
In mammals which retained the ability to make Vitamin C, it is made in response to all sorts of stress, especially the stress of infection. Under stress, the normal daily amount can be quadrupled.
http://www.vitaminc.co.nz/introduction-to-vitamin-c.htm

Nishikimi and co-workers observed that the gene that codes for gulonolactone oxidase is actually present in humans, but is not active due to the accumulation of several mutations that turned it into a non-functional pseudogene (Nishikimi & Yagi 1991). Not only all humans, but also gorillas, chimps, orangutans, and some monkeys have this inborn genetic flaw, meaning that the loss of vitamin C biosynthesis must have occurred first in one of our primate ancestors. But how can something so crucial for survival be eliminated through the course of evolution?
Inclusion of vitamin C in the human diet explains why our non-synthesizing ancestors did not become extinct, as they found this an effective compensation for the mutations in the gulonolactone oxidase gene. However, biochemists speculate that there may have been some concurrent advantage of this mutation. For instance, since one of the products of the reaction catalysed by gulonolactone oxidase is hydrogen peroxide, Halliwell suggested in 2001 that the loss of biosynthesis balanced the "cost" of production, since the advantage of producing one vitamin C molecule would be lost by the production of this reactive oxygen species (Halliwell 2001).

More recently, Grano and De Tullio proposed another hypothesis, based on the studies by Knowles et al. In 2003, Knowles et al. demonstrated that vitamin C regulates a key stress-induced transcription factor called Hypoxia Inducible Factor 1Î± (HIF1Î±), a protein that, when activated, regulates the expression of hundreds of stress-related genes. Notably, the activation of HIF1Î± occurs in the absence of adequate oxygen or vitamin C supply. Grano and De Tullio therefore proposed that organisms that have lost vitamin C biosynthesis have an advantage: they can finely regulate HIF1Î± activation on the basis of the dietary intake of vitamin C (Grano & De Tullio 2007). When vitamin C supply is sufficient, the HIF transcription factor is less active than in conditions of vitamin C deficiency. In other words, the lack of vitamin C biosynthesis allows our bodies to know more about our nutritional status and consequently set the proper baseline of HIF1Î± expression. It is like a sensitive titration system.

There is a third yet still unexplored possibility. We know from other studies that pseudogenes are not inert, but can have a significant role in epigenetic control of gene expression (Poliseno et al. 2010). Could this also apply to the human gulonolactone oxidase pseudogene? Time (and much research) will tell.
http://www.nature.com/scitable/topicpage/the-mystery-of-vitamin-c-14167861

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-23T13:44:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-65443>

It is believed that Vitamin C is in active trait loss because of how iodine is now used in biochemical reactions. Vitamin C has its place but it become more important the more DHA and iodine deficient one is........say for example in an AI or an Adrenal Fatigue case.

### GlenPDQ — 2012-11-24T13:51:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-65498>

> **Jack said**
>
> It is believed that Vitamin C is in active trait loss because of how iodine is now used in biochemical reactions. Vitamin C has its place but it become more important the more DHA and iodine deficient one is........say for example in an AI or an Adrenal Fatigue case.

I'm going to try out the new form here since the question asking if any humans still retain the ability to produce vitamin C has been on my mind for awhile.

It almost sounds that when we are sufficient in DHA and iodine, then also getting adequate vitamin C should assist in a powerful process when all 3 nutrients are abundant in the body. What more important things would the body rather be creating or doing when iodine, DHA, and Vitamin C are all adequate? Are there other processes which open up?

### ruby — 2012-11-26T05:51:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-65612>

seems like we still need it:

Â Vitamin C plays a central role in the antioxidant defense system and exists in two major forms. The charged form, ascorbate, is taken up into cells via sodium-dependent facilitated transport. The uncharged form, dehydroascorbate, enters cells via glucose transporter and is then converted back to ascorbate within these cells. Because dehydroascorbate and glucose compete for glucose transporters, hyperglycemia will exclude vitamin C from the cell and result in a decreased antioxidant capacity in some cell type that is dehydroascorbate dependent.

Tubular epithelial cells are dehydroascorbate dependent. Vitamin C exclusion from tubular epithelial cells through competition of glucose and dehydroascorbate for common transport mechanism in diabetes will deprive the cells of antioxidant ability and could lead to reactive oxygen species accumulation.

Â 

http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16118484

### Glen PDQ — 2012-12-01T14:57:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-66047>

Imagine the chagrin of the diabetic cell after it uptakes a phosphatidyl choline nanosphere (liposome), unwraps it, and finds vitamin C inside!

Â 

Also, the proteolytic enzymes like bromelain from pineapple, actinidain (from kiwi), and papain (from papaya) have interesting effects like making vitamin C and some other substances more bioavailable.Â

### ruby — 2012-12-01T21:28:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/have-some-humans-retained-vit-c-enzyme.4956/post-66086>

synergy, synergy, synergy as they say in real estateÂ  { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### ColdBren — 2012-11-23T14:50:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-progesterone-trigger-hashis.4958/post-65450>

I was just reading an article by a doctor saying progesterone triggers or worsens Hashimotos. Should people with Hashi's be concerned with taking progesterone?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-23T14:54:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-progesterone-trigger-hashis.4958/post-65451>

No progesterone wont trigger hashi's but it could cause an issue if the Hashi's is so bad the liver is involved and your are using the oral route. Thankfully this is not common. Most people use the topical or vaginal route unless sleep is a big problem.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-11-27T18:57:06+0200

I do not like losing the 'Ask Jack' forum. Nearly $600 is a dear price to read the posts and ask a few questions. Especially when you are having a time of it financially.

I understand Jack gives some very expensive knowledge (it was expensive to him to become schooled in some of the medical knowledge he lets out). But some of answers were mere sugestions as to which blog post to read to get the knowledge, so then after re-reading it, you may still be in the dark or have more and new questions.

But, I did notice on the old forum many times people would register and their first post would be to Ask Jack. No sense of community evident - just straight in for the free advice. As often as not it would be a question that could either be answered by reading the blog, the blog comments (not to be underestimated!), by googling - especially questions about various properties of foods etc (!)Â -Â or by asking the starfish.

It would be good to have aÂ thread started on each blog. Starfish could help newbies there, if they're so inclined, and Jack (cos he's just a good guy like this) would probablyÂ come in from time to time and set everyone straight if they were going _too far_ off course...but then again he may not.....we may just be all part of some experiment...

### youthfuliz — 2012-11-29T22:52:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-65913>

OK here are my comments on the new forum: (obviously there're a lot of good things about the new forum, but I guess you know those already, so I'll jump to the drawbacks directly if you don't mind)

1\. the text color is too light...why don't leave it black? I think quite a few people have complained about this. I have very good eyes, but it's hard to read.
2\. the background color is too bright... a light grey/blue would do a better job - that's gonna be much easier on our eyes
3\. space on the top of the forum is not used efficiently... I use a 17" laptop, when I open this forum I don't see anything but the banner, you're logged in as... and advanced search. You want to put as much information as possible at the top so you attract random viewers. I scroll down to see what's there, but I definitely would like it better if the space is used more efficiently.

I know it's a lot of work upgrading a forum and user feedback is what you need to improve...so I contributed. Hope this helps. And keep up the good work!

by the way, won't hurt to have more smileys!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-66925>

### endless — 2012-12-09T11:40:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-66932>

### Inger — 2012-12-09T12:06:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-66935>

Endless..

It should work when pushing the quote button and then you just go a bit under the quoted text and write what you want and it should work. And if you want to quote only a part of the text, just remove what you do not want.

But.

It does not always work that way. For me at last. This is when the quoted text shows in a smaller window, not the broad one with all the extra buttons and stuff. So what I do then.. is;
I just add \[quote \] manuallly, (you have to type the letters) before the text begins that I want to quote and then I add \[/quote \] at the end. Take the space away for sure, I had to add it or it would quote what I write right here..lol This ALWAYS works.

Remove the text you do not want to quote. Or copy directly from the post the part of the text you want to quote and copy it into your reply (quoted manually).

Hope this helps.. I am not good in explaining such stuff at all..

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/things-i-dont-like-about-the-fourm.4964/post-67891>

> **bigknitwit said**
> I am having a hard time getting back into reading theÂ forum regularly and staying on top of it, because I can't figure out how to do my main browsing/updating technique. Before,Â I used to simply look at the list of new posts, and go through them. I was generally always up to date, and if not, I had a backwards log of posts I'd missed. I can't seem to find that same feature on this new website, and so I'm fumbling around with the new posts window, which automatically closes after every read. This shouldn't be a big deal, but I've found my reading patterns and frequency have changed/reduced because of it, not deliberately. Is there a way to re-create this feature that I haven't found? I feel so silly that something so small is making such a big difference for me. I can't seem to get back into a reading "groove."

What I do is when you 1st enter the forum, you see on the right # topics with unread posts.Â  Then I click on the down arrow to the right of it.Â  Then you have to scroll up a little, but you will see all the unread posts with an indicator telling you how many new messages are in each thread.

When you're done, go back to top and click on the icon next to the arrow (mark all topics as read).

# Thread: Employer provided food on Black Friday
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/employer-provided-food-on-black-friday.4967/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-24T14:58:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/employer-provided-food-on-black-friday.4967/post-65509>

My employer provided pizzas from Pizza Hut on Black Friday so employees wouldn't have to fight traffic going to and coming back from lunch and be late clocking back in. I went to the supermarket and brought back freshly steamed shrimp and tilapia while the others were eating the pizza. I can call ahead and have the seafood waiting for me. There is a bottle of olive oil in my locker I use for drizzling. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### maggie — 2012-11-24T16:42:07+0200

Glen, way to think ahead! How did your co-workers react?

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-24T18:14:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/employer-provided-food-on-black-friday.4967/post-65522>

> **maggie said**
>
> Glen, way to think ahead! How did your co-workers react?

Most of them are used to my eating habits now. I told them I would feel like crap the next day if I chowed down on pizza. Nobody responded to the statement since they were probably happy to be getting free food. The majority of them are not clued in that what they eat can affect their next two unborn generations. I mentioned that detail of epigenetics to one lady. It was a friendly atmosphere so maybe some of them were listening.

It kind of blows away the defense of those who say they can do anything they want to themselves since they aren't hurting anyone.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-29T07:38:37+0200

totally agree.......

# Thread: How do you really start this Cold Thermogenesis thing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-really-start-this-cold-thermogenesis-thing.4969/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-11-25T02:51:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-really-start-this-cold-thermogenesis-thing.4969/post-65533>

So I amÂ tryingÂ to change my lifestyle. Â I am way too fat and need to drop some serious poundage. Â I read alot of the articles on here and I want to really get a crack at adapting to cold. Â Right now I need a jacket in rooms that others wear shortÂ sleevesÂ shirts, so I know I have a long way to go.

Â 

On day 1 I filled a bowl full of water and put 3 trays of ice in it and let it cool down while I made my high protein / high fat breakfast. Â When I tried to stick my face in the water I startedÂ hyperventilatingÂ  Â I mean I could not hold my head down for 1 second in the water because my immediate need to breath in was over whelming.

Â 

So I kinda tilted my head so I could get my forehead under the water but my mouth was still out so I could breath. Â Now its 5 days later and the same thing happens, even though I have done this every day. Â I don't last even 1 second with my face fully immersed. Â 

Â 

Since this is the baby step of the whole process, does it get any better? Â Would I be better off taking a cold shower in the morning? Â My water isn't truely that cold yet so I wasn't sure if they would provide any real benefit.

Â 

Any advice?

Â 

Best Regards,

Â 

Geoff

### Sunny Cowgirl — 2012-11-25T08:06:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-really-start-this-cold-thermogenesis-thing.4969/post-65539>

Geoff, I'm still learning my around all this information, but I think I recall reading that you need to get yourself leptin sensitive first and then start trying to adapt to cold.

Perhaps it would help if you started with cool tap water, (without the ice) and when you can handle that better, gradually drop the temperature. Baby steps.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-25T08:29:06+0200

# Thread: JOY.....anyone have any lovely stories of joy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joy-anyone-have-any-lovely-stories-of-joy.4970/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2012-11-25T03:54:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joy-anyone-have-any-lovely-stories-of-joy.4970/post-65534>

Something happened today that is pretty amazing. I was at the ocean this morning playing in the water with my grandsons. It was a beautiful sunny and warm day. Yachts bobbing in the harbour. Families out for a walk. Children building sand castles. skydivers overhead ...and I had tears of joy running down my cheeks. It is amazing because I usually have tears of sadness. Today all I could see was joy and beauty everywhere and that is thanks to Dr. Kruse and all of you on this forum .....so thankyou all very much. x

### prAna303 — 2012-11-25T04:31:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joy-anyone-have-any-lovely-stories-of-joy.4970/post-65537>

I think this is what happends when we start to get rid of all the filters we have put on to ourself. Everything from fastfood, fastculture, fast whatever. When we get into our elements of nature we start to cry of joy because everything is a big part of us as species... Think how we did classical music in the past, they did have a connection to the elements.

Â 

\[video=youtube\_share;Q0DSnxHYu9Y\]http://youtu.be/Q0DSnxHYu9Y\[/video\]

### caroline — 2012-11-25T07:12:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joy-anyone-have-any-lovely-stories-of-joy.4970/post-65538>

thankyou - that is so beautiful

### Inger — 2012-11-25T08:22:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joy-anyone-have-any-lovely-stories-of-joy.4970/post-65541>

Caroline.. that was beautiful...

I cry from joy too sometimes Caroline.... And sometimes there is a mix of joy and pain I guess.. or the pain comes out in the form of tears because the joy melts the ice..? When one sees / experiences something very beautiful, like children and love, the nature, a orgasm..?

After I started to eat much raw I too felt more.. I was not so unpermeable and numb anymore? I wonder why.. I just know it does me good.

To be numb in ones heart feels not so good.. I rather take the pain, and the joy for sure!

Somehow the modern foods numbed me down..

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-25T08:55:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/joy-anyone-have-any-lovely-stories-of-joy.4970/post-65545>

The more layers of illness one peels away from from your onion the more joy your brain begins to see in the life exposed around you.........the better antenna in your head the clear your mind sees its world. Â

beautifully said Inger. To be numb is not good - I lived like that for quite awhile. Finally the sadness comes out and then the Joy. I definitely feel it is eating raw too - don't really understand it - but - intuitively it feels so right.
